nohas /noÀàhaz/ [n äÀàxaz] (of economics) right-wing, conservative

< 1751, from Arabic ŸÜÿ≠ÿßÿ≥ (nu·∏•ƒÅs) with the same sense, and originally ‚Äúcopper‚Äù. Inspired by the Copper faction in the court of Malik Hisham Al-Rashid (reigned 1601-1610) of Libya, who petitioned against the poor reforms he oversaw. The Copper, and their opposition, the Tin, took their names from the two principal colours of the Libyan banner at the time, which was quartered into two black, one white, and one orange field. These two terms ‚Äì nohas and casdir ‚Äì were borrowed into various European languages through the success of the Vascon writer David BensaiÃÅd's treatise on statecraft The Three Cities, which was translated into over twenty languages. Compare for example French nouase, kasdir.

/fal i Ààd…™k c…îj i Àåtsi noÀàhaz pa ÄÀàmi siladÀåminist ÄaÀàtsj…în Àådot…î ÄÀàtaŒ∏ me aÀàka Ä/

Mondifex 2020 - 2m Janver

Ambrose¬π III (17 March 1435 - 29 February 1518) was king of Vascony from 1456 until his death, making him the longest ruler in Vascon history. He rose to the throne with the country at its greatest extent during his reign: from Bordew in the north to Port Cal√® in the south, although in practice Vascon rule barely reached as far as Braga.

King Ambrose III pushed for naval development, establishing the New Navarre Enterprise in 1458. He aimed to capitalise on Vascon supremacy in the fishing trade, trading cod from Dunclothe and Valland throughout the Middlesea. And of course, it was just such a ship that landed on Mendevan¬≤ shores in 1471 and introduced Eurasia to the Novomund.

Despite losing Vascony's Middlesea coast to Burgundy and Barcelon, and despite never marrying, King Ambrose III is remembered well for presiding over a period of relative peace and prosperity. His death presaged the decline of Vascony; it lost over a fifth of its population in the Second Great Dying, and ceded Portingale (its economic heart, having benefited the most from Novomundine trade) to Andalus after the Romantic Wars of the seventeenth century.

‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî

¬≤that is, North American.

5m Janver - 5th January

The Brethin of Kernow [1] (Boral: Y Brethin a Cornou) were in the late medi√¶val period the most influential school of the Brethin religious society. They saw a substantial increase in membership through the twelfth century and counted among their members (Roman) Pope Damian VI (1199-1208) and the saint Victor of Dunchurch.

The Brethin (named for their distinctive heavy cloak; in Old Welsh, their bretin) were founded in Tremonnow [2], at that time in Devon, as an order devoted to record-keeping and the maintenance of history. Originally, they preserved in particular the history of the Christianisation of Albion [3] and Ireland, but quickly expanded their remit to secular history. They spread rapidly through the Briton states and later made inroads into France, Burgundy and even Andalus.

The Brethin of Kernow were notable for being a single organisation which admitted both men and women into their orders, although their institutions were segregated by gender. Some members were sent out of the monasteries to advise in courts across Europe, most (in)famously in the Crovatian [4] House of Domago, whose Duke so favoured his Kernish advisors that his siblings organised a coup against him.

[1] Roughly, Brittany.
[2] Monmouth (Welsh Trefynwy).
[3] Great Britain.
[4] ‚Ä¶ Croatian.

##


8m Janver

- excerpted from ‚ÄúIn Sickness and in Health: Medicine in pre-Arnoutszen Europe‚Äù, second edition, published by Winchester Scholastic Primers, 1961

‚Ä¶ the death of up to one-fifth of the population.

Of course, despite the name, not everywhere in Europe suffered so badly from the Second Great Dying as did Portingale. Those more inland areas, such as Boham and Burgundy (having lost its coastal territory to House Amig√≤t in 1499) endured far fewer casualties simply by virtue of being further from the main mode of transmission: naval trade. It was not by chance that the first recorded death attributable to this plague was in Damvath in Borland [1], said at the time to be the most-trafficked port city outside the Middlesea [2].

It is a common misconception, popularised further by the famous scene in Maresca's 1931 film Frutto Amaro (Bitter Fruit), that people blamed cats and dogs for the sickness sweeping the continent. This idea, first attested in connection with the Second Great Dying only several decades after the fact, perhaps originated with the tale of the monastery at Bonn, which after several deaths to an ‚Äúunknown illness‚Äù in 1512 (probably luetic pox [3]) banished all animals from the monastery and (unsuccessfully) from the neighbouring village, claiming they were a Satanic influence.

In fact, the reaction to the Dying varied significantly across Europe. The countries of Albion [4], having for the most part escaped the first continental plague the century before, and furthermore still recovering from the Wars of Fealty, were perhaps the least prepared for an epidemic, and indeed‚Ä¶

‚Äî-
[1] Damva√∞, capital city of Istr Boral, in the local language.
[2] in Europe, that is.
[3] Syphilis.
[4] Great Britain.

compagn /komÀàp…õjn/ journeyman, tradesman, associate

< Old Boral compa»ùn ‚Äúhousemate, companion‚Äù, from Late Latin compƒÅni≈ç ‚Äúcompanion, messmate‚Äù, literally ‚Äúwith-bread‚Äù (a calque from Germanic gahlaib√¥ ‚Äúcomrade‚Äù ‚Äì cf. loaf). Enters Boral either directly from Late Latin, or via Old French compaing, from the same source.

A journeyman is one who has completed an apprenticeship and is employed by a master tradesman. May refer more generally to any competent but undistinguished member of a trade, or even to the rank-and-file members of a given guild or society.

Y grant atelier sta un tape√ßment de laboriostur; compagn agen a casc tabr.

11m Janver

‚ÄúLook through the glass, into the dark! Find me a star with no name, that we might pen the first chapter of its story.‚Äù

from Durgh'nen Shorzen Shouer ‚ÄúThrough a Black Mirror‚Äù by Elsebeth Sneider, 1795

___

excerpt: New Skies, Old Stories by Simon d'Estiala, published by Prindor Marsill√®s, 1980

‚Ä¶ in her twilight years she campaigned for reforms to Saxony's inheritance law, ultimately unsuccessfully, although her political society would (after her death) spearhead the suffrage movements in Saxony, Denmark, and across the Latin republics.

The Voidtale craze sparked by Sneider's seminal work persisted until the Second German War, when its optimistic and exploratory character dropped rather rapidly out of fashion. Nonetheless there are examples generally counted as Voidtale works to be found later: most notable is Narayanan's series of landscapes depicting the surfaces of other planets orbiting our Sun. Importantly, he portrays these places as barren and inhospitable, in stark contrast to the xenic [1] worlds of Sneider or her imitators. This illustrates the shift in tone in many areas of art following the war, as industry swept the world and the age of the Global Workshop dawned.

It is perhaps foolhardy to attribute the decline of Voidtale trevolds [2] solely to the gloom of post-war Europe. We might say, for example, that the idea of exploring new lands and meeting new civilisations had lost its shine, with the opening of Caddisow [3] leaving few ‚Äúmysterious lands‚Äù to discover, and the developing steeple-mesh [4] allowing people everywhere to communicate far more rapidly (in particular, to discover that distant nations were often much less exotic than one imagined!).

In any case, when it comes to parachthon romance [5], the middle of the nineteenth century was somewhat of a nadir. Even once Europe had recovered and the general prosperity of the Workshop decades had truly got going, the‚Ä¶

[2] novels; that is, stories written primarily for entertainment.

[3] sub-Saharan state occupying much of the territory of Botswana, Zimbabwe, and southern Mozambique.

[4] optical telegraphy, in the vein of semaphore; mostly superseded in the ensuing decades by the electric telegraph.

[5] speculative fiction, such as fantasy or science fiction.

For example, Thieves' Script was a simple replacement code used to convey secret messages between Merch forces in the final years of the Wars of Fealty. It is said to have been adapted from similar systems used by gangs of housebreakers in Nottingham, though how such systems came to the attention of King Andrew's court is unclear. It worked by converting each letter into a sequence of three symbols, and is a very early forerunner to codes like steeplescript [1] or even modern one-zeffre encoding [2].

##

17m Janver

-excerpted from A Cyclop√¶dia of British Persons (third edition), by Wolfston Potter, published by the Yievel Brethin Primers, 1870

King Vigo the Magnificent (Vigo Magnificens, rex Brittonum)

The Kernish kingdoms at the dawn of the eighth century had only recently been unified, ruled by widowed Queen Maud of Angeus. Her son Vigo ascended to the throne upon her death in 720, shortly after the Catumagin Accord which ended hostilities and established an official border with the Kingdom of the Franks.

[Recall that the region now known as Kernow had at the time many names, including Armorica, Brittania and Dumnonia (this taken from the region in Albion whence the Britons had emigrated); the modern name dates from the eleventh century when the House of Kernow allied with the Normans to take the throne.]

King Vigo is remembered chiefly for spearheading the reconquest of Devon, which had been under the sole authority of the Kingdom of Wessex since the final defeat at Gaulford in 656 (although much of Cornwall had never entirely submitted to English rule). He had married Guinnumar, daughter to King David I of Wynneth, and had managed to secure Welsh assistance with the invasion.

After over two decades of intermittent warfare with Wessex and the victory at Petherton, Vigo was crowned King of Greater Devon (rex Magn√¶ Dumnoni√¶), unifying Devon and Kernow. He had at least three children: among them were Cadern the Doubter, who would become king upon Vigo's death in 761, and his daughter Iseld, who later became the first patron of the Brethin of Tremonnow, and for whom the Iseld House in Bristow is named.

20m Janver

- in translation, from the collected correspondence of Absolon Mortenszen, playwright and commentator in Alburgh, Denmark

[Penned the morning of the 18th of March, 1560 Nascenti√¶]

To the Good Sir Anscon d'Aupont,

I hope you are well recovered from the ague of which you spoke in your last missive; I need not remind you to consider leaving the city until the bulk of the trading has ended. One never knows what foreign ills merchants may bring, as my grandfather could have attested [1]‚Ä¶

The town remains as dull since you departed as it was before we met, you will be pleased to hear. In point of fact, even the decent lodging-house on the strand is poorer for having banned all the interesting people in town; perhaps it speaks poorly of me that they yet allow me entrance, though I will at least avail myself of the views while I can.

My dear brother (you may remember his shoes) is celebrating the birth of his daughter in the usual way; I continue to be astonished Beatrys consented to marry him, though she has been managing to escape him and the baby often enough to visit myself and Clara. We, you will note, have not yet succumbed to the child-bearing impulses the new friar insists we all harbour within.

There is a new friar at the hostel, less talkative and (if you will believe my wife) less handsome than Daniel. More than anything he reminds me of the moralising mentor one sees in the dreadful morality plays they still show down in the city [2]; he seems the sort to dream of visiting Rome and upon arriving declare it insufficiently Christian! One supposes Lorenzo's new Machines of Helios sculpture about which you droned on at me for hours might send the good man directly into an apoplexy, which would at least be entertaining‚Ä¶

The new play is a mild success [3], despite the inclement weather rather betraying the fact that Alburgh is not, to my regret, Barcelon. Clara has had marvellous fun with the new fabrics and dyes; please accept my sincerest gratitude for the billrod [4], although it is far too expensive to use regularly.

I leave with you my kindest regards,

A. Mortenszen

‚Äî

[1] Records suggest Absolon's grandfather lost his wife and at least two brothers in the Second Great Dying.

[2] Presumably Copenhagen; although manuscripts of the mentioned plays have not survived, similar opinions are found in other contemporaneous letters.

[3] Mortenszen understates the matter here; his High Society was received so well that the royal family altered the route of their Grand Tour to visit Alburgh and see a performance.

[4] Literally ‚Äúbeetle-red‚Äù, a crimson dye produced from the shell of an insect and imported from Lower Mendeva.

26m Janver

from ‚ÄúAn Abridged History of Northern Albion‚Äù by Aidan Donblane, published by Cloddale Primers, 1940 (with commentary by his daughter Anhart, 1980)

‚Ä¶and thus, with the aid of Harald the Blue, established the Githel Kingdom of Scotland in what had been Pictland. Records of the Picts quickly become scanty, suggesting an acceleration of the encroachment of Githel language and culture from the west, into Murray [1].

With the Northumber conquest of Eidin (modern Edinbrough) in 928, the Kingdom of Dunclothe lost the remaining vestiges of its eastern coast. However, the extended Viking in the east had left many of their eponymous Isles severely underdefended [2], and over the ensuing decades, Dunclothe under Roderick would invade many of them: first Arran in 934, and most importantly, Mann fell in 942.

While the inevitable acrimony this expansion engendered with both the Irish kingdoms and with Denmark [3] is beyond the scope of this book, we shall briefly examine its effects on Northumber, on the new Scotland, and on Wynneth to the southwest.

Relations with the Kingdom of Northumber cooled from the moment Edinbrough fell; the common enemy both states had in the Norrish (recall the conquest of Yorwick) presumably helped speed the process along. According to the relevant copies of the English Chronicle, the bride of King Radulf, who succeeded his uncle in 951, was one ‚ÄúAtre of Cheswic‚Äù; this is suspected to be the same town as Cumbrian ‚ÄúKeswick‚Äù, although whether she was a daughter of King Roderick is unclear‚Ä¶

‚Äî

[1] Recent research calls into question the then-prevailing theory that Alba (and in turn Scotland) only experienced significant Norrish influence during and after the eleventh-century migrations. Placename analysis (see F. Berger, 1977) has been correlated with dig sites and suggests that the kingdom had a significant Norrish subtrate from its inception.

[2] This is a simplification: the Norrish ascendancy through the ninth century and the beginning of the tenth had at last reached its peak, and politics further east (as well as the interference of the French Normans) diminished the resources that the Sodrick kings had at their disposal.

[3] That is, the various states making up what are now Denmark and Horth, and so on.

generally-proven

oho, elements! what elements are known already? what are their names?

  Great question! :D I imagine by, say, 1950 at latest all naturally occuring elements had been discovered (in real life the last such was rhenium in 1925), but I have only fixed the Borlish names of seventeen of them so far. Here we go:

Connu√∞ des aubon dell'hour: / Known since antiquity:

argent - silver
aur - gold
far - iron
cuivr - copper
plom - lead
stan - tin (the above six all from Latin)
azauc - mercury (from Morrove Arabic via Portingale azouco)
brunstan - brimstone [1] (from Old English brynstƒÅn ‚Äúsulfur, lit. burn-stone‚Äù, often incorrectly linked to stan ‚Äútin‚Äù.

Aer: / Airs: [2]

aquifex - aquifex [3], from Scholastic Latin aquifex ‚Äúwater-maker‚Äù.
coronal - coronal [4], from Latin cor≈çnƒÅlis ‚Äúof the crown‚Äù, referring to the corona of the Sun, where it was first detected.

ignifex - ignifex [5], from S. Latin ƒ´gnifex ‚Äúfire-maker‚Äù.

muria - muria [6], from Latin muria ‚Äúbrine, salt liquor‚Äù, via muriatic acid.

xanthal - xanthal [7], from Ancient Greek Œæ·æ∞ŒΩŒ∏œåœÇ (xanth√≥s) ‚Äúyellow, golden‚Äù, with ending by analogy with coronal.

Cla√∞ dur: / Stiffs [8]:

adamant - adamant [9], from Latin adamans ‚Äúunbreakable, hard as steel‚Äù.
jast - jast [10], from S. Latin [1] iastum ‚Äúzinc‚Äù, originally from Hindi ý§úý§∏ý•çý§§ý§æ (jastaÃÑ) ‚Äúzinc‚Äù.
machovin - machovine [11], named for the Machow mine in Poland near where it was first isolated.
natron - natron [12], from A. Greek ŒΩŒØœÑœÅŒøŒΩ (n√≠tron) ‚Äúnitre‚Äù via Arabic and French, among others.

‚Äî

10m Fevruer

in translation from An Overview of Revitalist Art in Sixteenth-Century Rome, by Safourian Amig√≤t, published 1870 by Prindor Nic√™s

By 1550, Rome and the surrounding Italian states had for the most part played catch-up over the last century, with respect to the study of Classical mathematics and science. Having neither the Novomundine explorations of Vascony, Kernow and Morrack to fund patronage of the new inventors, nor the very new medical advances in Denmark (that is, in the Dane Concert) to incite renewed interrogation of Classical medical texts, the various Italian states were further disadvantaged by their unchecked monarchs; and of course, nowhere more so than Rome herself, seat of a papacy that at the time still governed the spritual lives of most of Europe south of Venise.

This is not the place to discuss the causes and immediate political effects of the Great Fracture; for our purposes it suffices to note that, while on paper the Pope in Rome remained an absolute ruler (along with political dominion over several smaller Italian and German provinces), his temporal powers were in practice much diminished. In particular, the venerable prohibitions over blasphemous arts and philosophical publications within the city limits were swftly dissolved by the new Triunvirato, who ordered the obtention of great minds from around Europe; for example, this led to the establishment of the first Brethin House in all of Italy. They undertook this course of action, one assumes, less out of a sincere zeal for scientific discovery (although the personal correspondence of Lord of Houses Josforo Desmono suggests a passing interest in the subject) than a wish to introduce various new political factions into Rome in opposition to the See.

The influx of artists surrounded by scholars of antiquity led to an outpouring of artworks taking inspiration from the great legends of the Greek and Roman pantheons, dubbed in retrospect the Rivitalisto period. Apart from the many paintings and stories (usually retellings of previous myths), probably the most well-known work from this time is I Macchine di Helio (The Machines of Helios) by sculptor Lorenzo dell'Acchedia, a stylised depiction of Helios the Sun and the six known planets gyring around him. The question of exactly where Lorenzo heard about the new astronomical theory remains open: he certainly had enough opportunity in the various city fora‚Ä¶

##

sevrar /seÀàv Äa Ä/ [s…™Ààv Ä…ëÀê] to separate, to split up, to wean (a child)

also mission sevrant /miÀàsj…în seÀàv Äant/ injective function, one-to-one map

< 11C; from Vulgar Latin sƒìper≈ç ‚ÄúI divide, I distinguish‚Äù, an alteration of Latin sƒìpar≈ç with the same meaning. Possibly via Old French sevrer ‚Äúto separate, to sever‚Äù.

13m Fevruer

The bookseller of Hawthorn Street was a handsome man of perhaps thirty years, and the first time Frederick met him, on a balmy summer evening in 1755, he had (by dint of some quick gossip with his sister) learnt two things about the man: first, he sold books of a higher quality and a lower price than anyone else in Sowbruck [1], or maybe even all of London, and yet always seemed as impeccably maintained as any professional in the borough (despite the reportedly small size of his household); and second, he would vanish for days at a time with little warning and still less in the way of excuses.

Fred had been privately convinced that the man was discreetly employed by the Lord Mayor's office, or by one of the better-concealed deparments of Her Majesty's administration - though bookseller seemed an odd role to foist upon a spy.

taken from the tale A Door, Once Opened by Clarissa Bellamy, published 1920 by Sagittarius Editions

Mr Talbot: Your most recent book has proven remarkably popular, especially for what many would argue is a parachthon [2] story, a genre which hasn't seen much critical success in recent years. It is also somewhat of a departure for yourself, having published primarily in the lovetale [romance] market until now. What inspired you to write this book?
Mrs Bellamy: In some ways I see it as a natural extension of the character development I had worked on before. Really, I had wanted for a while to read para-stories which focus on how people react to the fantastic around them, rather than great battles and politics and mysterious xenozones [aliens]. In the end my husband simply asked ‚ÄúWell then, why don't you write one yourself?‚Äù. So I did.

Mr Talbot: You have mentioned that the setting required a lot of research to portray accurately. What drew you to the London of the late eighteenth century?
Mrs Bellamy: I must continually thank my sister-in-law Heleth, who is a historical scholar at the New Lyceum; her interest and counsel were both invaluable to me. Though she focuses more on the Democratic Wars themselves, I found myself more interested in the aftermath, with London a newly independent state, surrounded by uneasy allies and vulture-like enemies, waiting for a single misstep.

Mr Talbot: How would you describe the main moral or theme of your work?
Mrs Bellamy: The most apparent image to me, as I was writing the trevold [novel], was about reciprocity, the idea that any relationship between two people, two places - or indeed two times - is a mutual one. In fact, the title only came after I had finished the first draft; there's a line in one of Davis' plays that goes ‚ÄúA door, once opened, can be stepped through in either direction‚Äù. Aside from referring directly to some of the tale's events, of course, I think that quote is emblematic of the relationship between Frederick and the bookseller.

questions from a 1921 colloquium [interview] with writer Mrs Clarissa Bellamy for the Trevold Weekly

‚Äî
[1] A borough in London, in much the same area as Southwark.
[2] A genre of writing including most of what we would call fantasy and science-fiction, although the central examples here tend to involve a secondary world of some description. The first story usually attributed to the genre is Saxon writer Elsebeth Sneider's Through a Black Mirror (about which see the 11th January post).

16m Fevruer

from The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d'Istr Boral), a school history textbook published 1982 by Cordin Editions

Let us compare how the fall of Rome affected Borland and Britain (when we talk about this part of history, remember that Britain refers to all of Albion, not just the Briton states, and not all of them, either). The end of Roman life in Britain occurred quickly during the first three decades of the fifth century. By contrast, although a lot of people did leave Borland for the continent during this time, it seems like the way Borland was a little more independent from the rest of the Empire helped it. In fact, there is recent evidence that some Romans left Britain to Borland, especially from the major cities of Londinium [A19] and Camulodunum [√ê17] (now London and the Merch town of Conster).

At this time, Germanic people, particularly the Angles and Saxons, came from the mainland of Europe and settled in Borland and Britain. In Britain there was a complete shift away from Roman culture over the fifth and sixth century; the Angles established East England [D15] and Northumber [B8] in the east, while the Saxons founded Wessex and Markland in the west.

The story in Borland is similar, but less drastic. Much of the south and west coast was settled by Angle immigrants through the fifth century, where they founded Angland [J11], Southbar [L13] and several more minor kingdoms. At the same time, the informal and decentralised political structures throughout the self-described Roman towns was changing to adapt to the new condition. Several towns in the south were abandoned completely to emigration or assimilation into the Angle kingdoms, and most of the more urbanised east was unified under the rule of Prase Victor Rossetus as the dioces boralis, the Diocese of Boral.

19m Fevruer

text from a steeplecard [1], sent on the 3rd of March 1822 and addressed to Matilda Torricks

Dearest sister Matty -

Wishing you a belated new years [2]. Office closed until this morning with the snow and the municipal holiday. Jane says I am to be secluded for at least a fortnight hence with the baby imminent. Anxious to be presentable for the melee [3] before the 75th anniversary celebrations start next month. We hear the whole south Tamside is to be transformed.

Do visit soon. Ebbasham [4] is not so distant after all.

- Your sister Vallie

excerpt: The Communication Toolset (El Herramente de Communicaz√≤n) by Joan Bensa√≠d, published 1951 by Quaterno Theor√©tico a Cordova

In the usual way of things, multiple nations claim the title of the first steeplemesh [5]. The very earliest incarnations of the idea arose during the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century, but these tended to be single lines of temporary signal towers set up to span terrain that made it too costly to simply send a rider with a message. Moreover, these were set up only to transmit simple messages; for example, an hour to launch an attack.

There were two major developments required for the first true steeplemeshes. First, the means of communication. Early wartime towers usually relied on lampfire and thus were reliable only at night, or else a collection of patterned flags, which were usable at day but required a shorter distance between watchtowers [6]. The two-arm system, allowing up to nine separate states to be transmitted, was probably first tested in the Willem town of Thynen [7], where the church's weathervane was reportedly commandeered to send the first lines of the Pater Noster to the neighbouring town Bost. (Hence, probably, the name ‚Äústeeplepost‚Äù.)

Second, the encoding of a true alphabet, rather than a fixed sets of messages. One early Andalusian attempt (from Cordova to M√°laga, usually given as the first line connecting two major cities, and essentially just formalising the signal system that had existed for the duration of their conflict with Morrack [8]) used a variant of the ancient Polybius square as discussed in Chapter 3, adapted for Arabic script. The first modern steeplescript was developed in Cracow [9] by engineer Sigmund Adamich and his son Andrei as part of the royal initiative to connect Cracow to Bitom, using a set of four symbols, traditionally referred to as ‚Äúdot, dash, bar and hook‚Äù.

‚Äî‚Äì

[1] Very much like a telegram, although somewhat less efficiently terse.

[2] The year in this world begins in March, not January, as was the case in ours across much of Europe until the sixteenth century.

[3] A high-society term for ‚Äúthe ton‚Äù, or ‚ÄúSociety‚Äù; that is, the gentry.

[4] A small town within the borders of independent London, on the site of real-life Epsom.

[5] An optical telegraphy network.

[6] Compare our semaphore system for boats.

[7] Tienen, Belgium. [8] Morroco. [9] Krak√≥w.

22m Fevruer

from The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d'Istr Boral), a school history textbook published 1982 by Hendric Tambour et al. for Cordin Editions

There was a steady influx of Christians into the Diocese of Boral (and later the First Kingdom of Borland) from the latter years of the fifth century, well into the seventh. Early on many of these were Roman or even Celtic Christians displaced by the Germanic migrations, and by the time of Pope Mercury II in 590N, Rome had taken a particular strategic influence in the small kingdom, which at the time was on the defensive against a stronger Southbar [1].

In the first instance, the new Kingdom was viewed as a vital Christian outpost from which Rome could send missionaries east and west to aid the conversion of Albion and the Danes. Thus the early Borlish monasteries and schools of theology were much more outward-focused than the comparatively insular Irish groups. It was to this end that the Golfhaun sanctuary was first erected (with the Latin name Asylum √Üstuarii [2], which contains the oldest surviving church building in Borland.

The opinions of the Papacy regarding Borland were as inconstant as the Italian lands it claimed and the men themselves. Many also saw the distant land as a convenient way to make sure inconvenient elements were far away and unable to make trouble. In this way, any given Borland monastery might house semi-heretical monks ‚Äúentrusted with the conversion of the pagans‚Äù, or perhaps several awkwardly-Hellenic men with a suspicious sympathy to one of the Greek heresies.

‚Äî-

[1] A medieval Anglian kingdom located along the south coast of Borland.

[2] The Estuary Haven, since it is situated on the large island Nestu in the Labba√∞ Sound (upriver of which lies the capital Damva√∞).

2m Mar√ß

from The Latin Republics: Jewels under Tyrian Standards by Jacob Trelaune, published by the Yievle Brethin Primers, 1902

Part VI: The Republic of Provence

It would be ahistorical to claim that the republics of the Middlesea were brought about due to the unusually totalist monarchies they neighboured, or even to stake out the somewhat more popular position that those Italian monarchies grew so tyrannical as a reaction to the republics. Nonetheless, one case in which the link between republicanism and totalism is undeniable is with the Republic of Provence.

In the first half of the second millenium, Provence was first a county in the West Francian Kingdom, and following the Emergency at Dijon, the largest province (so to speak) in the Kingdom of Burgundy. The county was elevated under the house of Savoy to a duchy in the thirteenth century, and as the direct link between the capital at Geneva and all of Middlesea sea-trade, it became known as lo panier de Borgogne (the Burgundian breadbasket).

However, with the Novamundine landfall in 1471, and the accompanying Ascendancy of Vascony under Ambrose III, piracy (which had long plagued the very coast which made Provence so profitable, and which was the scourge of the venerable republics of Jeans and Venise, among others) thrived off the general prosperity. In Marsella, the raiding became so frequent that many treated it more as an occupation, and many of the wealthy fled to safer pastures inland.
 
The aid requests presented to Rudolf VIII went largely unanswered. Charitably, one might say he was distracted by the ongoing Bavarn conflict, and the religious difficulty attendant to being the northwesternmost polity yet answerable to Rome. But it is generally allowed that the young king was simply concerned only with himself (or by extension his many lovers) and with resplendent lakeside banquets.

Things came to a head in the last years of the fifteenth century. Lord Daniel Menton of the house of Amig√≤t had secured the aid of King Marc V of France (France having finally reached reconciliation with Normandy and avoiding the tangle that was the Albion Wars of Fealty), on the advice of the foundling-general Guillen daus Sanz. Thus prepared, he and several other noble families declared independence from Geneva on the twenty-first of June, 1497 (which to this day is a national holiday in Provence). Though this declaration would not be formally recognised until Burgundy's defeat two years later at the Battle of Marg√®s, it was this event which cemented House Amig√≤t's position as primus inter pares in the new republic.

At least, that is, for the next eighteen years‚Ä¶

‚ÄúFor surely the Lord has not in his infinite majesty ordained Rome, the ancient seat of the Christ-killers themselves, to be the most holy city in this world? Heaven is high above and the Holy Land is yet distant, to the clockmaker [1] as it is to the pilgrim's steps.‚Äù

The years between Pope Paul I/XI's (Whether or not to include the predecessor Roman Popes, or indeed some other count including any of the antipope lines, was a controversial issue for much of the thirteenth century, so both appellations are seen. The reformist Damien I established that they should not be included, although Paul himself usually did.) first announcing the establishment of a sedes sanctissima in Augsburg and the eventual capitulation of Rome are frustratingly ill-documented, for obvious reasons. Nonetheless, we can reconstruct much of the sequence of events from outside commentary, such as the various Brethin Houses [2] to the west, and the exilic Lombard accounts among the Hellenes.

As we must start the analysis somewhere, let us begin with the 1210 declaration of a renewed Crusade for Jerusalem. It is nowadays usually acknowledged that Pope Just was persuaded to call the new crusade by King‚Ä¶

included in an 1901 theological edition [publication] of Proclamatio Divior, the scholastic writing "Comparing the German Secession to the Great Fracture [3]; how the Final Antipope emerged from a popular reaction to the Failed Crusade, and how to understand his successors' reactions to Rome in the sixteenth century."

1590 Nascenti√¶

Monday 17th April

Penelope told me this afternoon that there's a nobleman at the docks' lodginghouse, some French fop who's run away from his grandmother. Honestly I'm surprised he survived the crossing; not every such man would last two months with Marco's crew, especially with their new Libyan friends. They'd never have let him hide from work belowdecks, no matter how generous the bribe he gave them for discreet passage‚Ä¶

‚Ä¶moreover, said grandmother is allegedly the Countess [1] d'Avosche! One cannot imagine the scandal of the heir apparent's flight (for he was, we are to understand, to inherit everything) has not ruined her influence over King David's court. No-one has any idea why this mysterious newcomer should have exchanged a chasteau on the Rhin for our humble blankpine rooms on the Becouin.

Wednesday 19th April

Along with the usual bevy of whichever goods the Concert wishes to foist upon the MastoiseÃÄt (Father believes nothing will do as much good for our diplomacy as sending more bronze and iron, or at least more men to mine and smith any we can dig up for ourselves), Marco was good enough to stockpile the Marsella weekly papers, so we have news of the old country for the first time all year. Penelope wants me to read them all to her properly tomorrow morning, but a first glance suggests at least that our heretical cousins haven't yet found themselves on the wrong end of a short rope‚Ä¶

[1] This is Nad√Ýlla's mistake; Avosche was by this time a duchy.

23m Mar√ß

from Handbook of Natural Theory, by Jason Ridley and others, published 1967 in Markland English by the Lincon Collegiate Editors

In mechanics, ≈ìculux (commonly referred to simply as light) is a form of propagant lux that carries energy through the barrick extent, most commonly experienced as light or heat. In vacuo, ≈ìculux moves at the normal speed, commonly written ≈ì; in more common units, at a rate of 300 dichilomecon per second.

The word ≈ìculux is a chimer (to the certain dismay of our more classically-minded readers!): the first half is related to such words as ≈ìcumenical ‚Äúof the secular world‚Äù, and here is intended to denote ‚Äúuniversal, the entire extent of‚Äù. However, its origin is in fact the Greek Œø·º∞Œ∫ŒøœÖŒºŒµŒΩŒπŒ∫œåœÇ ‚Äúthe whole world‚Äù, literally ‚Äúthe inhabited places‚Äù. The suffix -lux, coming directly from Latin lux ‚Äúlight‚Äù, is more familiar, perhaps from the new threshold mills and their unsettling martial applications. It is important to keep in mind that luxes aren't just the scary invisible rays from a blacklayer film, but include all the colours of the rainbow.

We conventionally grade ≈ìculux by its period, since we interact with it in many different ways.

Light with the shortest periods are scattering, having enough energy to interact with atoms and give them a deficit of plenty; we say the atom becomes deficient. All shortform and penetrating ≈ìculux is scattering, and depending on convention some further surblavic light may be included. The maximal period of light which can in theory scatter an atomic base is around 320 trihekimecon, which is still shorter than any visible light. High exposure to light of such brief period is dangerous, and fortunately the atmosphere blocks the majority of such luxes that the Sun emits (although not all, as a sunburn will attest!).

    from One Quiet Day by Roberta Bluemore (published in parts from 1939 until 1943 in the Pattinson Issue HM), a retelling of the plot of the 1921 sensation The Day We Let the Chaos In, an illustred trevold collaboration between the siblings Martha and Dean Armstrong. Although, as with most generally-popular works, there were many such derivative pieces published (Markland having at the time an eighteen-year withholding), Bluemore's enjoyed widespread fame, leading to a run on Pattinson's primers and a copy shortage. The full work was edited singly in 1945, and translated into Borlish in 1946 by Margret Courleon.

##

22m Avril

    taken from Middlesea Meals, published bilingually in Wessern and Kernish 1892 by Iscombe Editors. It was translated from the acclaimed 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni, written by traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo and intersperses anecdotes from his journeys with his favourite dishes.

King James Stew (Frigotta de Rei Jaume)

Of all the fish stews I sampled along the solarium coasts of Barcelon and Provence (not to mention the singular mussel-olive dish I was served on the beaches of Mayurca), none compare to the King James. Locally and affectionately called the jaumetia ‚Äúlittle James‚Äù, this stew is named for James VI (1691-1749), the longest-ruling monarch in Barcelon since its establishment in 1067. Although similar stews existed in the region before his reign, most simply functioned to use up whatever seafood remained from a day's catch. The King James is perhaps the first such dish made for its own sake, and indeed should for best results use hake or haddock, or a similar whitefish.

Wares (for four)

5Œπ sweet aumonds
eq. grain garlic, olive oil
1 large onion per cubos
1 whole pepper, sliced
some sweet drypepper, petersly and fendle (fine)
¬Ω pint Empordan or other white
8Œπ pit shadome or sauce thereof
2–ø6Œπ whitefish per frangos
¬Ω limmon, juced

Method

First bake the aumonds until golden and grind to a coarse powder. In a broad pan heat the oil, then gently fry the aumond, garlic, onion and pepper for roughly fifteen minutes, or until the vegetables are soft.

Add the drypepper and half the herbs and stir together. After two minutes add a dram of the wine and allow it to mostly boil off. Now add the shadome sauce or chopped shadomes, pour in the rest of the wine, and cook for fifteen minutes more.

Salt the strips of fish and push them into the sauce. Cover the pan and leave for five minutes, until the fish is just cooked. Uncover, add the rest of the herbs (or whichever amount suits) and cast the juce over the dish. Serve bowled with hardbread.

##

1r May

    from the collected correspondence of Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725‚Äì56), illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia (the title having been created by King Frederick of Bavarn after the Blue Trans-Willem War and the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy).

[received at Castle Carlsfeld in the winter of 1744]

To my dear brother Thomas,

Albert suggested that I write to you first, and I was only too happy to do so; ideally your witticisms shall be as entertaining on paper as in person, and in any case I cannot help but feel some kinship with you. We are the two fledgeling sparrows cast out from Father's nest to fend for ourselves.

I am of course exaggerating; doubtless the King can provide for any number of wards (no matter how grudgingly accepted; your evenings with Father were perhaps not as private as you hoped! Shall we just say that everyone downstairs is aware of Prince M's Indiscretion?), but neither can I complain overmuch. Albert understated the extent of his cobbler's empire here in Lepzi; it cannot compare to home (as still I think of it) but I had been prepared to face far greater hardships when I accepted his hand.

You were, as ever, entirely correct to insist on extra language tutors (and entirely too generous in letting me attend!). The compulsory Latin has revealed itself less than useless‚Äîwe run in entirely the wrong circles‚Äîbut the Dutch they have here is almost as awful as that of those Flemish performers we had in some summers past. I expect I will acclimate, but for now I am having far too much fun speaking Burgund and Boheme with Albert's company.

Does the news we get of bread troubles have any basis? We know not to trust anything the Lepzi weeklies have to say about life south of the Danaw, but the rumours about Venise and Crovatia are bad enough; do abscond back to Stuttgart at the slightest sign Bavarn is along the same track‚Ä¶

Your sister,

F. Marian Siegert

##

7m May

    censor commentary on a question from the 1985 scitation paper Consequences of Viking Statecraft, set as part of Lester Academy's series on northwestern European history.

Question 1: What factors enabled Dunclothe in the mid-tenth century to conquer the heartland territory of the Dane Sodrick (also known contemporaneously as the Kingdom of the Isles)?

Censor: On the whole a well-answered question, with the majority of respondents correctly noting the troubles further east - a typical good answer included the following:

    the several famine years during the 920s and 30s across Denmark and Horth (some incorrectly referred to the Eurasian Warming, but this began only several decades later, and in any case did not lower crop yields in the region).
    the skirmishes between Dane-ruled Borland and Sothbar, now harbouring the exilic Roman king and his family (the wife, of course, being King Roderick's cousin).
    the inevitable slowdown of the flow of wealth obtained by raiding; excellent responses compared Sodrick to Danish Jarvick, which managed to transition to a more diplomatic stance by virtue of inheriting (so to speak) a city well-positioned in its trade mesh. By 980 N Jarvick had even successfully reunited Northumbre under its own rule, although Edinbrough and her fief remained de facto independent until increasing Gallic incursion necessitated a more active defence.

Less common but by no means unwelcome was discussion of the Norman French, to whose relative prosperity many left Sodrick entirely; the extent of the depopulation is difficult to quantify but we read several good arguments along these lines. Of particular note is one student; she managed to find convincing records of triplet brothers, born in Mull around 915 N, and mentioned again in reference to a Norman raid of Marsella in 946 N.

It was somewhat disappointing that few respondents mentioned the aggressor Dunclothe in any detail - the lack of decent contemporary sources outside the Tremonnow gravatory does make discussing Roderick's court a challenge. Nonetheless, we appreciated any discussion of, for example, Oscar of Eidin's influence among the northern lords.

< 13C platabac(q) ‚Äúsideboard, cupboard (often literally a board attached to a wall)‚Äù, from late Old French plat abaque ‚Äúsideboard‚Äù, literally ‚Äúflat board‚Äù, retaining a then-rare sense of abaque < Latin abacus ‚Äúboard, abacus‚Äù. The mathematical sense of abaque was so predominant that one occasionally sees the reanalysis plat √Ý bac ‚Äúraftboard‚Äù in the contemporary literature, despite the incongruous semantics.

    Des y platabac pris i dou peutr blau e pos i l'un a sy fra√∞r.

##

13m May

    taken from A History of Timekeeping (C—Ç–æ—Äƒ±—è O–±–º–µ—Ä–∞ –ß–∞—Å—É), written by Igor Arcadich and published 1961 by Halich Quaternus Editions.

‚Ä¶Meneva (now Ty√∞ewi, or in Wessern tradition Tythew, literally meaning ‚ÄúDavid's house‚Äù), and died on the first of March at the end of the seventh century. He was canonised by Pope Agatho (1001-1026); it is commonly told that this was a signal of Papal support for the foundering Kingdom of Wales, which had been losing people and land both to Markland and to Devon.

[Pictured left, the flag of the modern Kingdom of Wynneth (Y Deyrnas Gwyne√∞), using the colours of Saint David]
image

The story of Saint David, while interesting, is somewhat tangential to our plot. In the early years of the twelfth century, amidst the great successes of the First Crusade, Pope Nuntius instituted a reform of the heretofore universal Julian calendar. Most relevant to us was the shift of the New Year from the Annunciation on the 25th of March to David's Day, on the first. (In fact, during the Roman period, the first month was January, and to this days certain Eastern heterodoxies celebrate the yearturning at the beginning of that month). It is thus unsurprising that the Welsh New Year celebrations are a global legend. I myself was fortunate enough to be in Deganny for the 200th anniversary of the coronation of the People's King‚Äîmy hearing has never been the same!

Of course, despite Rome's unparalleled power at this time, not all of Christendom implemented the Nuntian reforms at once. The spiritual significance of the Annunciation proved a particular sticking point; this formed one of the famed Pauline Denunciations‚Äî‚Äúthe reckless diminution of our ordained measure‚Äù, as he characteristically put it. Thus did the Augsburg dominions retain the Julian calendar until the Rhine Compromises much later.

The second and more involved change to the Julian calendar regarded the placement of leap years. It had been noted that the equinoces under the Julian system would drift from their astronomical ideal by one day for every 128 years‚Ä¶

tarsc /ta Äx/ [t…ëÀêx] card, note, small paper, memo

< 11C from Old French targe ‚Äúsmall shield, buckler‚Äù, itself from Frankish targa with the same meaning. Probably conflated with the Norrish and English cognates, which would alone have given *tarc or *targ. The shift in sense to paper dates to 16C, from clipping of fougl tarsc ‚Äúbuckler sheet‚Äù, the term for the paper size one-half of a trevold. Specific meaning ‚Äúmemo‚Äù from 19C.

##

19m May

    excerpted from The Secret Guide to Ausbagn (English edition), sold 1967 for 1‚çö30 in the Calder Museum of Health's leavingstore [1]. Published first in 1962 on behalf of the Maglen Formeu√ß Caritativia, as part of a series of tourist guidebooks for cities along both coasts of the Rustigh Strait [2].

‚Ä¶reign, and this at the greatest extent of the First Drengotian Empire in the west. For context, the Ausbagn of the late twelfth century is the de facto seat of the County of Sothbar; by 1190 Count Steven is known to have resided year-round in the coastal town, bequeathing the old house in Heitri√ß to his younger brother.

The location of Ausbagn on one side of the Rustigh Strait proved to be of immense strategic value, both militarily and economically. The Great Flie Flood of 1171 (which created the island Texel, about which more later) swept away much of Lower Frisland, and was devastating for all of Willemy. This was not least because the Duke, one Lovis the Tall, was in Amstel at the time, and was killed in the deluge [3]. In any case, Ausbagn was for several decades afterward the obvious choice of harbour for any trader moving from the Danish lands through the Drengot domain and onward to Spain and the riches of the Middlesea.

More relevant to history (or at least to the good reader's moneypurse!) are the Order of Saint James, in whose name was built the Peninsular Monastery which overlooks the city. Founded in the tenth century under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges, they were implicated in a popular uprising against Geoffry II and forced to flee to Borland in 1067 [4]. Today the Order (with its modern name, the Jacobin Fraternity) is in the main known for its Jaibos breweries and for owning much of the land around the city, including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses‚Ä¶
image

[In blue, Ausbagn and Heitri√ß; in green, Amstel and Texel; in orange, the Rustigh Strait]

‚Äî

[1] in essence, a gift shop.

[2] the waters between the southern coast of Borland and what in OTL are the northern coasts of the Netherlands and western Germany.

[3] possibly; the elderly Duke may already have been dead at the time of the flood - he is known to have been suffering from consumption. The perennial theory that he was swept away on a raft and living his remaining years as a Borlish hermit can be safely ignored.

[4] believed to have the backing of local nobility - however, the emperor's supporters in Normandy were able to muster enough forces that the rebellion was quickly disavowed.

25m May

    first message sent using a Vilmanas Teleclavia, arguably the earliest household tachygraph [typewriter] capable of sending messages on the steeplemesh. It was sent on Michael's day in 1859 (less than a month after the Auroric Anomaly [1], about which much has been made in film retrospectives), and received manually by Vilmanas' friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste ‚Äì a machine capable of transcribing directly from the steeplemesh having not yet been developed.

A B C D E F G. HAEC TUA PENELOPE LENTO TIBI MITTIT ULISSE.
a b c d e f g. This your Penelope sends to you, long-delayed Ulisses (first line from Ovid's Heroines).

    extract from Vitoria Queiros' 1998 quaterno [textbook] Thirteen Inventions which Changed the World (Trize Inven√ß√µes que Refiz o Mondo), published by Pontudra Editions.

..be tempting to connect these early developments in distributed communication ‚Äì so vital to the modern concept of Vicinity ‚Äì directly to our own tachslates and meshfora [2], but it is important to keep in mind the machinery of the era. Even the self-contained tachygraph was still in its infancy, with most models only capable of setting type at at most half the speed of practised handwriting. The Armanburg Tachygraph (Tachygrafia Armangrod), which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygrafia (literally the ‚Äúslow-writer‚Äù, tachygraph meaning ‚Äúfast-writer‚Äù).

Moreover, the barrick [electric] steeplepost was still so young that it had not displaced the earlier flag-lamp system, widespread across Spain, Gaul and Italy. With some exceptions (notably the Cracow-Bitom line in southern Poland), the ground was clear for the spread of wired post across northern and eastern Europe, and indeed it is from these places that we get most of the barrick innovations of the late Workshop period (a similar phenomenon is at play in Arabian Cappatia and Lower Mendeva).

So the early wired tachygraphs were cumbersome to use and expensive to‚Ä¶

‚Äî‚Äî
[1] Known in our timeline as the Carrington Event, or the 1859 Solar Storm, this coronal mass ejection caused stong aurorae and wrought havoc on telegraph equipment from August 29th to September 2nd. Some operators even reported being able to send messages along disconnected wires.

[2] A tachslate (Kentish /Ààtak.sl…õjt/) is a handheld device capable of sending text messages to other such devices. A meshforum roughly corresponds to our idea of an Internet message board.

##

28m May

    from Portingale author Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico (released in Kentish as We Arive in Unwemmed Clothes, translated 1865 by Christopher Wood), after which is named the originally-Spanish masquira genre of stories. These typically feature vigilante characters and plots driven by hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes. It has some overlap with the later spycraft genre, especially in modern works.

    The narrative focus on characters with hidden identities has led several marginal groups to embrace masquira tales, most notably the semi-public tovarick communities in the Riverine Cities. Indeed, the tovarism connection was barely obfuscated in some works (see particularly Takach's We Will Burn Together), and the popularity of the genre is widely understood to have contributed to the Household Renovation.

##

31r May

     continued extract from Vitoria Queiros' 1998 quaterno Thirteen Inventions which Changed the World (Trize Inven√ß√µes que Refiz o Mondo); see 25th May.

‚Ä¶period (a similar phenomenon is at play in Arabian Cappatia and Lower Mendeva).

So the early wired tachygraphs were cumbersome to use and expensive to get hold of ‚Äì and furthermore there was little incentive for businesses, who could simply hire a tapper [1] already fluent in the Adamich system (a professional could transmit at a rate of two hundred words per minute). Thus was the wired tachygraph adopted slowly, in fits and starts.

However, in those places where there was early adoption, the machines proved astonishingly popular. These were usually dense cities and urban areas undergoing rapid growth. In Safford [2], for example, which already had a robust postal infrastructure, the young Mayor Crawford oversaw the installation of the first redirection banks in post offices. These pioneered the system in which a given missive would be prepended with a borough code ‚Äì for example, Makerfell's was MKF ‚Äì allowing the switchers [3] to send the message through to the correct local post office, followed by a house code ‚Äì eventually standardised to four digits followed by a letter ‚Äì whence the message could be transmit to the correct machine.
image

    (above) An outgoing redirection bank, 1879. Not pictured are the spools of paper on which the house codes would be printed in Adamich notation.

    (below) Sender's copy of a letter sent via the Aiga d'Angoixa tachygraph mesh, 1882; additions in parentheses

IVS (Illa de Vinna Salvatge [4]) 86814 J

Good evening, brother! My husband is hosting a evening sorty this weekend; are you and yours otherwise occupied? If not, you are certainly welcome to attend. He wants a lakeside bonfire and connoqueria (connock dance [5]) and everything. It'll probably be the last chance we have before the new-year thaw. Yours, Marta.

6m Jugn

    in translation from the collected correspondence of Michael Bervisson (17th June 1799‚Äì12th January 1849), political historian and social reformist; he and his wife Hilgart were influential in the Borrhatic League, especially with regard to calls for religious tolerance. This letter to his editors is on proposed alterations to his magnum opus Roma √Üternal: E√ß Vascel a These (An Eternal Rome: Ships of Theseus).

(received on the morning of Tuesday 5th November, 1844 N)
Kind sirs Osgaut and Yfaur,

May this missive find you and the Vithor Historical Institute in good health and well-mint. Samuel and Clovia do better for themselves every time they return from school, although their history tutor's opinions on the collapse of the Medieval Imperia are execrable. I expect they shall both be in the melee before this blasted book is published, in any case; Hilgart is already reading the illustrated costumery in the hopes that we might all be fashionable by March (kindly do not mention hats in her company!).

Only two points must I reiterate regarding the volume. First, I absolutely cannot condense The Wall and The Hedgerow ‚Äì contrasting Hadrian's policies in Britain and in Borland is vital for understanding their different fates in the post-Classical age, for his reign marks the beginning of our island's greater autonomy. There is one correction: I have not been consistent in writing signa (for example in the name of Aquƒ´s Balneƒ´s), and you may feel free to add them wherever necessary.

Second, I would like to divert as much criticism as we might possibly anticipate (in particular one aquiline fellow who shall not sully my quill) and rebut those arguments directly in the text. To this end, might we extend the chapter Twin Keys to emphasise the ways both the Drengotian Constant Empire to the west and the Malchassian Romans to the east used the Church (in the last century of its unity) to support their claims to continuity with Rome? I shall have written the requisite pages by Revillon, I assure you.

Do not hesitate to reply forthwith,
Professor Michael Bervisson

12m Jugn

    taken from A Comprehensive Cyclop√¶dia of Pre-Fracture Europe, written by Martin and Elisabeth Forman and illustrated by Goffrey Jordan; published 1937 by Winchester Scholastic Primers.

‚Ä¶was thankfully less variation across Europe in these units than in, for example, the league (as we have seen).

¬ßThe Second Tetrachy

Refers to the period of roughly one hundred and fifty years for which much of Europe was under the (occasionally theoretical) rule of empire. Known also ‚Äì especially in older texts ‚Äì as the Medieval Imperia, or as the Roman Decadence.

The most limited timeframe to which this title routinely refers is from 1091, with the consolidation of Bohemia and Lower Pannonia into the German Empire under Bertholdt II, until 1170, when the Emergency at Dijon led to the establishment of an independent Kingdom of Burgundy (and soon the dissolution of the Constant Empire). More usual is to include everything from the Norman-led subjugation of Kent (and installation of the House of Kernow in Dumnonia) in 1029 to the death of Daniel IV (the Drengotian domains being much divided among his family and friends) in 1191.

The four empires constituting the Second Tetrarchy were:

    the aforementioned Constant Empire, under the Drengot dynasty and comprising for most of its duration the kingdoms of West Francia, Wessex, Markland and Dumnonia.
    the German Empire, in particular under the Houghbrocken and Andex dual imperium, and including more than a dozen states ‚Äì principally the Kingdom of Saxony, the Duchy of Willemy and the Kingdom of Bohemia.
    the Empire of the Hellenes (also called the Second Roman Empire), especially considered from its territorial maximum in 1026, controlling lands from Corsica to the shores of the Hazaran Sea.
    the Single Caliphate ruled from Cairo, primarily after the centralisation reforms of the early eleventh century (although the further territories such as the Admiralty of Cordoba or the Turkish lands were never as closely-ruled).

Even at the height of imperial control, regions within these states often had significant autonomy. In 1105, the Kingdom of Greater Sicily even went to war with‚Ä¶

18m Jugn

    in translation from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto (in English, Developments in Philosophy since the Revitalist Period) by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto, researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus in Lombardy. 

(excerpted from Chapter 8, titled Forging the First Link: Problems of Infinite Regression)

‚Ä¶popularised by the works of Dufresne and Fayeda in the last decade of the seventeenth century, this theory asserted that all life had formed at the moment of Creation, and that each successive generation grew from miniature versions of themselves (called by Dufresne zoia, not to be confused with our modern use of the term for one-case life [1]), themselves containing yet-smaller forms for the grandchild generation, and so on. Contemporary diagrams showing a tiny human form inside a spermcase illustrate this theory along with the concurrent idea that the mother was merely the soil where the baby, planted by the father, grew.

Setting aside issues of predestination inherent in this approach‚Äîthough of course this rendered the theory anathema or even heretical in the Augsburg dominia [2]‚Äîthe immediate objection (see al-Siddik's 1696 work On the Children of Zeno) was that you would need to posit an infinite chain of progressively-smaller zoia to be present inside every male animal on the planet. There were two broad categories of response: first, Dufresne and many of the more theologically-minded argued that the Lord had merely provided enough zoia to last until the Apocalypse [3]. This argument could never satisfy all, however.

The response more interesting for our purposes is exemplified in Vallakis' 1702 treatise The Generic Impossibility of Self-Duplication within a Bounded Form. In the book Vallakis argues that any organism that can reproduce, or more generally anything capable of duplication, must have a sculptor guiding the division. The argument proceeds as follows:

    A self-duplicating bounded [4] structure A must contain within it all the structures necessary to create a copy of itself, which together we may call B. But then B contains everything necessary to create itself, say C. Thus we must have a descending chain of structures, each of which contains everything necessary to reconstruct the original A. But this is absurd, as A is bounded and cannot contain arbitrarily-small parts. Thus in fact A can divide only under the intervention of a guiding sculptor.

For several decades, thus, mainstream theory held only two opinions on the mechanism of life: either that all life contained an infinite chain of zoia within, or that each time new life was created it was guided in that moment by the plans of the Creator. This state of affairs would not change until the publishing in 1743 of‚Ä¶

‚Äî

[1] singular zoium, from Scholastic Latin and derived from a diminutive of Greek Œ∂·ø∑ŒøŒΩ ‚Äúanimal‚Äù. We would say ‚Äúmicrobe‚Äù or ‚Äúunicellular organism‚Äù.

[2] those lands under the religious authority of the Augsburg See.

[3] that is, the End Times, or the Second Coming.

[4] here intended to mean ‚Äúof finite extent and containing finite information‚Äù, or something similar. A mathematical understanding of quantity and extent would not be formalised until the nineteenth century.

21r Jugn

    in continued translation from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto [1] by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto, researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus in Lombardy. 

(excerpted from Chapter 8, titled Forging the First Link: Problems of Infinite Regression)

‚Ä¶plans of the Creator. This state of affairs would not change until the publishing in 1743 of Fable du Relieur Mut [1] by Rachel Desselut, daughter of explorer and infamous libertine Lord Bouvoin. The relevant portion is reproduced here.

    And yet he could not take Marcuccio as his apprentice, for how was he to instruct a pupil in the art of bookbinding? But although Ignazo could not speak, he could write. For a year and a day he wrote, describing all his methods and arts, from the pedigree of sheep for the parchment, to the age of the oak tree for the ink. In this way he taught Marcuccio in detail the production of a book exactly like the one he had himself written, save that the copy would be utterly blank. Upon the last page, Ignazio wrote his final instruction: Et omnia hic scripta scribas illic‚ÄîAnd all that is written here, you shall write therein. And thus Marcuccio received the book, and studied from it. And after many months did he return to Ignazio, twin books in his arms.

Desselut's work lays out the separation of Form and of Method, and how they allow for the unsculpted duplication of a bounded being. However, her work would go unrecognised for nearly two decades (due predominantly to the disruption of the Democratic Wars), during which time she would be forced to flee first to Norman Kent [2] and later to Provence, via Portingale. It was in these years that she first began corresponding with mathematician Raymond al Mustafa, who was at the time resident in Barcelon under the Duke of Aragon's patronage. Their letters offer a tantalising glimpse into early methodics [3], although both were more interested in the implications for life. Desselut inferred the existence of a vital language (in her own writing she called it the Divine Word) present in the cases [4] of the body. Despite later rumours, she probably did not believe this language was literally Hebrew, although she made several analogies in her publications.

In 1770 the first real self-describing book was written by an anonymous author and donated to the University of Hulm. The university has duplicated the book once every four years in as exact a way as possible, and the original, alongside the most recent copy, may be viewed in their Department of Heredity building. In a peculiar twist, since the early twentieth century the ink has had to be sourced from a different tree than is specified in the book, as the‚Ä¶

‚Äî

[1] Developments in Philosophy since the Revitalist Period; Parable of the Mute Bookbinder

[2] That is, Normandy and Flanders.

[3] Computing, or programming, although at this point it is not even at the level of, say, our Ada Lovelace and Charles Babbage.

[4] Cells: this vital language is of course DNA (known eventually in this world as heredian acid or, less commonly, as nucalic acid).

27m Jugn

    recipe taken from the 1763 mealbook A Complete Lithing of the Towns and Cities of Markland, for the Edification of the Good Food Amateur, written by independent traveller Lord Carrow, Baron of Ebbasham [Epsom]. Although popular across Albion, Ireland and Borland (the Borlish translation being infamously inaccurate), it never sold in large numbers on the continent or further afield. His later volumes, An Account of the Vittles of the Various German Peoples and On the Sundry Uses of the Shadome Fruit [tomato], were more enduring, being republished several times even after his death.

The Lonxter [Lancaster] Yatherpot

Lonxter is a castle town on the banks of the Lone, being the northernmost city and river along the western coastline of Markland. Its signature dish is the yatherpot, a filling mutton or lamb meal to be prepared for a large family with its distinctive sliced tartoffer covering. The name is simply Merch for ‚Äútogether pot‚Äù, as it is a dish one can cook in a single large pot or bowl.

Wares (for six)
5Œπ butter or lard
3–ø6Œπ lamb or mutton per frangos [strips]
4 sheep's codnere [kidney], trimmed
2 large onions, 6 carrots, sliced
3Œπ rough flour
some salt, molasses, vinegar, spices per gustibus
1¬Ω pint lamb or chick broth
3 leaves dried lorrer [bay leaf]
3–ø6Œπ tartoffer, peeled and sliced

Method

    Melt a little of the butter in a large shallow dish and sear the lamb in batches, remove to a plate, then repeat with 3 trimmed and sliced lamb kidneys, the onions and the carrots (these last two remaining).
    Dust with flour, leave for two minutes, then add the seasoning and broth, and allow to boil. Pour in the lamb, kidneys and lorrer and remove from the fire.
    Arrange the tartoffer by slices over the meat and then glaze with hot butter. Place on a lid and cook for 1+¬Ω hours on a low fire.
    Once the tartoffer are well-browned, remove from the fire and serve immediately.

Question 1 (60 minutes)
Write a short speech aimed at the interested layman advocating the three religious developments of the last millenium in Europe which you hold to have been most influential. You should introduce the events with appropriate context, discuss their similarities and differences, and justify their importance. For example, you might choose one of

    the German Secession; the Great Fracture; the Deviance Movement

    the First Crusade; the Muslim migrations northward into the Russias and Tartary; the Prodigal Faiths from Mendeva and Cappatia

Question 2 (120 minutes)
This question focuses in particular on the emergence of Deviance Theology (the Anti-Paradise Movement) in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries.

(i) What evidence is there that <Deviance> originates as a mistranslation from Wessern, and meant at first <defiance, rebellion>? Consider both the semantic plausibility and‚Äîcrucially‚Äîthe significance of <defiance> to the Promethean clubs across Albion; the term of course is better evidenced in their Danawan counterparts.

(ii) In both Albion and the Danaw Kingdom at this time there were significant religious minorities and the Church (of whichever stripe) was strongly associated with local authority. Was it these commonalities that made them fertile ground for Anti-Paradise thought?

(iii) To what extent was the resurgence of the Modest Arrangement in Spain and Gaul in reaction to these new religious ideas? Compare the fervence of Modest thinkers like the Marquis de Caillat or proto-Blue Marian Diana Theodora Queiroz to the philosophers of earlier Modest-like movements.

(iv) In your opinion, who is, or are, the most likely author(s) of the incendiary 1799 publication Et Nous Amerons les Fux (And We Will Love the Fire), a seminal work in Deviance Theology? Back up your theory with modern and contemporary sources, and contrast the likelihood of at least two other candidates.

15m Juglot

    from a review in the Fiellas Dimenja (Sunday Leaves/Sheets), a Marsellan weekly newspaper incorporated in 1871. It focuses primarily on recreation and entertainment, unlike the older weeklies dating back to the sixteenth century, which were limited more to ‚Äúhigh-brow‚Äù topics.

14th October, 1950

‚Ä¶welcomed back from the New Cambrick facilities; Captains Kerry and Montpiu landed safely at Fort daus Sanz early Friday morning.

REVIEW: Zachariah

A departure from Kiawaman's usual fare, this blacklair [horror] tale takes us back to the 1820s amid the disintegration of the Long Peace and the beginning of Reaction War. From the gaslit streets of independent Boxa [~Novi Sad, Serbia] to the recovering shell of Stamboul [Istanbul/Constantinople], this adaptation of Petcov's classic trevold [novel] Majat vCherveno (The Red Man) tries to recreate the dread and mystery of watching our four heroes slowly discover the true nature of the eponymous Zachariah.

Unfortunately, the beautiful landscape shots and the addition of a rich Tavancine tourist (whose plotline is mostly borrowed from the book's Elenor storyline) are the high points of an otherwise messy film. In an attempt to incorporate some of the more traditional aspects of vampire folklore, Kiawaman grants Zachariah mystic powers and has the heroes defeat him by means of a sanctified wooden stake (not, as in the book and in popular wisdom, by burning). These new powers, in the hands of a better writer, could have set up a dynamic between Zachariah and Vania as artists of the same craft‚Äîinstead, we are given the tired trope of a woman torn between the affections of two men. It doesn't help that the audience barely believes there is any choice to make; Vania has no chemistry with either lead.

The symbolism of fire and butterflies that permeated the book is also entirely absent from this adaptation. Apparently afraid to present Zachariah as light or good in any way, we get a cold manor house and bat-infested (!) roofs. Never mind the rich history of the vampire as a figure clinging to life in all its forms, in the blood of the living, in the dancing flames and in the metamorphosis of the butterfly. The film relies heavily on gory fights (with what is clearly wine as a bad stand-in for blood, in a disappointing reversal) and a pounding orchestra who leave the few quiet scenes no room to breathe.

This will be no classic: come only for the views (of beautiful forests, and a bevy of handsome actors, I concede) and two hours of mindless tragedy. - Jacint Brunat

21r Juglot

    taken from A Politic Mapbook of Middle-Age Europe (4th edition), by various authors, published 2005 by Winchester Scholastic

Albion and Ireland, 1028 Nascenti√¶

This year is the last in pre-Drengotian Albion. 1029 will bring the Kernovan Conjuring and Norman Accession, and the succeeding decades will see what might have been the new steady state of these islands disintegrate.

In the south, Kent under Rodulf is stable and much expanded from its position in the 9th and early 10th centuries, having subdued Wessex (minus Anglia, already lost to Markland in the Treaty of Oxford) but suffered renewed Dumnonian pushes from the west. Canterbury's most powerful vassals lie to the north in Essex across the Thames, ably run by Alfred the Tall. The elderly patriarch Rodulf of Kent lingers on his deathbed after eight decades of political man≈ìuvring.

To the west we have the ever-fractious kingdoms of Ireland, lately unified as but four (with the breaking of the Ultagh in the north-east): Tyrconnal, Connaght, Lenster and Munster. The tales of Tyrconnal and Lenster are dominated by wars at once with the Norrish to the east and the other Irish to the west, while the houses of Connaght and Munster vie for the position of High King of Ireland.

The north holds the confederate Kingdoms of the Danes, newly untethered from overseas rule. The once-mighty kingdom of Northumbria is rendered but one province of the whole, yet the city of Yorwick remains the richest city on Albion (thanks to economic ties in all directions). Still more northerly lie the two-minded Kingdom of Scotland and the last remains of Horther rule in Albion persist in Orkney and Shetland.

Between all these we find those bitter rivals, Markland and Wales. Cadwallan, erstwhile king of Wynneth, finds himself the unexpected ruler of all Wales‚Äîa fortuitous position of strength against equally formidable Markland under Oswin the Becoming.

History V: Europe from the Imperia to the Fracture (answers on sheet 233)

1. Catrin of Kernow was an influential figure in late twelfth-century Borland. But which king, who orchestrated the Realignment of Devon in 1225, was her younger brother?

(…ë) Toledo (Œ≤) Cunca (…£) Palma de Mayurca

3. The First Burning of London was in 1290, but approximately how many people were killed in the week-long disaster?
(…ë) 60 (Œ≤) 400 (…£) 1200

4. Under whose mayorship was the city of Wrozlav admitted into the Stadbund in 1341, despite the protestations of Bardwick and Bremen?

##

2m Aost

    taken from the 2008 fourth edition of condensed study materials for New Vithor course Histoir Concorrent, s16 : Mort, Vascel e√∞ y Motion Celest (in English, Concurrence History of the 16th Century: Death, Ships and the Movement of the Heavens).

‚Ä¶it is usual to consider the feudal system entirely defunct by 1550 N, at least outside of insular nations like Burgundy or Awry. There were a variety of reasons for this collapse, some more widely accepted than others.

Often cited is a labour shortage of serfs due to the Second Great Dying, allowing those peasants who remained to demand more favourable conditions from their lords. This is evidenced by the correlation [Iscombe 1967] observed between population loss and liberalisation (as well as how quickly the nascent middle class grew). For example, Borland under Joseph III lost over four hundred thousand people, one-seventh of its population; serfdom (already on its last legs) was legally abolished in 1564 N, but by that point it had not de facto existed on the island for a generation.

One counterexample to the above trend is Vascony, which, thanks to Novomundine profits in furs and other goods, actually saw in increase in population from 1500 to 1550 (excluding Portingale) due to immigration. The end of the Vascon Ascendancy with the War of the Two Queens would end feudalism in Vascony by the end of the century, however.

(It is worth mentioning that this was not the main factor in ending feudalism in the east, where the Islamic conquests of Kiew and Russian Cazany upended the nobility more directly.)

One, perhaps unexpected, effect of the decline of the feudal system was the strengthening of laws such as sumptuary codes. These were intended [Rone, 1988] to reinforce the social distinctions between serf, freedman and noble‚Äîalthough states had had similar laws almost since Classical times, enforcement of these rules became more stringent and draconian. There is even one account of an Emma Chasteron being executed in Dovres for wearing ‚Äúsilk in saphires purpel and petal embroderie‚Äù to church‚Ä¶

Of course, Vascony was not the only nearby state attempting to reach Asia by sea. Ambrose's nemesis Munir al-Hamdawi of Morrack had in 1463 established a rival marine enterprise in the New World Company (ÿ¥ÿ±ŸÉÿ© ÿßŸÑÿπÿßŸÑŸÖ ÿßŸÑÿ¨ÿØŸäÿØ), and just five years later the ship Orion (ÿßŸÑÿ¨Ÿéÿ®Ÿéÿßÿ±) voyaged from Anfa [Casablanca] to Aden. They made port in Vascon Sant Adrian [Bioko] and encountered Chinese merchants in Mombasa, whose reports to the Southern Emperor would change the course of history forever (see 1524-1600: The Collision and the Fracture).

After the Landfall, the Company's efforts were swiftly redirected to the Novomund [the New World] (as were the naval efforts, however paltry, of most of Europe at this time). However, a notable exception was the relatively-new Kingdom of Britain, where Howel's ships had already set up resupply ports along the African coast. Having profited from the ongoing Wars of Fealty which raged across the rest of Albion [Great Britain], the king was convinced more opportunity lay in connecting the European market directly to Asia. Doubtless he also wanted to displace the Borlish supremacy in the northern trade (although this ambition would not be achieved in his lifetime).

In any case, the tale of the twin voyages of the Cynthia and the Ph≈ìbos (of Morrack and Britain respectively) remains to this day one of the greatest stories of exploration and discovery the world has ever known, measuring up to Homer's Odyssey and Quoming's Peoples of the Sea. Directed by Umanick and Chinese traders, mac Kellot's Ph≈ìbos sailed to Gurjat, Madray and eventually made port at Borune in 1516. This was at the time almost the south-eastern extremity of the known Vetomund.
