##

26.5: Borland

Borland (in Borlish Istr Boral /Àà…™s.t ÄÃ© boÀà Äa…´/) is the easternmost of the three major landmasses in the Northwestern Isles, and also the name of the singular polity located thereon. The capital of Borland is Damvath, a port city on the east coast of the island.
Etymology

The name 'Borland' descends via from Old English Boralland, Borala land 'land of the Borlish people', where the first element is taken from Latin BorƒÅlƒ´ 'Borlish people'.

The origin of the Latin demonym is disputed. It is unknown whether the Romans knew the land as ƒ´nsula boreƒÅlis island in the north' before they settled Borland, or whether they borrowed the native Kelt name enis borag 'island of the dawn' (from its easterly position with respect to Albion and Ireland).
History

Borland Kelts

The Kelt population of Borland (self-described as the Bora Ale) is poorly documented. The few attestations of their language suggest only a recent divergence from the Briton tongue; they are thought to have arrived on the island from Albion, not directly from the continent.

This culture was connected to the European trade mesh via the export of tin and iron. Imported pottery of this period from finds across the island has been found from as far afield as Egypt and Persia.

The Kelts were organised into many tribes each covering small regions and ruled from hill fort structures. Several important religious sites of this time have been unearthed; most famously the √áadrosc labyrinth, which has sparked much unfounded theorising as to its origin and purpose.

Roman Borland

Borland developed diplomatic and trade links with Rome from the time of Julius Caesar and his expeditions to Britannia in 55 aN. The Roman conquest of Borland took place through the second half of the first century (especially during the Flavian period), building on their successes in Britannia.

Confederations of Kelt tribes resisted Roman occupation, especially in the north; several uprisings appear to have been explicitly imitating the successes of Buthick of the Iceni in Britannia.

In the second century, Emperor Hadrian adopted different strategies in Britain and Borland, characterised in Bervisson's 'An Eternal Rome: Ships of Theseus' as 'The Wall and the Hedgerow'. Possibly due to the lack of an analogous frontier to the Pictish lands, these policies are said to have led to significantly more autonomy in Borland, and in particular greater reliance on locally-born legions. There is evidence in writing of a sense of Borlish identity forming alongside the universal Roman citizenship.

It was also under Hadrian that Borland was made a separate province from Britannia; after the Diocletian reforms this became the Diocese of Boral.

Religion in Roman Borland

Just as the Romans did across the rest of the empire, the pre-existing sacred practices and divinities on Borland were interpreted through the lens of and recast in the mould of the Roman pantheon. This could be based on really quite tenuous analogies‚Äîfor example, goddess of trade and the ocean Nahalennia was absorbed into the cult of Pluto, and she was syncretised with his wife Proserpina. Other deities were more localised, like Reagimos, a river god whose name lives on in the river Reim.

The fourth century saw the arrival of Roman Christianity on Borland, leading to the destruction or repurposing of many pagan site, both those originally from the Borland Kelts and shrines built by the Romans themselves.

Sub-Roman Borland

There is evidence of considerable Roman emigration from Borland to the continent during the late fourth and early fifth centuries, primarily from the cities of the west and south such as Aquae Balneƒ´ and Pons Seianus. The urban east was less immediately affected, and even received some Roman immigration from Britannia.

At this time, German peoples (primarily Angles) came from the mainland and settled in Borland, while they and the Saxons also went further and settled much of further-collapsed Albion. By the end of the sixth century they had formed the Borlish petty kingdoms of Southbar and Angland, along with several minor outlying settlements.

Meanwhile, the political structure of self-described Roman Borland was changing rapidly. Without the external influence of Rome's authority, the decentralised network of towns in the east was unified under the rule of Prase Victor Rossetus as the 'Diocese of Boral'.

First Kingdom of Borland

By the late sixth century the Diocese of Boral had become a monarchy (described in retrospect as the First Kingdom of Borland), whose ruling dynasty claimed descent from Rossetus.

This period saw a continuous influx of Christians (both Kelt and Roman people displaced by the German migrations and, later, missionaries) to the island. Rome under Pope Mercury II took a particular strategic influence in Borland, viewing it as a vital Christian outpost from which they could send missionaries east and west to aid the conversion of Albion and the Danes. For this reason, Borland's early monastic culture had an outward focus, unlike the insular communities of Ireland. The oldest surviving church in Borland is located at the Golfhaun Sanctuary (As»≥lum √ÜstuƒÅriƒ´) which was consecrated at this time.

However, papal opinion of Borland was prone to change, and many saw it as a convenient place of unofficial exile for (politically or theologically) troublesome figures. Thus, any given Borland monastery might house semi-heretical monks 'entrusted with the conversion of the pagans', or else men with suspicious sympathies to one of the Grecian heresies (see in particular the role of Marmeronism).

The Tripartite Wars

The influx of English people into Borland continued throughout this period; for example, the Merch subjugation of East Anglia in the last decades of the eighth century sent many people east to the petty kingdoms of Angland and Southbar.

The question of fealty (that is, whether any kingdom of the island would received official recognition and deference from the others) vexed the island throughout this period, with conflicts leading to temporary alliances between Boral and Southbar or Boral and Angland depending on the political climate.

Finally, in the early ninth century, Roman king Stiglan married his son Marc to the only daughter of Alfegh of Angland, unifying their kingdoms and forcing Southbar to pledge fealty after the circa-810 N Battle of Fendal.

Dane-ruled Borland

Attacks on Borland towns and monasteries by Dane raiders along the coasts began in the late eighth century, spreading gradually westward as viking raids grew lengthier and more lucrative. Through the ninth century raiding is gradually upgraded to long-term settlement, especially in the northern half of the island. Eventually, Dane pretenders (coroncr) to the Borland throne arose and threatened to topple the kingdom.

After the fall of the First Kingdom in 898 (and the establishment of Dane Borland ruled from Damvath), the young King Jothey went into exile with his wife Queen Brenna of Barrow to Sothbar. There they remained at court for over twenty years, and had several children. Subsequently they were forced to flee again to Kent as Dane Borland conquered the remainder of the island, uniting it for the first time since the Roman Empire.

Medieval Imperia

In the context of the First Drengot Empire to the west and the German Empire to the south, independent Borland had significant strategic value both militarily and economically. This was especially true in the towns and cities along the south coast such as Axbane, which saw a great increase in harbour traffic during the eleventh and twelfth centuries.

This period also saw the arrival of the Jacobin Fraternity to the island. Forced to flee Flanders in 1067 after being implicated in a popular uprising against Emperor Nathaniel II, they built the Peninsular Monastery overlooking the city of Axbane.

Post-Tetrarchic Borland

With the collapse of the German Empire and growing sympathies towards the Augsburg See, Rome considered the Baptism of the North an urgent undertaking. The Rome-allied Duchy of Crain collaborated with Borlish missionary forces and the Convoy Australier to bring Christianity to Lithow and its more distant Graven neighbours.

The late thirteenth century sees the birth of a Borlish-language body of literature. Although we have earlier examples of treaties and religious texts in the vulgar tongue, works written in Borlish were hitherto sparse and either utilitarian or didactic.

In 1292 the chronicle 'The Life of Catrin of Kernow' (Y Vi√∞ a Catrin de Cornou) was written, detailing her early life, marriage into Borlish noblility and eventual assassination in 1233. It is notable for being the first long-form work of Borlish literature written about a layperson, rather than a saint or similar legendary figure.

The 14th and 15th centuries

The Noccair Lyceum of the 1300s exemplifies how the mediaeval university system was standardising across Europe. There, students were taught the Five Romantic Arts: Literature, Argument, Reckoning, Geometry and Songcraft. These were in essence the descendants of the seven Classical arts. Around 1400, as this system began to break down, the Lyceum began to teach alchemy (and a little later, post-Classical history).

The end of the fourteenth century marks the zenith of Borlish trade power, with cities along the Rustigh Strait (especially Axbane) trafficked both by the Dutch stadbund's fleet and Borland's own. In this way Borland served as a crossroads between the Vascon trade mesh in the west and the stadbund's to the east. Later, in the 1400s, Borland lost ground to the ascendant Wales and Vascony, which began to monopolise trade coming from the Middlesea and Africa.

In the first decade of the fifteenth century, Queen Natalia I and her husband King Joseph I ruled jointly. Their family had been and would continue to be beset by misfortune; all three of their sons predeceased them, as did their few male grandchildren (either in infancy or by misadventure).

The succession crisis prompted by their deaths in 1411 and 1416 spelt the end of their dynasty. From the ashes rose the Saulestran lineage of kings, whose banner comprised a white bar cutting a twilight-blue field.

Borland was not directly involved in the Albion-wide Wars of Fealty, but it received an influx of immigration from the polities involved, as people were displaced in the many border conflicts and attempted conquests. In particular, this period saw substantial Welsh, Devoner and Kernish immigration to the cities of southern Borland.

Borland after the Novamundine Landfall

With the dawn of the sixteenth century and the waning power of Vascony, the dominance of Borlish cities over regional trade reached its peak. Damvath was said to be the most-trafficked port in Europe outside the Middlesea at this time.

The Second Great Dying reached Borland in March of 1519; likely due to the volume of trade there, the island's first recorded death attributable to the plague was in Damvath during the year's green snowfall. Borland lost over four hundred thousand people‚Äîone-seventh of its population‚Äîto the plague.

After the death of Joseph III in 1534 N, his nephew Brandon II succeeded to the throne (King Joseph never succeeded in producing a natural-born heir, despite several recognised yet illegitimate children by his various mistresses). Having known he would inherit, King Brandon had made sure to be well-liked at court; upon his accession he managed to impose a terrene tax on the land despite significant opposition from his most powerful vassals.

Borland became entangled in the War for the Stavang Crown in the 1550s, a protacted succession crisis for the Dane Concert following the death of the king while undertaking a grand tour of his lands.

The practice of serfdom (already on its last legs) was legally abolished in Borland in 1564, but by that point it had not de facto existed on the island for a generation. This is often cited as being due to the labour shortage resulting from the disruption of the Second Great Dying.

The 17th and 18th centuries

Borland's involvement in the Romantic Wars was restricted to a failed invasion (culminating in the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632), along with unstable arrangements with neighbouring polities regarding military aid in their conquests.

Through this period, Borlish trade guilds were involved in the establishment of staddomains along Atlantic Cappatia and Africa. Initially little more than tradingposts, these would grow into significant polities over the next centuries.

Agricultural innovations in Borland began to accelerate in the eighteenth century. The Pentrose survey of the 1730s was a coordinated effort to find the most productive or otherwise useful cultivars of many plants which spurred significant productivity increases. Its crowning glory was the braxoch beet, a hybrid of Silesian and Cassuvian beets whose sugar content was high enough to make domestic sugar production economical.

Borland in the Long Peace and afterward

The location of the Borlish parliament had until this point not been fixed to one building‚Äîusually one member would donate use of an estate for the duration of a legislative session. In the late eighteenth century this state of affairs ended, with the completion of the Jacobin Estates (Pala√ß Jagovin) in Damvath.

During the inflamed period of Reaction War which followed the Long Peace, Borland participated in the Second German War. Borlish soldiers were present at the disastrously mismanaged Battle of Hulm in 1821, and they fought alongside troops from many nearby countries, such as Furlaine and Crovatia. The war and Borland's alliance with the Northern Concert sparked major mozard protests in cities across the country.

Borland in the Workshop decades

The steeplepost arrived late to Borland, with most of the island's flag-lamp lines being erected in the 1810s and 1820s (somewhat accelerated by strategic military considerations). Axbane was later connected to the continent at Texel in Willemy via air-steeples, but by 1860 this had been replaced (along with much of the island's steeplemesh) by a barrick line.

The spelling reform of 1870 (the last major revision to Borlish spelling conventions) primarily acted to standardise‚Äîat the time, many words had multiple forms in use reflecting more or fewer of the sound changes of the last few centuries.

The Good Game period

The genre of masquira which originated in Spain flourished in Borland in the late nineteenth century. The particular popularity of kevler romance (roman√ß kevlar) on the island produced several sensation trevolds and lead to the term being borrowed into several other languages.

The 1890s saw Borland join the growing Drengot Collusion (several Borlish cities, although not the capital, had already joined as part of the New Urban Mesh); Queen Natalia II abdicated on Revillon of 1894 due to the political upheaval of this process.

Culture

The celebration of Revillon is an ancient tradition in Borland; some form of the practice dating back to the time of the Insular Kelts. As part of the festivities, people bring pastries and other baked goods to grand Revillon Feasts (Fest Reveglon) held in churches across the country.

In Borland, if in a given year there is snow in spring after the yearturning (i.e. on or after the 1st of March), the first such snow is called the 'green snowfall' (neyan√ß vert). Years which have a green snowfall are generally considered unlucky, as a symptom of a long and bitter preceding winter.

There are several Borlish folk songs of unknown origin, including the 'Season Song' (Cant a Sa√ßon). This song, like many in its tradition, uses an idiosyncratic scale sometimes mistakenly conflated with the manoscard scale.

The figures of the graveyard fiddlers are a folk tradition peculiar to Borland. These spirits associated with death are said to be the source of a mysterious sound of string instruments heard especially in cemeteries.

A related group are the Wild Hunt (Cau√ß Ragnt), a party of hunters passing overhead said to be responsible for the sound of thunder and strong winds. A children's story common to many cultures across Europe, the Wild Hunt in Borland shows clear signs of a Dane origin‚Äînot least that it is traditionally led by Balegr, a kenning of Odin.
Language

By far the most commonly-spoken language in Borland is the Borlish language, the only surviving branch of Insular Romance. It share many features with Gallo-Romance, including loss of case and intervocalic lenition. It was also heavily influenced in the first millennium by Old English and Old Norrish.
Geography

Regions and territories

    Angland, a historical region in the west whose capital is Vithor
    √áadrosc, an area of moorland in the northwest which features a labyrinth built in Kelt times
    Lascoum, a small county in central Borland whose coat of arms features a butterfly
    Southbar, a county in the southwest which was once an independent kingdom

Towns and cities

    Aukir√ß, a market town in the northwest
    Aus√ßagm, a coastal yet hilly town not far from the capital
    Axbane (Ausbagn), an old Roman town on the south coast with a surviving historical baths
    Barraut, a river town boasting a famous lodging-house
    Combert, a city in the south-east and the location of the Borrhatic School of History
    Cordin, the hometown of publisher Cordin Editions, which from the late twentieth century on has provided textbooks to school nationwide
    Dambourne (Damborn), an industrial town some way upriver of Damvath
    Damvath (Damva√∞), the capital city of Borland located on the river Dam
    Gambrig, a village and nearby monastery noteworthy for its medieval chronicle in a variety of Old English
    Golfhaun, the site of a medieval monastic community on the isle of Nestu which contains the oldest church building in Borland
    Haldanvion, a city in the south known for its alchemical refining mills
    Haubrath, a market town in the north
    Hawkirch (Haukir√ß), a town in Angland and site of the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632
    Heitri√ß, a castle town in Sothbar and former seat of the County of Sothbar
    Humerstoun, a village which was in the ninth century the seat of the Petty Kingdom of Sothbar
    Jelu√∞rou, a market town in the river valleys of the north

    Naus√ßod, a small town near Axbane notable for being the closest inhabited place in Boral to the continent
    Neuvort, a central city which grew significantly in prominence during the Workshop period
    Noccair, a town which has the oldest university in Borland, the Noccair Lyceum
    Pentrose (Pentros), a port town on the Rustigh Strait with a substantial population of British descent
    Vithor (Vi√∞or), a city on the west coast known for the New Vithor University, a major research institution

Natural features

    Dam River, a major east-flowing river which passes through Damvath and empties into the Labbath Sound
    Du√∞am River, downstream portion of the largest watershed in the northern half of the island
    English Strait (Naur Anglesc), the sea diving the west coast of Borland from the east coast of Albion
    Labbath Sound (Bugt Labba√∞), a large estuary and bay on the east coast where the river Dam empties into the sea
    Nestu, the largest island in the Labba√∞ Sound, known for the medieval Golfhaun sanctuary
    Rustigh Strait (Naur Rustig), the narrow channel of water separating the southern coast of Borland from the mainland

People

Monarchs

    Stiglan of Borland (fl. 810), who unified the First Kingdom of Borland with the Petty Kingdom of Angland
    Marc of Borland (fl. 810), first king of a unified Roman Borland and Angland
    Jothey of Borland (fl. 920), who fled into exile following the Dane invasion of Borland
    Victor Rossetus (fl. 500), first Prase of the Diocese of Boral and nominal ancestor of the royal dynasties of the various Kingdoms of Borland
    Joseph I (d. 1411), who ruled jointly with his wife
    Natalia I (d. 1416), last of her dynasty, whose death sparked a protracted succession crisis
    Joseph III (d. 1534), who ruled through the ravages of the Second Great Dying
    Brandon II (fl. 1534), who instituted the land's first terrene tax

27.4: nia√ß a fuyo a√Ør "live in sin"

nia√ß a fuyo a√Ør /njats a faÀàjo …õÀàj…™r/ [n…™Àà ùats a f…êÀà ùo …™Àà ù…™Àê]
- to live in sin, to cohabit as if legally wed without being married;

Etymology: idiom first attested in the nineteenth century, especially in association with otherwise-deviant (or Deviant) behaviours such as blasphemy unpalatable to the mores of the Modest Arrangement. The phrase literally means "to have a robin's nest", presumably in reference to robin's nest-building behaviour (which occurs after they have formed monogamous couples for the mating season).

The noun nia√ß "nest" is an extension of Old Boral ni, which directly reflects synonymous Latin nƒ´dus; fuyo "robin" is of uncertain origin, with theories ranging from a dialect pronunciation of figl "child" to a hypothetical Kelt etymon related to "sparrow".

They've been living in sin for two years.

##

27.5: New Provence

New Provence was a coastal polity in the east of Mendeva. It is named for Provence, a small Middlesea state whose trading ships first set up trading posts along the coast of Mendeva in the early sixteenth century.

The country bordered Ambrosia to the north, the states of Gulf Mendeva to the south, and it shares a short border with Hasiny to the southwest. It extends inland in places as far as the Sturgovan Mountains.

In the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries the trading posts along the New Provincial coast dealt primarily with Mastoiset peoples, exchanging (for example) metal tools for beaver pelts.

Although by the 20th century the polity was politically independent from Provence, cultural ties remained strong, including shared newspapers and substantial movement of people in both directions. They also shared (until at least 1943) more restrictive morality laws than their neighbours, being the strongest remnants of the Modest Arrangement of the century prior and notable non-signatories of the 1850 Laic Declarations of Belgrade.

Geography
Becouin River, the northernmost major river in New Provence
Santrafew (Sant-Rafeu), an early trading post along the New Provincial coast, founded in 1564 and located at the mouth of the Becouin River
Media
Jet White (Blanc Sabaje), a 1951 masquira film adaptated from Cadogan Torrior's 1890 trevold 'Men of Jet and Diamond'
People
Political figures
Sauveir d'Orz (r. 1620‚Äì26), mayor of Santrafew
Carol Zancoud (d. 1630), town reverend of Santrafew who wielded significant political power by virtue of his popularity
Other
Nadalla fi Carol (fl. 1590), daughter of Carol Zancoud remembered for her preserved diaries illustrating daily life in early Santrafew

28.1: A Door, Once Opened

excerpt in translation from the first chapter of Clarissa Bellamy's 1920 work A Door, Once Opened; the translation into Borlish was done by her friend Gunna Despath.

"Ac vespr, bossir," salu√∞au y livrer, y test ponent nacleu d'un fa√ßon stant cortal e conjura√ßonnant y dou‚Äîa veg me√∞es solemn demay por un vocationner ma parcevent cos manifest demay por un actour doit. "Jo crey noc nos ayau acconnosc. Jo dign Brandon Cavendish."

"Frederick Walton, at your service," Frederick replied, extending his hand. Cavendish's grip was dry and cool, his handshake firm without being crushing.

"So√∞ vos ac por gila√ßar, Sr Walton, ben n'un tom particuler tenent all'idea?"

"Then you've come to the right place," the bookseller said with a smile. "Let me show you what we have."

28.2: y parol robar "to choose one's words"

y parol robar /i paÀàr…îl roÀàbar/
- to phrase, to choose one's words in a particular way in order to achieve a desired effect;
- to dissemble, prevaricate, to disguise one's true intentions by employing specific phrasing

Etymology: idiom first attested in the fifteenth-century poetry of Manuel Kigman, literally meaning "to dress (up) one's words". The noun parol "word" descends directly from Vulgar Latin paraula "(the power of) speech, language", from parabola "comparison, parable"; the verb robar "to dress (someone)" is a derivation from rob "robe, dress, longcoat", which is a borrowing from Old French.

##

28.5: Saxony and Libya

Saxony is a polity in Europe bordering places such as Poland, Willemy and Bohemia.
History

During the Second Tetrarchy, the Kingdom of Saxony was a major vassal state of the German Empire administered from Bardwick, and many of their Hohenbrocken dynasty ruled the empire outright. The region in the twelfth century is characterised by tensions between Saxony and Bavarn, the other major vassal kingdom of the empire.

The Kingdom of Saxony was affected by the collapse of the Second Tetrarchy in the thirteenth century; the nascent Stadbund of mercantile cities in the north seceding and reducing Saxony to its lands upstream in Maidburg and Lepzi.

Saxony is considered to have profited most from the First German War; in its aftermath the polity controlled more territory than it had at any point in its history.

In the nineteenth century, the Blue Marian Society (which was founded in Lepzi) spearheaded movements for reform to inheritance and suffrage laws.
People

Monarchs and rulers

    Brunow III (fl. 1120), who ruled the German Empire jointly with King Conrad II of Bavarn
    Ruprecht II (fl. 1200), under whose rule Bardwick removed many tariffs on imported goods
    Baldur (b. 1295), who was deposed as king and reduced to Margrave of Maidburg at the age of four

Theorists and scholars

    Johans D√ºmmeyer (fl. 1827), mathematician whose work 'Sets and Functions' attempted to resolved ongoing definitional debates in the community

Writers

    Witkind Defenter (fl. 1902), who wrote 'Cities of the Stadbund before the Landfall' on behalf of the Bardick Library of Trade
    Elke Hannawald (fl. 1946), writer during the Spring Tide of masquira works known for her trevold 'Outrun the Dawn'
    Elsebeth Sneider (m. 1772, d. c1830), pioneer of the genre of parachthon romance with her work Through a Black Mirror

---

Libya is a Middlesea polity on the north coast of Africa, located between Morrack to the west and Egypt to the east.
History

During the Second Tetrarchy of the early second millennium, Libya (under the name Ifricia) was an important admiralty of the Single Caliphate, ruled from Tunis. As imperial hegemony in Europe and northern Africa began to fall apart, so did the Single Caliphate; an independent admiralty in Libya was declared in 1237 (at the same time as the Maghrebi admiralty to the west).

Although Libya's navy was not yet trading directly with the Novomund in the sixteenth century, seamen from the region were commonly employed in the merchant fleets of Morrack and (especially) of Provence.

In the early seventeenth century, the banner of Libya comprised two black fields, one white and one orange. At this time, the polity warred intermittently with Morrack over control of trade into the eastern Middlesea.

In the modern day, Libya is a significant refiner and exporter of cuproses.
People

Monarchs

    Hisham Ar-Rashid (r. 1601‚Äì10), whose reign is notable for inspiring the terms political terms 'copperplate' and 'tinplate'

##

29.1: A Letter from Bervisson

excerpt in translation from a missive sent by Michael Bervisson to his editors as he worked on his great work An Eternal Rome: Ships of Theseus.

Samuel e Clovia son megl s'a√∞elent casc veg lonc ig il reun a repa√∞r de schol, malgra√∞ y no√ßon de lourell'instroyour sur y sfondran√ß dell'Empir Mita√∞-Asc sein vre√ßous.

Primer, jo po√∞ bogr cos total Y Mur e√∞ Y Clojon rendr picquessem‚Äîvital es y ci mod d'ouvran√ß de Hadrian ne Brittan e n'Istr Boral por nos comprenau lour y doum inequal durant l'asc posc-classic, dec con sy regn vien l'inception d'autonomy dicerrem all'ei√∞el nostr.
First, I absolutely cannot condense The Wall and The Hedgerow‚Äîcontrasting Hadrian's policies in Britain and in Borland is vital for understanding their different fates in the post-Classical age, for his reign marks the beginning of our island's greater autonomy.

##

29.5: Morrack

Morrack is a polity primarily in northwestern Africa.

History
Morrack was a local trading power in the late medieval period, with a mercantile domain bringing them into irregular conflict with the overlapping meshes of Britain and Borland meshes to the north (and later that of Vascony during their ascendancy).

In 1457, Morrack (which at this time controlled also Andalus) subjugated the southern part of Portingale, which had previously enjoyed a level of autonomy under Vascon stewardship.

Morrack was one of the earliest Vetomundine powers to undertake extensive exploration of the Novomund. The prophetically-named New World Company was established by Malik Munir al-Hamdawi in 1463, and almost as soon as news of the Novomundine Landfall had reached court were ships of the Company sent westward. It was this fleet which made contact with the peoples of the Arcabil, as well as the Mashick and Tavancine empires.

Morrack ships were the first to circumnavigate the world, having reached the Great Borunesk Meeting of 1519 via Cappatia.

The death of Tavancine Emperor Cariwasari in 1660 is suspected to have been an assassination carried out on Morrack orders, purportedly to allow for more profitable trade in silver from Tavancy‚ÄîMorrack was at the time running low on funds to support their ongoing wars in Vascony and Libya.

Morrack was embroiled in several conflicts through the mid-eighteenth century, particularly as part of the world-spanning Democratic Wars. An attempted war of reconquest against Andalus to the north was the most protracted of these, lasting de jure even into the years of the Long Peace.

The Good Game period of the late nineteenth century saw a brief fad for 'Morrack fashion' in countries across Europe and the Novomund, in areas such as dress, architecture and food. There was, for example, a trend for exposed stonework draped in tapestry and curtains in contemporary buildings.

In the early twentieth century, Morrack was still in the habit of banning books containing subversive politics (and, unofficially, certain personal immoralities), having inherited a somewhat-scrupulous climate from its participation in the Modest Arrangement of the previous century.

The Japetos III submarine link between Morrack and Ambrosia was completed in 1950, after delays due to budgetary concerns and the outbreak of war.

People
Monarchs and rulers
Munir al-Hamdawi (b. 1429), remembered for establishing the New World Company and for his lifelong rivalry with King Ambrose III of Vascony
Theorists and scholars
Zacki al-Hamid (fl. c14), mathematician who wrote a treatise on circular functions
Other
Fredun al-Cazmi (fl. 1516), captain of the Cynthia on its great journey westward

##

30.5: Masquira Romance

Masquira romance is a genre of stories which typically feature criminal (or crime-fighting) characters, as well as plots driven by hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes. It overlaps with the slightly-later spycraft genre, especially in modern works which draw on both literary traditions.

The name 'masquira' derives from Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico "In Either a Mask or a Suit", which is widely considered to have pioneered the genre.

The kevler romance is a subgenre of masquira focused on glamorous thefts and schemes, particularly popular in nineteenth-century Borland.

History
Although many of the tropes of the genre have been use in literature since time immemorial, we see the birth of a distinct genre of masquira romance in the late Workshop period, through the prolific work of Portingale author Ezio Carvallo. His dozen-strong series of trevolds, for example, almost single-handedly created the concept of a kevler story (although we can see a parallel trend in the older Cathayan tapsue "magpie" tales).

The popularity of masquira supplanted to some extent the dwindling voidtale fashion in Europe, as well as various herdtale traditions across the Novomund.

In the 1940s and 50s there was a resurgence in popularity for masquira romance, christened the 'Spring Tide'; this period saw many film adaptations the genre's classics and thus cemented its place in modern popular culture.

Social Detaxion
The narrative focus in masquira romance on characters with hidden identities has led several marginal groups to embrace masquira tales over the decades. Perhaps most notably, the genre was very popular early on with the semi-public tovarick communities in the Riverine Cities. These communities spread the influence of masquira through Europe and the Novomund, particularly along with the broader Deviance movement.

Indeed, the tovarism connection was barely obfuscated in some works (see particularly Takach's We Will Burn Together), and the popularity of the genre is widely understood to have contributed to the Household Renovation.

The popularity of the subgenre of kevler romance in Borland during the Good Game period has attracted much commentary. In the context of a scandal-ridden royal family and a generalised class-levelling instinct pervading the politics of contemporary Borland, it is perhaps unsurprising that "stories of heroes entering high society as outsiders in connit in order to make away with the hoarded treasures of the intolerably wealthy" struck a particularly harmonious chord.

Notable works
A Question of Identity (Identa√∞ Partenent), 1981 tale by Borlish author Carl Havatr
Apollo on Holiday (Apollon Cogma√∞er), 2009 story written by Endric Kirennaga
In Either a Mask or a Suit (Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico), 1860 trevold by Ezio Carvallo which named and pioneered the genre
Men of Jet and Diamond (Dynnon o Vuhu√∞ a Diamont), 1890 tale by Cadœâgan Torriœâr of Hasiny later adapted into a wildly-successful film in New Provence
One Evening Will Have to Be Enough (Un Seir Deura Soffire), 1913 trevold by Rone author Joseph√®ne Foilliers notable for being the first book on record to sell more than a million copies in its first year
Outrun the Dawn (Die Demrung √úverholn), 1946 work written by Bronswick native Elke Hannawald remembered for subverting the trope of the masked hero by revealing the protagonist's identity in the book's second chapter
Waging World Peace (Pa√ß Mondial Accommettent), 1968 giftale written by Neuvort author Dion Becolla
We Will Burn Together, written by Takach Domoko and notable for its openly tovarick characters

    excerpt in translation from the correspondence of Dewock Barclythe (1452-1539 N),Friar of Tremonnow; these lines are from a missive sent to his acquaintance Lluprando da Treviso at the University of Saint Marinus.

Quemant es locutionnar ig cas y plan d'Euclid, dou form moza√∞ calscon son tojorn parallel, oc a digr il hamtn un plan parey.
It is good to remark that in the context of Euclid‚Äôs plane, all mozate forms are parallel, which is to say that they are coplanar.

L'oc parceu√ßon a nossy Jarle√∞ mena√∞ all'apponment ig form moza√∞ posn un ongit por asmuth posse√Ør inna√∞, cos equal com se po√∞ un tramou fornir con l'un asmuth des dou ou√ßon.
This observation has led our Jarleth to conjecture that mozate forms might possess themselves a sort of direction, in the same way that a line segment may be endowed with one of two directions.

L'ongit por asmuth y tesquallessem posse√Øbr par un form com circol ben sca√∞r es lon dout l'ongit aut par un orlosc soler.

Calscon exact vel signfiar ig un cercol ay voltisc soler ben guyer, ne diagram defagl no‚Äôs l'oc voltisc depegntar; y vellisqment normal de saget beno√ßar suffig ac fort just com cas form lign.

31.5: The Deviance movement

The Deviance movement, also known as Anti-Paradise theology, is a religious, artistic and political ideology characterised by non-conformity as well as exploration of the avant-garde in all areas of society.

The term ‚Äúdeviance‚Äù has been applied to the movement almost since its inception, with both positive and negative affect. Some scholars have theorised that the term reflects the Wessern dialect pronunciation of standard ‚Äúdefiance‚Äù, although this is not widely accepted.
History

The Deviance movement arose in the late eighteenth century during the Long Peace, in an environment of rapid scientific progress. In Europe, much of the development of Anti-Paradise theology took place within the Promethean clubs of Albion and Danaw. These regions were both home to significant religious minorities and the Church (of whichever stripe) was strongly associated with local authority.

An important early work to the Deviance movement was the anonymously-published ‚ÄòAnd We Will Love the Fire‚Äô (originally Et Nous Amerons les Fux), which began circulating in 1799 to immediate moral outrage. Though almost a dozen plausible candidates for its authorship have been seriously proposed, no consensus has ever been reached on the issue.

The early Deviance movement coincided with the height of the voidtale craze launched by Elsebeth Sneider‚Äôs 1795 trevold 'Through a Black Mirror‚Äô. Many of the ideas conjured in voidtale works were incorporated into Anti-Paradise theology, and Sneider‚Äôs works in particular were embraced by the movement. However, Sneider herself despised Anti-Paradism; in her own words:

    ‚ÄúIt is not only their strange ideas and lonely, nihilist universe, dear Thif√§ne, but that so many of their number have approached me as if I were their most natural ally!‚Äù

The early public reaction to the Deviance movement varied significantly. It has been blamed for the resurgence of the Modest Arrangement in Spain and Gaul.

In 1850 a tide of secularisation began with the passing of the Laic Declarations in Belgrade, which have largely been credited to the strong influence of Anti-Paradise theology in the Riverine Cities after the collapse of Danaw.

In the later nineteenth century the Deviance movement was connected to the spread of the genre of masquira romance, possibly via their shared connections to tovarick communities.
Features

Many composers of the early nineteenth century were affiliated with the Deviance movement, and considered their music to be in the Deviant spirit. The music of Deviance composers emphasised emotion and the manipulation of the audience, and this helped make music the least controversial part of Anti-Paradism in its first few decades.

The Deviance movement was interested in folk traditions and stories; one example is the European tale of the Wild Hunt, which was portrayed in the younger Crayenschot‚Äôs opera Ad Pentaculum. They were particularly interested in those figures which had been cast as demons or evil spirits by the Church.

Supporters of the early Deviance movement are often portrayed as wearing lewd or otherwise-inappropriate clothing; it is difficult to ascertain the extent to which this reflected a real practice, as opposed to it being an invention by their detractors.
Notable supporters

    Dr Mickel Cr√°yenschot (fl. c19), physician in French Willemy and early public tovarick
    Lord Simon, sixth Baron Mon√ßating (b. 1770), infamous libertine of the Damvath melee
    Poshack Nerwaman (fl. c19), famed lady explorer originally of Mashick, sister to the composer Mollam Bushta
    Ton Cr√°yenschot (fl. c19), composer remembered for the opera Ad Pentaculum, marked entirely in five

##

32.5: Monarchs of Borland

summary of the lives of two rulers of Borland in the first millennium.

Victor Rossetus 'the Red-haired' (fl. c6) was the first Prase of the Boral Diocese. He was the first unitary ruler of an independent polity comprising a significant fraction of the island of Borland. He unified the urbanised east of the land while the west and south were settled by Angles to form Angland and Southbar, respectively, and it was under him that so-called 'Roman Borland' first began to succesfully repel the new settlers.

---

Jothey, King of Borland (fl. 898), known in Borlish as Jo√∞eg / íoÀà√∞ij/, was the last ruler of the First Kingdom of Borland before it fell to the Danes in 898. The kingdom was conquered when Jothey was only a young man, shortly after his marriage to Brenna of Barrow, cousin of King Roderick of Dunclothe.

However, the life of King Jothey (as recorded in Brethin chronicles) continued as a monarch-in-exile for several more decades. He fled with his wife to Sothbar, where they had several children. They remained at court there for over twenty years, until the skirmishes between Sothbar and Dane Borland finally ended with Sothbar's capitulation and the unification of the island for the first time since being part of the Roman Empire.

Fearful of imprisonment or even execution so as to fully extirpate the former royal line and thus nullify the possibility of future rival claimants, Jothey fled once more with his wife and children to Kent.

##

33.1: Film Review

Un sgaran√ß deull'ouvr usual de Kiawaman, l'oc fabr zortlasc nos ramen vars y dezein 1820, parmy y scadriscen√ß dy Pa√ß Lonc e√∞ y commet de Rix Reagent.
A departure from Kiawaman's usual fare, this blacklair tale takes us back to the 1820s, amid the disintegration of the Long Peace and the beginning of Reaction War. 

Des y rout accis sou aer ny Boxa autonom vars l'ossatur parsanant de Stamboul, l'oc adaptation dy trevold canonic de Petcov 'Majat Cherveniat' (Y Segr Roug) atten a form redar ag gur e l'enigma ne nossy catr heros sgardar y natur vray dy Zacharia eponym descovrir cos lent.
From the gaslit streets of independent Boxa to the recovering shell of Stamboul, this adaptation of Petcov's classic trevold 'Majat Cherveniat' (The Red Man) tries to recreate the dread and mystery of watching our four heroes slowly discover the true nature of the eponymous Zachariah.

Infeli√ß es ig y ci bel horam d'≈ìcumen e l'affixion d'un cogma√∞er risc tavancin (dont y cain√ß secu√∞ es majorta√∞er empronta√∞ des y ci d'Elenor ny livr) sein y zantr soltumesc n'un film scompiglous ceter.
Unfortunately, the beautiful landscape shots and the addition of a rich Tavancine tourist (whose plotline is mostly borrowed from the book's Elenor storyline) are the high points of an otherwise messy film.

- brain, brains, organ of the human or animal body responsible for perception, cognition and memory;
- intellect, mind, the capacity for intelligent or rational thought;
- (botany) pith, the soft and spongy white matter on the interior of a plant

Etymology: descending from Old Boral cea(r)vel, cerbel "brain", and reflecting Latin cerebellum "little brain, intellect", a diminutive of cerebrum "brain, skull". In the last sense it appears to be a Revitalist-period calque, since the Latin etymon could also refer to a plant's pith.

def brain of 3s.ps sister be.3s celebrate-p.pst

##

33.5: The Wars of Fealty

The Wars of Fealty were a series of conflicts between the polities of Albion in the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries which lasted almost fifty years.

History
The traditional account asserts that the Wars of Fealty broke out due to a dispute over the location of the Redstone Chair, a low-backed wooden throne inlaid with a sandstone block said to have been the stone over which the tenth-century Elved Marriage (which ended hostilities between Dane Northumber and Markland for almost a century and on which many subsequent monarchs had been crowned) was solemnised.

As political alliances enmeshed the polities of Albion, over some years the entire island was dragged into the conflict. It threatened to spread further still: France spent nearly two decades with growing animosity towards Kent under the Two Henries to the north, but the two polities reached reconciliation in the early 1490s.

The relatively-new Kingdom of Britain (formed of Wales and its outlying tallaths, along with Kernow and Sodrick) started from a position of strength, and managed to extract many concessions in the early years of the wars. This culminated in the 1483 Battle of Rexam, which expanded the borders of Wales in the north and killed the last Duke of Chestre; after this, Britain was able to avoid the harsher conflicts of the wars' latter years.

In the final years of the Wars of Fealty, a code known as Thieves' Script was employed by the forces of Andrew III of Markland, apparently adapted from similar systems used by gangs of housebreakers in Nottingham. It is remembered today as a very early precursor of codes like steeplescript or modern one-zeffre encoding.

Aftermath
The poorer economic conditions of the Albic polities in the wake of the Wars of Fealty and the ongoing population displacements/migrations throughout the island left them woefully vulnerable to the ravages of the Second Great Dying. Almost twice as many deaths per capita are recorded in Markland and English Kent compared with Norman Kent or Willemy across the British Sea.

The advantageous position of the Kingdom of Britain, by contrast, contributed to the successses of King Howel of Britain's expansion of naval trade (typified by the ship Ph≈ìbos circumnavigating Africa and reaching Borune in 1516).

To the east, Borland (never directly involved in the Wars of Fealty) had received an influx of immigration from Albion, as people were displaced in the many border conflicts and attempted conquests. In particular, this period saw substantial Devoner and Kernish immigration to the cities of southern Borland.

##

34.5: The Drengot Collusion

The Drengot Collusion was a multinational alliance-federacy in north-western Europe. Named for the older Drengot Empires (themselves named for the Norman noble family who first united much of Gaul and Albion), it grew from various treaties in the mid-nineteenth century into the principal power in the region until its dramatic collapse in 1961.

History
The Drengot Collusion arose among the French polities of the Global Workshop period. Shortly thereafter the New Urban Mesh (a collection of somewhat-autonomous cities including independent London, as well as Axbane on Borland) was subsumed‚Äîor took control of, depending on one's personal biases‚Äîthe nascent Collusion.

The entirety of Borland entered the Collusion in 1894 as part of a series of events there that also led to the abdication of the Queen Natalia II and the concomitant dissolution of the Borlish monarchy.

Alongside the cities of London and Paris, Kent was the political centre of the Drengot Collusion, with many governmental buildings located in Rone.

Tensions between the Collusion and their southern neighbours (to wit, Vascony and Provence) grew particularly tense in the two decades following the Millstone War. For this reason it is notable that the Fiellas Dimenja newspaper (headquartered in Marsella) enjoyed considerable readership across the Collusion even in the 1930s.

Although Portingale was never part of the Collusion, it occupied a peripheral position in its orbit. This led to diplomatic tensions with Vascony, located directly between the Collusion and Portingale. For a time, Drengot astraphor supplied (via undersea powerline) much of the baric needs of Portingale, thus relying on Vascon goodwill not to interfere. This situation changed in the 1950s, with Portingale funding threshold mills to supply its own astraphor.

The Furore of 1961
At the time of the Furore, the Collusion was being administered by a tinplate government under Romney Darchild.

One of the scandals of the Furore was the revelation of the clandestine activities undertaken by the Red House (the popular name of the Collusion's Ministry for Information) in Rone. These activities, enumerated in the Darchild memorandum, acted as a powderkeg, first bringing down the current administration and shortly thereafter the entire Collusion.

Aftermath
One effect of the collapse of the Drengot Collusion was the implementation of the Kentish ostracon reforms, which would go on to be used as a model in several polities across the globe.

##

Certan cos anneyant es y nom adaptau y mythos ir√∞onnesc d'Oil Breasail, un heilant a vest d'Ir√∞on appos star covart d'un vel a nievr jamay for l'un jorn par casc set anna√∞.

##

35.5: Verona & War

Verona is a city in the north-east of the Italian peninsula.

History
During the tenth century and into the time of the Second Tetrarchy, Verona and its province were contested between the Kingdom of the Lombards (and latterly, Lombardy) and the German Empire. The Verona branch of the Adelchian dynasty ruled Lombardy during the eleventh century.

In the late twelfth and early thirteenth centuries, Verona was part of (and the most prosperous city in) the duchy of Furlain.

Verona was badly affected by the Second German War of the early nineteenth century. The arena in Verona, a classical amphitheatre, was reopened for performances shortly after the end of the war.

People
Chiarina Bruno (fl. 1824), librettist and composer who wrote the opera Wings Afire
Jacob III (1087-1130), king of Lombardy, who was the last of the Verona branch of the Adelchians to grow up in Verona

---

The Second German War (1815‚Äì23) was a conflict centred on German lands but involving almost all the polities of Europe. It took place in the early nineteenth century and marks the conventional end of the Long Peace. This war is considered part of the global phenomenon of Reaction War, which includes politically unrelated but contemporaneous conflicts around the world.

Although Vascony at first managed not to become embroiled in the conflict, once its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened, then-king Hernand IV had no choice but to declare against the Northern Concert and in favour of the Convoy.

Borland participated in the war, with Borlish soldiers present alongside Bavarn and Furlaine fighters at the disastrously mismanaged Battle of Hulm in 1821.

The Northern Concert were a major power involved in the war, and they were allied with Borland, not uncontroversially‚Äîthe alliance sparked many mozard protests in cities across the country.

The Kingdom of France was seen to have profited unjustly from the Second German War, gaining significant territory while (according to many) not investing much manpower or material resources.

In the 1820s and 30s, immigration to the Novomund (and especially to New Provence) spiked from those countries which had been most devastated by the war.

##

36.5: Wars and Rumours of Wars

a compilation of my notes on several major events in the history of the world around Borland.

The First German War (‚Äì1770) was a conflict in eighteenth-century Europe which cannot neatly be lumped in with the somewhat-earlier Democratic Wars. Its conclusion is usually taken as the start of the Long Peace.

Saxony was ascendant immediately following the First German War, controlling more territory than it had in any point in its history. This stood in in good stead for early industrialisation, especially in the capital of Lepzi.

-----

The Democratic Wars were a collection of international and civil wars which took place in the mid-eighteenth century.

The earliest incarnations of steeplepost technology are seen during the Democratic Wars; usually these consisted of lines of temporary signal towers which could only send simple coded messages, such as an hour to launch an assault. One more advanced and permanent system was used to connect Cordova to Malaga in Andalus during their particular conflict with Morrack.

Albion saw many changes in the aftermath of the Democratic Wars. Long contested between Markland and Kent, the city of London found itself newly independent for one final, lasting time.

The end of the Democratic Wars and the concomitant massive migrations of people is suspected to have contributed to the global spread of pennackit dystentery, which begins in earnest in 1753.a compilation of my notes on several major events in the history of the world around Borland.

The First German War (‚Äì1770) was a conflict in eighteenth-century Europe which cannot neatly be lumped in with the somewhat-earlier Democratic Wars. Its conclusion is usually taken as the start of the Long Peace.

Saxony was ascendant immediately following the First German War, controlling more territory than it had in any point in its history. This stood in in good stead for early industrialisation, especially in the capital of Lepzi.

37.2: seucq "footpath"

seucq /sawk/
- footpath, trail, a way set intentionally for people to walk along;
- trail, tracks, footsteps, those marks left by an animal or vehicle after it has passed by and which can be followed;
- legacy, aftereffects, impact, the consequences of a person's life and the ways in which they have changed the world;
- (in particular) descendants, progeny, one's children and their children etc. as opposed to one's forebears

Etymology: from Old Boral seucque, siuke "track, path", from Borland Latin silqua, sƒìlqua "track, trail (of an animal)". Thought to reflect a Borland Kelt word *seilquar, from earlier *sleiquro (in which case cognate with Welsh ≈Çœâybr "path").  Extended in sense to metaphorical "things left behind one" at least by the fifteeneth century but probably earlier.

Nos trovaum un seucq forga√∞ de laprot.
/n…îz troÀàvom …™n sawk f…îrÀàgaŒ∏ de laÀàpr…ît/
[n…îz t Ä äÀàvom …™n Ààsawk f äÀêÀàgah de l…êÀàp Ä…ît]
1s find-pst.1p indef path create-p.pst from rabbit
We found a path made by rabbits.

37.3: svattour "scout"

svattour /svaÀàtur/ [zv…êÀàt äÀê]
- scout, a person or group of people sent out to perform reconnaissance or otherwise bring in information (especially in a military context);
- investigator, private detective, an agent hired by a person for the purpose of evidence gathering;
- harbinger, failsafe, early warning, a person or thing which gives advance notice (especially of danger or other unwanted occurrences)

also svattar /svaÀàtar/ [zv…êÀàt…ëÀê] to scout, investigate, act as an early warning

37.4: fouceur "handsome"

fouceur /fuÀàtsawr/ [f äÀàtsa.w…ê]
- handsome, pretty, having a pleasing appearance and especially (of a person) being physically attractive;
- charming, charismatic, exuding personal charm and inducing people around to like one;
- (archaic or affected) wily, sneaky, full of clever tricks making one difficult to keep up with or predict

Etymology: from Middle Boral, originally in the last sense as an attributive use of the (now-obsolete) noun fouceur "rascal, trickster". This reflects Old Boral fo(r)cevr "rogue, blackguard", a direct descendant of Latin furcifer "scoundrel, (derogatory) slave", literally "yoke-bearer". The shift in sense from "deceptive > wily > charming > handsome" is somewhat extreme, yet every stage (occurring over approximately two centuries from 1650 to 1850) is well-attested in the historical record.

##

38.5: Steeplepost

Steeplepost refers to any of a group of similar technologies (flag-lamp, barrick, longform, gyre, etc.) used for transmitting messages over long distances.

Development
The very earliest incarnations of the steeplemesh arose during the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century, but these tended to be single lines of temporary signal towers set up to span terrain that made it too costly to simply send a rider with a message. Moreover, these were set up only to transmit simple messages; for example, an hour to launch an attack.

These early wartime towers usually relied on lampfire and thus were reliable only at night, or else a collection of patterned flags, which were usable at day but required a shorter distance between watchtowers. The two-arm system, allowing up to nine separate states to be transmitted, was first tested in Thynen (a town in Willemy), where the church's weathervane was reportedly commandeered to send the first lines of the Pater Noster to the neighbouring town Bost. (Hence, probably, the name ‚Äústeeplepost‚Äù.)

By 1820 Ambrosia had unveiled its Steeplepost Tapestry in Aiga d'Angoixa, which promised to allow the (very slow) transmission of images along the lines by communicating the brightness of each pip in an enormous grid, in the manner of a cross-stitch. It was on the Tapestry that the infamous Lies of 1824 were first displayed.

The very first cross-sea steeplepost lines were established around the same time, with air-steeples in the Rustigh Strait connecting Borland to Willemy during the 1830s. This link would last only until 1854, however, when it was replaced with a barrick line.

The middle of the nineteenth century saw the flag-lamp system gradually superseded by barrick lines generally. Uptake was uneven owing to the twin constraints of geography and vested interests; for example, Mendevan powers tended to embrace the new technology more enthusiastically than their similarly-situated Vetomundine counterparts.

The new lines made partially-automated transmission much easier to develop. A prototype wired tachygraph, the Vilmanas Teleclavia, first succesfully sent a message via the steeplemesh in 1859.

Encoding
Steeplescripts, or methods by which a message is translated into a transmissible form, have many forerunners in secret codes used for spycraft and warfare. For example, Markland under King Andrew III during the Wars of Fealty employed "Thieves' Script" to send secret messages, converting each letter or common word into a series of three symbols.

One early line in Andalus (from Cordova to M√°laga, usually given as the first line connecting two major cities, and essentially just formalising the signal system that had existed for the duration of their conflict with Morrack) used a variant of the ancient Polybius square adapted for Arabic script.

The first modern steeplescript was developed in Cracow by engineer Sigmund Adamich and his son Andrei as part of the royal initiative to connect Cracow to Bitom, using a set of four symbols, traditionally referred to as "dot, dash, bar and hook".

Cultural Detaxion
The spread of the steeplemesh reduced overland communication times extremely rapidly (by the end of the nineteenth century some places boasted thrice-daily post delivery, not to mentioned the nascent wired tachygraphs), and undersea barrick links would further connect the world.

Steeplemesh may have contributed to the decline of the voidtale fashion, demystifying formerly-unknowable distant lands and weakening the cultural narrative of the pioneering documentarian explorer.

##

39.5: Andalus

Andalus is a polity in the south of the Spanish peninsula. The languages of Morrove Arabic and Mostara Spanish are both widely spoken in the region.

History
Ninth-century Andalus saw the flowering of theoretic advancements; in particular, glassmaking techniques accelerated rapidly due to a cut-off of supply from the east, leading to the prodigious export of so-called "jackdaw glass".

The Brethin religious order spread into the Christian-majority areas of Andalus from the north during the eleventh and twelfth centuries. Around this time Andalus lost significant territory in the north and east to Christian polities, such as the newly-established Kingdom of Barcelon.

In the fifteenth century, while its neighbour Vascony was ascendant, Andalus was beset by struggles. Controlled from across the straits by Morrack, its issues included two successive succesion crises as well as the 1457 Lisbon Massacre.

Vascony was forced to cede Portingale, its economic heart, to Andalus after a series of military successes in the Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century.

Andalus was embroiled in conflict with Morrack (as its neighbour attempted a reconquest) for a prolonged period in the eighteenth century; some but not all of the involved skirmishes are traditionally grouped with the concurrent Democratic Wars unfolding globally. A signalling line from Cordova to M√°laga set-up at this time would develop into the world's first steeplepost connection between two major cities.

People
Abdul Raman IV (fl. 1188), ruler of the Admiralty of Cordova under the Single Caliphate
Joan Bensa√≠d (fl. 1951), author of the 1951 scholastic quire 'The Communication Toolset' (El Herramente de Communicaz√≥n)
Jorala (fl. c18-19), landscape artist born in Cazris whose later work was influenced by the voidtale craze
Judeta Barracin (fl. 1919), author who helped pioneer the genre of social comedy with her work 'Flaxen Hearts'
Tal of Murcia (fl. 1211), medieval scholar who in 1211 wrote 'On Unknown Areas Scribed by Arcs'

Necun no'rn y commoven√ß dy dannesc vecina√∞ com piglment; ag dou cost dy Canal caf batman beno√ßaurn l'instabilta√∞ politic (oc a digr y sfondran√ß Wessaxon ag nor√∞ e√∞ y ci querel lombard ag sou√∞) por e√∞egl concarr e fragossau instan√ßar.

40.2: batman "vigilante"

batman /batÀàman/
- vigilante, one who acts outside the law to right a perceived wrong (often to avenge a murder or similar act of violence);
- mercenary, hitman, one who enacts violence (especially assassinations) for hire;
- warfarer, warrior, combatant, one engaged in warfare as a member of a band of soldiers

Etymology: seventeenth-century clipping of earlier abatman "combatant, mercenary", which descends from Old Boral abat manc/√ß "mercenary (band), hired soldier", literally "war-seller". The first element abat "war" is a deverbal noun from abatr "fight, make war" (modern abattr), while the second manc/√ß "seller, stall, booth" is of unclear origin.

The status of the final consonant in manc/√ß (since lost) is disputed. If palatalised /mants/, the word is thought to reflect Insular Latin manentia "shop, household, inn", nominal derivative of mane≈ç "I stay, remain, spend the night" (compare fen√ß "split, break" from Latin findentia < find≈ç "I cleave, split"). The alternative theory that it descends from Latin merx "goods, wares" (modern mar√ß) is semantically tempting but phonetically implausible.

If instead the word is unpalatalised /mank/, it is generally agreed that it reflects Latin mang≈ç "dealer, seller (of slaves)", either directly from the Latin‚Äîalthough one would expect to see also *mangon < mang≈çnem‚Äîor via the Old English descendant mangian "to trade, traffic in". In either case, the final consonant does not appear anywhere after the eleventh century, perhaps due to influence from the English suffix -man "person, man".

Se po√∞e noc l'e√ß rumour d'un batman corroular.
/se poÀà√∞e n…îk l…õts riÀàmur d…™n batÀàman Àåko.ruÀàlar/
[se p äÀà√∞3 n…î l…õs  Ä…™Ààm äÀê d…™m b…êtÀàman Àåko. Ä äÀàl…ëÀê]

##

40.5: Albion

Albion is the largest of the Northwestern Isles, situated directly north of Gaul. To its west and east lie Ireland and Borland, respectively.

History
Germanic migration from the continent into Albion took place through the fifth and sixth centuries, leading to the establishment of Markland, Kent, Wessex, Anglia, Northumber and several smaller and short-lasting kingdoms.

The westward encroachment of Saxons drove a emigration of Kelts from (what would become) Devon to Kernow, and for the period between the Battle of Gaulford (656) and Vigo the Magnificent's reconquest the entire peninsula was under the sole authority of Wessex.

The original calling of the Brethin religious order, founded in the eighth century, was to chronicle the Christianisation of Albion and Ireland.

Vikings raids began in Albion with the destruction of Halgrive in 824, although news of Dane incursions would have travelled west from Borland for some years by that point. While Northumber would fall completely to Dane rule, neighbouring Markland withstood (due perhaps to a fortunate lull in its perennial conflict with the Welsh kingdoms) even as much of its eastern territory was settled by the Danes.

The Guithle kingdom of Scotland was established in the ninth century with the help of Harald the Blue of Sodrick, displacing or merging with the existing Pict culture and language.

Sodrick lost much of its territory to the kingdom of Dunclothe in the tenth century, as it had been weakened by extended vikings to the east and the short-term decline in the Dane Supremacy.

By the turn of the second millennium, the south of the island is dominated by an expanded Kent (having recently subdued the remains of Wessex) below Markland and a now-unified Kingdom of Wales. To the north lie the confederate kingdoms of the Danes and then a Scotland even now influenced by Norrish and English peoples.

Much of southern Albion was conquered in the eleventh century by Normandy (with the aid of Devon in the form of the Kernovan Conjuring), consolidating the island bar Wales as far north as Edinbrough into what would become the First Drengot Empire.

The kingdom of Wales began to expand in the thirteenth century, conquering Devon after a long campaign. Tensions with Markland remained high (as they had done for almost a millennium), with the reignition of the Mallor Wars and the sack of Car Vantel/Rexam in 1301.

The late fifteenth century saw much of Albion (and some of its neighbours) embroiled in the Wars of Fealty, which lasted for forty-six years. While other polities in Europe sought to expand their trade meshes (either westward following the Novomundine Landfall or exploiting new routes to Asia), the majority of the wealth and armies of Albion was dedicated to war.

The polities of Albion were still recoving from the Wars of Fealty in 1519 and thus were extremely vulnerable to the Second Great Dying.

Albion saw many changes in the aftermath of the Democratic Wars of the eighteenth century. Long contested between Markland and Kent, the city of London found itself newly independent for one final, lasting time.

The Long Peace around the turn of the nineteenth century saw the Deviance movement take root across Albion, initially via the mesh of Promethean clubs that had arisen over the preceding decades. The movement flourished perhaps due to Albion's many religious minorities and the strong association of the Church with local authority.

Polities

- order, ordering, arrangement, programme, a particular sequence or way of setting out a collect of people, objects or events;

##

41.5: Gurjat

Gurjat is a polity on the western coast of India.

History
The medieval period saw a flourishing of mathematics across India, and Gurjat was no exception. For instance, the now-familiar angle addition identities for vatre and arrhumb [sine and cosine] were first noted by the mathematicians of the city of Uj√©n.

Gurjat was one of the stopping points for the British ship the Ph≈ìbos under Captain mac Kellot during its famed voyage in the early sixteenth century. The ship spent several months at port there in 1512, after having circumnavigated Africa and before continuing on to Madray [Madurai] and thence to the Great Borunesk Meeting of 1516.

Gurjat enjoyed a significant trade empire in the seventeenth century, spanning from African ports like Ungotia [Zanzibar] and even Vascon-controlled Sant Adrian [Bioko] in the west to Cathay (and eventually New Leudong) in the east. Gurjat traders made up a good fraction of those merchants expelled from Moscat in 1652, several months before the fall of the Ibadine Imamate to Hadramot forces.

People
- Manesh Camdar (fl. 1813), life theorist whose research on fecundation in marine sponges provided the first proof that it comprises the union of sperm and egg cases

42.3: mur√∞on "ten thousand"

Etymology: from Old Boral mur√∞, mir√∞ "countless, very many; ten thousand" with the addition of then-productive augmentative suffix -on (from Latin nominal derivational suffix -≈ç, -≈çnem). In turn this word is a semi-learned form via Late Latin m»≥rias, m»≥riadem from Ancient Greek Œº·ø°œÅ·øê·æ∞ÃÅœÇ (m≈´ri√°s) "ten thousand, countless".

The forms with <u> (reflecting [ è]) instead of expected <i> is unlikely to be any remnant of Hellenising Latin <y> /y/ (which was never common as far afield as Borland). More plausible is a sporadic labialising effect from the preceding /m/, also seen in words like mug < Latin mƒ´ca "crumb".

In the Old Boral period it is most often used indeterminately for any large quantity; its more specific sense returns to common use (initially in merchant accounts) in the thirteenth century.

42.5: Two Pens

collected notes on a couple of the many famous people from across the history of this alternate world.

Ezio Carvallo (fl. 1860) was a Portingale author most remembered for his contributions to the development of the genre of masquira romance.

Works
Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico (In Either a Mask or a Suit), a 1860 work which would be the first in a twelve-strong series of tales following the vigilante thief Matthias o Grallon.
The book was released in Kentish in 1865 as 'We Arive in Unwemmed [Immaculate] Clothes', translated by Christopher Wood; Anscon Polgat translated the book into Boral under the title 'Cuscon Re√∞r Cognit' (Whoever [We Are] Behind [Our] Disguises)

--

Catrin of Kernow (d. 1233) was an influential figure in Tetrarchic Borland.

Family
Catrin of Kernow was the elder sister of King Alan VI of Greater Devon, who orchestrated the Realignment of Devon in 1225. Their uncle was Damian the Wise, the first (Roman) Pope to be elected from among the Brethin religious order.

Legacy
In 1292 the chronicle 'Y Vi√∞ a Catrin de Cornou' (The Life of Catrin of Kernow) was written, detailing her early life, marriage into Borlish noblility and eventual assassination in 1233. The chronicle is particularly important for being the first longform work written in Borlish (as opposed to Latin) about a lay-person.

43.3: mordeth "expedition"

mordeth /m…îrÀàd…õŒ∏/
- expedition, an often-military undertaking involving travel either into enemy territory or some analogous target;
- raid, foray, incursion, a short-term and violent move into a region to ransack it for spoils or to inflict damage;
- jaunt, adventure, a short excursion for diversion or pleasure especially involving some (possibly planned) excitement;
- (historical) crusade, conquest, any of the military expeditions undertaken in the medieval Middlesea under varying degrees of Papal authority

Originally used in particular for the various wars of conquest in the Middlesea conducted at a significant distance during the eleventh to thirteenth centuries. While some of these had ostensible religious authority motivating them, many (especially further from the Masreck [Levant]) were more transparently opportunistic.

43.5: Barcelon

Barcelon is a Middlesea polity in the east of the Spanish peninsula.

History
In its early existence from the ninth to the thirteenth century, the county of Barcelon (soon to be the duchy and indeed from 1067 the kingdom) was ruled by the House of Barcelon. It had perennial skirmishes with Tolose to the north, so that even when Tolose became a vassal state to the First Drengot Empire in the eleventh century, it maintained a standing army for protection against Barcelon.

By the late twelfth century, the Kingdom of Barcelon controlled significantly more territory at the expense of Andalus, including the population centres of Saragossa, Valencia and (briefly) Toledo.

Barcelon conquered the region of Tolose from Ascendant Vascony in the first decade of the sixteenth century, thereby (along with territorial gains by Burgundy) depriving it entirely of its Middlesea coastline. In the same period, the War for the Barcelon Succession took place, although notably it only lasted for four days.

The early sixteenth century also saw Barcelon rattle sabres with the newly independent Provence, involving piratical raids on Provincial cities (and especially Marsella) supported financially by Barcelon. Part of the resolution involved the region of Narbona having double obligations to King James III of Barcelon and to Lord Amig√≤t in Provence.

The longest-reigning monarch of the Kingdom of Barcelon is James VI (Jaume VI), who ruled into the eighteenth century for 58 years and after whom is named the famous jaumetia, a whitefish stew.

People
Monarchs
Martha II (Marta II, crowned 1186)
James III (Jaume III, fl. 1517)
James V (Jaume V, fl. 1670), who spent much of his childhood in a Cordin wardship in Borland
James VI (Jaume VI, reigned 1691‚Äì1749), longest-reigning monarch in the history of the Kingdom of Barcelon
Other
Raymond al Mustafa (fl. 1750), mathematician who worked with Rachel Desslut on the nascent field of methodics while he was under the patronage of the Duke of Aragon

44.1: Borrowings from Borlish

excerpt in translation from Daughters of the Sea: The Northwestern Isles, written in 1956 by Merch author Challow Huddresfell.

Nentamen, √∞a me√∞es par proximta√∞ soul, y lenga√ß anglesc son cos parmanent vanagl borallesc adaptant de ja√ßol com y Mita√∞-Asc.
Nonetheless, if only by virtue of proximity, the English languages have been adapting Borlish terms even from the medieval period.

Com bustr, degnant ig nos ligau y ci siecr tost nell'Istr posc-roman con migration des Albion vars Istr Boral (cos particuler, par issent dy reduction markesc d'Anglia Auster ny siecr nouf),
For example, while we associate the early centuries of the post-Roman Isles with migration from Albion into Borland (in particular, after the Merch subjugation of East Anglia in the ninth century),

e√ß parol com danny "un treublant, applattiscent", bather "ba√∞r con fil" e parren "sojornour, un alanda√∞, corrova√∞" entraurn Ve√∞r Anglesc des Ve√∞r Boral ny siecr deg soul (lonc lour y preu yen√ß ne chronic de monastir).
words like danny "a nuisance, an irritating person", bather "stringed musical intrument" and parren "traveller, newcomer, novice" enter Old English from Old Borlish in the tenth century alone (according to their first attestations in monastic chronicles).

44.2: jou follant "on the fence"

jou follant / íu f…îÀàlant/ [ ùu f äÀàlan(t)]
- on the fence, undecided, weighing pros and cons, not having chosen between one of many (especially two) options;
- between a rock and a hard place, facing a dilemma, being in a difficult situation with no obviously good options

Etymology: idiom first attested in the seventeenth century, literally meaning "walking on a balance beam", with the image of an acrobat balancing on a seesaw. The noun jou "yoke, carrying pole, balance beam, seesaw" descends directly from Latin iugum with the same meaning, while the verb follar "to walk along, to tread" reflects Old Boral follar "to stamp, crush, tread on", from a Latin noun via Late Latin full≈ç "I trample, I full (cloth)".

He was on the fence about taking the job at the bureau.

44.3: carrug mettr avant naumagl "get ahead of oneself"

carrug mettr avant naumagl /kaÀàraj Ààm…õtrÃ© aÀàvant noÀàm…õjl/
- get ahead of oneself, count one's eggs before they hatch, jump the gun, to start on some activity without being adequately prepared;
- put the cart before the horse, make a mountain out of a molehill, to mix up one's priorities and treat minor things as being disproportionately important (or vice versa)

Etymology: idiom used since the medieval period in various languages but first seen in its modern form in Boral in the sixteenth century. The phrase literally means "to put the cart in front of the oxen", in which the noun carrug "cart" descends via Old Boral carru»ù from synonymous Late Latin carr≈´ca (from Gaulish).

Meanwhile, naumagl "ox, castrated bull employed as a draught animal" enters the language in the early Middle Boral period from Norman un naumail /nawÀàma é/ "an ox/a steer", rebracketed from earlier un aumail, itself a masculine variant of aumaille "cattle, livestock". This comes from earlier almaille, armaille, from Latin neuter plural animƒÅlia "animals" reinterpreted as a feminine singular.

Mettau noc carrug avant naumagl; ja n'eu nos y muncef me√∞es saut.
/m…õÀàto n…îk kaÀàraj aÀàvant noÀàm…õjl |  ía naw n…îz i m…™nÀàts…õf meÀà√∞…õz sot/
[m…™Ààto n…îk k…êÀà Äaj …êÀàvan n äÀàm…õj ä |  ùa naw n…îz i m…™nÀàts…õf m…™Àà√∞…õz sot]
put-1p.sbj neg cart in.front ox | yet neg-have.1p 1p def area even leave.p.pst
Let's not get ahead of ourselves; we haven't even left the borough yet.

hurstar /h…™rÀàstar/ [h…™ÀêÀàst…ëÀê]
- equip, be supplied with, to obtain something necessary (especially weaponry or similar tools) in order to carry out a specific action or task;
- don, put on, to clothe oneself with some article of clothing or accessory;
- be taught, self-teach, train oneself in, to learn a particular skill in preparation for some specific situation

##

44.5: Cordin and Brenna

collected notes on a major population centre on Borland, as well as the last queen of the First Kingdom of Borland.

Cordin is a city in Borland. It is known for its prolific press Cordin Editions, which from the late twenthieth century on has provided textbooks to schools nationwide. Its name is usually considered to be unrelated to the Borlish word cordin "string".

History
In the late 1660s the young James V of Barcelon was a ward in Cordin.

By the Long Peace period Cordin had become one of the largest cities on the island, with a thriving high society to rival that of the capital in Damvath.

People
Professor Marc Jeichey (fl. 2019), lecturer in concurrence history at the University of Cordin who spent some years working in peripatetico at the Dijon New School
Theign Roufler (fl. 2016), scholar and author of book 'From Tablets to Slates: The Story of Education' (Des Delt vars Dois : Y Stoir d'Education)

---

Brenna of Barrow (fl. 898) was the cousin of King Roderick of Dunclothe. She married the young King Jothey of Borland shortly before the First Kingdom of Borland fell to the Danes in 898. Subsequently she fled with her husband to Sothbar, where she bore several children.

Two decades later Sothbar surrendered to Dane Borland and the family were forced to flee once more, this time to Kent. Much of our knowledge of the remainder of her life is preserved through the Brethin copies of her correspondence with her aging uncle in Dunclothe.

excerpt in translation from 1999 scholastic quire The Grimmer Hall: Developments in Early Methodics (originally published in Cambrick as ≈Åys y Grymrage√∞: Dasplygad nhy Methodig Kysevin), written by New Leudong historian and methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin (Brisbane).

45.2: sortir ag vousc "to tryst"

sortir ag vousc /s…îrÀàt…™r …õj vux/
- to tryst, rendezvous, to meet somebody in private to be intimate with them;
- to hook up, to have a casual sexual experience with someone

Etymology: idiom attested from the late eighteenth century but probably older, literally meaning "to go out to the strawberries". The verb sortir "to go out" is a descendant of Latin sortior "to draw by lots, to distribute" with an unclearly-motivated but very early semantic change (compare cognate forms across Gaul and northern Italy).

The noun vousc "strawberry" reflects Old Boral ba»ù vousc (cf. modern Boral bag /b…õj/ "berry"), presumably a calque of Old English‚Äôs synonymous str√©awber»ùe "strew-berry", so called for its plant growing low down, the fruits appearing to have been strewn across the ground.
