##

45.5: Willemy

Willemy is a polity in northern Europe, situated east of Norman Kent and France.

History
The Duchy of Willemy was a minor polity within the German Empire during the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries. Forming much of the empire's border with the First Drengot Empire to the west, defence against the marksmen of Willemy led Drengot Emperor Roger II to spend greatly on castle fortifications from Flanders to the Alps.

The Great Flie Flood of 1171 swept away much of Lower Frisia in Willemy, creating several new islands in the Rustigh Strait (such as Texel) and killing then-ruler Lovis the Tall, who was in Amstel at the time. The devastation in the region is thought to have allowed Borlish Axbane to flourish as a trading post in the following century.

In the Revitalist Period and for some time afterwards, Willemy was split between neighbouring powers and portions of its territory changed hands regularly. The duchy of Avosche was unusually stable and held by France for over a century.

Trading operations from Willem and its cities in the Stadbund (the Br√≥derrat) contributed to staddomains in Atlantic Cappatia, most notably Neuwillem, whose Br√≥derratcronike (comprising a record of its activities from 1561 to 1597) is an important first-hand account of conditions in the region at the time.

Willem was the focal point of the seventeenth-century Blue Trans-Willem War, usually numbered among the Romantic Wars. This war led incidentally to the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy and the creation of the Duchy of Far Suebia in Bavaria.

The two-arm system for sending steeplepost was first deployed in Willem in the early nineteenth century, to send the first lines of the Pater Noster from the church in the town of Thynen to neighbouring Bost. This event is likely what led to the name 'steeplepost' being applied to the technology.

People
Rulers
Duke Lovis the Tall (d. 1171), who perished in the Great Flie Flood
Adaill√© Nassow, Duchess d'Avosche (1524-1608), infamous during her life for her influence at the court of David of France
Other
Carlyn van Stede (fl. 1965), author of parachthon giftale 'While Gold Yet Runs in My Veins' (Zolang Gout d√≥r myn √Ådren Noh Vl√≥ye)
Dr Mickel Cr√°yenschot (fl. c19), noted early supporter of the Deviance movement and public tovarick even as France embraced the Modest Arrangement.
Nikl√°s der Fecht (fl. 1828), a mathematician in the city of 
Tewis Camigner (fl. c16), theorist who noted the high sugar content of beet syrup, though betraver sugar would not be produced commercially for another two centuries

46.2: bro "dude"

bro /bro/ [b Äo]
- (vocative) dude, bro, mate, term of address for a (usually male) friend used generally in an admiring or appreciative context;
- macho, jocky, masculine (person), a person typified by the impression of physical strength and other male-associated characteristics

46.5: Channels

The Rustigh Strait (Borlish: Naur Rustig /nor r…™Ààstaj/, Frisian Nao Rustig /n…ëw Ààr è.st…™g/, literally "calm strait") is the channel of water separating the southern coast of Borland from the northern coasts of Flanders and Willemy.

The strait's position often gave it great strategic importance, in terms of both military and trade. For example, coastal cities on the strait like Axbane and Amstel [Amsterdam] were natural stopping points for ships trading between Dane lands to the east and the Welsh and Vascon trade meshes to the west.

During and following the Albic Wars of Fealty, British communities of immigrants spread along the cities on both sides of the Rustig, such as Pentrose.

An air-steeple connection was set up in the 1830s across the Rustig from Naus√ßod on Borland to Laicouche in French Guillenne (now Lagc√≥ch), where the strait was less than seven leagues across. In 1854 this was superseded by the creation of an undersea barrick cable.

--

Methodics is the field of study pertaining to the discovery, development and improvement of methods. In this scholastic context a method is any series of instructions that may be unambiguously and repeatably followed to produce some result.

History
For millennia methods were developed sporadically without much awareness of general methodical principles. For example, an early method in the historical record is Euclid's procedure for finding the coarsest scale common to two whole numbers. Later we find methods involving tables of pre-computed values, such as Nadim's Method for multiplication by means of the Trapolitan identity for arrhumb [cosine].

Methods with more immediate practical use were continually developed; by the sixteenth century the threepoint method [triangulation] for calculating distances was vital in creating accurate maps for the explorations of the Intertwining Century.

One the first major results in abstract methodics came from the study of life, with Rachel Desselut's 1743 work Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, which argued that in order for living matter to duplicate, it must contain within itself an explicit method describing its construction. Although heredian acid [DNA] would not be discovered for another century, her work accurately predicted the necessary separation of Form and Method that would become the bedrock of the study of heredity.

An incresingly-important category of method from the eighteenth-century onward has been procedures for encrypting text, either to conceal it or else simply to convert it into a suitable form for a given steeplepost.

References
Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d'Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa

47.1: King James Stew

excerpt in translation from 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni, written by traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo.

De to√∞ y ragost a pescot meyon prova√∞ lonc y rimmot solary ne Barcelon e Proven√ß (noc obstroint y desc unic a pallort e√∞ olif me sarvi√∞ sur y ci play ne Mayorca), alcun den po√∞ acavar ag Rey Jagm.

##

47.5: The Modest Arrangement

The Modest Arrangement was a social movement in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries characterised by more stringent enforcement of morality codes, especially with respect to familial powers.

The Arrangement saw a resurgence in the regions of Gaul and Spain in the early nineteenth century after something of an ebb during the Long Peace. This has been attributed to the rising popularity of the Deviance movement inspiring a backlash among its opposers; certainly Modest ideology depicts itself as less secure and more embattled in its later incarnations.

One of the most salient aspects of the Modest Arrangement from the modern perspective is its propensity to censorship, particularly of works deemed contrary to decency. Famously, author Ant√≤in Herrel (latterly Tony Smith) was forced to flee Vascony to Kent after attempting to publish his blasphemous work 'Earthly Scene' (Vista Terrena).

The Second German War of the 1820s saw the grip of the Modest Arrangement weaken in many places, if only incidentally‚Äîas faith in existing power structures waned so too did people's trust in those powers' enforcement of the law. The days of Modest Vascony were numbered almost as soon as its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened and the Vallabr√¨ administration under King Hernand declared an alliance with the Convoy.

Although the Modest Arrangement would not survive the 1850 Laic Declarations in Belgrade in any official capacity, polities like Vascony, (New) Provence and Morrack would retain some hallmarks of the Arrangement well into the twentieth century.
Proponents

    Ruvien Baume, Marquis de Caillat (fl. 1810), noted for his fervence in opposition to Deviance Theology
    Diana Theodora Queiroz, considered to have laid the groundwork for the later Blue Marian movement

##

48.1: Study Materials

excerpt in translation from the condensed study materials for the New Vithor course Concurrence History of the 16th Century: Death, Ships and the Movement of the Heavens.

Un bustr l'oc euta√∞ dessur contradignt es Vascony; l'oc a gra√ß a profit Novomondin des pelur e√∞ autr enascment un a√∞oin√ß cas pouvlan√ß souva√∞ de 1500 vars 1550 (Portingale noc ig inclus) com issent d'immigration.
One counterexample to the above trend is Vascony which, thanks to Novomundine profits in furs and other goods, actually saw in increase in population from 1500 to 1550 (excluding Portingale) due to immigration.

Y fin dell'Ascenen√ß Vascon con y Var dy Dou Quen vole denou mettr ag model feudal ne Vascony pre fos y siecr fis, portant.
The end of the Vascon Ascendancy with the War of the Two Queens would end feudalism in Vascony by the end of the century, however.

(Val svoccar ig l'oc no sta kynous y dicerrem vars y model feudal finir nell'aust, yon y ci concarn√ß islamesc de Kieu e de Cazany russesc souvartoy y nobilta√∞ cos colombandessem.)
(It is worth mentioning that this was not the main factor in ending feudalism in the east, where the Islamic conquests of Kiew and Russian Cazany upended the nobility more directly.)

L'un effeit parcan√ß imprefaibr dy diminution dy model feudal sta y reitfian√ß de ley com cod suntuer.
One, perhaps unexpected, effect of the decline of the feudal system was the strengthening of laws such as sumptuary codes.

48.2: flottisc "fleet"

flottisc /fl…îÀàt…™x/ [fl äÀàt…™√ß]
- fleet, flotilla, convoy, a group of ships or occasionally of other vehicles;
- (by extension) navy, the collective naval force of a country;
- backup, reinforcements, relief force, additional troops or equipment sent to support a military action (also in metaphorical use)

Etymology: originally in the first sense only in Old Boral flotisc, deverbal noun from verb flotir "to float, to be still in the water", itself a borrowing likely from Old English flotian "to float".

48.3: pernix "nimble"

pernix /p…õrÀàn…™ks/ [p…™ÀêÀàn…™ks]
- nimble, dextrous, deft, agile, skillful with one's hands and able to make very precise movements;
- swift, fleet, rapid and graceful in motion;
- subtle, crafty, insidious, manipulative, being able to direct someone else's decisions without their knowledge

Etymology: learned borrowing in the Middle Boral period from Latin pernix "nimble, agile, swift", thought to be connected with perna "ham, haunch, gammon" (cf. inherited Boral parn "bacon"), although the expected Latin form would be *pernƒÅx.

Its latter sense develops in the eighteenth century under the malign influence of unrelated perni√ßous "harmful, pernicious, deleterious", from synonymous Latin pernici≈çsus, a derivative of pernec≈ç "I kill, slay, annihilate".

[lje Ààdut. Ä‚Äøi v…™Àà Ä…îj äl‚Äøan dit p…™ÀêÀàn…™ks]

48.5: 2nd Lexember

sarzin "buckwheat"

sarzin /sarÀàz…™n/ [s…êÀêÀàz…™n]
- (obsolete) Muslim, Saracen, a person who is a follower of Islam;
- (archaic or dialect) non-Christian, pagan, practioner of a religion other than Christianity and especially one performing practices associated with pre-Christian peoples or peoples not yet exposed to Christianity;
- (historical) cairn, a conical heap of stones placed by the pre-Roman Kelt people around Borland or a modern such heap resembling these;
- (historical, by extension) milestone, a pillar or pile of stones placed at the side of a road to mark distance;
- buckwheat, an originally Asian plant whose fruit is widely used in cooking as a pseudocereal

Etymology: in its only surviving modern sense, a clipping of the earlier bla sarzin "Saracen wheat" which is first attested in the fifteenth century, in reference to its eastern origin (the plant having been brought to Europe via the Turkish Riyalid Sultanate or possibly via Russian Cazany).

Likewise, in the senses "cairn, milestone" it is a clipping of pie√∞r sarzin "Saracen stones", from the perception that these piles of stones were pre-Christian monuments to pagan gods.

The word itself is a borrowing from Old French sarrazin "Muslim, Saracen" reflecting Late Latin Saracƒìnus and Ancient Greek Œ£·æ∞œÅ·æ∞Œ∫Œ∑ŒΩœåœÇ (Sarakƒìn√≥s). These originally referred to an Arabic or para-Arabic people of North Arabia.

Y cociner en√ßa voug cas hojol ne sarzin.
/i Àåko.tsiÀàn…õr …õnÀàtsa vuj kaz hoÀà í…îl ne sarÀàz…™n/
[i Àåk…î.ts…™Ààn…õÀê Ä‚Äø…™nÀàtsa Ààvuj kaz h äÀà ù…îw ne s…êÀêÀàz…™n]
def cook start-ipf wrong wrt pancake in buckwheat
The cook was terrible at buckwheat pancakes.

---

excerpt from The Delicious Story of Dough and Oil, a work of popular history detailing the history of many related foods across the globe. Published (originally in Awrish as T√©sta Olaivan Vl√°xerte "A World of Dough in Oil") in 1963 by first-time writer and famed chef Shomoji Taxon, who in his later years would go on to present the first cooking programme shown simultaneously on both sides of the Atlantic.

‚Ä¶see its eastern origins by its names in the languages of Europe‚Äîwhether in Horther m√∏urick ("Moorish wheat") or Bavarn creshke (originally from a Russian word meaning "Greek"). Nonetheless, sarzin [buckwheat] flour has since its introduction become a key component of diets across the continent.

We turn first to a dish which originated in Vallack communities and spread west upriver as the region was united politically under the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube] during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Even after the kingdom's collapse and the rise of the Riverine Cities in the nineteenth century, farama dry-pottage has remained an important tradition from Pojon [Bratislava] to Gollamo [Ruse].

Farama seems originally to have been associated with wedding anniversaries, as an inexpensive (at least, before the recipe was augmented with unseasonal berries or spices like dallasine [cinnamon]) of celebrating each passing year. Over time the traditions around farama have shifted; in many places the sarzin dish is now served on birthdays.

Along with the area's distinctive hodgel-cakes [in this case, blini], farama enjoyed an explosion in popularity worldwide (usually divorced from its vicine traditions) after their prominent inclusion in the film‚Ä¶

gallai√∞ "galaxy"

gallai√∞ /gaÀàleŒ∏/ [g…êÀàleh]
- galaxy, collection of stars all orbiting a common centre;
- seemingly-uncountable collection of people of things conceptualised as comprising a vast variety;
- (dated) the Milky Way, the galaxy in which the Sun and Earth are found and which is most readily visible in the night sky

Etymology: from late Old Boral galaix, galais "the Milky Way", learned borrowing from synonymous Latin galaxiƒÅs and Ancient Greek (Œ∫œçŒ∫ŒªŒøœÇ) Œ≥·æ∞Œª·æ∞Œæ·øêÃÅ·æ±œÇ, literally "milky circle". Altered in the early Middle Boral period under the influence of lai√∞ "milk", from Latin lac, lactem "milk" (or possibly from a Borland Kelt form; compare Cambrick ≈Çaeth "milk").

Used as a proper noun until the discovery of other galaxies in the Good Game period of the late nineteenth century. Metaphorical use for other vast collections of things is seen since the seventeenth century, especially in poetry.

Vos vou√∞ cas mendrem outr bon me fair par noc desmanne√ßar a Paris, cita√∞ de vare√ß e carogn, √∞a me√∞es tu derra pie√ß Fransc Ustol parsir por gra de ty fem.

laundar "slip"

laundar /lonÀàdar/ [l änÀàd…ëÀê]
- slip, skid, slide, glide, to move along a slippery surface without traction;
- trip, stumble, stagger, to walk clumsily so as to (almost) fall over;
- err, misspeak, misstep, to be mistaken about something;
- pass, elapse, (of time) to be spent, especially without one achieving anything of import

Etymology: from Old Boral laundar "slip, err", either from an unattested synonymous Latin verb *lƒÅbund≈ç or a contemporary verbal derivation of laund "slippery, unsteady" (the modern launt "slip, misstep, faux pas" is likely a later deverbal from the Middle Boral period). 49.3: 7th Lexember

garaf "grip"

garaf /gaÀàraf/ [g…êÀà Äaf]
- grip, friction, purchase, the state of being able to hold onto something without slipping;
- handhold, foothold, handle, part of a surface which allows one to hold on so as not to be moved;
- help, assistance, encouragement, physical or emotional aid from somebody that enables one not to fail;
- mastery, understanding, competence, the state of having learnt everything about something so that one can act with confidence in that domain

Etymology: from Middle Boral garaf "grip, handle", a borrowing from Portingale garafa "handle", of indeterminate origin. Either an alteration of earlier grafio, garfio "hook", from Latin graphium "stylus, pen", or else borrowed from Andalus Arabic ÿ¨Ÿéÿßÿ±ŸíŸÅŸéÿß‚Äé (g√°rf√°) "handful, claw".

epu "express"

epu /eÀàpi/ […™Ààpi]
- (of a shop or similar) express, mini, providing a more limited but more focused service than usual;
- cheap, good value, having a gratifyingly low price for the level of quality offered;
- bitesize, mini, having been stripped down in complexity and/or size for easier immediate consumption;

49.7: 11th Lexember

stoccadar "lunge forward"

stoccadar /Àåst…î.kaÀàdar/ [Àåsto.k…êÀàd…ëÀê]
- thrust, lunge, push, to make an advance with force and especially while wielding a melee weapon;
- advance, assault, to move forward as part of a military operation;
- lobby, campaign, to push for a particular policy proposal

Etymology: verbal derivative from stoccad "a lunge, thrust" either in Middle Boral or in the synonymous French estoccade, estoccader before their adoption into Boral. In either case the noun comes from Italian stoccato "thrust", past participle of stoccar "to thrust (with a sword)", itself from now-obsolete stocco "rapier, shortsword". This noun, which is a borrowing from Dutch stooken "to poke, stab", is reflected in Boral stoc "truncheon, club".

jannestar "sweep"

jannestar /Àå ía.n…õÀàstar/ [Àå ùa.n…™Ààst…ëÀê]
- sweep, brush, to clean (a surface) using a broom or similar implement;
- (archaic or humorous) involve, embroil, drag in, to cause to be overly involved in;
- brush off, disregard, dismiss, to pay no attention to;
- (colloquially) vacuum, hoover, to suck up dust or similar detritus using a machine to create negative pressure

Etymology: verbal derivation of jannest "broom, cleaning brush", from Old Boral janeste, geneste "broom, broom shrub" (the former originally from northern dialect), from Latin genesta "broom shrub". The noun jannest has been supplanted in its botanical sense by originally-Kelt banag "broom shrub" (see also Norman balay, Cambrick banadyl). In the last sense, attested since 1941.

- trawl, to fish from a slow-moving vessel using a trawlnet;
- (by extension) sweep, scour, ransack, comb through, to make an exhaustive and methodical search over a defined area or down an explicit list of options;
- search, query, index, locate, to perform a search of a information organisation system (e.g. in a library) or, later, of a database or of webpages using a computer

Etymology: originally in the first sense, borrowed along with the noun javig "trawlnet; later, (a) methodical search" from synonymous Portingale xaviga in the thirteenth century; the initial voicing is unexplained and has been attributed to dialect variation. The Portingale word is a borrowing from Arabic ÿ¥Ÿéÿ®ŸéŸÉŸéÿ© (shabaca) "fishing net", likely via Mostara Spanish.

    We searched the whole library for syntheses involving gold.

opening of an 1813 letter sent by Dorothe Shock, proprietress of Miszen [1] emporium De Marnoshring, to her mother Johanne in her home village of Siorow. This missive is taken from the collected correspondence kept in trust at the Marnosh Gravatory [archives], the enterprise having grown dramatically from a single shop selling trinkets to a quasipolitic guild [multinational corporation] controlling much of the market for children's toys and various material hobbies. 

My dearest mother,

First I must reassure you that [her brother] young Crazi has arrived in Miszen safe and in good spirits. He has not taken to commerce as enthusiastically as you had hoped (or at least not as well as he has taken to nights out with soldierly friends and woodheaded mornings!) but his allegedly-charming features seem to have contributed to a string of advantageous deals with the usual suppliers.

You shall I expect be unsurprised to hear that the Potchware [2] woodcarvings refuse to be bought at even half the cost of importing them (never mind Hamburg's stanglehold on the Elbe); eventually I managed to convince a particuarly-credulous customer from Crovatia that they were the only attested artworks of a remote people of Chrysia, which limited our losses to an acceptable amount.

The mayor continues to promise better roads out west to Lepzi‚Äîit is nonsensical that one cannot make the journey in a day and must suffer an uncomfortable night in the only Ossatz lodginghouse with tolerable chambermaids. I cannot see that such projects will find funds for themselves so long as the garrison demands its gold, and so long as the Boheme menace rattles its sabre across the southern‚Ä¶

---

[1] An urban regional centre in Saxony on the site of our Meissen.
[2] A staddomain in Cappatia [South America] known at the time for its sugar and cocoa exports, and not (yet) renowned as a centre of artistic expression.

excerpt from the introduction of Modern Developments in Benjamine Detaxion [Aromatic Synthesis], a 1942 quire written by alchemist Ferris Bewly through the Conster Health Edifice and intended to provide a reference on the recent synthesis of various new substances and medicines around the world.

‚Ä¶Jawan frankincense.

The first major section of this work will cover those hebetant substances [painkillers] which have come out of workshops globally over the last two decades. The sterling example is undoubtedly cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine (CD) [1], sold under various names and perhaps most familiar to Markland as the coveted 'Tommy pills', is a product of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong and the University of Rimack in Tavancy [2] to find hebetants with faster action and fewer adversions.

Comprising (as the longform name implies) branches of cozebdone oil and maschal [3] in opposed position, the substance was first synthesised in 1932 and has already surpassed salix-derivative pills [4] in annual sales everywhere from Sangathy to Adighe [5]. This is due to the former's greater efficacy in relieving pain, which finds it more widely used than the previous bevy of remedies which may merely help to reduce symptoms of fever.

In sufficiently high concentrations CD has also been demonstrated to function in unguento, although the inefficiency and commodity of this‚Ä¶

---

[1] Here the longform unambiguous name for ibuprofen.
[2] Polities in southeastern Chrysia [Australia] and northwestern Cappatia [South America].
[2] Or as we would call them, isobutane (2-methylpropane) and propionic (propanoic) acid.
[4] For example, aspirin.
[5] Polities in far-inland Mendeva [North America] and the Caucasus.

Hints at the discrete nature of the composition of atoms (a phrase which itself would not have sat well with either side of the Atomicity Rift; atomicists were yet wedded to the intrinsic Leucippic model, while their opposition regarded the entire scheme as a merely useful fiction) had been encroaching into the understanding of matter for decades. At the same time, the apparently-unrelated phenomenon where the lightest corporals had all been measured to have weights almost exact multiples of that of aquifex [hydrogen], while this was not true at all of heavier ones (even such prosaic substances as copper!), demanded explanation. In 1881‚Äîa dozen years before the invention of a device that could even test her theory‚ÄîKievan theorist Nike Sarantylack proposed to resolve both questions at once. ##

53.5: Brasil

Brasil is an island in the North Atlantic off the eastern coast of Mendeva. In the Novomund it was both the first location to be temporarily settled by people from the Vetomund and also the inception of sustained contact following the Landfall of 1471.

Unlike the name Mendeva, it is not known when the name Brasil was first used for this land. What is understood is that it was inspired by the Irish myth of Oil Breasail, a landmass west of Ireland that was said to be shrouded in mist except for one day every seven years. Islands labelled Brasil or similar wordforms appear sporadically on late-medieval maps of the Atlantic coast, usually much smaller and further east than the real place.

The Princess Alexandra of Vascony (sister of Ambrose the Ascendant) was the first Vetomundine royal to set foot in the Novomund, landing in Brasil in 1477.

By the mid-20th century Brasil had been fully incorporated as a region of Ambrosia, retaining only a few vestiges of local political independence. In 1950 the Brasil town of Paratzon formed one end of a new Atlantic link between Ambrosia and Morrack.

Places
Paratzon, a coastal town in eastern Brasil which is home to an observatory
People
Fabian Most√Ýf (fl. c19), astronomer at the Paratzon observatory and grandson of mathematician Raymond al Mustafa
Crist√≤f Unquenjica (fl. 1971), author of popular history text Going to Brasil, an account of the first few Vascon voyages to Mendeva

54.1: Halloumi Hash

translated recipe from 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni (Middlesea Meals), written by world-traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo.

Primer dragm ougl raccaldar n'un frigsoir la√∞ e tatover frigsar sur foy preu√ß un cart hour, mout togla√∞ (fin a y morn√ß se crostn e√∞ y carn se molfin).

Bulb de ceul e√∞ y pevr jarras affigr e ci cocinar longhessem na√∞usc.
Add in the stalk-onion bulb and the jarraspepper and cook a little longer.

Mistur souse√Ør, ougl ancour raccalder ny frigsoir, e√∞ y kes frigsar dessur jusc aure.
Subside the mix and heat more oil in the pan, on which fry the cheese until golden.

Tatover e kes condensar e√∞ envouvr cos total tandic aromar con sal e pievr sec ne calscon quanta√∞ casc quemant.
Bring together the tattover and cheese and fully involve while including salt and drypepper in whichever amount suits.

Farrago por√ßonnar ne bol covaucli√∞ e ci retenir jos y foy, soulor to√∞ ou coroccar sur y frigsoir savora√∞ e ci frigsar volonter.
Divide the farrago by covered bowls and hold near the fire, then crack the eggs into the seasoned pan and fry as desired.

Cos final casc ou mettr dessur dy desc e√∞ y ligt dy ceul fair scadrir atras.
Finally place the eggs atop each dish and scatter with the rest of the stalk-onion.

##

54.5: Medieval Mathematics

Nadim of Trapoles (fl. c12) was a mathematician at the Observatory in Trapoles, which at the time was a major Middlesea port for the Single Caliphate during the Second Tetrarchy. He is remembered for formulating the Trapolitan identity

[image]

which allows one to multiply two quantities using only addition, subtraction and enumerations of arrhumb [cosine tables].

---

Jambor of Hungary (fl. c12) was an early king of Hungary following the state's succession from the principality the century before. He oversaw significant expansion of Awrish influence, entering into personal union with eastern principalities such as Halich and Vallack, and becoming an ally of the Malchassian Romans to the south.

The great Library of Sobjan was built during his reign, an enduring gravatory of contemporary works. These included mathematical texts from the Single Caliphate which were at that time being translated into Latin in great quantities.

---

Saint Marinus (Italian: San Marino) is a historical city in the Italian state of Lombardy. It is recognised for the renowned Autonome University, which having been founded in the eleventh century is one of the oldest still-running educational establishments in the world, and especially for its Sovereign Library on the slopes of Titan.

Scholars of the Autonome University were among the first to translate many mathematical texts from Arabic into Latin; for instance, they compiled a liber numerorum in 1216 N which (surviving in a copy at the Library of Sobjan) contains the earliest-known enumeration of arrumb values in Europe.
People

    Jovanna Ariperto (fl. 1998), deixist researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus
    Dijaca Rofotto (fl. 1997), historian of medieval religion for the Sovereign Library
    Lluprando da Treviso (fl. 1491), engineer at the Sovereign Library remembered for his work on the augrim of mozate forms

##

55.5: Portingale

Portingale is a polity in the western part of the Spanish peninsula, bordering Vascony and Andalus, both of which have in the past conquered parts of its territory.

History
Portingale had been established as an independent Christian kingdom in the eleventh century, having previously been a subordinate county in Leon.

Much of northern Portingale was conquered by Vascony during the Vascon Ascendancy under Ambrose III, as far south as Braga and (nominally) Cal√®s. The wealth that came to Vascony in the decades after the Novomundine Landfall of 1471 N accrued in particular to the coastal towns of conquered Portingale and to Leon.

The rump Portingale in the south (which had retained some meagre autonomy under Vascon stewardship) fell to Morrack in 1457 N as part of the expansionist campaigns of King Munir al-Hamdawi.

With its population concentrated along the coast, Portingale was ravaged early and mercilessly by the Second Great Dying in the years following the plague's arrival in Europe in 1519 N, with one fifth of the population dying within the decade.

After a series of military defeats during the Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century, Vascony was forced to cede its Portingale territory (its economic heartland) to Andalus.

Portingale at first attempted to remain neutral during the Democratic Wars of the eighteenth century, and as such received a great number of war refugees in this period. However, the All-Saints' earthquake of 1755 N severely weakened Portingale's position; as such its warring neighbours invaded Portingale soon after.

The Drengot Collusion eventually grew to count Portingale within its orbit during the first decades of the twentieth century (and perhaps held some responsibility for the Portingale Anarchy of the 1920s and 30s), although the polity was never a full member of the alliance-federacy. This led to diplomatic tensions with Vascony, located directly between the Collusion and Portingale.

For a time, Drengot astraphor supplied (via undersea powerline) much of the baric needs of Portingale, thus relying on Vascon goodwill not to interfere. In response to this precarity, in the 1950s and 60s Portingale became a pioneer of threshold force, building enough mills that by 1970 it was exporting astraphor as far afield as Lombardy.

Places
Braga, a town renowned for a continent-leading lineball team and their 82,000-seat stadium
Corunne, Portingale's northernmost city and home to the Roxa Comba, a weekly paper affiliated loosely with the Peujon Rous media guild
People
Eustace II (fl. c12, crowned 1151), longest-ruling monarch of Portingale
Ezio Carvallo (fl. 1860), author credited with the invention of the genre of masquira romance
Marcio Dourao (fl. 1930), author of lovetale _Galatheo_ which was banned in both Vascony and Morrack
Victor Fidalgo (fl. 1950), collocker for the Roxa Comba
Maya Tavares (fl. 1950), political theorist for the de Sarumptice Diplomatic Edifice

##

L'e√ß gran accommission corrogationnal descrein y liesc romannesc, con tan l'e√ß dezein nolen l'ig stikettan√ß accarr pre un siecr may.

L'oc nom es primer beno√ßa√∞ posc y Rix Democratic, candon y mita√∞ nuverrem ny siecr setto√ß er stima√∞, lon ra√ßonnabilta√∞, stant un final d'asc aure cas relo√∞e√ß e civilisation.
The name is first used after the Democratic Wars, when the latter half of the seventeenth century was seen, nonsensically, as the last golden age of refinement and civilisation.

Defagl es compara√ßon noc fair entrell'e√ß stat insoler e querellant n'Europ ny dezein 1660 e, com bustr, y ci gran veya√ß geoscopic de mac Kellot e√∞ al-Casmi ny siecr derran, ben l'envou Novomondin repleni√∞ ny sevent.
It is hard not to contrast these insular, squabbling European states of the 1660s with, say, the great geoscopic voyages of mac Kellot and al-Cazmi of the previous century, or the renewed Novomundine interest of the next.

Nentamen, nell'oc asc es ig poum nos y cart modern e suetessem vistar en√ßant a smargr tras Europ.
Nonetheless, it is in this time that we can see the familiar modern map begin to emerge across Europe.

56.2: corrogar "build infrastructure"

corrogar /Àåko.roÀàgar/ [ko. Ä äÀàg…ëÀê]
- build infrastructure, develop, to undertake public development by providing somewhere with (say) transport links and utilities like water and electricity

##

56.5: Vascony

Vascony (Vascon: Vasconna), or more fully the Vascon Kingdom of Navarre and Aquitain (Vascon: Lo Reiame Vasc√≤n a Navarr'i Aquitanna, Vask: Navarron t'Aquitainon Vasca Herria) is a polity of western Europe occupying parts of southern Gaul and northern Spain.

History
Vascony was formed from the political union of Aquitain and Navarre, later expanding to vassalise Leon in the twelfth century.

The fifteenth century saw the Vascon Ascendancy, which began by most accounts in the reign of Ambrose II and reached a peak with his grandson Ambrose III. At the start of this period Vascony was at its territorial maximum: it had conquered northern Portingale and subjugated the rest as a nominally-independent client, as well as much of southern Burgundy (which is to say, enough of Provence to share a border with the Italian states of Jeans and Lombardy).

In 1458 Ambrose III established by royal decree the New Navarre Enterprise, whose primary aim was to solidify Vascon supremacy in the trade of North Atlantic fish through the Middlesea. British dominion over the Atlantic had long frustrated Vask fishing families, members of which still predominated in the aristocracy. It was ships of this guild that in 1471 sighted Brasil and brought news of the Novomund to Europe.

The decades following the Novomundine Landfall brought new prosperity to Vascony, and in particular to Portingale. At the same time, the polity suffered militarily: the Kingdom of Burgundy retook Provence in 1494 (sparking the 1497‚Äì99 War of Provincial Independence), and a decade later the remainder of Vascony's Middlesea coast (primarily the region of Tolose) was taken by Barcelon.

Being a maritime power, Vascony had explored parts of the African coast (as far south as Sant Adrian [Bioko]) during the fifteenth century. However, for much the same reason Vascony lost over a fifth of its population to the Second Great Dying in the years after the plague's arrival in Europe in 1519. Despite this, thanks to Novomundine profits in furs and other goods, the polity actually saw in increase in population from 1500 to 1550 (excluding Portingale) due to immigration.

The Vascon Ascendancy is generally considered to have ended with the War of the Two Queens near the end of the sixteenth century, which also saw the formal end of feudalism within the polity.

The parts of Mendeva first explored by (and whose trade was monopolised by) Vascony‚Äîto wit, the island of Brasil and the lands westward thereof‚Äîwas named Ambrosia in honour of Ambrose III.

During the Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century, Vascony fought primarily with Morrack to the south. The polity was forced to cede Portingale, its economic heart, to Andalus (then controlled by Morrack) after a series of military defeats.

In an example of early international cooperation, a canal system connecting Bordew to Audaz in Barcelon was completed in 1679.

Vascony was at the vanguard of the Modest Arrangement, a social movement in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries characterised by more stringent enforcement of morality codes, especially with respect to familial powers. Famously, author Ant√≤in Herrel (latterly Tony Smith) was forced to flee Vascony to Kent after attempting to publish his blasphemous work 'Earthly Scene' (Vista Terrena).

At first, Vascony managed not to become embroiled in the Second German War of the 1820s. Once its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened, however, then-king Hernand IV had no choice but to declare against the Northern Concert and in favour of the Convoy.

Into the twentieth century, Vascony maintained strong ties with its neighbour Provence by virtue of a shared enmity towards the Drengot Collusion to their north.

People
Monarchs
Ranolf VI, (crowned 1170), during whose reign Leon became a vassal of the kingdom
Ambrose III "The Ascendant" (17 March 1435‚Äì28 February 1518), who presided over the Vascon Ascendancy
Hernand IV (fl. 1820), who reigned during the height of the Modest Arrangement and the Second German War
Other nobles
Princess Alexandra of Vascony (b. ca. 1430), first European royal to set foot in the Nvomund
Prince Mark of Tolose (d. 1453), who predeceased his son Ambrose III
Princess Natalia of Vascony (b. ca. 1440), younger sister of Ambrose III
Prince Victor of Astorga (b. ca. 1440), younger brother of Ambrose III
Writers
David BensaiÃÅd (fl. 1751), author of the influential political treatise "The Three Cities"
Ant√≤in Herrel (fl. 1820), who was forced to flee to Kent after his work was banned in Vascony
Gon√ßala Jaub√®t (fl. 1902), who later in life moved to Valencia
Galar√ßa fi Molhom√© (fl. 2014), lovetale writer turned popular historian
Garc√≠a Ramires (fl. 1920), who translated Bellamy's "A Door, Once Opened" into Leon

##

57.5: Damvath

Damvath is the capital city of Borland, located on the River Dam some leagues inland from the coast.

Toponymy
The name Damvath derives from the Norrish name Damva√∞ "ford on the Dam". The river's name is pre-Roman and is likely cognate with the Tames in Albion and the Tanga in Portingale.

History
Damvath was established first as the capital of Dane-ruled Borland after the exile of King Jothey to Sothbar in the last years of the ninth century.

In the late fifteenth century, Damvath became the most-trafficked port city in Europe outside the Middlesea. This was no doubt helped by the city's stability in comparison to the cities of contemporary Albion, embroiled in the bloody Wars of Fealty.

14th Sep: Arrived at Brest. Storm inland delayed delivery of beef so departed several hours behind schedule.
18th Sep: Did not make port at Bournemouth. Cannonfire and conflagration even in the harbour. Will resupply instead at Honeflower.
‚Äî logbook 1492 for the myton ‚ÄúSan Tom√©‚Äù, en route from Cal√®s to Damvath.

The first Borlish victim of the Second Great Dying was in the docks of Damvath in 1519.

During the Long Peace of the late eighteenth century, the location of the Borlish parliament was fixed following the completion of the Jacobin Estates on the (then) outskirts of Damvath. In these years the city saw increased immigration from nearby polities such as Denmark and Willemy, looking for greater economic prosperity, as well as the establishment of an early detective force with the so-called 'tuppence scouts'.

In the modern era Damvath is renowned for extensive Christide celebrations.

58.4: zi "yeah"

zi /zi/ [zi]
- yeah, sure, yep, mhm, informal form of oc or ja "yes" used to denote agreement or assent;
- innit, y'know, colloquial form of ou used as a sentence-final particle in polar questions

[used only in informal speech and only recently in writing]

Etymology: Contraction of standard es i […™Ààzi] "is it", part of a series of similar contractions of the copula with pronouns, like sco "am I", sti "are you (sg)" which despite their verb-subject inversion are also nonstandardly used in declarative clauses (sti'n connart "You're a dick").

Its use in polar questions seems to date back to the sixteenth century (though usually written out as es i until the twentieth), while using it to mean "yes" is much more recent and was initially restricted to correcting negative questions. In this it resembles French si "yes, in response to a negative question", and indeed on the basis of phonetic similarity direct French influence has often been supposed.

Zi, sco'n bal n'oc ort.
/zi | x…în bal n…îk …îrt/
[zi | x…în baw n äÀàg…îÀêt]
yeah | i'm=indef ball in-s.px rise
Yeah, things're looking up.

58.5: Fiellas Dimenja

Fiellas Dimenja (English: Sunday Sheets) is a weekly newspaper almost continuously published from Marsella since its incorporation in 1871. The paper's notional remit comprises entertainment and recreational news, although in practise it has regularly covered weightier subjects.

History
Fiellas Dimenja was established in 1871 by Corsican businessman Toscobardo Matutte in concert with the Guiraud Colloquium.

Fiellas was an early adopter of machinal broadcast direct to homes via their tachygraphs, first offering digests in this form in the 1920s.

The paper developed a significant international readership through the twentieth century‚Äîby 1934 it was republished synchronously in Vascony and New Provence, and was even read in Collusion territory, despite the rising tensions of the post-Millstone Europe.

By 1950 it held the record for the longest uninterrupted weekly mass-market publication, without interruption by the 1937 Veldsvindung nor the Millstone War (although it briefly moved its printers to Man√≤sca during the worst of the bombings).

Fiellas' renown grew further after its significant role in the Furore of 1961, which (not undeservedly) earned the paper a reputation for investigative journalism alongside its ostensible purpose of delivering entertainment and recreational news.

Correspondents
Jacint Brunat (fl. 1950), reviewer of books and films
For the best experience I recommend you spend the afternoon drinking Empordan and singing as many songs of glory as you can think of. [The Ascendant (1934)]

This will be no classic: come only for the views (of beautiful forests, and a bevy of handsome actors, I concede) and two hours of mindless tragedy. [Zachariah (1950)]

59.5: 19th-century Espionage and the Invention of Software

    collected notes on two entirely unrelated works of literature.

Men of Jet and Diamond (originally published in Welsh under the title Dynnon o Vuhu√∞ a Diamont) is an 1890 trevold written by Hasinic Welshman Cadogan Torrior, shortly after he graduated from the University of Nadacow [capital of Hasiny, located not far from the coast on the banks of the *Mississippi] as a scholar of diplomacy.

Published by Blackburn Editions, the work is a rather late member of the first wave of masquira romance. The book was modestly popular in eastern Mendeva [North America], but became much more widely known after being adapted by a New Provence guild into the wildly-succesful 1951 film 'Jet White' (New Provincial Blanc Sabaje).

---

The Parable of the Mute Bookbinder (originally published in Burgund as Fable dew Reillour Mut) is a philosophical treatise written in 1743 by noblewoman Rachel Desselut in response to Vallakis on the problem of the bounded self-duplication of living things (in brief: how can a finite being contain within itself the ability to create a full copy of itself?).

Desselut's work lays out the separation of Form and of Method, and how they allow for the unsculpted duplication of a bounded being. She argues that in order for living matter to duplicate, the Form must contain within itself an explicit Method describing in some readable language its construction. Thus she inferred the existence of a vital language (in her own writing she called it the Divine Word; we now know it as heredian acid [DNA]) present in the cases of the body.

60.1: Malice Aforethought

##

60.5: Santrafew

Santrafew (Merch /s…ônÀàt…πa.f…ôw/; Provincial Sant Rafeu /sanÀàtra.vaw/) is the capital of New Provence. Located on the Mendevan coast on the banks of the Becouin River, it is one of the oldest European settlements on the continent's mainland.

History

Santrafew was founded in 1564 as a trading post for Provincial merchant guilds. It was named for the archangel Raphael in his guise as a patron of travellers. In its first century, pocket communities of people appeared in Santrafew, as the fleets of Provence employed mariners from across the Middlesea.

Early imports into Santrafew included horse stocks and metal tools, traded in exchange for furs and new foodstuffs.

In 1590 Derek Nassow, the heir apparent to the Duchy d'Avosche in Willemy, fled overseas to Santrafew for unclear reasons. This led to an incendiary scandal that rocked courts across Western Europe and especially that of King David of France.

The 1621 Becouin Treaty outlined the legal relationship of Santrafew to the Mendevan peoples of the region for some generations. Following the British Sack of Santrafew, in which the Mastois√®t in particular were alleged to have collaborated, the treaty was broken by Mayor Alexandre fi Marco's embargo and ten-league exile of the Mastois√®t in particular (and other Mendevan groups in practice).

Immigration into Santrafew spiked dramatically in the 1820s, in the aftermath of the Second German War. The newcomers arrived in greatest numbers from Barcelon and Lombardy, spurred on by a combination of wartime displacement and presumably some familiarity with Provincial language.

Santrafew had means sufficiently-fast commnication across the Atlantic Ocean by the 1930s that papers such as Fiellas Dimenja could be published simultaneously there and in Vascony.

People

Alexandre fi Marco (fl. late c17), mayor responsible for the exile of Mendevan peoples from the vicinity of Santrafew
Sauveir d'Orz (fl. 1620‚Äì26), town mayor remembered only for his impotence
Carol Zancoud (d. 1630), reverend who accrued paramount (but unofficial) political power over the town
Nad√Ýlla fi Carol (fl. 1590), daughter of Carol Zancoud whose extensive diary has survived to the modern day and is a vital window into early life in the settlement

References

The Making of Mendeva, a notoriously-inaccurate account written by In mac Harlach in 1888
Physician, Heal Thyself: The First Century of Santrafew (originally in Borlish as Medic, Cur Tey : Y Preu Siecr a Santrafeu), focusing mostly on diary extracts from various settlers

Portan √∞a veleu nos l'anna√∞ aval de Wolfstan II de Nor√∞umbr comprendr, nos deyeu sgart mettr a dou siecr amont, ag bon preu gazou viking sur y rimmot d'Albion e d'Ir√∞on.

61.4: avant sy rau "shamelessly"

avant sy rau /aÀàvant si ro/ […êÀàvan si Àà Äo]
- shamelessly, brazenly, flagrantly, with no regard for someone's comfort or propriety;
- in full view, openly, in public, exposed, easily visible to someone (especially of something you would expect to be concealed)

Etymology: from early Middle Boral au sy rau […ôu si r…ôu] "brazenly, in a way that provokes ire", literally "in front of the disdain (of someone)". The preposition au "in front of", from Latin apud "by, near, unto", had obsolesced and been supplanted by synonymous avant (from ab ante "from before") by the nineteenth century.

The noun rau "disdain, scorn, (esp. societal) disapproval" is also obsolete outside this idiom, and is furthermore of unclear etymology. If of Latin origin all of reus "guilty", rabidus "furious" and an unattested *r≈çdus "slander" (from r≈çd≈ç "I slander") have been suggested; if German then perhaps some cognate of Merch rough "unkind, rude". Attempts to connect the word to Welsh rhew "frost, disdain" are semantically tempting but otherwise unsubstantiable.

Synchronically the idiom is usually misinterpreted as containing the unrelated word rau "uncooked vegetables, salad" (from Kentish raw "raw"), as though avant my rau meant "in front of my salad".

    L'er mell'yam sleghant avant my rau!
    /l…õr m…õÀàljam sliÀàjant aÀàvant mi ro/
    [l…õÀê m…™Ààljam zl…™Ààjant …êÀàvan mi Àà Äo]
    3s-cop.ipf 1s.gen=uncle slander-p.prs in.front.of 1s.gen ‚ÄÝdisdain
    He was shamelessly slandering my uncle!

61.5: Parachthony

Parachthon art refers to works depicting a world different from the real world, encompassing everything from merely divergent histories to entirely invented settings. The word parachthon was coined in the mid-nineteenth century and derives from the Ancient Greek prefix œÄŒ±œÅŒ±- 'beside, contrary to'" (cognate with Latin prae- and English fore-) attached to the root œáŒ∏œéŒΩ 'ground, soil, earth, world' (cognate with Latin humus 'soil'.)

History

A seminal work of modern parachthon literature is Elsebeth Sneider's 1795 trevold 'Through a Black Mirror' (originally written in Saxon Dutch as Durgh'nen Shorzen Shouer), arguably the first true voidtale work. Its publication sparked the so-called 'voidtale craze' of grand voyages through the void visiting colourful, bustling xenic worlds‚Äîin this respect this tradition recalls the popular imaginings of the Earth-bound geoscopic voyages of the Intertwining Century nearly three hundred years earlier.

The voidtale craze persisted until the 1820s, at which point its optimistic and exploratory character dropped rather rapidly out of fashion faced with the horrors of the Second German War. By then the fashion had spread as far afield as Madray and China, with the renowned landscapist Narayanan commissioned to paint vistas of the seven other planets then known.

The mid-nineteenth century was somewhat of a nazir for parachthon art. Romance moved on to masquira and herdtale's more mundane settings, while historical revivals populated the world's stages. With the maturation of genres in the twentieth century we begin to see purposeful melding of parachthon elements in stories of other sorts, like lovetales and √¶nigmata; this and the incorporation of related movements like Futurism brought parachthony back into popular art for good.

The progress of technology was often presaged by parachthon works, and so many items of vocabulary for modern contrivances have their origin there. As examples, the 'stafemesh' (now usually just the 'mesh') is lifted from the pages of Ashford Pasquier's 1968 trevold Starsail, and the use of 'slate' for a flat surface displaying information can be traced all the way back to Hadasse Docquerl's 1872 work 'Dealsome Onomatory of the New London' (originally published in Borlish as Un Onomatoir Partial dy Novel Londr).

References

New Skies, Old Stories by Simon d'Estiala, published by Prindor Marsill√®s, 1980

Y tatover despleyar ne trenc sur y carn e soulor e√ß voncirar con bou√∞r calt. E√ß covauclir e cogr un hour e demy sur foy preu√ß.

62.5: The Mysarnos Codex

The Mysarnos Codex is an illustrated manuscript written entirely in an unfamiliar script which eluded decipherment for centuries.

Context
The Codex was composed in the sixteenth century and first stored by somebody (presumably a monk) at the Library of Saint George the Martyr, one of the largest buildings in the sprawling church complex located north of the city of Mysarnos.

Mysarnos was at the time the capital of Mystra, a mordether vassal of the Roman Empire. Located at the western end of the Athenian Way, Mysarnos flourished as a hub for the Revitalist movement during the sixteenth century, receiving half of all Greece's imports and swelling to a population of nearly thirty thousand.

Authorship
The author of the Mysarnos Codex has never been conclusively identified, although several candidates have been nominated. Despite extant and complete membership records for the area's monasteries, no mention is made of the production of the Codex in contemporary chronicles.

Content
The Codex is thought to have originally comprised over two hundred vellum illustrated folios (several are lost but gaps can be deduced from various tallies). The illustrations include both mundane scenes such as poppy fields as well as fantastical vistas like armoured men on dragonback.

The text consists of a set of approximately 180 different glyphs (there is disagreement on whether some pairs of glyphs are mere variants or indeed separate), arranged into words and paragraphs.

Decipherment
For centuries scholars presumed the Mysarnos glyphs to have been entirely invented by their author. The Good Game period of the late nineteenth century saw this narrative collapse, with artefacts first from old Pylos (by then a neighbourhood of Mysarnos) and then from Crete surfacing bearing the same glyphs.

It became clear that the author of the Mysarnos Codex had had access to a store of writing in this script, and perhaps had even known how to read it (many words within the Codex matched the truly ancient examples). The revelation that the Mysarnos glyphs were being used to write a form of Greek only came in 1939, with the unearthing of the lost First Folio at the venerable Library of Sobjan. This text began with a fictionalised biography of the author written both in contemporary Greek and in the codex's script, which allowed for direct comparison and thence, with work, a full decipherment.

References
The Mysarnos Problem: the Enigma of an Inconvenient Codex, a popular narrative history written by classicist Theodora Riph√¶na in 1943

63.4: cosogr "jointly"

cosogr /koÀàz…îjrÃ©/ [k äÀàz…îj…ê]
adv.
- jointly, collectively, en masse, together, considered as a single set of things;
- in unison, as one, moving or acting all in the same way and at the same time;
- (of people) together, in a romantic relationship with one another
prep.
- together with, alongside, at the same time as or in the same set of things as;
- (of people) dating, with, in a romantic relationship with

Etymology: from Middle Boral adverb cosogr "as a pair, as a group"; prepositional use descends from the construction cosogr a X "in a set with X". This comes from Old Boral noun coso»ùre "the other of a pair, (in the plural) a matching pair of things" and homonymous adjective "corresponding, respective, concomitant".

The noun coso»ùre is a broadening of Borlish Latin c≈çsocer, c≈çsocrum "the other party to a contract", which is a local spelling and sense of Classical c≈çnsocer "the father of one's child's spouse"; the fathers of a marrying couple being prototypical counterparties to a contract.

Cain l'e√ß sonc a ven√ßar for cosogr.
/ken l…õts s…înk a v…õnÀàtsar f…îr koÀàz…îjrÃ©/
[ken l…õs s…înk a v…™nÀàts…ëÀê f…îÀê kuÀàz…îj…ê]
fall-3p def-p.px thing to sell-inf only as.a.set
These items are not to be sold separately.

##

63.5: Devon

Devon (Welsh Devneint, locally Dewna; Borlish Dorogn) refers geographically to the southwestern peninsula of the land of Albion, and politically to one of the most longstanding tallaths of Britain.

History

Devon is named for the Dumn≈çniƒ´ (the Latin name reflecting Classical Kelt Dubnonƒ´ or Dubnantƒ´, meaning "(people) of the deep valley"), a Kelt people who inhabited the region in Roman times.

Devon was conquered piecemeal by the Saxons, with the defeat at the |Battle of Gaulford in 656 putting the area under the sole authority of the Kingdom of Wessex (although the westernmost region is thought never to have fully submitted to its rule).

In the eighth century, King Vigo the Magnificent of the Britons spearheaded a reconquest of the peninsula with the support of David I of Wynneth, obtained via his marriage to King David's daughter Guinnumar. Over two decades of intermittent warfare were ended with the victory at Petherton, and Vigo was crowned the first king of Greater Devon (rex Magn√¶ Dumnoni√¶), uniting Devon with the region later known as Kernow in Gaul.

The Brethin religious order was founded in eighth-century Tremonnow, at that time in Devon (now in Glousing).

Devon remained in political union with Kernow through the Kernovan Conjuring of 1028 and the concomitant formation of the First Drengot Empire, which claimed control over much of Albion and most of Gaul. Greater Devon became an ambitious and independent-minded vassal of the Empire, with the House of Kernow always closely aligned with the imperial Drengot family.

The collapse of the First Drengot Empire caused political upheaval in Greater Devon. The Realignment of 1225 saw Devon become de facto independent from Kernow, now ruled by a cadet branch of the royal family.

However, Devon's independence would not last long; Ivan of Cartaw conquered Devon as part of the various Welsh territorial gains of the thirteenth century. Soon thereafter David IV of Wales declared the Tallath of Devon (and awarded rule thereof to Ivan's son Marduth).

People

Monarchs of Greater Devon

Vigo the Magnificent (Vigo Rag√≥rwyh, d. 761), who led the reconquest of Devon from Kernow
Cadern the Doubter (crowned 761), son of Vigo the Magnificent
Alan III (crowned 883), who lost territory in Kernow to the Franks
Vigo IV (fl. 940), last monarch to be crowned in the original cathedral of Nauned
Vigo VII (fl. 1120), who attempted without success to conquer the southeastern principalities of Wales
Alan VI (fl. 1225), who orchestrated the Realignment of Devon

Nobility

Golleck/Gorvan of Kernow (fl. c9), granted dominion over Devon by his uncle Alan III
Iseld of Devon (fl. 750), daughter of Vigo the Magnificent and first patron of the Brethin
Margaret of Iscombe (Marerit o Gar Ix, fl. 1435), mother of Vascony's Ambrose the Ascendant

Others

Arteth V√Ωn (fl. c19), friend of Vascon author Ant√≤in Herrel (Tony Smith) after the latter's exile to Devon
Damian the Wise (fl. c12), the first Pope to be elected from among the Brethin religious order

64.1: Electromagnetic Radiation

Ustol es ig nos dau grad a ≈ìculux par sy mesur, dec nos lour eu envou con de tan fa√ßon divers.
We customarily grade ≈ìculux by their span, since we interact with them in many different ways.

Lumner ant y mesur cortessem es scadriscent, ant nis suffignt por atom toccar e cossy lou fornir de defait a plenta√∞; dighau l'atom dien defaint.
Light with the shortest spans is scattering, having enough energy to affect atoms and give them a deficit of plenty; we say the atom becomes deficient.

To√∞ ≈ìculux breyessem e penetrant y dou son scadriscent, e lonc ustol se po√∞ certan lumner surblavesc jagerrem admettr.

Y mesur y longessem a lumner po√∞ent in experientia un fondamen atomic scadrir es tor 400 magr pol√ß, ja  no stant lonc com lumner visibr calscon.
The longest span of light which can in theory scatter an atomic base is around 400 scant-inches, which is not yet as long as any visible light.

High exposure to light of such brief span is dangerous, and fortunately air blocks the majority of such luxes that the Sun emits (although not all, as a sunburn will attest!).

##

64.5: First Drengot Empire

The First Drengot Empire, or occasionally the Constant Empire, was the westernmost of the four empires comprising the Second Tetrarchy (also the Medieval Imperia). At its greatest extent it claimed control over much of Albion and most of Gaul. Its major vassals were the kingdoms of Northumber, Markland, Kent, Greater Devon, France and Burgundy, along with several lower-ranking demesnes.

The Empire is named for its ruling family House Drengot, formerly rulers of Normandy alone. The lesser-used name Constant Empire has been employed sporadically by historians or propagandists attempting to claim the prestige of the Classical-era District of Constantius, which occupied roughly the same geographical extent.

The structure of the Empire was quite decentralised; see for example the standing army kept by vassal Tolose for its perennial skirmishes with neighbouring Barcelon. The emperor usually held court in Paris (moved during the eleventh century from Roun)

History

The beginnings of the First Drengot Empire lie with the Kernovan Conjuring, through which the House of Drengot in Normandy invaded and conquered Kent with the support of Greater Devon's House of Kernow. As recompense for their support, the Drengots helped install the House of Kernow on the Devoner throne. This invasion was triggered by the death of the elderly patriarch Rodulf of Kent, who had ruled since childhood for nearly eight decades.

The Empire was most stable in the decades around the turning of the twelfth century, having reached its greatest territorial extent. However, by 1120 much of the treasury had been spent on defending the long eastern border with Willemy. In particular, the construction of a new Great Castle at V√©son√ßon had been completed the year before, the last point in a defensive line spanning from Flanders to the Alps.

In 1170 the Kingdom of Burgundy ceased to be a vassal of the First Drengot Empire, due to the political turmoil of the Emergency at Dijon.

The First Drengot Empire formally ceased to exist in 1191 upon the death of Daniel IV; his demesne was divided between his immediate family and his allies.

Emperors

Nathaniel II (fl. 1067), who quashed a popular uprising in Flanders
Roger II (fl. 1121), under whose aegis Devon attempted to invade Wales without success
Daniel IV (d. 1191), last ruler of a united Drengot Empire

##

65.5: Second Roman Empire

The Second Roman Empire, seldom or historically referred to as the Empire of the Hellenes, the Empire in Byzantium, Malchassian Rome (specifically denoting the centuries of rule by the Malchassian dynasty) or the Eastern Roman Empire (which extends somewhat further back in time), was the political and cultural continuation of the (Classical) Roman Empire following the reconquest of the city of Rome from the Eastern Goths by Emperor Justinian I. The Empire persisted in some form for over a millennium, with its final collapse occurring in the seventeenth century.

History
Many Christian heresies had their origins in the Second Roman Empire, especially in the first millennium. These include all the so-called 'Grecian heresies', as well as Marmeronism. When individuals in the Empire who espoused such viewpoints acquired significant influence, the Empire had a propensity for exiling them (contrast the habit of bodily mutilation practised on political, as opposed to religious, dissidents) to far-flung monastic communities.

During the Second Tetrarchy, the Empire reached a territorial maximum (with the capture of Derban in 1026), controlling lands from Corsica in the west to the Hazaran Sea in the east. However, by this time the outlying regions enjoyed significant autonomy, especially where the independent power of the Roman Papacy loomed larger than the might of Constantinople.

Following the fall of the northern Lombards in Italy, the ruling family fled to the Empire and managed to obtain appreciable clout there; some members were appointed to administer the Empire's western themes, and the written accounts of others are history's strongest sources for certain events during the turmoil of the German Secession in the thirteenth century.

Having fallen into somewhat of a state of disrepair, the fourteenth century saw extensive reconstruction of the Hagia Sophia along with expansion of the complex, headed by Burgund architect Nicol√Ý s'Avignon. (It is for this reason that s'Avignon is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic events that would take place within the Hagia Sophia three hundred years later.)

##

66.1: The "Metric" System

from Handbook of Natural Theory, by Jason Ridley and others, published 1967 by the Lincun Collegiate Editors.

Cas y mesur 'degm' noncupa√∞ er tandem l'axiom de se sarvent d'un los soul par casc quanta√∞ compromis, e retenu√∞ ern pie e lau y dou ne beno√ß com mesur a tagl sengr.
The so-called 'dime' measures eventually compromised the principle of having a single base for each quantity, and retained both the foot and the league as primary measures of length.

Y pie er normalisa√∞ cos quidam na√∞usc stant y mojol entr dou mesur un picq pog inequal: y "pie romannesc" e√∞ y "paum accenser" cathayan.
The foot was standardised somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly discrepant "Roman foot" and the Cathayan "surveyor's span" [‚âà3dm].

Y lau, stant jusc oy irruniscablessem me√∞es tras Europ ag pie, ern fis a pla√ß d'un mur√∞on a pie;
The league, which had been even less consistent across Europe than the foot, was fixed at ten thousand feet [‚âà3km];

y mesur cas ustol y "migl" vole cavir inclus parmy y mesur degm com cinq mil pie, ben un demy a lau.
the traditional measure of the "mile" would be folded into the dime measures as five thousand feet, or half a league.

L'oc contingent es autra√∞ alcant soul dagormay, candon y pie (e cossy scient y lau) se cou un redefinition ne 1946 por ig y velo√ßta√∞ normal ≈ì tey cos exact deg gran lau par second.
This state of affairs has changed only once since, with the redefinition of the foot (and therefore the league) in 1946 setting the normal speed [the speed of light] ≈ì to precisely ten great leagues per second.

67.3: stovir "be necessary"

- (archaic or elegant) be necessary, be needed, be obligatory, to be logically or legally required;
- lack, be missing, to (potentially) suffer from not having;

67.4: jougl "knock-out blow"

jougl / íujl/ [Àà ùu.j ä~Àà ùw…™w]

[i p Ä…õs …õj d…êÀàv…ëÀên v äÀàle n äÀàsi Àà ùw…™w]
def price at.def hotel will-imp 1s.gen KO
The hotel's price would be the clincher.

##

67.5: Romantic Wars

The Romantic Wars were a loosely-circumscribed collection of wars, uprisings and other conflicts across Europe which took place through the middle of the seventeenth century. As a whole they are sometimes considered the last truly localised conflicts, since by the next century wars of similar scales would begin to involve the Novomund, and would feature alliances between the powers of Europe, Africa and Asia alike. The years 1640 to 1670 cover the 'central' conflicts of the Romantic Wars, although some more peripheral fights may spill earlier or later.

The Romantic period (to wit, the decades during with the Romantic Wars took place) were characterised by great corrogation projects. For example, the Bordew-Audaz canal was completed in 1679, connecting the Atlantic and the Middlesea without having to pass through Morrack-controlled seas.

Perennial adversaries Vascony and Morrack continued their protracted fight through the seventeenth century. Having been on a marked decline for a century, Vascony was forced to cede its economic heart in Portingale to Andalus, which at that time was controlled by Morrack.

Several polities and vassal states did not survive the Romantic Wars; for example, the Principality of Mystra in Greece, which had existed as a vassal of the Second Roman Empire since the eleventh century, was formally dissolved in the aftermath of the Romantic Wars.

Willemy was the focal point of the Blue Trans-Willem War (not to be confused with the White Trans-Willem War), which indirectly led to the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy and the creation of the Duchy of Far Suebia in Bavarn.

Borland's involvement in the Romantic Wars was restricted to a failed invasion (culminating in the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632), along with unstable arrangements with neighbouring polities regarding military aid in their conquests.

Much has been made of the involvement of pirates (to a greater or lesser extend endorsed by political powers) in the Romantic Wars, although recent historiography tends to downplay how much impact these factions really had.

The Roman conflict in Armenia of the 1640s is contemporaneous with the Romantic Wars, but its inclusion within them is a matter of scholarly dispute. Opponents point out that this fight was merely the latest in a series of essentially-identical conflicts between the Second Roman Empire and Arab (and later Turkish) peoples over control of the Hazaran Sea.

##

Cay confluyen√ß dy ci pouvr germannesc ny sou√∞ e nell'ovest con y pouvr romannesc nell'aust e ny nor√∞ se produgnt for lent, interim, tras y plusour siecr sevent.

Tandem, ny siecr novem joun y rey roman Stiglan dau sell'heir Marc ne mari√∞a√ß ag dottr soul a Alfey d'Anglont, cossy lorry reyaum unfiant e Sothbar sfor√ßant a vassala√ß eunovir posc y Battagl a Fendal n'atorn 810 N.

##

68.5: The German Secesssion

The German Secession was a schism in Christendom which took place in the first half of the thirteenth century. Often considered on of the most influential religious developments of the second millennium, it led to the permanent formalisation of the Ausburg See as independent from Rome with its separate papal line beginning with Paul I/XI.

The Secession generally refers to the period from Paul I/XI's Denunciations and declaration of the sedes sanctissima in Augsburg in 1225 to the capitulation of Rome in 1251, although religious strife due to the schism would wrack the polities of Germany and surrounding regions for centuries to come.

The 1210 declaration of a renewed crusade for Jerusalem by Pope Just, and the military catastrophes that occurred in the course of this push, were a major source of discontentment in Rome. Further, Europe was at the time in the throes of the First Great Dying, a plague that would be surpassed in severity only by the Second Great Dying of the early sixteenth century. (These events are related, in that the plague is thought to have arrived in Europe carried by returning crusaders).

The decades after the Pauline Denuncations saw each individual duchy (and in some cases each county!) declare either for Rome or for Augsburg, in an unstable patchwork of allegiances that would lead to the collapse of the German Empire in spectacular fashion.

The ongoing Baptism of the North (the project to fold the northeasternmost part of Europe into Christendom) was inflamed by the Secession. Along with a new, more totalist Roman Pope in Hermeneus II, it was feared that Augsburg might spread heresy faster than Rome could reach the North.

The events of the Secession have been a perennial subject of dramatisation, most famously (and perhaps most innacurately) in 1961 historical epic 'With the North Star to Guide Us'.

References
'A Mapbook Compendium of Europe, from the Division of Rome until the German Secession (450 - 1250 N)', published by the Tommarth Open School of History in 1997
'A Baptism of Fire: Christianity and the Graven' by Agata Naziunas, published 1988 by Leid√©iai Givat√©s
'Comparing the German Secession to the Great Fracture; how the Final Antipope emerged from a popular reaction to the Failed Crusade, and how to understand his successors' reactions to Rome in the sixteenth century', scholastic writing in the 1901 publication of theological journal 'Proclamatio Divior'

69.5: Avosche

Avosche (Paris French /aÀàv…îÀê É/, Willem Dutch /Ààa.vusx/) is a political subregion of Willemy centred on the city of Axton (Grand-Aix /g ÄaÃÉÀàd…õiks/, Aughen /Àà…î.…£nÃ©/), with a strong regional identity. At various points a county, a duchy and a march, it has seen both continual territorial instability (situated between French and Dutch powers) and major urban development into the modern period.

Etymology
The region Avosche is named for its first ruling house, which in turn takes its title from its founder Cuonrat Aihuosc (seen in Latin as Corradus Aevoscus). The name is of obscure origin; the first element presumably reflects Classical German ehu- "horse", while the second may be from suffix -iscas "-ish" (though the vowel is problematic).

Compare the English surname Davush (Merch /daÀê É/, Kentish /ÀàdaÀê.v…™ É/), which derives from an illegitimate branch of House Avosche.

History
The County of Avosche emerges shortly after the split of the Kingdom of the Franks and the concomitant creation of Willemy. The founding house of Avosche was soon after superseded by marriage by the neighbouring House Nassow (which originated in Francony).

The county was elevated to a duchy in the early sixteenth century, with Roger d'Avosche becoming the first duke. At this time the region was ruled by France. Roger's daughter, Adaill√© Nassow, succeeded him and went on to rule for many decades until her death in 1608.

During her reign Adaill√© accrued significant influence over her liege David of France. This was punctured by the scandal of her grandson and heir apparent D√©ricque fleeing to New Provence in 1589.

After a century of stability as a vassal of France, the seventeenth century was particularly eventful for Avosche; it changed hands as the result of various wars and peacetime treaties on five separate occasions. Especially bloody was the Blue Trans-Willem War, which also dissolved the Kingdom of Burgundy.

References
L√°t os Danse: De Hartoin ‚Äòn Avosche (Let us Dance: The Duchess d'Avosche), biography published in 1919

70.5: Monks and Metropoleis

The Jacobin Fraternity (Borlish Fraternta√∞ Jagovin), historically also known as the Order of Saint James, is a religious organisation based in the city of Axbane on the south coast of Borland.

The Fraternity were founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges. In the eleventh century they were implicated in a local popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffry II and forced to flee over the sea to Borland. There they built the Peninsular Monastery which still overlooks Axbane.

Funds from the Fraternity contributed to the Jacobin Estates on the outskirts of Damvath, which on their completion in the late eighteenth century became the fixed location of Borland's parliament.

Today the organisation is known mostly for the guild they own, Jaibos Breweries, as well as for owning significant lands in the city (including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses there).

---

The Riverine Cities were an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danaw River and its tributaries linked by treaty and trade in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. Each state consisted primarily of a single urban area and the surrounding rural hinterland, though the enforcement of territorial borders was often rather nominal.

The political structure of the Riverine Cities grew out of the ashes of the Kingdom of the Danaw, which had collapsed in spectacular fashion after the Second German War of the 1820s.

The Cities were a hotbed for underground or subversive organisations, including the Deviance movement, and were host to several semi-public tovarick communities. This environment was likely fostered by the presence of significant religious minorities and the strong ties between the Church and the local urban authorities.

Members of the Riverine Cities included Belgrade, Boxa, Gollamo and Pojon.

##

71.1: Atomic Theory

excerpt in translation from Lux √Üterna: The First Century of Threshold Force, written in 2002 by Cawen ba Dirxen at the Varon√∏w Higher Edifice.

Des posc y preu enombran√ß corporal d'an 1840 er venu√∞ all'idea oc orn dar a corporal cos na√∞eu lonc pondran√ß mena vars coglen√ß fatuous √∞a volir nis prestar a lorry natur alchemic
Since the first periodic tables in the 1840s it had been realised that naively ordering elements by atomic weight led to inelegant groupings when chemical properties were taken into account

(y celeberrem ig stove de ra√ßon starc janthin inquillir parmy sy confra√∞r detaxional com muria),
(most notably the strong motivation to place iodine with similarly-reacting substances like chlorine),

e√∞ alcun motivation theoretic por e√ß tal per de corporal trocar nell'orn nole soumission cavir pre dezein.
and no scientific basis for swapping such pairs of elements in the order would be proposed for decades.

A veg temporane, l'occorren√ß pariscent lon conjogntur yonoscon ig lonc mesuran√ß y corporal y legeressem teyen tout pondran√ß atomic monfia√∞ pres exact com l'ig a aquifex,
At the same time, the apparently-unrelated phenomenon wherein the lightest elements had all been measured to have atomic weights almost exact multiples of that of hydrogen,

cant oc se mantene bogr cas y ci lordessem (corporal tal cojan me√∞es com es cuivr!), flagita expostion cavir.
while this was not true at all of heavier ones (even such prosaic substances as copper!), demanded explanation.

##

71.5: Threshold force

Threshold force [nuclear fission power] is the force derived from splitting the nuxes [nuclei] of atoms. The name 'threshold' refers to the so-called 'threshold heft [mass]' of an unstable substance like killdark [uranium], above which one has a self-sustaining detaxion [1].

History
The story of threshold force begins in 1902, with the discovery of the atomic nux‚Äîthat is, the discovery that almost all of the heft of an atom is located near its centre. This took place at the N√østad Atellier (then privately owned and run by substantial theorist [2] Lohic Courvoisier de N√østad) in the Cappatian city of Warn√∏w [3].

The first threshold mills capable of supplying force for general use were in operation by the 1960s. These, and the unsettling martial applications of threshold force which had already been demonstrated on a small scale, brought the concept of harmful shortform luxes [gamma rays] into the popular consciousness. Many a blacklayer [horror movie] of the period and thereafter features unstoppable invisible rays inflicting horrors on the characters.

The corporal used in the vast majority of threshold mills, killdark, was first isolated in the early nineteenth century in the Irish shire of Killdara (whence, via Welsh kild√°rc 'from Killdara', the substance's name).

Portingale was a pioneer of threshold force, funding research into its effectivity in the 1950s in a bid to reduce their dependence on astraphor [electricity] exported by the Drengot Collusion. The polity's efforts were repaid in full; Portingale became a net exporter of astraphor in the 1970s.

References

Lux √Üterna: The First Century of Threshold Force, written in 2002 by Cawen ba Dirxen

---
[1] a generic term describing any process involving the change of one thing into another; synthesis, reaction.
[2] a chemist focused on the properties and structures of atoms and single molecules.
[3] from a *Dutch rendering of *Guaran√≠ + √∏w 'island', located where Vit√≥ria (ES), Brazil is.
