##

72.5: Markland

Markland is a polity of Albion, situated east of Wales, north of London and Kent, and south of Northumber and Sodrick.

Markland 2000.png
(The approximate extent of Markland proper in 2000 N.)

History
Markland originates with Saxon settlements in sub-Roman Britannia, which in the west formed (what would go on to be named) mearce land "borderland". Compare the related demonym Mierce "people of the border", whence the Latin name Mercia and native Merchman "man from Markland".

The petty kingdom of Anglia was annexed by Markland with the Treaty of Oxford in the late eighth century, at the dawn of the Viking Age. This established the persevering border between the mouths of the Severn and the Tames, and incited renewed influx of people into the petty kingdoms on Borland.

Markland weathered the Viking Age much more successfully than neighbouring Northumber, though much of its eastern coast was settled by Danes; the city of Lincon served as the de facto border of Danish rule for several decades. In part this was the inevitable result of tensions along the Welsh border; even during peacetime Theodric the Awefast maintained substantial levy forces.

The tenth-century Elved Marriage (which took place near the modern city of Leeds) ended hostilities between Markland and Dane forces in the east for almost a century. It is said to have been solemnised over a sandstone block later inlaid into the Redstone Chair, a low-backed wooden throne on which many subsequent monarchs were crowned.

Around the turn of the second millennium, Markland was conquering territory from the northern reaches of Wales (incidentally leading to Pope Agatho's canonisation of the Welsh St David). The perennial conflicts between Markland and Wales would come to be collectively referred to as the Mallor Wars.

The First Drengot Empire of the eleventh and twelfth centuries counted Markland as a major vassal, conquered from the south following the subjugation of Kent by Normandy and Greater Devon in 1029.

The Mallor Wars were reignited after a quiescent period by the martial-minded King Andrew II, with the Sack of Rexam in 1301 during which the original St Brigid's Abbey burned to the ground.

Markland was embroiled in the Wars of Fealty in the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries. Tensions over the location and possession of the Redstone Chair boiled over into a conflict that would last forty-one years. Markland conceded several battles to the young Kingdom of Britain in the early years of the wars, culminating in the 1483 Battle of Rexam which expanded the borders of Wales in the north and killed the last Duke of Chestre.

In the final years of the Wars of Fealty, a code known as Thieves' Script was employed by the forces of King Andrew III, apparently adapted from similar systems used by gangs of housebreakers in Nottingham. It is remembered today as a very early precursor of codes like steeplescript or modern one-zeffre encoding.

The poor economic conditions and population displacement in Markland following the Wars of Featly led it to suffer almost twice as many deaths per capita from the Second Great Dying compared to polities like Willemy.

Long contested between Markland and Kent, the city of London found itself independent for one final, lasting time following the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century.

The spellings of various towns and cities in Markland were rationalised during the Long Peace period of the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries (for example, Conster for former Colncester /Ààk…în.st…ô/).

The 2010 ≈ícumen Count, a regular census, recorded that Utter Safford was the secondmost populous city in Markland proper with 4.4 million inhabitants, after the capital of Tommarth.

People
Monarchs
Theodric the Awefast (fl. 800), repeller of several attempted vikings into Merch territory
Oswin the Becoming (fl. 1029), last ruler of an independent Markland before the First Drengot Empire
Andrew II (fl. 1301), who reignited the Mallor Wars with the Sack of Rexam
Andrew III (fl. 1500), ruler of Markland during the latter years of the Wars of Fealty
Authors
Ferris Bewly (fl. 1942), alchemist responsible for the quire 'Modern Developments in Benjamine Detaxion'
Laurent Burnows (fl. 2015), famous for children's series 'Back to Front' and later for the more mature series 'An Inconvenient Armageddon'
Anna and Ralph Highwater (fl. 1930), cowriters of the series of books 'Games for Sorties and Excourses'
Challow Huddresfell (fl. 1956), historian who wrote 'Daughters of the Sea: The Northwestern Isles'
Jason Ridley (fl. 1967), who with others wrote the quire 'A Handbook of Natural Theory'
Dr Harold Westcamp (fl. 1997), who wrote the series of textbooks 'Albic Medicine in the Workshop Decades'
Others
Sarzan Dawkinholm (fl. c19), long-serving and much-lambasted Outland Minister

##

73.5: Ambrosia

Ambrosia (Vascon/Vask Ambroxa) is a polity of north-eastern Mendeva, comprising several islands and much of the mainland up to the Five Seas. It borders New Provence to the south.

History

The story of Ambrosia begins with the Novomundine Landfall of 1471, when Vetomundine travellers encountered Mendeva for the first time since sporadic visits by the Norrish. A Vask fishing expedition (part of the New Navarre Enterprise) landed on the island of Brasil, which would go on to host the first European settlements in Mendeva.

The parts of Mendeva first explored by (and whose trade was monopolised by) Vascony‚Äîto wit, the island of Brasil and the lands westward thereof‚Äîwas named Ambrosia in honour of Ambrose III of Vascony.

Ambrosia achieved political and economic independence from Vascony in the early nineteenth century as part of the continent-wide phenomenon of the Mendevan Unmooring.

The first long-distance transmission of an image took place in Ambrosia in 1820, employing the so-called Steeplepost Tapestry (consisting of a wall of tiles painted black and white on each side). This device would soon go on to be the subject of the infamous Lies of 1824.

By the 1880s the most urbanised parts of Ambrosia were connected by wired tachygraph, allowing messages to be sent from home to home.

In 1950 the Japetos submarine Atlantic link conveyed its first messages. It was laid down between Gadire in Morrack and eastern Brasil.

Places

Aiga d'Angoixa, a major city on the Oxagre River and location of the original Steeplepost Tapestry

Illa de Vinna Salvatge, a built-up island in the Oxagre

Paratzon, a town in eastern Brasil

People

Fabian Most√Ýf (fl. c19), astronomer who worked at the observatory at Paratzon

Crist√≤f Unquenjica (fl. 1971), author of popular history text Going to Brasil

74.5: The Pistol Mesh

The pistol mesh (Borlish sar pistol), also referred to simply as the mesh, is the global system of long-distance communication which employs a single normalised method for transmitting messages from one endpoint augrimmer to another.

Etymology
The phrase "pistol mesh" (in the Roun French form mail pistre) was first used by writer Ashford Pasquier in his 1968 parachthon work Starsail, describing technology that could transmit written messages, sounds and moving images between people in different star systems.

The phrase literally means "network for messages": the word pistol reflects post-Classical Latin pistola "epistle, written decree", aph√¶retic form of synonymous epistola; the word mesh descends from Old English masc "net".

History
The pistol mesh grew out of several earlier technologies, all the way back to the first flag-lamp steeplepost lines in the eighteenth century allowing short texts to be sent between signal towers.

A century later sees the emergence of tachygraphs, devices allowing one to type text directly onto the barrick steeplepost from the comfort of one's home. However, it was only with the rise of general augrimmers in the twentieth century that we start to recognise the modern pistol mesh.

By the late twentieth century, the pistol mesh was sufficiently mature that whole enterprises could be conducted using it as a medium. For example, at this time the Tremonnow Open School (formerly the Brethin Mesh Institute) offered history courses across Albion entirely via mesh. Another impactiful development was the blossoming of 'distributed bookhouses', repositories for free-to-access information (much of it lifted from books out of withholding) to which anyone could contribute.

##

75.5: Burgundy

Burgundy (Burgund French Borgogne /b…îgÀàg…î…≤…õ/) is a polity in Gaul, situated south of France, west of Bavarn, north of Provence and east of Vascony. It has for most of its history been ruled from the city of Geneva.

History

During the Second Tetrarchy period of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, Burgundy was a major vassal of the First Drengot Empire. Following the Emergency at Dijon in 1170, which presaged the collapse of said empire, Burgundy became a de facto autonomous kingdom.

In the early fifteenth century with the Vascon Ascendancy, Burgundy lost its Middlesea coast to Vascony. Burgundy managed to wrest the territory of Provence back from Ascendant Vascony in 1494. This would however be a very temporary state of affairs: the region seceded with the 1497‚Äì99 War of Provincial Independence, ending in Burgundy's defeat at the Battle of Marg√®s.

No longer having any coastal territory, Burgundy weathered the Second Great Dying, which began in 1519, comparatively well; the principal vector of plague transmission was via naval trade. This relative stability may be related to the kingdom's (unusually late for the region) formal retention of the feudal system until the dawn of the seventeenth century.

The title of King of Burgundy was dissolved after severe losses to neighbouring Bavarn in the Blue Trans-Willem War of the late seventeenth century. The former capital Geneva became a subordinate city in the Duchy of Far Suebia.

A polity named Burgundy was formed once more (now without a monarch) in the nineteenth century, as part of the diplomatic process leading to the formation of the Drengot Collusion.

76.2: caum "piece"

caum /kom/ [kom]
- (obsolete) prayer, psalm, canticle, a religious text when set to music;
- hymn, any song of praise or worship;
- (more broadly) song, piece of music, an individual work of musical expression with or without lyrics;
- poem, artwork, piece, any artistic creation and especially one which is written;
- article, story, report, piece of nonfiction writing as might be published in the press;
- (mesh) post, any original work (prototypically writing) published to the mesh

Etymology: disputed. Semantically it should derive from Latin carmen "poem, incantation, prayer", but the phonological development is troublesome. Vocalisatio of /r > u/ is otherwise only attested in the presence of following /rÃ©/, as in Maucr "Wednesday" < *Marcre < Mercuriƒ´; this has led some scholars to postulate an Insular Latin variant *carmus, carmera, but this is unattested.

Reanalysis of the plural carmina as a feminine singular is, however, not out of the question. One might then suppose that dissimilation produced *carmira, but again this is unattested.

The word has seen significant semantic bleaching over time; the sense "poem" is first seen in the fifteen century, the sense "article" in the eighteenth, and the sense "(mesh)post" in the late twentieth.

##

76.5: The Workshop Period

The Workshop period, or the Global Workshop, refers to the middle decades of the nineteenth century. It is characterised by the rapid expansion and development of industry across the world. Usual historiography places the Workshop period between the Long Peace and the Good Game.

The Workshop period brought prosperity to parts of Europe which had been devastated by the Second German War of the 1820s, such as the Riverine Cities.

The impact of the Workshop period can be seen in artistic trends, with the shift away from voidtales and towards (on the one hand) realistic portrayals of the industrial working class and (on the other) optimistic speculation on future material advancements.

The guild VS (Vindrons Sairzaus), which sold the first widely-sold aphlox wine, would in this period expand its scope to other high-pressure and sealing components in manufacture, securing a very profitable niche.

The late Workshop period saw many barrick breakthroughs, concentrated in eastern Europe and Lower Mendeva.

The Drengot Collusion arose among the French polities of this period. The contemporaneous New Urban Mesh (including London and Axbane, among others) was subsumed by‚Äîor took control of‚Äîthe nascent Collusion.

Many items of vocabulary were innovated in the Workshop period, including recoil "manufacture", underwrit "logo", counterzoic "anti-microbial" and retainer "IUD".

References
Albic Medicine in the Workshop Decades, a series of volumes by Dr Harold Westcamp published by the Conster Health Edifice, funded by the Markland Ministry for Health.

##

77.5: Continents and Conjugality

The Novomund, also called the New World, refers to the lands to the west of the Atlantic Ocean. Inhabited for over a myriad years, the history of these lands comes together with that of the Vetomund following the Novomundine Landfall of 1471.

The Novomund is composed of the two great landmasses of Mendeva and Cappatia [North and South America], along with over a dozen major islands (such as Brasil [Newfoundland] and Cocain [Cuba]) and innumerable lesser ones.

Many crops now cultivated worldwide were first farmed in the Novomund, including shadome fruit, tartover, shockle and mikecorn [tomatoes, potatoes, chocolate and maize]. Products such as coshow [rubber] and kistmaw [tobacco] also derive from plants native to the Novomund.

---

The Household Renovation of the late nineteenth century was an attitudinal shift towards non-traditional familial structures, the institution of marriage, and the social role of children.

It was prefigured by the deemphasis of religion in government in those countries party to the 1850 Laic Declarations of Belgrade, as well as contraceptive medical innovations of the time like coshow bed-sleeves [latex condoms] and stauron retainers [IUDs].

The popularity of the masquira [~heist/vigilante] literary genre, with its hidden identities and lampooning of upper-class mores, is usually understood to have come hand in hand with the Household Renovation (not to mention the enduring popularity of masquira in tovarick [queer] circles).

Early embracers of the Household Renovation included Britain, the Riverine Cities and New Leudong [a polity in south-eastern Australia]; the ethos would spread (especially via Britain's cultural cachet in Gulf Mendeva) elsewhere over the subsequent decades.

##

##

78.5: The Good Game period

The Good Game period refers to the final decades of the nineteenth century, especially when construed as a time of rising global powers formed of alliances and meshes of trade and technology binding the world together. It conventionally follows the Global Workshop period of spreading industry and ends with the outbreak of the Millstone War.

The same Laic Declarations of Belgrade in 1850 which prefigured the social and moral shifts of the Household Renovation also set the stage for the political realignment of the Good Game decades, which new alliances between states that could not have countenanced such ties even a generation earlier.

This period sees the establishment of several alliance-federacies that would go on to define the geopolitics of the twentieth century, such as the Drengot Collusion in north-western Europe (whose expansion concluded in 1894 with the accession of Borland).

The perception of the Good Game period as a time of glamorous espionage and political machinations behind closed doors is not entirely deserved‚Äîcompare the popular perception (via books and film) of the 1887 Desarden affair involving a London ambassador and the king of Nackon Day with the tawdrier reality. Nevertheless, the period has cemented itself as a popular setting for spycraft and masquira stories.

The kevler romance arose as a distinct subgenre of masquira in this period, featuring elaborate plans and schemes usually aiming to steal some priceless item.

The 1880s saw a brief fad for 'Morrack fashion' in countries across Europe and the Novomund, in areas such as dress, architecture and food. There was, for example, a trend of incorporating exposed stonework draped in tapestry and curtains in contemporary buildings.

Scientific and technological advances of the Good Game period include discovering other galaxies, determining the normal speed, the first household mesh-linked tachygraphs, and stauron retainers.

##

79.2: jagotta√∞ "jagged"

- jagged, notched, serrated, rough, being unevenly cut or having a similar texture to something so cut;

79.4: cay seg a ve√∞errem "elephant in the room"

cay seg a ve√∞errem /ke sij a veÀà√∞…õ.r…õm/ [ke sij a v…™Àà√∞…õ. Ä…êm]
- (int.)  tactful acknowledgement of a lie or secret everyone knows about but nobody will call out;
- (n., usually hyphenated) elephant in the room, such a lie or secret

Etymology: idiom first attested in this form in the late eighteenth century but having many variants beforehand; literally "it takes six (months) for the eldest (child)". This refers to a couple's first child being born only six months after the wedding, a situation where it is clear to everyone what happened but to mention it explicitly would be impolite.

surely no person won't def harbinger of elephant.in.the.room bring.up-inf

##

79.5: Voidtale

Voidtale (Borlish trevogt /treÀàv…îjt/, portmanteau of trevold "novel" and vogt "outer space") is a subgenre of parachthon romance which focuses on voyages through outer space visiting alien worlds.

The genre was pioneered by Saxon author Elsebeth Sneider with her 1795 work Through a Black Mirror, which sparked a three-decade long fashion for the subject known as the "voidtale craze". This first period is characterised by a strongly pre-empirical understanding of conditions on other worlds: no particular attention is paid to the details of breathable air or bearable gravity, and each bustling planet is peopled by very human societies. Indeed, more than anything else these stories resemble the "geoscopic voyage" tales of the previous century, which depict sailors exploring unknown corners of the Earth and encountering the peoples there.

The voidtale craze is usually said to end with the Second German War in the 1820s, although later works like Narayanan's landscapes (which portray barren and inhospitable surfaces of the other planets orbiting the Sun) are generally counted as part of the same tradition.

It has been claimed that the voidtale craze inculcated an enthusiasm for astronomy in its generation, and contributed to the cosmic discoveries of the mid-nineteenth century‚Äîincluding the discoveries of the planet Janus and the speed of light.

References

New Skies, Old Stories, 1980 work written by Simon d'Estiala and published by Prindor Marsill√®s

- bank, riverbank, the area along the sides of a river or stream;

##

80.5: Tavancy

Tavancy (Merch /t…ôÀàvan.s…ôi/, Run'allo [Quechua] Tawanden /taÀàwan.d…õn/), formerly the Tavancine Empire, is a political in western Cappatia [South America] occupying the coast between the Isthmus [of Panama] to the north and Maboutian [~Chilean] lands to the south.
Toponymy

The name Tavancy (Latin Tavantia) is a back-formation from the adjective Tavancine (Latin tavantƒ´nus). This comes from the earliest Latin name imperium tavantƒ´num first attested in the sixteenth century, which was borrowed via Morrove [Morrocan] Arabic as a somewhat-mistaken partial calque from the Run'allo tawantin-suyu "the quartet of provinces".
History

The first Vetomundine people to document the Tavancine Empire were Morrack [Morroco] traders with the New World Company (ÿ¥ÿ±ŸÉÿ© ÿßŸÑÿπÿßŸÑŸÖ ÿßŸÑÿ¨ÿØŸäÿØ), who had already begun dealing with Arcabil [Caribbean] peoples and with Mashick [the Aztec Empire]. Through the sixteenth century several wayports along the west Cappatian coastline were established by said traders, some of which would grow into substantial staddomain [~export colony] settlements.

The death of Tavancine Emperor Cariwasari in 1660 is suspected to have been an assassination carried out on Morrack orders, purportedly to allow for more profitable trade in silver‚ÄîMorrack was at the time running low on funds to support their ongoing wars in Vascony and Libya.

In the early 1930s, a collaboration between alchemists at the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong and the University of Rimack in Tavancy led to the discovery of cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine [ibuprofen] (in pill form familiarly called "CD" or, in Markland, "Tommy pills") as an effective hebetant [painkiller] without significant adversions [side effects].

81.4: vis "in fact"

vis /v…™z/ [v…™z]

    evidently, of course, manifestly, as you know, used to introduce information which one feels is sufficiently obvious that it should have come up by now;
    in fact, actually, in reality, indeed, used to introduce a statement which corrects some part of a previous understanding;
    it transpires, it turns out, apparently, used to introduce surprising new information

Etymology: extracted in the late Middle Boral period from expressions like vis es‚Ä¶ "it is seen that‚Ä¶", as the past participle of veir "to see" < Latin vide≈ç "I see". Speculation that it is instead a retention of Old Boral ivis "sure, certain", a borrowing from synonymous Old English gewiss, is unsupported by any intermediate attestations (though cf modern Merch awice /…ôÀàw…ôis/ "indeed, yes").

The word is often found in the collocation Or vis‚Ä¶ "Well, actually‚Ä¶", especially when it is used at the start of sentences.

    Vis no som nos jamay ei√∞ a Dunclo√∞.
    /v…™z no s…îm n…îz  íaÀàme iŒ∏ a d…™nÀàkl…îŒ∏/

##

81.5: Madray

Madray is a polity in the southeast of India governed from the city of the same name [Madurai].

The British ship Ph≈ìbos in the early sixteenth century stopped at the port of Romeswarm [Rameswaram] in Madray on its geoscopic voyage around Africa and on to Borune [Borneo] (where it would meet the Morrack [Moroccan] ship Cynthia travelling in the other direction).

The death of King Rahulamathavan in 1735 is thought to have been due to an early outbreak of pennackit dysentery [cholera] (although global outbreaks of the disease would not begin in earnest until 1753).

As part of the general Democratic Wars period in the mid-eighteenth century, many states quarrelled for control over parts of India, including Madray. Both relatively nearby powers like China and distant trade interests like Britain became embroiled.

The eighteenth century also saw many technological developments out of this region. For example, the use of pedal holes in musical instruments was pioneered by the peria nadaswarm (the apex of arms races to widen pitch ranges and increase volume) which would influence instruments like the torriot into their modern forms.

The voidtale craze begun by Elsebeth Sneider in 1795 reached as far as Madray; the renowned landscapist Narayanan is especially remembered for his series of works depicting the planets from their surfaces.

##

82.2: vogt dy foscur "dead of night"

vogt dy foscur /v…îjt di foÀàx…™r/ [v…îjt d…™ f äÀàx…™Àê]
- pitch blackness, inky blackness, a particularly deep darkness;
- dead of night, small hours, witching hour, the part of the night conceived of as the darkest and farthest from day

Etymology: idiom first attested in the poetry of 15C writer Drominir, literally "emptiness of darkness". The noun vogt descends from Vulgar Latin *vocitus "empty", a variant of the synonymous vacuus in Classical Latin. The noun foscur is a noun derivative of adjective fosc "dark" which descends from Latin fuscus "dimly lit, black".

##

82.5: London

London (Borlish /Ààl…în.drÃ©/) is a city and polity on the island of Albion. The city is downstream on the River Tames, and the polity comprises much of the Tames' watershed. It is bordered to the north by Markland, the south by Kent, and the west by Britain.

History
London was established by the Romans shortly after their invasion around 50 N, named from a Kelt placename referring to the local floodplain.

After time as a secondary city in Albion, London became the imperial capital of the region once more during the Second Tetrarchy as part of the First Drengot Empire.

The First Burning of London took place in 1290; at least 400 people were killed in the ensuing week-long disaster. This was one of several conflagrations in the first half of the second millennium, many due to the city being perennially conquered from the north or south.

The Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century saw London finally gain lasting independence from its neighbours in 1748 under Queen Margaret, who could trace her ancestry to the royal families of both Markland and Kent. The office of Lord Mayor remained politically powerful enough to rival that of the new monarch, however.

London is home to the New Lyceum, a preeminent research university established in the late eighteenth century.

Seventy-fifth anniversary celebrations in 1823 saw significant infrastructural investments in the region, with a great deal of building along the south bank of the Tames.

London's population grew rapidly during the Global Workshop period of the mid-nineteenth century. It became a lead member of the New Urban Mesh of several major cities in the region, and spearheaded the Mesh's takeover of (or submission into) the larger Drengot Collusion.

The independent polity of London has been long associated with spycraft, with events like the 1887 Desarden Affair (involving the London ambassador to Nackon Day) and the many Londoners in the Roun Red House (the intelligence centre for the Drengot Collusion) implicated in the Furore of 1961 cementing this image in the popular imagination.

Places
Sowbruck, a borough south of the Tames
Ebbasham, a minor spa town
People
Monarchs
Matthew Drengot (fl. 1120), younger brother of then Drengot Emperor Roger II; Matthew ruled the northern half of the Empire in his brother's name
Margaret (fl. 1748), first ruler of an independent London
Other
Clarissa Bellamy (fl. 1920), author of many lovetales and the parachthon work A Door, Once Opened
Heleth Bellamy (fl. 1920), researcher at the New Lyceum specialising in the Democratic Wars
Gunna Despath (fl. 1920), translator who was born Gonnilt Dispa√∞ in Borland

##

83.5: Loveless Lepzi

Lepzi is the capital city of Saxony, located on the Bleak Alstre.

History
Lepzi first acted as political centre for the First Kingdom of Saxony, very briefly just prior to its collapse at the close of the thirteenth century. The Houghbrocken dynasty ruled from there for some years before being removed to Magdeburg when the house was reduced to the four-year-old Marksman Baldur.

With the conclusion of the First German War in 1770, Saxony was ascendant and controlled more territory than it had at any point in its history. Lepzi was subject to attendant early urbanisation and trebled in population in just fifty years. It was one of the vanguards of the Global Workshop period, exploiting vast amounts of locally-mined coal power.

The Blue Marian Society, which agitated for inheritance and suffrage law reforms, was founded in the nineteenth century in Lepzi.

People
Albert Siegert (fl. 1744), owner of a leatherworking and cobbling industry based in Lepzi
Elsebeth Sneider (m. 1772, d. ~1830), author and originator of the voidtale craze with her 1795 work Through a Black Mirror
Otto Sneider (m. 1772), scholar at the Leipzi Sterncundhaus

---

Rachel Desselut (fl. 1743) was a French philosopher. She is best remembered for her 1743 work The Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, which discusses problems of infinite regression and of identity.

She was born to Maurice Desselut, eighth Lord Bouvoin, who was a writer and explorer. Fittingly he was also an infamous libertine (the house name Desselut deriving from an Old French word meaning dissolute, philandering).

She was the first to lay out in our modern terms the philosophical distinction between Form and Method, which allows an entity to self-replicate (constructing its Form from the Method's instructions, then transcribing the Method blindly). These concepts would go on to be vital in the study of methodics and to general augrimmer practice.

Desselut inferred from her work the existence of a vital language, now known to be composed of heredian acid.

##

84.5: Lower Mendeva

Lower Mendeva (Borlish Mendeva Bassem /Àåm…õn.deÀàva Ààba.s…õm/) is a geographical region comprising the southern section of the continent of Mendeva, located west of the Mendevan Gulf. The term sometimes also includes the islands of the Arcabil, which are located within said Gulf.

Contact between Lower Mendeva and the Vetomund began in the late fifteenth century with merchant ships from the Morrack New World Company (established by Malik Munir al-Hamdawi in 1463). In turn, traders therefrom encountered the peoples of the Arcabil, the Mashick Empire in Lower Mendeva, and further south the Tavancine Empire in Cappatia.

The region is predominated by the polity of Mashick, which stretches from coast to coast and so far north as to border Mivock in the west and Taisha in the east.

Many barrick innovations of the late Workshop period came out of Lower Mendeva, likely due to practical concerns around the rapid spread of wired post (there being little previous adoption of the flag-lamp steeplemesh in the region to displace).

By the late twentieth century, this region had become major exporters of foods such as mause and amback.

Many trade goods originated in Lower Mendeva, including billrod dye, shockle, shadome and tashban.

85.1: The Jacobin Fraternity

excerpt in translation from Y Gi√∞ Scos por Ausbagn (The Secret Guide to Axbane), sold for 1‚çö in the Calder Museum of Health's leavingstore.

##

85.5: Laic Declarations

The Laic Declarations were a series of diplomatic documents produced by an international committee of delegations from several dozen polities in Belgrade in 1850. These documents outlined procedures for uncoupling established religions from administrative state functions, and the legal status of people with minority faiths.

At this time, Belgrade was one of the Riverine Cities, an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danaw River and its tributaries. These cities had risen from the ashes of the Kingdom of the Danaw, which had collapsed in spectacular fashion after the Second German War of the 1820s. As such, Belgrade was an early hotbed of new religious and legal thinking, with a renowned Promethean club associated with the Deviance movement and a semi-public tovarick community.

Notable non-signatories of the Declarations included New Provence (at the time probably the most Vetomund-oriented polity in Mendeva) and Persia.

In some respects the Declarations were prefigured by the waning of the censorious and moralistic Modest Arrangement. They laid the groundwork for the Household Renovation of the late nineteenth century, which comprised further shifts in social attitudes around family and marriage. The fact of such major international collaboration in writing the Declarations also prefigured the dawn of the subsequent decades' Good Game period, characterised by alliances and espionage.

Laic adoption would go on to spread globally; for example, in Gulf Mendeva the persistent social prestige of Britain is thought by some to have expedited the contemporary social changes of the region.

The hundred-and-twentieth anniversary of the Laic Declarations was celebrated in academia with the Long Future project, including the synchronised publication of dozens of popular works chronicling the Declarations' origins, course and aftermath.

References

Conjugation, Operation e Diabolica in Musica: The Early Deviance Movement, 1970 work by Borlish popular historian Catrin Ve√ßount

The Deviance Incorporation of Folk Tales, 1970 sociological work by Dr Tanasio Capretti

##

86.1: Letter to the Editor

excerpt in translation from the collected correspondence of Mich√¶l Bervisson (17th June 1799‚Äì12th January 1849), political historian and social reformist. This exceprt is from a letter to his editors concerning proposed alterations to his magnum opus Roma √Üternal: E√ß Vascel a These (An Eternal Rome: Ships of Theseus).

Dovem, m'ajoutabr vel improbation redirigir tan com sera parcan√ß nos prevenibr (cos particuler l'un bougr aquilin nolent my plum teurfiar) e√∞ y ci argument refutar cos direct ny scrit.
Second, I would like to divert as much criticism as we might possibly anticipate (in particular one aquiline fellow who shall not sully my quill) and rebut those arguments directly in the text.

86.3: hapr "groceries"

hapr /Ààha.prÃ©/ [Ààha.p…ê]
- groceries, produce, commodities, shop-bought foodstuffs and sometimes other household supplies;
- (loosely) shopping, recently-bought goods that are relatively inexpensive and characteristic of daily or weekly purchases;
- (strictly) fruit and veg, goods which may be bought at a greengrocer's

The appearance of the initial h- is unexplained, but may be a hypercorrection; at this point /h/ was absent in native Borlish vocabulary and was being reintroduced via Germanic loanwords. Its modern sense is influenced by the compound haprman "greengrocer; (later) wholesaler".

86.4: bougr "fellow"

bougr /Ààbu.grÃ©/ [Ààbu.g…ê]
- fellow, chap, cove, bloke, guy, a man and especially an ordinary man of the street;
- (archaic) scoundrel, rogue, cad, blackguard, someone (usually a man) without honour or virtue

This reflects Medieval Latin Bulgarus "from Bulgaria", from Old Slavonic, and was probably introduced via contemporary French legal codes.

##

86.5: Bavarn

Bavarn (B√≥arn /ÀàpoÀê.…ëÀên/) is a centrally-located German polity of Europe. Among others, it borders Bohemia, Burgundy, Furlaine, Saxony, and Willemy.

History

During the Second Tetrarchy, the Kingdom of Bavarn was one of the two main players in the German Empire; the Empire was for some time ruled jointly by the Houghbrocken and Andex dynasties of Saxony and Bavarn respectively.

Thirteenth-century Bavarn was the epicentre of the Pauline Denunciations and the German Secession, leading to the establishment of the Augsburg See. The concomitant strife saw each vassal in Bavarn declare either for Rome or for Augsburg, in a powderkeg patchwork of allegiances that would collapse the Empire entirely. Bavarn itself would never regain as much Adriatic coast as it had enjoyed just prior to these events.

In the fifteenth century, Bavarn was embroiled in an extended series of conflicts with Burgundy. By this time it had also become a technological centre, with workshops developing entirely new glassmaking techniques.

Following the Blue Trans-Willem War of the late seventeenth century, Bavarn gained significant territory from the Kingdom of Burgundy, which subsequently dissolved. As result, then-king Frederick created the Duchy of Far Suebia, ruled from Burgundy's former capital Geneva.

In the eighteenth century, famine and a shortage of grain led to popular unrest that threatened to destabilise the state; the examples of Venise and Crovatia to the south demonstrated how badly things could go for the current regime.

The Second German War embroiled Bavarn, allied with (among others) Borland and the Northern Concert; a particularly significant event during this war was the disastrously mismanaged Battle of Hulm in 1821.

People

Monarchs

Conrad II (fl. 1120), who ruled from Regensburg

Adalmar (fl. c12), who was succeeded by Empress Katharina of Germany, the last ruler of a united Germany for over five hundred years

Frederick (fl. c17), who created the Duchy of Far Suebia

Nobility

Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia

Others

Wilhelm Bruez (fl. 1225), mayor of Augsburg

Paul von Burchardt (fl. 1225), writer of the Pauline Denunciations and instigator of the German Secession

Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725-1756), illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen

scitation questions in translation from the 2007 course Religion in the Second Millenium: Belief, Community and Division, set by the  University Faculty of Domain History.

(ii) Nell'istr Albion e√∞ y reyaum Danau y dou temporane sceyen mendran√ß moulous cas religion e√∞ y Cligs (e lorry gendr calscon) stan liga√∞ cos starc accougl con autorta√∞ vecin. Desig l'e√ß qualta√∞ commun ben ceter denoirn il ama√ßgat √∞escant por cogta√ßon Contr-Paradis?
(ii) In both Albion and the Danaw kingdom at this time there were significant religious minorities and the Church (of whichever stripe) was strongly associated with local authority. Was it these commonalities that made them fertile ground for Anti-Paradise thought?

##

87.5: Merch

Merch is an English language of the German family of languages which evolved on the island of Albion. It is closely related to other English languages like Kentish and Wessern, and is the primary language of the polity of Markland.

Contemporary Merch descends form the Early Merch spoken in Markland, which itself descends from Anglian varieties of Old English.

This article principally describes the acrolect of Merch spoken in and around the capital city of Tommarth.

Phonology
Consonants
The consonant phonemes found in Merch are:
/m n/
/p t t É k/
/b d d í g/
/f Œ∏ s  É h/
/v √∞ z/
/w l …æ j/
and they are subject to the following orthographic conventions:

b d f h k l m n p t v w z represent their IPA symbols.
j r y represent /d í …æ j/ respectively.
c usually represents /s/ before i e y and /k/ elsewhere.
awice /…ôÀàw…ôis/ "indeed" < OE ƒ°ewiss "certain"
coholl /k äÀàh…îl/ "sulfide" < Ar ŸÉŸèÿ≠ŸíŸÑ "kohl"
g usually represents /g/ but may represent /d í/ before i e y.
eagre /ÀàeÀê.g…ô/ "acid" < L ƒÅcer
nargill /ÀànaÀê.d í…™l/ "coconut" < Ar ŸÜŸéÿ±Ÿíÿ¨ŸêŸäŸÑ "coconut palm"
girl /geÀêl/ "young person"
q appears almost always in the diagraph qu to represent /kw/.
quoit /kw…ôit/ "tyre, torus"
s represents /z/ between vowels and word-finally (unless doubled), and /s/ otherwise.
Brasil /b…æ…ôÀàz…™l/ "Newfoundland" < Ir Breasail
betimes /b…™Ààt…ôimz/ "earlier"
surblavick /soÀêÀàbl…õÀê.v…™k/ "ultraviolet" < L blavus "blue"
x represents /gz/ between vowels, and /ks/ otherwise.
- oxy /Àà…îg.ziÀê/ "stubborn"
- ≈ìculux /Àà…õ.kj…ôÀål äks/ "EM radiation" < Gr Œø·º∂Œ∫ŒøœÇ "house", L lux "light"
There are several digraphs of letters with -h: ch gh ph sh th wh.
ch usually represents /t É/, but sometimes also /k/.
chalk /t Éalk/ "chalk"
yacht /jakt/ "cult" < Du jaght "hunting party"
gh usually represents either / É/ (following i) or /f/ (following u).
wight /w…™ Ét/ "person" < OE wiht "thing, creature"
ough /uÀêf/ "bad" < OE w≈çh "wrong, crooked"
NB I'ght /Àà…ôift/ "I must", contracted from I ought
ph represents /f/.
aphlox /Ààa.fl…îks/ "carbon dioxide" < Gr Œ¨- "without" and œÜŒªœåŒæ "flame"
sh represents / É/.
shaken / É…õÀêk…ô/ "to shake"
th usually represents either of /Œ∏ √∞/, and sometimes /t/.
thrift /Œ∏…æ…™ft/ "energy" < ON √ærift "prosperity"
yeathre /ÀàjeÀê√∞…ô/ "together" < OE geador
wh represents /f/.
- white /f…ôit/ "white"
There are several situations in which written consonants may be silent.
In certain endings: participle -end /…ôn/, infinitive -en /…ô/, superlative -est /…ôs/.
discurrend /d…™Ààsk ä.…æ…ôn/ "different" < L discurrƒìns "roaming"
chammen /Ààt Éa.m…ô/ "to chew" (poss. onomatopoetic)
tharvest /ÀàŒ∏aÀê.v…ôs/ "least sociable" < OE √æeorf "unleavened"
Coda r lengthens the preceding vowel and word-final -re is pronounced /…ô/.
interpel /…™nÀàt…õÀê.p…ôl/ "interact with" < L interpell≈ç "I disturb"
sundre /Ààs än.d…ô/ "many" < OE sundor "separately"
Post-tonic -st- immediately before a closed syllable is pronounced /s/.
hirstend /ÀàheÀê.s…ôn/ "extra" < OE hyrstan "to ornament"
pistol /Ààp…™.s…ôl/ "message" < L epistola
Likewise, post-tonic -v- is lost in some common words.
morovre /m äÀà…æoÀê(…ô)/ "as well"
evre /…õÀê(…ô)/ "always"
Vowels
The vowels of (this variety of) Merch comprise the short vowels:
/a …õ …™ …î  ä …ô/
the long vowels:
/aÀê …õÀê eÀê iÀê …îÀê oÀê  âÀê/
and two diphthongs:
/…ôi …ôu/
which are subject to the following orthographic conventions:

In stressed syllables, "short" a e i~y o u represent /a …õ …™ …î  ä/ (noting that schwa cannot be stressed).
samn /samn/ "set, collection" < OE samnian "gather"
whelp /f…õlp/ "baby animal"

In unstressed syllables, a e i~y o u instead represent /…ô …ô~…™ …™  ä  ä/‚Äîexcept word-finally, where /…™  ä/ become /iÀê …ô/.
defectend /d…™Ààf…õk.t…ôn/ "positively charged"

"Long" a e i~y o u represent /…õÀê iÀê …ôi uÀê j…ôu/.
- besake /b…™Ààz…õÀêk/ "because"

There are plenty of digraphs representing vowels.
Coda -r: ar er ir or ur represent /aÀê …õÀê eÀê oÀê oÀê/.
davarn /d…ôÀàvaÀên/ "grand hotel, resort" < W tafarn "inn"

fain /f…ôin/ "willing(ly)"
-o: oo represents /uÀê/.
soon /suÀên/ "as soon as"
-u/w: au eu ou and aw ew ow represent /…ôu …ôu …ôu~uÀê/.
blew /bl…ôu/ "blue"
coshow /Ààk…î. ÉuÀê/ "rubber" < Quechua kawchu

88.4: desveyar "deviate"

##

88.5: Convoy Australier

The Convoy Australier (Merch /Ààk…în.v…ôi …ôÀàst…æ…õÀê.liÀê…ô/), officially the Order of Saint Hemma of Still Water, is a Christian organisation aligned with Rome which emerged in the Second Tetrarchy period. It has undergone several major transformations of purpose since its inception.

History
The Convoy began in the eleventh century in the Duchy of Crain, a member of the German Empire subordinate to Bavarn. In the first decades of its existence the Convoy was in essence a (quite literally) glorified band of mercenaries.

The Convoy was heavily involved in the first crusades and related ventures, from the Principality of Mystra in Greece to the then-unsuccessful forays in Jerusalem. It was appointed to administrate the new states of Antioch and Port Simon soon after their conquests in the twelfth century.

Pope Just revoked the Convoy's rulership of Antioch and Port Simon in 1208 as the culmination of a escalating inter-faction struggle. However, it is generally agreed that this act saved the Convoy a great amount of trouble in the long run (given the imminent disaster of the Failed Crusade). The Convoy was subsequently assigned to the courts of Svonimir of Crovatia and Taxon of Hungary; it was thus ideally placed to lead the Baptism of the North, even as the troubles of the German Secession loomed.

The German Secession increased the urgency of the Baptism of the North; Rome feared that Augsburg might spread its new heresy faster than Rome could reach the North. As a consequence, the Convoy grew substantially in numbers and wealth during the thirteenth century, and outlying chapters were founded as far afield as Borland and (eventually) Lithow [Lithuania].

89.2: yeul ovart "thoroughfare"

yeul ovart /jawl oÀàvart/
- thoroughfare, highway, main street, a major road especially conceived as connecting two towns or district
- (computing) a high-speed network connection

Etymology: collocation first seen in the sixteenth century, literally meaning "open street". The adjective ovart "open, public" descends from Vulgar Latin opertus, alteration of Classical apertus "open". The noun yeul "street, wide road" reflects Old Borlish ieol "unpaved road" (as opposed to vei "paved road"), which given Welsh heol "route, passage" is presumably of Borland Kelt origin. In the latter sense attested from 1977.

89.3: annex denover "afterthought"

annex denover /aÀàn…õks Àåde.noÀàv…õr/ […êÀàn…õks Àåde.n äÀàv…õÀê]
- afterthought, an action or reflection done after the fact;
- something done at the last-minute or in an improvised manner, like a correction, addition or improvement

Etymology: idiom attested from the nineteenth century, literally "final-stage addition". The noun annex "supplement, appendix, addition, boost, enhancement, refinement" is a deverbal from verb annexar "to supplement, append, boost, refine", which comes via Scholastic Latin annex≈ç from Classical annexus "attachment".

The adjective denover is a contemporary derivation from denou "conclusion, ending, denouement; the final stage of any process", itself also a deverbal from denovar "untie, unravel; end". This is an Old Borlish derivation from nou "know, tangle", from the synonymous n≈çdus in Latin.

89.4: sveldr "whittle"

sveldr /Ààsv…õl.drÃ©/ [Ààzv…õl.d…ê]
- pluck, to remove the feathers from a bird;
- whittle, carve, to cut or shave a piece of wood with a knife;
- reduce, thin, cut away at, to gradually make something smaller or less numerous;

Extension to woodworking is attested by the thirteenth century, in abstract use it is seen from the fifteenth, and the colloquial sense first arose in the late eighteenth before a revival (or re-coining) in the twentieth.

##

89.5: Second Tetrarchy

The Second Tetrarchy, in older works also called the Medieval Imperia, refers to the period of roughly two centuries at the dawn of second-millennium Europe when a good deal of the land was (at least nominally) under the jurisdiction of four empires: the First Drengot Empire, the German Empire, the Second Roman Empire, and the Single Caliphate.

The name "Second Tetrarchy" was first used in the eighteenth century by the writings of Vascon historian David Bensa√≠d, in reference to the Roman Tetrarchy of the early fourth century, although the two political regimes had little else in common.

The First Drengot Empire began with the Kernovan Conjuring of 1028, when Normandy conquered Kent with the support of Greater Devon. Over the next years House Drengot went on to consolidate lands from the Middlesea to Edinbrough.

This unity endured until the Emergency at Dijon in 1170, when the Kingdom of Burgundy ceased to be a Drengot vassal; the empire was formally dissolved in 1191 and divided among the heirs and allies of Daniel IV.

The German Empire descended from the eastern half of the expansive Kingdom of the Franks after its scission in the ninth century, combining the Eastern Franks with Willemy. In its first centuries it expanded east and south; the kingdoms of Saxony and Bavarn became the empire's preeminent vassals, with their Andex and Houghbrocken dynasties ruling in turn.

By the end of the twelfth century, the northern cities were developing into the mercantile Stadbund and away from imperial authority. The slow erosion of the German Empire accelerated rapidly during the German Secession of the thirteenth century, and by the end of that century the fiction of a surviving German Empire was laid to rest with the corpse of the first Kingdom of Saxony.

The Second Roman Empire had existed since the (short-lived) reconquest of Rome from the Eastern Goths by Justinian I in 536; it would persist in some form for over a millennium. The eleventh and twelfth centuries did see the empire reach a territorial maximum, controlling territory from Corsica in the west to Derban on the Hazaran Sea in the east.

It was only in the late seventeenth century that the Second Roman Empire ceased to exist in any meaningful sense (despite several periods of only nominal continuity), due to a succession crisis and a series of dramatic civil wars.

The Single Caliphate stretched from Spain to Persia, and was the product of several centralisation reforms from Cairo consolidating authority over the various successor states to the Abbasid Caliphate (although the further territories such as Cordova or the Turkish lands were never as closely-ruled).

- serious, committed, for keeps, risky, undertaken with something staked on the outcome;

The participle lu√Øscent "playing" comes from lu√Ør "to play", which descends from a late Insular Latin l≈´dƒ´re, l≈´dƒìscere "to play". These are both conjugational regularisations of l≈´dere in Classical Latin. See also the following idiom involving this construction:

Com es oc lu√Øscent a ney ne Nivon? "What's that got to do with anything?"

- general interjection in response to a perceived non sequitur, cf. English "What's that got to do with the price of fish?"

The noun ney "snow" reflects Latin nix, nivem "snow", while the placename Nivon "Japan" is a sixteenth-century borrowing eventually from Êó•Êú¨, contemporaneously pronounced /niÀà…∏on/.

##

90.5: Sandrine

Sandrine (Merch /Ààsan.d…æ…ôin/) is a soluble substance found in citrus and other fruits and vegetables. It is one of the substances essential to mammal life; a severe defect of sandrine is what causes scurvy.

(Do not confuse this substance with mandrine, a deadly poison.)

The curative effects of citrus fruits for scurvy were suspected as early as the fourteenth century. By the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, with the much longer geoscopic voyages following the Novomundine Landfall of 1471, efforts to prevent scurvy became more active. For example, King Ambrose III of Vascony (the 'Ascendant') instituted the Sandrine Design in 1485 by which ships would be required to stock dried fruits and syrups. This scheme, named for his sister Alexandra and the first European royal to set foot in the Novomund, is where the name sandrine originates.

However, such schemes did not have lasting effects. This was partly due to expense but much more significantly, due to the lack of understanding of the mechanism by which fruit prevented scurvy. Thus, fruit would be preserved in ways which removed their sandrine content (for example, boiling or use of copper vessels) and sailors would suffer scurvy anyway, leading guilds to abandon stocking fruits altogether.

Truly theoretic understanding would not come until the seventeenth century, when it was discovered by chance that the guy-coney can also develop scurvy (humans and guys are rare among animals in not creating sandrine in our bodies from sugar). This allowed for much more rigorous and time-efficient testing on guys, to determine what preparations of fruits and vegetables were effective at preventing scurvy. By the 1700s it had become common practice to stock lemon juice diluted with strong alcohol on long sea voyages.

##

91.5: Millstone War

The Millstone War (1897‚Äì1908) refers to the period of global military conflict often considered as the dawn of modern warfare. Though not all of the wars involved spanned the entire eleven years, they are all characterised by a fundamental asymmetry: defensive tactics had outstripped offensive technology, and so front lines worldwide ground to barely-moving trench fortifications.

The Millstone War marks the end of the Good Game period; its aftermath saw a comprehensive realignment of the previous system of alliances.

The last years of the Millstone War saw the first military use of aircraft, as well as air-bombing of cities including Marsella in Provence.

Political tensions rose globally in the decades after the Millstone War, as the inadequacy of the post-war settlements became apparent. In particular, relations between Drengot Collusion and their southern neighbours of Vascony and Provence quickly soured.

---

Honorine Maignard (fl. 1890‚Äì1940) was an author in France active for over fifty years in the early twentieth century. She was the granddaughter of the Lombard diplomat Audace Saviano, who during the Good Game period was instrumental in the formation of the Italian Concert.

During the Millstone War she served in the Second Medic Force of Valois for more than six years.

---

Vincent Margouille (fl. 1960‚Äì1990) was a Norman author and professional lineball player. In 1965 he led the Kent team to its only victory in the Lineball Global Tourney to date. He is the son of Vr√©queu Margouille, who also played for Kent in the 1935 LGT.

From 1981 on, he authored his best-known series (beginning with Upstairs, Downstairs), which follows the people living in a manorhouse during the Good Game period. Later instalments portray their experiences during the Millstone War.

##

92.2: scagl a natur "the way of things"

scagl a natur /x…õil a naÀàt…™r/ [√ß…õil a n…êÀàt…™Àê]
- convention, the way of things, elements of society or the wider world considered innate and unchangeable;
- hierarchy, pecking order, a social economic or political system in which people or things are ranked and given corresponding status

Etymology: literally "nature's staircase", derived in the Middle Boral period from the Scholastic Latin scƒÅla nƒÅt≈´rae "the Great Chain of Being" (literally, the ladder of nature). This is the theological concept of the hierarchy of all matter and life, thought by medieval Christianity to have been decreed by God, and adapted from similar ideas in Platonic and Aristotelian philosophy.

Often in the phrase step ny scagl a natur "part of the natural order of things, one's place in a hierarchy", where step "step, rung, foothold" is a borrowing from Old English.

Oc voscon accommis va contr y scagl a natur.
/…îk voÀàx…în Àåa.k…îÀàm…™z va Ààk…în.trÃ© i x…õil a naÀàt…™r/
[…î v äÀàx…în Àåa.k äÀàm…™z va Ààk…în.t Ä‚Äøi √ß…õil a n…êÀàt…™Àê]
s.px 2p.ins undertake-p.pst go against df ladder at nature
What you've done is against the natural order of things.

##

92.5: Marsella

Marsella (Provincial /masÀàse é…î/; Borlish Marsegl /ma ÄÀàsijl/) is a city on the Middlesea [Mediterranean] and the capital of Provence.

History

A raid by itinerant Norman mercenaries sacked the city of Marsella in 946 N, nominally on behalf of the Malchassian Romans. As it happens, records of this raid contain the first unambiguous mention of identical triplets (Danes born on the isle of Mull around 915) in European history.

In the Second Tetrarchy period of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, Marsella was a major population centre in Burgundy within the First Drengot Empire.

During the Vascon Ascendancy, the region of Provence (including Marsella) was conquered by Vascony. Burgundy's reconquest in 1494 caused so much instability that the region rose up in revolt, sparking the 1497‚Äì99 War of Provincial Independence.

Newly-independent Provence led by Daniel Menton, Lord Amig√≤t, established Marsella as its capital. It is generally understood that he wanted to monitor the piratical allies of Barcelon who had ruled the city de facto for some time.

By the turn of the seventeenth century there were multiple weekly newspapers operating from Marsella. None of these remained in print until the modern day; the earliest extant publications‚Äîsuch as the entertainment and recreation newspaper Fiellas Dimenja‚Äîwere incorporated in the nineteenth century.

During the Millstone War in the early twentieth century, Marsella suffered heavy air-bombing as part of the world's first such use of aircraft in wartime.

Today Marsella is famed for its midsummer celebrations which also commemorate the anniversary of its independence from Burgundy.

##

excerpt in translation from the collected correspondence of Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725‚Äì56), illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia.

Tu aye, com tojorn, bon cos enter ne flagitar a√∞elent lengacer lava√ß a√Ør (e carta√∞ demay cos enter ne my presen√ß parmettr!).

Jo prefay volir habituar, ma interim jo so gavent auvorn demay ne borgogn e bohemian parolant coll'e√ß soi√ß d'Albert.

No scey alcun los ag non√ßamen noscon caut d'eromn cas pan?
Does the news we get of bread troubles have any basis?

93.5: Lexember 2023 - 1: ceu "basically"

ceu /tsaw/ [tsaw]
- basically, essentially, pretty much, in effect, for all intents and purposes, not literally but sufficiently close that any difference is irrelevant;
- like, kinda, sorta, a filler word used to hedge a statement;
- (in negative sentences) hardly, barely, only just, to a minimal extent

Etymology: from Middle Borlish, original a legal term of art used to distinguish an act from a legally distinct act with the same consequences or punishment. In this use it was borrowed from Latin ceu "just like, just as if, as (if) it were". By the eighteenth century it had moved into general use, along with the innovated negative sense (originally restricted to the phrase ceu nien "barely anything, almost nothing").

There were hardly any sandwiches left on the buffet table.

---

Sodrick (Merch /Ààs…î.dr…™k/, Welsh Hele√∞ /heÀàl…õ√∞/) is a region of the Northwestern Isles comprising many small islands north of Wales and west of Scotland.

Toponymy
The name Sodrick was originally a demonym for the people of Soder (Old English Sodor), which descends from the Old Norrish su√∞reyjar "southern islands".

History
Sodrick was settled during the Norrish ascendancy of the eighth and ninth centuries, at the same time as Danes arrived on the shores of Albion and Ireland. Harald the Blue of Sodrick contributed to the establishment of the Kingdom of Scotland in the ninth century. Even the Welsh island of Mondwell was half controlled by Sodrick for some time after a partition agreed between Sodrick and Wynneth that was recorded in both the Ulstre and Carlile chronicles.

During the tenth century, the kingdom of Dunclothe under Roderick invaded and conquered many of the isles of Sodrick from the Danes, including Arran in 934 and (most significantly, as a power centre) the isle of Man in 942. The decline of Dane power in Sodrick has been connected to the decreased efficiency of obtaining wealth by raiding over time.

As Dunclothe took control of Sodrick, many fled or were expelled to places like Dane-ruled York and Ireland, or to relatively prosperous Normandy. Among their number is recorded the first unambiguous mention of identical triplets (Danes born on the isle of Mull around 915) in European history.

By the fifteenth century, Sodrick was subordinate to Wales, and it was incorporated as an outlying tallath of the Kingdom of Britain when it was formed.

Cordin Editions (Borlish Edition Cordin /Àåe.diÀàtsj…în k…îrÀàd…™n/) is a publishing house founded in the mid-twentieth century and based in the city of Cordin on Borland.

The company, which is affiliated with the University of Cordin, is recognised for producing educational books for national schools. It also published syllabi covering schooling throughout childhood which were taken up as a standard by most schools in the polity.
Publications

    The Boral Province in the century after Hadrian (Y Provin√ß Boral par y siecr posc Hadrian, 1952), written by late classical historian Robiaut Scarvon
    The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d'Istr Boral, first edition 1982, second edition 1993), a school history textbook
    Shepherds of Hambrick: the Small History of Dane Borland (Acr a Sau√∞ Vellig, 2008), written by Sconet Ydre√ß about Borland in the tenth century
    From Tablets to Touchscreens: The Story of Education (Des Delt vars Dois: Y Stoir d'Education, 2016), written by Theign Roufler in the style of an academic textbook but intended to be read by a large lay audience

---

Southbar (Borlish Sothbar /s…îŒ∏Ààbar/ [s ähÀàb…ëÀê]) is a region of Borland in the southwest of the island. Its largest population centre is the city of Axbane.
History

The petty kingdom of Southbar was founded by Anglian immigrants in the fifth century, along with Angland and several more minor kingdoms. It quickly established itself in opposition to Angland and to the rump sub-Roman Borlish state. At the turn of the ninth century it saw an influx of immigration from East Anglia after it was subjugated by Markland.

After an alliance between the First Kingdom of Borland and Angland and the Battle of Fendal (810), Southbar was forced to swear fealty as a subordinate kingdom.

Southbar held out for some time against the Danes after the fall of the First Kingdom of Borland, and even harboured the exiled King Jothey and his wife Brenna of Barrow. Nonetheless, by the end of the tenth century Sothbar had been incorporated into Dane Borland.

In the twelfth century Southbar had become a county. Its de facto seat was Axbane; by 1190 Count Steven is known to have resided year-round in the coastal town, bequeathing the old house in Heitri√ß to his younger brother.

Celtic Borland

Y pouvlan√ß celtesc d'Istr Boral (y Bora Ale noncupa√∞ temporane) no's fort mis a regest. Y pog bustr tesmogn de lorry lenga√ß sousonn an dehiscen√ß for recent des y lenga√ß britton; creut es ig il alandau sull'istr des Albion, e noc des y tarmagn cos direct.
The Celt population of Borland (self-described as the Bora Ale) is poorly documented. The few attestations of their language suggest only a recent divergence from the Briton tongue; they are thought to have arrived on the island from Albion, not directly from the continent.

L'oc cultur er conjognt ag sar marcer d'Europ par mejan dell'enascment de stan e de far. Jarry innaja√∞ dell'oc epoc es yent trova√∞ tras toll'istr des loy allors remot com Egypt e Pers.
This culture was connected to the European trade mesh via the export of tin and iron. Imported pottery of this period has been found across the island from as far afield as Egypt and Persia.

Y Celt stan compos ne form de bempart tribu; cascun afferjarau picq region e√∞ er regna√∞ des fortalle√ß colliner. Pla√ß religious di√ßr plusour temporane son desseveli√∞; y celeberrem es y labyrinth a √áadrosc, oc ayent large√ß d'apponment lon fondamen cas sell'origin e bers.
The Celts were organised into many tribes, each covering small regions and ruled from hill-forts. Several important religious sites of this time have been unearthed; most famously the √áadrosc labyrinth, which has sparked much unfounded theorising as to its origin and purpose.

---

Roman Papacy

The Papacy in Rome is the spiritual seat of much of Christendom.

Despite intermittent conflicts with the Greek patriarchs and several abortive antipope lineages, the Roman Papacy remained the ultimate authority over all Christendom until the German Secession of the early thirteen century. This created a lasting competing seat in the Augsburg See which did not recognise the Roman Papacy's legitimacy.

The Great Fracture of the mid-sixteenth century severely diminished the temporal powers of the Papacy. While on paper the Pope in Rome remained an absolute ruler (along with political dominion over several smaller Italian and German provinces), in practice the new Triunvirato had political dominion over the city of Rome.

People

Mercury II (fl. 590), who took a particular interest in Borland as a stronghold of Christianity in the north
Agatho (1001‚Äì1026), who canonised David, patron saint of Wales
Nuntius (fl. ca. 1100), who instituted a reform of the Julian calendar and moved the New Year to the 1st of March
Damian V (1110-1121), born Humbert of Ravenna in 1047
Damian VI (1199‚Äì1208), called 'the Wise', and first member of the Brethin in the role
Just (1208‚Äì), who called for a renewed Crusade for Jerusalem in 1210 (known to history as the Failed Crusade)
Hermeneus II (fl. ca. 1250), more totalist than his predecessors in reaction to the German Secession and instigator of the Baptism of the North

Revillon

Revillon (Borlish Reveglon /Àåre.vijÀàl…în/) is the Borlish term for the eve (and often the entire day) before Christide. It refers also to the specific cultural practice on the island of the Winter Feast (y Fest Reveglon), a celebration held on Revillon in churches across the land to which everyone in attendance brings food.

The Borlish name Reveglon means "vigil", and is a Middle Boral borrowing from the synonymous French reveillon, itself originally from Latin evigil≈ç "I wake up". Earlier names include jolveisre (modern jolleistr "excursion, jaunt"), taken from Old Norrish j√≥lveizla "Yule feast", and fle√∞oul (of unknown ultimate etymology), both of which referred specifically to the celebrations.

Evidence of similar winter solstice festivities involving communally-prepared feasts on Borland predates even the Roman invasion, making Revillon one of the longest continuously-practised folk traditions in Europe (bearing in mind that the practice has evolved considerably with time). Third-century Roman historian Ammian Marcellinus provides us with an early written mention of the Winter Feast, which appears (under its original name Flid≈çlus, which gave the aforementioned fle√∞oul) in one of his asides on the Northwestern Isles. Post-imperial sources in the first millennium almost exclusively criticise the tradition as pagan and unholy; however, by the twelfth century the practice is fully appropriated by the Church.

Queen Natalia II of Borland abdicated on Revillon of 1894 as part of the political upheaval surrounding Borland's entry into the Drengot Collusion.

Food
Foods traditionally associated with the Revillon Feast include:

the tort dell'ivan /Ààt…î Ät Àåde.liÀàvan/ ("child's pie", in reference to the Christ child), a savoury dish often of lamb. The practice of scoring a cross into the lid and hiding a small wooden object inside (traditionally also a cross) is a later innovation, but compare the similar galette des rois seen on the continent. This name for the dish is attested as early as the tenth century in Old Boral torte del ifan /t…î…æ.t…ô d…õl iÀàfan/, although the modern recipe including lamb and potato is not seen in writing until the eighteenth century.
jonnovar zucarra√∞ /Àå í…î.noÀàva Ä Àåzi.kaÀà ÄaŒ∏/ "candied butterflies", a sugar-spiced cakebread particular to the island which ever since their earliest iterations have been generously spiced (usually with dalassine bark and inchroot). At this time of year they are formed into butterfly shapes, popularly thought to represent the metamorphosis of a dead winter into the new life of spring.

DNA in the 18th century

    excerpt in translation from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto.

Desselut ratiocinau ig exist un lenga√ß vital (parmy sy scaun√ß a luy i le nommau y Parol Divin) present ny ci casel dy corp.
Desselut inferred the existence of a vital language (in her own writing she called it the Divine Word) present in the cases of the body.

Rumour tardessem noc obstroint, plausibr no's ig i stimaus l'oc lenga√ß hebreu a verb, con tan l'exponois comport plusour ne sell'edition.
Despite later rumours, she probably did not believe this language was literally Hebrew, although she made several analogies in her publications.

D'an 1770 y preu livr se descreint sceint er scaut par un scrittour lon nom e lor condona√∞ vars l'Universta√∞ ne Houlm.
In 1770 the first real self-describing book was written by an anonymous author and donated to the University of Hulm.

L'universta√∞ a y livr facsimila√∞ alcant sur y quadrennial cos exact largecer com possibr, e l'original, a cost y scavr y nuverrem, es sgardabr nell'edifi√ß la dell'Andron a Heredtatur.
The university has duplicated the book once every four years in as exact a way as possible, and the original, alongside the most recent copy, may be viewed in their Department of Heredity building.

Britain

Britain (Cambrick Prydein) is a polity of Albion centred on Wales but extending through Devon and Dunclothe and out to Kernow and parts of Ireland.

History
The Kingdom of Britain was founded in the fifteenth century, formalising the suzerainty of Wales over the tallaths of Devon and Dunclothe. The state profited greatly from the Wars of Fealty of the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries which raged across the rest of Albion.

Britain's economic success at this time drove ventures in Africa. Resupply ports along the western coast eventually allowed for the flourishing of direct sea trade between Europe and Asia.

The seventeenth century saw the dawn of British exploration of the Novomund, founding staddomains along the southern Gulf. These would coalesce into several polities known collectively as British Mendeva over the next centuries, the largest of which is Hasiny. Britain here found itself in conflict with Provence's settlements on the east coast; for example, the Sack of Santrafew was undertaken by an alliance of British and Mastoiset forces.

After the Chrysian Landfall, emigration from Britain (along with more formal state-building enterprise from Cathay) helped form the modern polity of New Leudong.

In the latter years of the nineteenth century, British Mendeva overtook Britain in population and industry. Nonetheless, Britain retained some cultural prestige over the Novomundine polities. In part this was sustained via migration of many members of the 'new rich' across the Atlantic, and the resultant intertwining of the respective upper-class societies through marriage. It is debated whether this prestige accelerated the export of Household Renovation social mores from Britain to Mendeva.

References
A Cyclop√¶dia of British Persons (third edition), written by Wolfston Potter and published by the Yievle Brethin Primers in 1870

##

98.5: Tachygraph

A tachygraph (Merch /Àåta.k…™Ààg…æaf/, also informally a tachy or tacky /Ààta.k…ôi/) is a device with a keyboard for typing text onto paper, or more recently directly onto a slate.

Etymology

The word "tachygraph" originates in the mid-nineteenth century as a brand name. It is derived from Ancient Greek œÑ·æ∞œá·øÝÃÅœÇ ‚Ä¢ (takh√∫s) "quick, fast" and Œ≥œÅ·æ∞ÃÅœÜœâ ‚Ä¢ (gr√°ph≈ç) "draw, write".

History

Early tachygraphs in the 1830s and 40s were extremely cumbersome, with most models only capable of setting type at a fraction of the speed of practised handwriting. The Armanburg Tachygraph (Tachygrafia Armangrod), which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygrafia (literally the "slow-writer").

Since very early on, attempts have been made to connect tachygraphs to the steeplemesh, so that one could type a message onto paper and simultaneously transmit a copy of the message to someone else (ideally, automating a tachygraph to type the message out also on the receiving end).

The first tachygraph specifically able to send messages on the steeplemesh was the Vilmanas Teleclavia, designed in Lithow by Tomas Vilmanas in 1859. Its first message (the first line from Ovid's Heroines, reproduced below) was sent on Michaelmas of that year to his friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste.

¬∑ A B C D E F G ¬∑ HAEC TUA PENELOPE LENTO TIBI MITTIT ULISSE ¬∑
a b c d e f g. This your Penelope sends to you, long-delayed Ulysses.

Historical films have made much of the fact that this message was sent less than a month after the 1859 Auroric Anomaly, but there is no evidence Vilmanas considered its effects (though he must have been aware of the stories of messages along unpowered steeplepost wires).

In the late nineteenth century, the Faxim brand of tachygraph was sold across Borland.

##

100.5: Voyage of the Ph≈ìbos

The Ph≈ìbos was a flagship of the Kingdom of Britain in the early sixteenth century. Having profited greatly at the expense of the other polities of Albion [Great Britain] during the Wars of Fealty, Britain under King Howel sought to extend its trade mesh past its posts down the west African coast, and trade with Asia directly by sea.

To this end, a coterie of ships led by the Ph≈ìbos (and itself captained by Martan mac Kellot) was tasked with sailing as far east as possible. After rounding Africa and making port in such places as Ungotia [Zanzibar], they were directed by Umanick [Omani] and then Chinese traders. Via Gurjat and Madray [1], they eventually arrived in Borune, at that time the southeasternmost land known in the Vetomund.

The Ph≈ìbos was thus present at the Great Borunesk Meeting of 1517. The ship's meeting with the Morrack ship the Cynthia in the port city of Sinquan is the definitive moment in the course of the Meeting, and it appears in several book and film adaptations. It is popularly assumed that the crews could not understand one another; in fact at least a dozen crewmembers of the Ph≈ìbos spoke some Arabic, as the British and Morrack trade meshes had overlapped for centuries.

Unlike the Cynthia, the Ph≈ìbos would not attempt to circumnavigate the globe; tales of the magnitude of the oceans ahead dissuaded mac Kellot from such a course.

---

[1] polities of India whose names are cognate with IRL Gujarat and Madurai.

101.2: raussi√∞ "backwards"

##

101.5: New Urban Mesh
 
The New Urban Mesh was an alliance of autonomous cities in Northwest Europe in the nineteenth century. Among its members were London, Conster, Axbane, Pentrose, Amstel, Anvers and Gant.

History
The Mesh emerged contemporaneously with similar political structures elsewhere, such as the Riverine Cities along the Danaw. This rise of such federacies of city-states has been attributed to the rapid population growth and expansion of cities in the run-up to the Global Workshop period. Several of the Mesh's cities were simultaneously also part of pre-existing polities (for example, Axbane and Pentrose were never legally independent from Borland).

The cities of the Mesh are popularly associated with wheeling-and-dealing policy, as well as aggressive and global use of espionage. However, the extent to which the Mesh was (for example) able to steer politics in the East to their trade benefit is likely overestimated.

In the late nineteenth century (ca. 1890), London led the Mesh to unite politically with the Drengot Collusion, at the time a nascent alliance of the French polities of Norman Kent, Paris and Burgundy. Whether it is more correct to say that the Mesh was subsumed into the Collusion, or that the Mesh took control of the Collusion, has long been a topic of heated debate.

References
Mr Desarden, Prince Consort (1968), a film based on the 1887 scandal around James Desarden, London's ambassador to Nackon Day. The affair is widely thought to have been politically motivated and a product of direct meddling from the Mesh.

##

102.1: The Jacobin Fraternity

Y Fraternta√∞ Jagovin, cos historic tan connu√∞ sou nom dell'Orn a Sagnt Jagm, es un communta√∞ religious y cita√∞ Ausbagn hamtant ag rimot sou√∞er n'Istr Boral.
The Jacobin Fraternity, historically also known as the Order of Saint James, is a religious organisation based in the city of Axbane on the south coast of Borland.

Y Fraternta√∞ er instaura√∞ ne Flandr ag siecr deg sou y patronnement d'Amegl de Brug. Ag siecr on√ß l'oc er envolu√∞ com conjura√ßonnour n'un smout pouvlancer vecin contrell'Emperour Drengo√ßan Joffrey II e√∞ obliga√∞ a enfugr tras mar vars Istr Boral. La costroyeurn il y Monastir Peninsoler ancour Ausbagn surplombant.
The Fraternity was founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges. In the eleventh century it was implicated in a local popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffry II and forced to flee over the sea to Borland. There they built the Peninsular Monastery which still overlooks Axbane.

Mone√∞ des y Fraternta√∞ droughau ag Pala√ß Jagovin ny ci bandlau atorn de Damva√∞, e√ß posc lorry compliscen√ß ag siecr ogto√ß nuverrem denent y posson fis por y parolment borallesc.
Funds from the Fraternity contributed to the Jacobin Estates on the outskirts of Damvath, which on their completion in the late eighteenth century became the fixed location of Borland's parliament.

Sceint oy l'orn es reconnu√∞ cos majorta√∞er por y colluy seyon aut, Yeustman Jaibos, tan com ei√∞el spanent ny cita√∞ posseyent (ig inclus y pla√ß dell'un parmy y bagn roman antic la).
Today the organisation is known mostly for the guild they own, Jaibos Breweries, as well as for owning significant lands in the city (including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses there).

##

102.5: British Mendeva

British Mendeva refers to the polities of Mendeva which have undergone significant settlement from or otherwise been significantly influenced by the Kingdom of Britain. The region is mostly contained within the larger region of Gulf Mendeva. The polity of the region with the most population and global influence is Hasiny.

History

In the seventeenth century, exploratory voyages by ships of the Kingdom of Britain founded staddomains along the Mendevan Gulf, which would grow via settlement, treaty and conquest into the polities of British Mendeva.

The 'herdtale' tradition of storytelling which originated in nineteenth-century Mendeva was brought to wider recognition via several popular works published jointly in British Mendeva and Britain. Conversely, the masquira genre overtook the region later in that century following its popularity in the Vetomund.

British Mendeva overtook Britain in population and industry in the latter years of the nineteenth century. Nonetheless, for some time Britain retained a position of cultural cachet; it has been suggested this contributed to British Mendeva's rapid adoption of the ethics of the Household Renovation (as compared to much more ambivalent New Provence and Mashick, for instance) during this time.

Unlike earlier centuries, the nineteenth century saw significant transmigration of the √©lites between British Mendeva and Britain, as well as much more intermarriage. In part this can be attributed to the newness of an autochthonous √©lite west of the Atlantic, but mostly this is simply due to faster ships and greatly-reduced travel times.

Y viol cemtir intermann un jout cyclant ny cont a pouvr borallesc.
The graveyard fiddlers are a recurring motif in the folklore of Borland. 

Postula√∞ es ig il sein gast a mort sont ovitta√∞ con y broit enigmatic e lontan de ba√∞r viol o√Ø√∞ cos particular jos cemtir e√∞ all'hour prell'ort sol.

103.5: Making History

shortform scitation questions lifted from the 2021 edition of the Tremonnow Open School's domain history course 'Making History', which covers the process by which events and people are compiled into historical narratives.

(questions given approximately in order of complexity)

1. Which 14th-century historian first referred to the First Drengot Empire of three centuries earlier as the 'Constant Empire' in an attempt to link it to the District of Constantius (the unrelated part of the fourth-century Diocletian Tetrarchy ruling over Roman Brittania and Gaul)?

2. Name three international conflicts (not including the First German War) which took place during the 'Long Peace' period, here defined as lasting from 1770 until 1815.

3. Who wrote 1872 work 'An Incomplete Dictionary of the New London'? As the crowning example from the Futurism period of parachthon romance, it contains the first attestation of 'slate' for a screen displaying information, along with what other lasting coinages?

4. During which decades of the twentieth century did the Portingale Anarchy take place? Which legal developments of the prior years were abruptly reversed as a result of the breakdown of state control?

5. Compare the stability of the Christian states in the Holy Land before and after the Failed Crusade in the early thirteenth century. Which organisation had their authority over Antioch and Port Simon revoked by Pope Just in 1208, thus saving them from said debacle?

6. The Deviance movement has been credited with contributing to the decline of the Modest Arrangement through the first half of the nineteenth century, particularly in Gaul and Spain. To what extent is this narrative supported by the evidence?
