104.5: An Authorial Assortment

brief references for some writers previously mentioned in my worldbuilding notes.

Olexi Harchenko (fl. c19) was a Zahid Russian [1] author from the city of Boxa [~Novi Sad, Serbia]. He wrote the widely-translated mystery work –Ý–∞–∑–≥–∞–¥—Ç–æ (D√©nouement), whose title was borrowed into Italian as rasgatto "resolution, unravelling of a mystery", and from there into many European languages.

Thoffina'h Girmont (fl. 2005) is a British author who wrote Arthurian romance Y √êreic o Redr…Øth (The Dragon of Redrouth). The book is loosely based on the old tales codified in eleventh-century England (and reworked in post-Imperia Wales), but owes much more to the ahistorical reimaginings of the Long Peace [~1800] period.

Heudar Fi√∞aut (fl. c14) was a Borlish writer who is an important source of vernacular Middle Borlish texts. His most famous work is Catre√∞ejnt Lagrem (Twenty-four Laments); his works together contain many first attestations of Borlish words (for example, gra√ßant "merciful").

Tanasio Capretti (fl. 1970) was a theologian and humanist at the University of Florence. He wrote sociological work L'Inclusion Deviante di Conti Polari (The Deviance Incorporation of Folk Tales) to commemorate the 120th anniversary of the 1850 Laic Declarations of Belgrade, a landmark accord for secular governance in many polities.

Yollamaniza Elcabir (fl. 1943) was a Mashick [~Mexican] author and one-time spy. He wrote the spycraft novel La te Hozuho Sineton (No Slumber Will Seize Him) in 1943 partly based on his own experiences, in the aftermath of the Lovac Wars and the bombing of his home city of Moshtar [~San Francisco]. His writings were revealing enough that governments across Gulf Mendeva [Mexico Gulf states] issued warrants for his arrest.

---

[1] A Slavic ethnic group present in the northern Balkans and descended from westward-migrating immigrant communities.

105.1: Parachthony

excerpt in translation on the topic of parachthony (speculative fiction/sci-fi and fantasy).

Y siecr novo√ß a pla√ß meg intermane un nazir na√∞usc cas art parachthon.
The mid-nineteenth century was somewhat of a nadir for parachthon art.

Roman√ß desmanne√ßa vars y jagat cojanessem de masquira e cont de kiveu, cant resorgen√ß historic hamtan y scen mondial.
Romance moved on to masquira and herdtale's more mundane settings, while historical revivals populated the world's stages.

Cosogr y pluottan√ß de spai√∞oir ag siecr veint en√ßau nos a l'incorpora√ßon volonter veir de morn√ß parachthon afont de stoir allors, com cont d'amour e√∞ enigma;
With the maturation of genres in the twentieth century we begin to see purposeful melding of parachthon elements in stories of other sorts, like lovetales and √¶nigmata;

l'oc tan e√∞ y sousun√ßon de barstant comportant com Futurism ramenaurn parachthony alche√∞r jug nell'euta√∞.
this and the incorporation of related movements like Futurism brought parachthony back into popular art for good.   

Y defas de machina√ßon er mout ammont par ouvr parachthon, e donc sonc plusour nell'onomatoir or√∞enjan√ß modern nommant aun lorrell'orn ec destr.
The progress of technology was often presaged by parachthon works, and so many items of vocabulary for modern contrivances have their origin there.

Com bustr, y 'sar pistol' vien colombant des y fougl dy trevold d'an 1968 'Kenopleus', par Ashford Pasquier.
As an example, the 'pistol mesh' is lifted from the pages of Ashford Pasquier's 1968 novel 'Starsail'.

105.3: √∞onc "crooked"

##

105.5: Astraphor

Astraphor (also called barric force) is the set of phenomena associated with the presence and movement of plentiful substances, or the thrift that one can harness from such movement.

Etymology

The word 'astraphor' was coined in the seventeenth century to describe the theoretical source of the sparks certain substances (amber, fur, etc.) would create in darkness after being rubbed. It derives from Ancient Greek roots ·ºÄœÉœÑœÅ·æ∞œÄŒÆ (astrap·∏ó) 'lightning' and -œÜœåœÅŒøœÇ (-ph√≥ros) "bearing"; although the expected form would be 'astrapophor', even in the earliest texts we see only the shorter word (possibly due to transcription error).

The modifying form 'barric' derives from parallel work, and originates in Arabic ÿ®Ÿéÿ±ŸíŸÇ‚Å© (barq) "lightning".

History

The deep theoretical connection between astraphor and light was explored thoroughly in the mid-nineteenth century; the unimaginatively titled magnum opus Astraphor and Light by Merch theorist Wexter Hathwall managed to fully characterise the mechanics of astraphor in a single equation.

At this time, the earlier flag-lamp steeplemesh was in the process of being displaced by barric meshes. Places without a pre-existing extensive flag-lamp system were in many cases faster to adopt the barric mesh; for example, by 1870 the world's longest wired post line on land spanned the length of Lower Mendeva.

The first undersea barric lines were laid in the 1850s in Kent (Dover to Cales), and from Texel in Willemy to Axbane in Borland, each distances of less than twenty leagues. By the following century hundreds of such lines crossed the world's oceans.

In the 1950s, the Mortar coalition in Portingale funded research into using threshold force to produce astraphor so as to reduce dependence on imports from the Drengot Collusion; by the 1960s the polity was a world pioneer in constructing threshold mills, becoming a net exporter of astraphor in 1971.

Or√∞enjan√ß barric plusour dell'epoc d'Atelier jagerrem smargoy des Mendeva Bassem, deut plausibr a reclam addo√ßmental cas y respanment rapid d'acca√ß filer (no sceint pog adop√ßon paravant dy sar a gonfan e lucern por deslocar ny region).

##

106.5: Axbane

Axbane (Borlish Ausbagn /ozÀàb…õjn/) is a major city on the southwest coast of Borland. It is located at the narrowest part of the Rustigh Strait, less than twenty leagues from Willemy on mainland Europe.
Toponymy

Both the English and Borlish names descend from (the dative case of) the original Latin name for the city Aquƒ´s Balneƒ´s "to the bathing waters". Compare the city of Bath in Albion and of  (Grand-Aix /g ÄaÃÉÀàd…õis/, Aughen /Àà…î.xnÃ©/) [Aachen] in Willemy.
History

As a Roman city, Axbane was home to an extensive bathhouse complex, for which the city is named.

With the German migrations of the early medieval period, Axbane was demoted to a minor port in the Anglian kingdom of Sothbar, which comprised the southwestern portion of Borland.

The Peninsular Monastery which overlooks the city was erected in the name of the Order of Saint James (the Jacobin Fraternity), which had fled to Axbane in 1067 after a popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffrey II in Flanders. In the modern day, the Order remains a powerful presence, owning much land in the city and a successful series of breweries.

The Axbane of the late twelfth century is the de facto seat of the County of Sothbar; by 1190 the earl Steven (Steign) is known to have resided year-round in the coastal town, bequeathing the old house in Heitri√ß to his younger brother. At this time, the location of Axbane on one side of the Rustigh Strait was of immense strategic value. The Great Flie Flood of 1171 swept away much of Lower Frisland, and Axbane was for several decades afterward the obvious choice of harbour for any trader moving from the Danish lands through the Drengot domain and onward to Spain.

Axbane eventually became one of the star cities of the Stadbund, an association of mercantile cities in the region. The throughput of the Axbane port is thought to have accounted for over a third of European trade outside the Middlesea by 1400 N. The late fourteenth century also saw this Borlish city develop a particularly close relationship with Bremen.

The 1850s saw the Axbane-Texel undersea barric line, one of the world's first such steeplepost connections across the sea. At this time, Axbane was part of the New Urban Mesh along with independent cities like London.

References

    The Secret Guide to Axbane (Y Gi√∞ Scos por Ausbagn), published first in 1962 on behalf of the Maglen Formeu√ß Caritativia, as part of a series of tourist guidebooks for cities along both coasts of the Rustigh Strait.

##

107.5: Grimmers

A grimmer (Merch /Ààg…æ…™.m…ô/, Borlish grimmour /g Ä…™Ààmu Ä/), formerly and formally an augrimmer, is a device that can be given methods to carry out sequences of arithmetic or logical operations. These methods enable grimmers to perform a wide range of tasks. Grimmers are the substrate of the pistol mesh, which links together billions of such devices.

Calculating devices from the earliest abaci prefigure the grimmer, but its true potential (the ability to perform essentially arbitrary computation, given the correct instructions) arose from theoretic discussions on the possibility of self-replication. In the eighteenth-century, Rachel Desselut argued that in order for living matter to duplicate, its "Form" must contain a readable "Method" describing its construction. In this way she predicted the form-method separation essential to modern grimmer operation.

Etymology

The word grimmer is a clipping of augrimmer, literally "one who calculates, one who performs mathematical computation"; the word referred to a profession until the mid-twentieth century. This derives from noun augrim "computation, calculation", which originally meant "calculation using Arabic numerals" in particular, and which was a loan via French augrisme from Medieval Latin algorismus. This reflects the name of Persian mathematician al-Khwarismi, whose works introduced the positional number system to Europe.

References

Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, 1743 philosophical treatise by noblewoman Rachel Desselut

The Grimmer Hall: Developments in Early Methodics, 1999 scholastic quire written by New Leudong historian and methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin

108.1: Sandrine

Cossy sceint, frugt sta conserva√∞ par mejan lorry matier sandrinal eliminant (com bustr, par boglir ben beno√ß de vas ne cuivr) e donc mo√∞naut sofren de scurvog to√∞ cas, ig colluy indugnt vars plu frugt bacagettar enter.

Un grap theoretic cos vray nole avenir pre siecr setto√ß, candon sceye descovart par bonaventur ig laprot guy poun doutr scurvog accarr (human e guy son soltumnesc parmy l'≈ìcumen animal cas noc po√∞ir sandrin detaxionnar cos autonom des √ßucar).
Truly theoretic understanding would not come until the seventeenth century, when it was discovered by chance that the guy-coney can also develop scurvy (humans and guys are rare among animals in not creating sandrine in our bodies from sugar).

L'oc parmettoy experien√ß fort scrutiniosessem e√∞ expedi√∞essem sur guy, por y bon ploghan√ß de frugt e leum discernir par effectualta√∞ cas scurvog co√Ør.
This allowed for much more rigorous and time-efficient testing on guys, to determine what preparations of fruits and vegetables were effective at preventing scurvy.

108.5: Tommarth

Tommarth (Merch /Ààt…î.m…ôŒ∏/) is the capital and largest city of Markland. It had a population of over 5 million people by the year 2010. The dialect of Merch spoken by many in Tommarth is widely considered a standard to emulate.

The area was settled by the Angles in the sixth century; by the ninth it had become the primary city of the nascent Merch Kingdom. It was fortified significantly over the next centuries in defence against the Welsh to the west and then against the Danes to the East.

In the modern day Tommarth has an extensive underground rail system known locally as the Dusker, with stops including Kibbrough and Cathedral.

Toponymy

The city takes its name from the Tome river on which it stands. Formerly (before the modern spelling reform) spelt Tomwarth, the local area was known in Old English as Tomwor√∞ig "enclosure, field by the Tome."

Institutions

D'Aumesty Tellard Guild (fl. 1849), organisation of cartographers
Lady Maud Orchestra (fl. 1976), professional group of musicians receiving public funding

Royal Tommarth Castinghouse (fl. 1930), publisher of nonfiction which once held great cachet but latterly of no particular merit
Tommarth Open School of History (fl. 1997), known for publishing reference works

Tommarth University (fl. 1976), oldest institution of higher education in the city

People

Laurent Burnows (fl. 2010), author of children's fiction including beloved series Back to Front

Anna Highwater (fl. 1930), nonfiction author collaborating with her husband

Ralph Highwater (fl. 1930), nonfiction author collaborating with his wife

Morency Rivellerson (fl. 1976), clavierman and author of music textbooks

    notes in translation on the Romantic Period (ca. 1640‚Äì1700).

L'epoc Romannesc sta descreut par gran accommission corroga√ßonal. Com bustr, y canal entr Bordeu e Audaz er compli√∞ d'an 1679, l'ocean Atlantic e√∞ y Miterran conjognent lon de√Ør e√∞egl maritim trasir ec domina√∞ par Morrac.
The Romantic period was characterised by great infrastructure projects. For example, the Bordew-Audaz canal was completed in 1679, connecting the Atlantic and the Middlesea without having to pass through Morrack-controlled seas.

Polity e stat vassal plusour survescoirn noc a liesc dy Rix Romannesc; com bustr, y Principalta√∞ de Mystra ne Gre√ß, oc sceint com vassal dy Scon Empir Roman des y siecr on√ß, er cos solemn dissolu√∞ com faughan√ß parmy e√ß dy Rix Romannesc.
Several polities and vassal states did not survive the Romantic Wars; for example, the Principality of Mystra in Greece, which had existed as a vassal of the Second Roman Empire since the eleventh century, was formally dissolved in the aftermath of the Romantic Wars.

Etymology: 1910, backformation from the somewhat-earlier adjective (1904 bo√∞our sobottal "radio transmitter", 1907 ≈ìculux sobottal "radio waves"). The technology having been invented in Hasiny, the word was borrowed indirectly (probably via Vascon) from Hasinic Welsh sobota≈Ç "signal, message". This in turn comes from Jaughta √ßobota "smoke; message", after the practice of sending signals via smoke.

Par y sobot coum nos addoit dy cain√ß.
/par i soÀàb…ît kum n…îz aÀàd…îit di kents/

We heard the news on the radio.

109.5: Guillen daus Sanz

Guillen daus Sanz (b. ca. 1450, Provincial /giÀà éeÃÉj daus s…êÃÉts/) was a strategist in Provence in the years leading up to the War of Provincial Independence, and became a powerful political figure in the decades afterward.

Born a foundling (the surname daus Sanz "of the Saints" was widely given to children in orphanages at the time), he joined the (then-Burgund) army at a young age and rose to the position of general after receiving the patronage of Daniel Menton, Lord Amig√≤t.

It was on the advice of daus Sanz that Lord Amig√≤t was able to secure the aid of King Marc V of France; France had reconciled with Normandy to avoid the tangle that was the Albion Wars of Fealty. With this support, he and several other noble families declared independence from Burgundy on the twenty-first of June, 1497 (which to this day is a national holiday in Provence).

The following quote is popularly attributed to daus Sanz on the eve of the Battle of Marg√®s (1499). It mocks the states of Albion which had been embroiled in the Wars of Fealty for the past two decades.

Perhaps we should have sent for a Kentish witness! They might learn the subtle craft of ending the wars they start.

After Daniel Menton's rule over independent Provence ended in 1517, his cousin Antoin was elected by the Concert under the close attention and martial guidance of daus Sanz.

Fort daus Sanz, a military base in Provence in active use during the mid-twentieth century, was named for Guillen daus Sanz.

Y parceu√ßon dell'epoc Bel-Jou com un asc ant spionna√ß opulent e pisment conjura√ßonal cas politic sou teneur covart no's cos enter digna√∞‚Äîse po√∞ compara√ßon fair entr y parceu√ßon pouvlancer (par mejan de livr e lasc) dell'affer Desarden d'an 1887 un ambasctour Londresc e√∞ y rey a Naccon Day contr y realta√∞ cojannessem.

110.4: n'oc hour "for a while"

- for a while, for a short time, awhile, from now until a point not far in the future;
- for now, provisionally, temporarily, presently, at the moment but likely not for long;
- (sometimes proscribed) soon, imminently, about to occur

##

110.5: Redstone Chair

The Redstone Chair (Merch Redstoan Sete /Àà…æ…õd.stoÀên ÀàsiÀêt/) was a wooden throne used by the monarchies of Albion during the medieval period.

History

The Redstone Chair was fashioned in the late tenth century around a block of sandstone; the name of the carpenter is forgotten. This block was (allegedly) the block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised, ending hostilities between the two polities for almost a century.

Many subsequent monarchs of Albion, and especially of Markland and Northumber, were crowned on the Redstone Chair. During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, most of the Drengot Emperors sat upon the Chair, and as such it was relocated to London. After the Empire's collapse, this placed the Chair in the politically turbulent region between Markland and Kent. Some historians have conjectured that the First Burning of London in 1290 was in part motivated by a plan to acquire the Chair; the responsible party varies in the telling.

According to the traditional account, it was a dispute over the location of the Redstone Chair which sparked the Wars of Fealty, which lasted almost fifty years over the turn of the sixteenth century and which embroiled almost all the polities of Albion.

Unfortunately, the Chair was lost in the tumult of the wars at some point between 1505 and 1509. Despite several searches and at least two hoaxes, the Chair has never been recovered, and the modern understanding of its appearance rests entirely on depictions in a few portraits and coronation scenes.

##

Ne souc dy Gazou Bre√∞onnesc a Santrafeu, yon rumora√∞ er ig y Mastois√®t cos propr soloirn droug entarn, y travoda√∞ er broya√∞ par mejan dy coyen√ß marcer effectua√∞ de Bourgmer Alexandre fi Marco tan con sell'exiglment dy Mastois√®t a verb (e√∞ autr tribu Mendevan a gest).

- guinea pig, mammal of the genus Cavia;

111.4: a gest "de facto"

a gest /a  í…õst/ […êÀà ù…õst]
- de facto, in practice, in fact, regardless of official or legal status;
- actually, in fact, used to introduce a contradictory remark

##

111.5: Battle of Rexam (1483)

The Battle of Rexam (Merch Feight at Rexam /f…õ Ét …ôt Àà…æ…õks…ôm/, Cambric Brwydr Car Vante≈Ç /Ààb…æuÀê.d…ô kaÀê v…ônÀàt…õ…¨/) was a major combat between Markland [~Mercia] and the Kingdom of Britain [~Wales+] which took place in 1483 during the Wars of Fealty.

Rexam (formerly in the Merch language spelt Wrexham) had been a contested town on the border of Wales and Markland almost from the moment the latter was founded. In 1301, in an act which reignited the Mallor Wars, King Andrew II sacked the town.

The relatively-new Kingdom of Britain (formed of Wales and its outlying tallaths [provinces], along with Kernow [Brittany] and Sodrick [Man and the Hebrides]) started from a position of strength, and managed to extract many concessions from Markland in the early years of the wars. This culminated in a decisive victory at the Battle of Rexam, where the last Duke of Chester was killed.

After this battle, Britain was able to take lasting control of the area around Rexam (though the attack on the city of Chester shortly thereafter was rebuffed). The polity then managed to recuse itself from the continued conflicts of the Wars of Fealty; the rest of the polities of Albion would remain embroiled for another thirty years.

The battle and its aftermath were portrayed in 1931 work "Children of the Battle of Rexam" (released originally in Cambric as Pa √êywdon y Plant Car Vante≈Ç "What the Children of Car Vantel Said"), a fictionalised account of the three orphaned children of the Duke of Chester written by schoolteacher Jon Paldreth.

10. What was the name of the first Chinese ship to alight in the Novomund?

113.4: souvolar "sweep"

souvolar /Àåsu.voÀàlar/ [Àåsu.v äÀàl…êÀê]
- sweep, be swept, to be caused to rise up into the air from the ground (as of the clothing of a person spinning, or of dust);

114.2: xivol "waltz"

xivol /ksiÀàv…îl/ [ks…™Ààv…îl]
- waltz, xivola, a style of dance originating in Vascony during the Long Peace (1770‚Äì1815) characterised by triple time and a rapid tempo;
- metaphorically, the state of many people simultaneously carrying out independent tasks and moving around each other without getting in each other's way

Etymology: via French xivole from Vascon x√¨vola /Àà Éi.vo.la/ "waltz". This itself is a borrowing from the earlier and slightly different Italian dance scivola "waltz, word stressed on the third-from-last syllable", from verb scivolare "glide, slip". This is plausibly a descendant of Latin exsƒ´bil≈ç "I hiss forth".

Although the original xivola dance had a rather restrained tempo, the average performance speed of xivola music has greatly increased over time. Xivola is usually conceived of as a crowd dance, in which an entire roomful of people participates.

##

114.5: On the Bookshelf

In Sickness and in Health: Medicine in pre-Arnoutszen Europe is a quire chronicling the development of medical practice in Europe from antiquity until the time of physician Laurz Arnoutszen in the sixteenth century (the adoption of whose techniques constituted a sea-change in the effectiveness of medicine). It was written by Andrew Knowles in the mid-twentieth century. It was published in Kent by Winchester Scholastic Primers in multiple editions; the second edition was released in 1961 with a foreword by Dr Laura Norris, a lecturer in Heredity at Canterburgh University.

New Skies, Old Stories (originally published in Provincial French as Nov√® Cial, Anzan Conte) is a work of popular history detailing the origins of the various threads of parachthon fiction. It was written by Simon d'Estiala and published by Prindor Marsill√®s in 1980. Its thesis is that many of the tropes often considered to have been invented by particular parachthon authors are in fact simply examples of existing tropes used in new settings.

Men in Black (originally published in Parisian French as Hommes en Noir: une √âtoire d'Espionnage) is a layperson's account of the history of spycraft across the world. It was written by Agathe Bisset and published by Paris University Editions in 2003.

The Alphabetical Albion is an encyclopaedia of the political history of Albion. It was written by a consortium of authors including Lucas Osborne and published by Winchester Scholastic Primers in 1920. The book organises itself into twenty-six sections, one for each letter of the English alphabet in order, with varying levels of contortion required to justify the conceit. Headings include "F: The Wars of Fealty", "Q: Unanswered Questions" and "X: The Necessity of Xenia".

1500: The length in leagues of Japetos III, the new Atlantic link laid down between Morrack and Ambrosia which conveyed its first messages this last Wednesday. 115.5: Turbulent Times

collected notes on various events and periods which took place at the time of the Long Peace (ca. 1770‚Äì1815).

The Decadence of Belgrade refers to the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube] in its final decades before the Second German War (1815‚Äì23) and the subsequent dissolution of the state. The Decadence also represents the territorial maximum of the kingdom, having wrested Bulgary [Bulgaria] from the suzerainty of the Second Roman Empire [~the Byzantines] after the latter's final collapse in the seventeenth century, and more recently having consolidated control over lands at the northwestern border with Bavarn [Bavaria].

The Mendevan Unmooring (Borlish Deshormejan√ß Mendevan /deÀåz…îr.meÀà íants Àåm…õn.deÀàvan/) is the collective name for the various wars and diplomatic developments at the turn of the nineteenth century which led to the weakening and sometimes severing of formal ties of polities in Mendeva [North America] from those in the Vetomund [Old World]. The more explosive conflicts such as the War for Ambrosia [~east and north of the Great Lakes] and the Hasinic Revolution [~lower part of the Mississipi watershed] (against Vascony and Britain, respectively) often overshadow quieter events like the Departure of Mashick [~Mexico].

The Romantic Revival (Borlish Resorgen√ß Romannesc /reÀàz…îr.g…õnts Àåro.maÀàn…õx/) was an artistic and political movement which arose towards the end of the eighteenth century. It was characterised by optimistic portrayals of heroism, and especially a belief in a 'brighter past' in the seventeenth century that could be recaptured. It is due to this movement's depiction that a loose congeries of wars in the mid-seventeenth century have become known as the Romantic Wars.

116.3: ve√∞rvent "vane"

- vane, weathervane, weathercock, a  mechanical device rotating around one axis and attached to an elevated object such as a roof for showing the direction of the wind;

##

116.5: Kernovan Conjuring

The Kernovan Conjuring (Borlish Conjura√ßon Cornovan /Àåk…î…≤. í…™Ààrants Àåk…îr.noÀàvan/) was a collaboration between the Kingdom of Greater Devon and Roger Drengot, Duke of Normandy to press the Duke's claim to the throne of Kent. This plot is considered the proximate cause for the creation of the First Drengot Empire across Gaul [France] and southern Albion [Great Britain].

The Conjuring was carried out in 1029, following the death of King Rodulf of Kent. Kent under the long-reigning Rodulf had prospered, with recognised supremacy over the rump Wessex (albeit not uncontested by Devon) and over Essex to the north. After eight decades of political manoeuvring, Rodulf lingered on his deathbed and the succession was deeply uncertain.

Although no references to a written treaty have survived, it is presumed that the House of Kernow aided Normandy in their bid for the Kentish throne in exchange for support in becoming the new ruling dynasty of Greater Devon. This bargain was upheld, but it only took a couple of decades for Greater Devon to become a vassal of the First Drengot Empire.

In what was either impressive long-term strategy or merely opportunism, the victorious Roger of Normandy would go on to conquer Kent's neighbours, including Essex and Markland, and then venture south to annex much of the rest of Gaul. He was crowned Emperor Roger I in the midst of this territorial expansion in 1038.

The recently-unified Wales under Cadwallan (erstwhile King of Wynneth) managed to remain mostly free of Drengot control. This was likely achieved by steering a course between Greater Devon and the Drengot, neither of whom wanted to other to have dominion over Wales.

## 

117.1: New Urban Mesh

notes in translation on the subject of the New Urban Mesh.

Y Nou Sar Urban intermane un alleyan√ß de cita√∞ autonom na√∞usc n'Europ nor√∞ovester durant y siecr novo√ß.
The New Urban Mesh was an alliance of somewhat autonomous cities in Northwest Europe in the nineteenth century.

Parmy sy sodal stan Londr, Conster, Ausbagn, Pentros, Amstel, Anvers e Gant.
Among its members were London, Conster [Colchester], Axbane, Pentrose, Amstel [Amsterdam], Anvers [Antwerp] and Gant [Ghent].

Y Sar smargoy cos contemporane e parallel a apprestan√ß politic comportant allors, tal com y Cita√∞ Floyan lonc y Danau.
The Mesh emerged contemporaneously with similar political structures elsewhere, such as the Riverine Cities along the Danaw [Danube].

Plusour parmy y cita√∞ ag Sar stan cos simoltane part de polity ja sceint (com bustr, Ausbagn e Pentros no stan jamay sovran a verb deur Istr Boral).
Several of the Mesh's cities were simultaneously part of pre-existing polities (for example, Axbane and Pentrose were never legally independent from Borland).

L'oc ort de tal federa√ß de stat cita√∞er se caf ascreut all'a√∞oin√ß pouvlancer e√∞ y crescen√ß de cita√∞ rapid tras l'approism dell'epoc all'Atelier Mondial.
This rise of such federacies of city-states has been attributed to the rapid population growth and expansion of cities in the run-up to the Global Workshop period.

Y Sar cos particuler sta ag rostr de machina√ßon cas vascel costroin√ß, e√∞ i po√∞e un ron√ß remarcabr cavir dy recoglen√ß de carbon mondial.

117.2: mallovicher "na√Øve"

mallovicher /Àåma.lo.viÀàk…õr/ [Àåma.l ä.v…™Ààk…õÀê]
- na√Øve, rose-tinted, ingenuous, overly optimistic or blind to obvious pitfalls and downsides;
- chipper, bubbly, pert, peppy, cheerful in an annoying way

Etymology: 19C, adjectival derivation from mallovic "someone who is na√Øve and cheerful to a ridiculous or irritating extent". This is a borrowing from synonymous Merch mallowick or directly from the character Mall-Lowick /Ààmal Ààlu.…™k/ and her eponymous novel, in which she is a young girl subjected to various travails but never losing her hopeful demeanour. Her name is a compound of Mall, a hypocoristic form of Mary, and Lowick, which comes via Old French Lo√Øc from Frankish Hlo√∞wig (cf. Vascon Luixa and Napolitan Ludgia [and IRL Louise]). The name was applied almost immediately in a mocking way to a person seen as similar to Mall-Lowick.

J'osc sy cigl novel; i's mallovicher sad demay.
/ íox si tsajl noÀàv…õl | …™z Àåma.lo.v…™Ààk…õr sad deÀàme/
[ ùox si tsajl n äÀàv…õl | …™z Àåma.l ä.v…™Ààk…õÀê sad d…™Ààme]

##

117.5: Sound Changes from Vulgar Latin to Borlish

The dialects of Borlish are the only surviving descendants of the Insular variety of Vulgar Latin, which comprised Borlish Latin and British Latin. This variety was not overly divergent from the contemporary (ca. 3rd to 5th century) Latin of the continent, with the following exceptions:

    The loss of intervocalic  /w/, as evidenced in inscriptions by e.g. clam "key" (continental clavem) and denio "I reach" (continental devenio).
    The loss of wordfinal  /s/, probably preceded by a period of debuccalisation where final  was pronounced /h/. The dating of this sound change is controversial; cf. fifth-century *a femna* "the women (acc.)" (Classical hƒÅs fƒìminƒÅs "these women").

The second of these local changes was grammatically significant, as it weakened the distinctions between forms in noun declensions. Indeed, already by the Old Borlish period all number and case distinctions in nouns have been lost.

    Posttonic vowel loss
    V > ‚àÖ / "VC_CV
    Final syllable simplification
    m > n / _# if stressed
    m s > ‚àÖ / _# otherwise

e.g. amictate /Àåa.mikÀàta.te/ "friendship" < amƒ´citƒÅtem

    Palatalisation part 1
    [t k]j [d g]j > t É d í
    k g > t É d í / _[i e]
    k > j / _C except [r l w j]
    Fortition
    w > Œ≤ /#_
    j > d í / #_, V_V

e.g. iazo /Ààd íat.t Éo/ "I lie down" < iace≈ç

    Consonant lenition part 1
    p t k b d g s > b d g Œ≤ √∞ …£ z / V_[V m l r n j w]
    Palatalisation part 2
    nj lj > …≤  é
    Cj > C ≤ > jC except w or word initially
    Final vowels reduce to schwa (unless stressed)
    V > …ô / _# unless stressed

e.g. sabe /Ààsa.b…ô/ "he knows" < sapit; saibe /Ààsaj.b…ô/ "I know" < sapi≈ç

    Weak consonants lost
    [Œ≤ √∞ …£] > ‚àÖ / V_V
    Mid-vowels break in stressed open syllables
    …õ e …î o > je e o ow / stressed open
    …õ …î > e o / elsewhere
    Lenition part 2
    p t k b d g > b d g Œ≤ √∞ …£ / V_V
    C: > C
    Loss of much final schwa
    …ô > ‚àÖ / except after consonant clusters

e.g. liei /ljed í/ "halt" < Insular laedicum "collision"

    Affricate shift
    t É d í > ts  í
    sk palatalises (fills gap with  í), Œ≤ shift
    sk Œ≤ >  É v
    Vowel u is fronted
    u > y
    Mid-vowel rhotic shift
    e > a / _rC
    e > i / _r#
    Full loss of schwa
    …ô > ‚àÖ
    Epenthesis before r
    ‚àÖ > d / [n …≤]_r
    ‚àÖ > b / m_r

e.g. cendre /Ààtsen.drÃ©/ "ash" < cinerem; pu√∞ir /pyÀà√∞ir/ "rot" < p≈´tƒìre

    Vowels become lax in closed syllables
    i e o y > …™ …õ …î  è / closed syllables
    Final consonants devoice
    b d g v z √∞  í > p t k f s Œ∏  É / _#
    l-vocalisation and y-diphthong shift
    [l y] > w / _[C #]
    Front vowels merge in weak position
    […õ …™  è …õj] > …™ / #_sC
    […õ …™  è] > …õ / _w[C #]
    Diphthong shift part 1
    ai …õi …™i > e i …ôj
    aw …õw …îw > o …ôw u
    Palatals and …£ are lost (regenerating some diphthongs)
    …≤  é > n l / #_
    …≤  é …£ > jn jl j

e.g. aut /ot/ "had" < awt < ayt < ayd < aud < aÀàŒ≤u.d…ô < aÀàbu.t…ô < aÀàbu.to < Vulgar *hab≈´tus;
ismargre /…™sÀàmar.grÃ©/ "arise" < …õjsÀàmar.gr…ô < ejsÀàmer.gr…ô < …õksÀàm…õr.gre < Vulgar *exmergere

This is the stage, around the turn of the thirteenth century, usually referred to as late Old Borlish:

    ‚Ä¶sovravnt il deft nostre sa»ùnta√∞esem e√∞ atavalesem n i»ù atrevre golfhavn se»ù hamar dont y verb divin ismetre ac povre paian. pe√∞ev soul ez font istovent por vn nov cli»ùs d istroienz istablir‚Ä¶
    /soÀàvront …™l d…õwt ÀànostrÃ© Àåsajn.taÀà√∞…õs…õm …õ√∞ aÀåta.vaÀàl…õs…õm n…ôj aÀàtr…ôwrÃ© g…îlfÀàhon sej haÀàmar d…înt i v…õrb diÀàv…™n …™sÀàme.trÃ© ak ÀàpovrÃ© paÀà ían ‚Äñ peÀà√∞…ôw sul ets f…înt …™s.toÀàv…õnt p…îr yn nu cl…ôjs d…™sÀàtr…îj…ônts is.taÀàbl…™r/
    ‚Ä¶uphold our most sacred and ancient duty to let Gulfhaven be the centre from which we will send the Word of God to pagan lands. We ask only for the necessary funds for a new teachinghouse‚Ä¶

Moving into the Middle Borlish period:

    Rhotacisation of syllabic l
    l > r / C_# except [j w]_#

This gives Borlish its signature pairs like capabr "competent"; capablessem "more competent".

    Aph√¶resis of initial isC
    …™ > ‚àÖ / #_sC

Like IRL Italian, this means that many words gain an initial vowel only to lose it a millennium later: Latin stannum "tin" > Old Borlish istan "tin" > Modern Borlish stan "tin". However, this process also deletes initial vowels that were there from the start: Latin historia "story" > Old Borlish istoir "story" > Modern Borlish stoir "story".

    Break-up of sr clusters and hiatus
    ‚àÖ > t / s_r
    ‚àÖ > v / […î o u]_V
    uv > ov

The second of these rules is particularly interesting, since it regenerates some <v> which had been lost in the time of Insular Latin. For example, Latin novellus "new" had Insular form noellus "new" > Old Borlish noel "new" > Modern Borlish novel "new"

    Front rounded vowels are lost
    y  è > i …™

This provides Borlish with another of its signature features, namely <u> /i …™/: tu /ti/ "you (sg)"; prun /pr…™n/ "plum". The loss of front rounded vowels led to some homophonous pairs like lun lin /l…™n/ "moon; flax". In many cases this led to one of the pair falling out of use (in this case the latter was replaced with the competing form linsc).

    Voicing of final s
    s > z / _#

The precise timing of this sound change is hard to determine, since it does not interact with any nearby sound changes. Likely this originated as a sandhi effect before a following vowel-initial word, e.g. tu es un /ti …õ‚Äøz…™n/ "you are a...", before being extended to all contexts.

    Diphthong shift part 2
    aj ej ij > …õj i aj
    …ôj …ôw > aj aw

Borlish's answer to the IRL Great Vowel Shift, these changes are somewhat more modest (and take place a little later, between 1600 and 1800). In combination with the loss of front rounded vowels, however, these changes determine much of Borlish's least intuitive orthographic behaviour. For example, we have muy /maj/ "crumb", where <uy> underwent the changes /yj > ij  > aj/.

The previous shift reflects the outcomes in the Damvath Standard dialect of Borlish; these recent changes differ greatly in applicability across the island. We clos with an 1800s sound shift:

    Late consonant backing
     É r > x  Ä

The latter of these appears to be part of a general areal phenomenon of backing rhotics. A single word reflecting both these changes is scuir /xaj Ä/ "boy".

118.1: London

notes in translation on the subject of the city of London.

Posc un liesc com cita√∞ seconder n'Albion, Londr redenoy alche√∞r y cafloy imperial ny region durant y Scon Tetrarchy ne sona d'un part dy Preu Empir Drengo√ßan.
After time as a secondary city in Albion, London became the imperial capital of the region once more during the Second Tetrarchy as part of the First Drengot Empire.

Y Preu √êurn√ß a Londr fis loy d'an 1290; ag mendrem 400 hom ern occis deut ag catastroph d'un semman cohernt.
The First Burning of London took place in 1290; at least 400 people were killed in the ensuing week-long disaster.

L'oc no sta for un parmy conflagra√ßon plusour ny preu demy dy scon millenner, bempart dec y cita√∞ se cou cos sa√ßonner concas des y nor√∞ ben y sou√∞.
This was one of several conflagrations in the first half of the second millennium, many due to the city being perennially conquered from the north or south.

Posc l'e√ß Rix Democratic a pla√ß meg ny siecr ogto√ß yemoy Londr cos final un autonomy durabr deur sy pa√Øs vecin d'an 1749 sou Quen Margaret, po√∞ent sy ca√∞en ancestral ligar ag lina√ß reyal de Marclont e Cant y dou. L'offi√ß de Segr Bourgmer remane possent cos politic a√∞ief por hast pare√Ør coll'o√ß dy nou monarch, interim. 
The Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century saw London finally gain lasting independence from its neighbours in 1748 under Queen Margaret, who could trace her ancestry to the royal families of both Markland and Kent. The office of Lord Mayor remained politically powerful enough to rival that of the new monarch, however.

118.2: rostr "prow"

rostr /Ààr…î.strÃ©/ [Àà Ä…î.st…ê]
- prow, bow, the front part of a ship or boat;
- vanguard, the leading members of a convoy of travellers or the leading part of a vehicle (especially of a train);
- (in extended use) forefront, leader, the front part of anything and especially in metaphor to denote the earliest adopters of a technology or custom;
- nib, the point of a pen or similar tool;
- tip, any pointed extremity

Etymology: an uncomplicated descendant from Latin r≈çstrum "bill, beak, snout, muzzle; prow". The latter meanings are thought to derive from the anatomical senses of the Latin word. Extension from boats to other vehicles and then into metaphorical usage occured during the Middle Borlish period.

118.3: balis "beacon"

- beacon, signal fire, a warning light to communicate some message (especially in a military context);

118.4: soun√ß "release"

##

118.5: Hasiny

Hasiny is the largest and most populous of the polities of British Mendeva. This refers to the region of Mendeva which have undergone significant settlement from or otherwise been significantly influenced by the Kingdom of Britain.

Hasiny is centred on the lower parts of the watershed of the Bahassassin River, but its lands extend sufficiently far northeast so as to share a short border with New Provence along part of the Sturgovan range. It borders the polity of Taisha to the west, and its capital city is Nadacow, which lies on the Bahassassin.

The name Hasiny derives from the confederation of the same name, which extended across lands which are now part of Hasiny and Taisha.

The languages of administration in Hasiny are Jaughta (Jaht√°, the Mendevan language spoken by a majority of the populace) and the local variety of Welsh (which differs in important ways from the dialects of British or Chrysian Welsh).

Hasiny was the homeland of the herdtale genre of fiction, which sought to depict a rural idyll with a focus on animal husbandry.

The resort town of Y Davarn Heul (The Sun Hotel) was constructed in the nineteenth century for Hasiny's ruling Tamaha dynasty, gaining such fame that the word "davarn" has entered many languages with the meaning "grand hotel, resort".

The first radio was built in Hasiny in 1903. The fourth Lineball Global Tourney (LGT) in 1935 took place in Hasiny, the first time it was held in Mendeva.

People
Bayac Cosœâteg (fl. c19), diarist and political agitator
Kjartan Rhysvor (fl. 1889), author of spycraft work While Embers Cool (Pan Oera'r Glo)
King Maldwen Tamaha (fl. 1950), long-reigning monarch
Pe√∞r Tamaha (fl. 1950), bon-vivant prince notorious for perennial debt troubles
Cadœâgan Torriœâr (fl. 1890), author of masquira work Men of Jet and Diamond (Dynnon o Vuhu√∞ a Diamont)

119.1: Libya

collected notes in translation on the polity of Libya.

Durant y Scon Tetrarchy loy fignt ny scon millenner primal, Libya (y nom temporane Ifricia dignant) sta un admiralta√∞ majour ny Calipha√∞ Sengr, regna√∞ de Tunis.
During the Second Tetrarchy of the early second millennium, Libya (under the name Ifricia) was an important admiralty of the Single Caliphate, ruled from Tunis.

Tandic prepondran√ß imperial n'Europ e n'Afric nor√∞er inceveu sy dissolu√ßon, tan cossy y Calipha√∞ Sengr; un admiralta√∞ autonom ne Libya er depos d'an 1237 (a veg ceu temporane coll'admiralta√∞ Morrou nell'ovest).
As imperial hegemony in Europe and northern Africa began to fall apart, so did the Single Caliphate; an independent admiralty in Libya was declared in 1237 (at roughly the same time as the Morrove admiralty to the west).

Noc obstroint ig y flottisc Libyan n'er ja commar√ßant cos direct ag Novomont ny siecr se√ß, mo√∞naut des y region stan frequent arma√∞ par y flottisc marcander de Morrac e (cos particuler) de Proven√ß.
Although Libya's navy was not yet trading directly with the Novomund in the sixteenth century, seamen from the region were commonly employed in the merchant fleets of Morrack and (especially) of Provence.

119.3: a squid "on the take"

a squid /a skw…™d/ [a skw…™d]

119.4: rubic "sporty"

rubic /riÀàb…™k/ [ Ä…™Ààb…™k]
- pertaining to sports;
- sporty, athletic, inclined to physical activity;
- competitive, cutthroat, eager to win against others

Etymology: a 19C backformation (based on analogy to the adjectival -ic ending) from rubicon "sports, athletic activity". This itself is an 18C folk-etymological form (evoking the idiom y Rubicon trasir "to pass the point of no return; to start a fight"), possibly via a French or English language, from Willem Dutch robicong "competition, sport, athletics". This is a compound of rob "fight, contest, brawl" and iken "to be compared, compete".

Le scey tojorn quondant con un √ßod rubic.

119.5: Seventeenth Century Studies

shortform questions lifted from a 2024 scitation paper for the course Details of the Seventeeth Century (Y Siecr Setto√ß a Sgart Jusser), an avenue of concurrence history offered by the Jacobin University of Axbane.

1. Which groups were expelled from Moscat in the Ibadine Imamate in 1652, and which local power would go on to seize the city some months later?

2. Which two rivers of Gaul were connected by a canal for the first time in 1679, in a bid to connect the Atlantic to the Middlesea without having to pass through Morrack-controlled seas?

3. Where was the treaty of 1621 which outlined the legal relationship of Santrafew to the local Mendevan peoples signed?

4. For how long did Emperor Carivasary of Tavancy rule until his suspected assassination in 1660?

5. Which future monarch of Barcelon spent several years of his childhood in Cordin as a ward of the Borlish royal family during the 1660s and 1670s?

6. Which power attempted to invade Borland in the seventeenth century, leading to the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632?

7. The terms "copperplate" and "tinplate" in political theory originate in David Bensa√≠d's treatise The Three Cities; he coins these terms in his description of the court of which seventeenth-century monarch of Libya?

8. What astronomical event of 1677 inspired theorists from across the world to undertake long journeys, including the Danish astronomers Gudmund and Rebecca Scrander's storied voyage to the Seldivan court?

9. What did the invention of the timehold in the mid-seventeenth century allow people to measure accurately for the first time?

10. The trade mesh of which Indian polity stretched from Sant Adrian to Cathay by the end of the seventeenth century?

120.2: ne fousc "broke"

ne fousc /ne fux/ [n…™Ààfux]
- broke, penniless, destitute, having no money;
- bankrupt, ruined, in a position of not having enough money to pay one's debts

Etymology: nineteenth-century idiom literally meaning "in (a) pit". The noun fousc "pit, hole in the ground; trap, snare, pitfall" descends with meaning pracatically unchanged via Old Borlish fouz /fut É/ from fovea in Latin. The coventionalised intensifier for this idiom is ar√∞ "steep", which has been analogised to other expressions for poverty; cf. povr ar√∞ "very poor".

120.5: Borland before Borlish

excerpt from Borland before Borlish (Istr Boral pre Borallesc), a history textbook published in 1983 though the New Vithor University.

‚Ä¶discussion of the Kelt peoples who lived on Borland before the Roman conquests of the Flavian period is limited by our lack of written sources. The few inscriptions we do have are mostly translations of placenames and the names of tribes in Roman sources, appearing since the onset of diplomacy and trade with Rome since Julius Caesar's expeditions to Britannia in the first century aN.

One important exception is the name the Borland Kelts appear to have used collectively to distinguish themselves from the Britons: the Bora Ale. There has been speculation for centuries that the name Borrhaloi (which the Greek explorer Pytheas gives alongside Bretannike, and which became Latin BorƒÅlƒìs) is of Kelt origin, and not merely a derivative of Greek ŒíŒøœÅœÅ·æ∂œÇ "the North Wind, the North". The 1932 unearthing at the Livaucr site of a burial marker reading IANTUMOR ABONDUU ALE "Jantumarus of the Abonduvii" proved that ales (pl. alƒì, cognate with Welsh ail "second", Latin alius "other") was used locally to mean "people, tribe".

Hence, Bora Ale is reconstructed as meaning "people of the East", with bora(g) "East, sunrise" corresponding to Welsh bore "morning" and Irish m√°rah "tomorrow". It may even have originated as a exonym applied by the Britons, which would explain the hypothesised sense development of a word which originally meant "others".

Of course, the Borland Kelts did not always conceive of themselves as an indivisible group, and over a dozen tribal names are attested‚Ä¶

##

121.5: Pluralisation

Final /-s/ is lost really early on in the development of Borlish; it's one of the very first sound changes that distinguishes it from the mainland Romance languages, occuring by around 500 AD. This, along with the loss of the genitive case (replaced with the ablative) makes the noun declension of typical nouns look like this:
Image

Notice that masculine nouns typically have only two forms, and feminine nouns only three. Once final vowels also get neutralised to schwa (and eventually lost entirely), the noun paradigm for regular nouns has completely collapsed.

A few nouns briefly retain an irregular paradigm of the following shape, with a separate nominative singular:
Image

However, by the late Old Borlish period these have all been levelled to (usually) the oblique form; sour "sister" is the only counterexample where instead the nominative singular survives.

Several strategies for marking noun number were innovated during the Old Borlish period, mostly involving demonstratives like ille, iste and ipse. However, the forms that survive to the present derive from the demonstrative hic "this". Specifically, we have:

    oc "singular" < hoc, neuter singular
    ec "plural" < haec, neuter plural

In the late Old Borlish period we see the redevelopment of a proximate-distal contrast, with new distal demonstratives formed using the adverb i "there" (since lost; cf. Modern Borlish la < illƒÅc):

    o√ß < oc i "that" < hoc ibi
    e√ß < ec i "those" < haec ibi

Coda: Some Modern Borlish Names

There are a few Celtic names which survive in modern Borlish, for various reasons.

A very few names entered Borland Latin from the local Celtic during the Roman Empire. As a possible example, the male name Ivocq is thought to reflect Borland Celtic *iwok- "like a yew tree".

More Celtic names enter Borlish at various stages since the medieval period. One of the earliest is the female name Jonnovar, cognate with Guinevere and Jennifer, and which is also used as a common noun meaning "butterfly".

##

122.5: Vascon Ascendancy

    collected notes on the period of Western European history known as the Vascony Ascendancy.

The Vascon Ascendancy (Vascon: L'Ascenden√ßa Vasconna; Vask: Igualdea Vascoina) refers to the polity of Vascony from the early fifteenth century until the late sixteenth. At this time it enjoyed a territorial maximum due to several successful military conquests, as well as a general increase in prosperity.
Image

(Vascony, 1456 N)

The beginning of the Ascendancy is usually placed at the start of the reign of Ambrose II, who was crowned in 1410. His reign was marked by a succession of wars, both west into northern Portingale (taking all of Galice and even down to Braga), and east into Burgundy (taking the wealthy coastal regions of Tolose and Provence).

Ambrose III "the Ascendant" (for whom the period was retrospectively named) succeeded his grandfather in 1456. He established the New Navarre Enterprise two years later, an expanded merchant fleet with the dual aims of monopolising the trade of North Atlantic fish through the Middlesea and of creating trade ports down the western coast of Africa. This led to greatly increased fishing activity, and it was Vask ships travelling north past Valland [Greenland] that encountered Brasil [Newfoundland] in the 1471 Novomundine Landfall.

Trade with the Novomund, especially once the far more favourable southern route was discovered, enriched Vascony and especially the port towns in Portingale and Leon. Cities flourished, and there was also an outpouring of new artistic techniques from painting to poetry.

The Ascendancy is by most accounts over by the War of the Two Queens in the second half of the sixteenth century. By this time the land of both Portingale and Provence had been lost and the polity was reduced to its heartland. Furthermore, the Second Great Dying killed almost one in seven people in Vascony (although immigration was high enough to offset the loss of population by 1550).
Art

    I am Ambrose (Sou Ambroxo), a 1923 film in Portingale by director Agochino, which is a character study of King Ambrose the Ascendant through his long life.
    The Ascendant (Hwn a Guodo√∞), a 1934 film in Welsh by director Dormaget, which chronicles in particle the years immediately after the 1471 Novomundine Landfall.lm in Welsh by director Dormaget, which chronicles in particle the years immediately after the 1471 Novomundine Landfall.

##

123.5: Dart Geometry

excerpted from Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d'Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa.

‚Ä¶tempting to draw a straight line from Euclid's Elements until the introduction of new instantiations of geometry in Ferenc's pioneering 1819 work Geometri√¶ Inquadrabiles, seeing any developments in the meantime as merely the proving of ever more particular theorems. However, this fails to acknowledge the truly conceptual advancements in the field, which would go on to influence diverse parts of mathematics and theory.

In this section, we chart the development of the "dart method" as an extension of Euclidean geometry. In all the theorems of The Elements, no distinction is made between a line AB and its reverse BA. It is only in the late fifteenth century that scholars like Barclythe and da Treviso begin to assign directions to lines (it is unclear whether the parallel developments at the Hicma Academy in Baghdad are due to direct communication or simply an independent invention).

It was this seemingly trivial addition that enabled geometers to do two important things. First, it brought ablate [negative] quantities into the realm of respectability in European mathematics; although the idea of "less-than-no things" was absurd (nonetheless, ablate numbers were already being used in the account-books of Venise and other trade cities to denote debts), no-one was perturbed by the concept of returning to one's starting point by trdavelling from A to B, and then from B to A. Second, it allowed for stronger ties between geometry and the newly-imported algebra; adding together two directed lines was conceptually as easy as laying two arrows point-to-vane.

In this way, one could translate a geometric statement like "the lines a, b and c form a triangle" into the algebraic equation "a + b = c". Indeed, over the proceeding decades mathematicians worked to render all the proofs of the Elements into this form. Extending the theory to mozate [two-dimensional] shapes took‚Ä¶

##

124.1: Historical Linguistics Exam

excerpt in translation from sample scitation papers for the University of Lester's Faculty of Domain History, published in 1990.

Entrou 5 (trent minut) Argument fair ou ne promo√ßon ben n'opposson cas dou calscon parmy l'ec thes.
Question 5 (thirty minutes) Argue either in favour of or in opposition to any two of the following theses.

(i) Y parol Anglesc 'silver' a un origin incertan‚Äîparol referent all'o√ß matier son accart des un lenga√ß vars un autr tras millenner par mejan de sar commarcer la√∞. L'origin y plausiblessem dell'oc parol (v. sudrabs ne Lithovan, 'asrvae ne Hausan, zirar ne Vasq) com parol trocher es íÄ´íÅçíåù (surpum) n'Acadian ataval.
(i) The word 'silver' is of uncertain origin‚Äîwords referring to the substance have been borrowed between languages for millennia via extensive trade networks. The most likely origin of the word (cf. Lithowan sudrabs, Hausan 'asrvae, Vask zirar) as a trade-word is in ancient Acadian íÄ´íÅçíåù (surpum).

(ii) Y beno√ß de run Sinnesc lonc son, un issar por nom certan e por autr parol d'origin foran des anticta√∞ e tesmogna√∞ ne polity ve√ßinant (v. lettran√ß maniocan primal) des y sie√ßr settem N, caf reconnoscen√ß ne Cathay sou y regn Kitannesc deut a gimmolt volonter por y polity sevrar deur sell'e√ß ve√ßin sou√∞er.

124.2: entr hugt e gavisc "torn"

entr hugt e gavisc /Àà…õntrÃ© hajt e gaÀàv…™x/ [Àà…õn.t…ê hajt e g…êÀàv…™√ß]
- torn, unable to decide between two (good) options;
- living it up, going from strength to strength, experiencing a continual abundance of good fortune

Etymology: idiom from the fifteenth century (Kigman), literally "between happiness and joy". The noun hugt "happiness" descends from Old Borlish hu»ùt, a borrowing from Old English hyht "hope"; the noun gavisc is a deverbal from gavir "enjoy, feel happy", which descends directly from Latin gaude≈ç "I rejoice, make merry". Used primarily with negative valence (portraying the necessary choice as an imposition) until the nineteenth century, at which the latter sense begin to be attested.

L'indrec de my sour es se trovant entr hugt e gavisc.
/l…™nÀàdr…õk de mi sur …õz se troÀàvant Àà…õntrÃ© hajt e gaÀàv…™x/
[l…™nÀàd Ä…õk de mi s äÀê‚Äø Ä…™z se t Ä äÀàvant …õn.t…ê hajt e g…êÀàv…™√ß]
df=charity of 1s.gn sister be rf find-p.prs between happiness and joy
My sister's charity is going from strength to strength.

124.3: fis "done"

fis /f…™z/ [f…™z]
- set, stuck, immovable, placed somewhere on a more-or-less permanent basis;
- done, complete, finished, having nothing else of an activity or project to do;
- spent, exhausted, empty, having been entirely used up;
- despairing, hopeless, resigned to the inevitability of failure or defeat;
- screwed, finished, in a hopeless situation

Etymology: past participle of figr "set down, place down, lay" used also as the past participle of figr a "finish, finish off, complete, stop" (where perhaps fis la would be more regular). In the sense of having run out of something, the word is seen from the sixteenth century; in the last senses it is seen from the seventeenth.

Nossy foyail es fis e scein lau pre yonoscon.
/n…îÀàsi f…îÀàjel …õz f…™z e xin lo pre Àåjo.noÀàx…în/
[n äÀàsi f äÀà ùel …™z f…™z e √ßin lo p Äe Àå ùo.n äÀàx…în]
1p-gn fuel be done and there.be-3p league before wherever
We're out of petrol in the middle of nowhere.

124.4: dignant "appropriate"

dignant /dajÀànant/ [d…êjÀànant]
- becoming, suitable, appropriate, fit, done, conforming to standards of behaviour or aesthetics;
- named, called, known as, AKA, alias, having been given an appellation

Etymology: present participle of dignar "merit, deserve, be worthy of", itself a verbal derivation from Old Borlish di»ùn "worth, merit". This reflects Latin adjective dignus "worthy, appropriate, meet". The latter senses are seen from the late Middle Borlish period.

Tal inquit no son dignant de mell'yam.
/tal …™nÀàkw…™t no s…în dajÀànant de m…õÀàljam/
[tal …™≈ãÀàkw…™t no s…în d…êjÀànan de m…™Ààljam]
so quote ng be.3p fit of 1s.gn=uncle
Such words are unworthy of my uncle.

##

124.5: Who's Who (1700s Edition)

    collected notes on various figures active during the eighteenth century.

Raymond al Mustafa (fl. 1750) was a mathematician and philosopher. He was resident for some time in Barcelon under the Duke of Aragon's patronage. During this time he corresponded with fellow theorist Rachel Desselut, and their letters outline some of the concepts that would go on to define the field of methodics.

He spent his latter years travelling, eventually settling in Brasil [Newfoundland]. His grandson Fabian Most√Ýf became an eminent astronomer at the Paratzon Observatory, using mathematical techniques developed in part by Raymond (like the waveform display [Fourier transform] to compute celestial orbits).

-

Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725‚Äì56) was the illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia [~a region west of Swabia]. She grew up at the ducal seat in Castle Carlsfeld as a recognised bastard, and was close with the Duke's younger legitimate children. She married Albert Siegert, a merchant in leather and a magnate of the shoemaking industry, at the age of eighteen, and moved with him to Lepzi [Leipzig].

From her regular correpondence with her half-brother Thomas she is known to have learnt to speak Burgund as a child and Bohamish [Czech] as an adult. With Albert she had several children, the eldest of whom would become the pioneering author Elsebeth Sneider.

-

Lord Mathurin Carrow, Baron of Ebbasham [Epsom] (fl. 1763) was a traveller who published accounts of his journeys. He is remembered in particular for his mealbooks which compile recipes from the places he visited.

An early work of his, A Complete Assembly of the Towns and Cities of Markland for the Edification of the Good Food Amateur, was popular across the Northwestern Isles [Britain, Ireland and Borland] (the Borlish translation being infamously inaccurate) but failed to sell further afield. Later volumes like An Account of the Vittles of the Various German Peoples and On the Sundry Uses of the Shadome Fruit [tomato] were far more enduring, being republished several times even after his death.

125.1: Early Modern Morrack

excerpt in translation from notes on the polity of Morrack [Morocco].

Y Colluy dy Nou Mont nomma√∞ cos auspecer sta corporalleya√∞ par Malic Mounir al-Hamdawi d'an 1643, e pres pront la non√ßamen dell'Abanc Novomondin fos yent a cort ern vascel dy Colluy acca√ßa√∞ vars l'ovest.

Cay l'oc flottisc ayent communeyan√ß fait con y gent dell'Arcabil, tan com y dou empir Mascic e Tavancin.

Vascel Morrac stan y primer a y mont circonnavigar, stant parvenu√∞ ag Gran Rajognt Borunesc d'an 1519 via Cappatia.

Souspeut es ig y mort dell'Emperour Tavancin Carivasary d'an 1660 fo vera√ßter un assassinat accommis lonc commant Morrac, cos cuida√∞ por y c√¶ros ovrir d'un commar√ß cas argent profitandessem des Tavancy.
The death of Tavancine Emperor Cariwasary in 1660 is suspected to have been an assassination carried out on Morrack orders, purportedly to allow for more profitable trade in silver from Tavancy.

125.2: laur d'asn "drudgery"

laur d'asn /lor Ààda.znÃ©/ [loÀê Ààda.z…ên]
- drudgery, drudge work, toil, grunt work, menial and tedious labour;
- tedium, ennui, boredom, a general sense of listlessness and lacking of engaging activity

Etymology: idiom meaning "donkey's work", attested from the eighteenth century. The noun laur "work, job" is a deverbal from laurar "to work"; the noun asn "donkey; dunce" descends directly from synonymous asinus in Latin.

J'azia ne spien ig un hom interromois my laur d'asn.

[ ù…êÀàzja ne spj…õn aj …™n …îm …™nÀåt…õ. Ä äm…îjz mi loÀê Ààda.s…ên]
1s-stand-ipf in hope cmp indf person interrupt-ipf.sbj 1s.gn toil of=donkey
I stood, hoping someone would interrupt the tedium.

125.3: √∞orn de hanc "sideswipe"

√∞orn de hanc /√∞…îrn de hank/ [√∞…îÀên de ha≈ãk]
- swipe, sideswipe, hook, a punch or other strike from the side;
- bolt from the blue, a completely unexpected event (especially one for which you are utterly unprepared)

Etymology: phrase meaning "punch from (the) flank", used figuratively to describe an unexpected event since the late eighteenth century. The noun √∞orn "punch" is a deverbal from now-obsolete a√∞ornar "to punch", itself directly from synonymous addurn≈ç in Insular Latin (of ultimate Kelt origin). The noun hanc "hip, hipbone; flank, loin, side" descends from Vulgar Latin hanca "hip", from a German source related to ankle in English.

125.4: govondry "canteen"

govondry /Àågo.v…înÀàdri/ [Àågo.v änÀàd Äi]
- canteen, cafeteria, a small cafe inside a larger establishment (e.g. military base, school, prison or workplace);
- tuck shop, lunch counter, snack bar, a counter selling small items of food

Etymology: Middle Borlish govonry "inn, eatery", place derivation from now-obsolete govon "innkeeper, barkeeper". This appears to descend directly from Latin caup≈ç "tradesman, innkeeper". It specialisation to small eateries and counters within organisations takes place first in a military context during the eighteenth century.

J'acatau dou quignou de nossy govondry.
/Àå ía.kaÀàto du kwajÀànu de n…îÀàsi Àågo.v…înÀàdri/
[Àå ùa.k…êÀàto du kwajÀànu de n äÀàsi Àågo.v änÀàd Äi]
1s=buy-pst two pastry of 1p.gn canteen
I bought two pastries from our canteen.

##

125.5: Denmark

Denmark is a peninsular polity located between the North and Graven Seas.

History
Denmark was part of the homeland of the Danes during the Dane Supremacy in the medieval period, from which viking raids and colonisation projects left. The rulers of Borland and much of Dane England during this time were dynasties originating in Denmark.

During the sixteenth century Denmark was part of the Dane Concert, a monarchic union of northern states. The royal family undertook a grand tour of their realm in the years around 1560, visiting places like Alburgh. It was this period which saw Revitalism reach Denmark, with renewed interrogation of Classical texts.

Instability in Denmark belying the Long Peace of the late eighteenth century led to an uptick in emigration, both to nearby polities like Borland and also the distant staddomains of Cappatia.

The expansion of suffrage in Denmark took place through the latter half of the nineteenth century, parallel to similar movements in Saxony and the Latin republics. During this time Denmark was drawn into the orbit of the Drengot Collusion, although it was never fully incorporated.

People
Elisa Arnoutszen (fl. 1540), nurse in the household of Duke Roger d'Avosche and later a physician in her own right
Laurz Arnoutszen (fl. 1540), famed physician whose treatise on medical handiwork revolutionised European practice
Absolon Mortenszen (fl. 1560), playwright and commentator who lived in Alburgh and is remembered for his play High Society
Gudmund & Rebecca Scrander (fl. 1677), astronomers who voyaged to Seldiva to observe a transit of Venus

excerpt in translation from the 1930 work Notas a Hurt "Secret Notes", an example of the spycraft genre written in Vascony by author Micolau Zorriqueta (whose similar series of works about a Huzatre detective is more widely remembered).

Martin scoutau y ba√∞rment durant hour monfeya√∞ e fo mesteya√∞ n'yembr sci√∞ posc i lan√ßau ogl a sell'orlosc a ca√∞en ig for veint minut n'ern launda√∞.

126.5: Chemical Etymology

excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers.

- moron n. /Ààm…îÀê.…æ…în/ [proton] a deficient [positively charged] particle as found in atomic nuxes [nuclei]. 1888 < Kiew [Kyiv] Russian –º–æ—Ä–∞ ¬∑ mora (1882) < Ancient Greek ŒºœåœÅŒøŒΩ ¬∑ m√≥ron "mulberry, blackberry", in reference to Nike Sarantylack's "mulberry model" of the atom as containing a number of each of two different sorts of particle.

- lucifer adj., n. /Ààl…ôu.s…™.f…ô/ [radioactive (substance)] of a substance, spontaneously producing subatomic particles or ionising light; such a substance. 1894 < Vascon lucifer < Latin l≈´cifer "light-bringing", from l≈´x "light" and -fer "carrying". Connected only incidentally with proper noun Lucifer "Satan".

- killdark n. /Ààk…™l.daÀêk/ [uranium] the corporal [element] whose atoms have 92 mora, a lucifer [radioactive] grey metal. 1810 < Welsh kild√°rc "killdark" (1805) < the Irish shire of Killdara, where the corporal was first isolated. Plausibly respelt after "dark"; the Wessern English form darick /Ààda.…æ…ôk/ "dark" may also have played a role.

- lix n. /l…™ks/ [potassium] the corporal whose atoms have 19 mora, a soft grey metal acted on by air and water and thus never found in tesquo [in nature] as a metal. 1797 < Latin lixa "lye", which is the caustic of lix [potassium hydroxide].

- janthine n. /Ààd ían.Œ∏…ôin/ [iodine] the corporal whose atoms have 53 mora, found as a grey solid, a purple-black liquid and a purple gas. 1791 < Scholastic Latin janthƒ´nus < Ancient Greek ·º∞Œ¨ŒΩŒ∏·øêŒΩŒøœÇ "violet-coloured".

127.1: Medieval Empire

excerpt in translation on the Second Roman Empire [~the Byzantines] in the high medieval period.

Ne souc dy ruin dy Lombardesc nor√∞er n'Italy, y lina√ß regnant enfugheurn vars l'Empir e sceurn autorta√∞ remarcabr alla√ßar la.

Pie√ß sodal se courn lorry ascreu√ßon com agathin por y them ovester nell'Empir, e√∞ y tesmogn scaut par e√ß autr intermann l'inquit historic y confiablessem cas occorren√ß particuler durant y scompigl dy Secession German ny siecr tre√ß.

It is for this reason that s'Avignon is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic events that would take place within Saint Sophia's three hundred years later.

127.2: y c√¶ros ovrir de "make possible"

y c√¶ros ovrir de /i keÀàr…îz …îÀàvr…™r de/ [i k…™Àà Ä…îz  äÀàv Ä…™Àê de]
- enable, make possible, allow for, permit, to create conditions such that (something) is more feasible or more likely to occur

The noun c√¶ros "window of opportunity, opportune moment" is a modern borrowing from Ancient Greek Œ∫Œ±ŒπœÅœåœÇ "period of time, season; opportunity".

127.3: broncar "flinch"

broncar /br…înÀàkar/ [b Ä ä≈ãÀàk…êÀê]
- flinch, wince, recoil, to reflexively move away from something perceived as unpleasant or a threat;
- stumble, trip, slip, lurch, to make a sudden unsteady movement;
- balk, back out, chicken out, get cold feet, to refuse to act due to (espeecially sudden) fear or uncertainty

Etymology: from Old Borlish broncar "stumble, misstep", cognate with synonymous bronchier in Old French but of unknown further origin. Perhaps connected to Latin broccus "buck-toothed" or truncus "docked, cut off", but neither is particularly semantically tempting. Unhelpful also are the equally unsourceable examples of Lombard bronco "thorny branch" and Barcelon bronc "knot in wood".

Jo vole pogntar apar ma jo broncau.
/ ío voÀàle p…îjnÀàtar aÀàpar pi  ío br…înÀàko/
[ ùo v äÀàle p äjnÀàt…êÀê‚Äø Ä…êÀàp…êÀê pi  ùo b Ä ä≈ãÀàko]
1s will-ipf poke-inf through but 1s balk-pst
I meant to flirt but I chickened out.

127.4: snuvr fair "sniff"

snuvr fair /Ààsn…™vrÃ© fer/ [Àåzn…™.v…ê feÀê]
- sniff, sniffle, snivel, snort, to make an audible inhalation through the nose (especially when the nose is blocked);
- scoff, turn up one's nose, sneer, to react with contempt or scorn

Etymology: verb phrase from snuvr "sniff, snort", itself a derivation from the verb snuvlar, which had become obsolete by the seventeenth century. This descends from Old Borlish isnuvlar, a borrowing from Old English snyflan "to have a runny nose". In the latter sense the phrase is attested from the eighteenth century.

No stou bogr snuvr fair dell'o√ß c√¶ros.

[no stu Ààb…î.j…ê Ààzn…™.v…ê feÀê d…™Ààl…îs k…™Àà Ä…îz]
neg be.necessary at.all sniff do.inf of-df=s.dt opportunity
The opportunity's nothing to sniff at.

##

127.5: Deadly Disasters

notes on the subject of various (alt) historical disasters.

The Great Flie Flood was a natural disaster in 1171 Willemy which submerged or swept through large parts of Frisland. Inflowing waters of the Rustig Strait created the island of Texel, and the freshwater Lake Almere (which fed into the Strait along the Flie river) became brackish and eventually fully saline as a maritime gulf.

The then-duke Lovis the Tall, who had been staying in the environs of Amstel for some months, is the most famous (and most historically influential) casualty of the Flood, but estimates have suggested a death toll approaching ten thousand.

The destruction is thought to have spurred the development of Axbane on Borland as an alternative, safer harbour for traders passing through the Rustigh Strait.

---

The First Burning of London was a conflagration in 1290 which lasted for a full week and killed approximately four hundred people. This was the earliest of several such burnings, at least two of which are more accurately described as sackings or conquerings. Unlike (say) the Fourth Burning, it is purely conjectural that this particular fire was deliberately set as part of a plot to steal the Redstone Chair (presumably orchestrated by some Markland or Kentish noble).

---

The Failed Crusade was an undertaking which aimed to recreate the March of Jerusalem. The city and its surrounds had been captured during the First Crusade and the first Marksman of Jerusalem, Savino the Just, had acceded in 1119, but it had fallen less than seventy years later.

This crusade was called in 1210 by Pope Just (a direct descendant of Lord Savino), and it was utterly unsuccessful. The campaign went so badly that the Convoy Australier having their control of Port Simon and Antioch revoked in 1208 is generally regarded as a boon for the Convoy.

The various military catastrophes stoked discontent in Rome and elsewhere, along with the outbreak of the First Great Dying (a plague thought to have been brought to Europe by returning crusaders).

128.2: fardig "solo"

fardig /farÀàdaj/ [f…êÀêÀàdaj]

    solo, music played by a single performer;
    chance to shine, big moment, moment in the spotlight, an oppoortunity to be noticed to do demonstrate one's skill;
    moment of truth, critical point, a time when one's skills or resolve are finally tested (especially after a period of complacency or uncertainty)

These come from a derivative of the Arabic root ŸÅŸéÿ±ŸíÿØ ¬∑ fard "alone, single, solitary". Extension to non-musical contexts is seen from the late nineteenth century.

128.3: lau pre yonoscon "in the middle of nowhere"

lau pre yonoscon /lo pre Àåjo.n…îÀàx…în/ [lo p Äe Àå ùo.n äÀàx…în]

    in the middle of nowhere, isolated, a long way from anywhere of interest like settlements or amenities;
    stuck, lost, making no progress on a problem

Etymology: early nineteenth century idiom literally meaning "leagues before wherever". The noun lau "league" comes via Old Borlish from Insular Latin lauga "league" (cf. leuga, legua), originally a Gaulish unit. The pronoun yonoscon "wherever, anywhere" is an extension of yon "where" < Latin ubicumque "wherever" by analogy to words like calscon "whichever" < Latin quƒÅliscumque "of whatever quality". In metaphorical use from the early twentieth century.

128.5: Mathematical Etymology

excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mathematics, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers.

zeffer /Ààz…õ.f…ô/ [zero] the number of things in an empty collection, the quantity one fewer than one. Before 1350 < Scholastic Latin zefer, zefrum (alt. zephirus by false connection to Zephyr) < Arabic ÿµŸêŸÅŸíÿ± ‚Ä¢ ·π£ifr "empty, void" < in sense from Sanscrit ý§∂ý•Çý§®ý•çý§Ø ‚Ä¢ ≈õ≈´ny√° "empty, void".

ablate /Ààa.bl…ôt/ [negative] fewer than zeffer, as of a debtor's money or a distance backward. 1481 < Lombard ablad < Scholastic Latin ablƒÅtus "taken away, withdrawn, removed", first used in financial accounts and taken up by mathematicians some decades later.

dart /ÀàdaÀêt/ [~vector] a segment of a line, considered with a choice of which end is the head and which the vane. Around 1470 < Early Merch dart "throwing spear, dart" < Middle French dart, dard < Medieval Latin dardus < Frankish *dar≈çth < Classical German *dar≈çthus "light spear, javelin, dart". Extended to its mathematical sense by the pictorial analogy of drawing arrowheads on line segments.

augrim /Àà…ôu.g…æ…™m/ [~arithmetic] the mathematics of calculation with numbers; computation more generally. Around 1220 < Drengot augrime, algrisme etc < Old French algorisme < Scholastic Latin algorismus "the use of Arabic numbers" < Arabic ÿßŸÑÿÆŸéŸàŸéÿßÿ±Ÿêÿ≤ŸíŸÖŸêŸäŸë ‚Ä¢ al-·∏µawƒÅrizmiyy, a name of the Persian polymath whose mathematical works introduced much of mathematics to Europe.

arrhumb /Ààa.…æ…ôm/ [cosine] the sending of an angle to the quotient of the height of a rod rotated by that angle from the vertical to the full length of the rod. Early Merch arrum, arrom (the modern spelling is influenced by unrelated rhumb "compass bearing") < Arabic ÿßŸÑÿ±ŸÖÿ≠ ‚Ä¢ ar-rum·∏• "spear, shaft". This appears to be a metaphorical extension related to the use ofŸàÿ™ÿ± ‚Ä¢ watar < Sanscrit ý§úý•çý§Øý§æ ‚Ä¢ jyƒÅÃÅ "bowstring" for vater.

129.4: kirt ag madou "catalyst"

kirt ag madou /k…™rt …õj maÀàdu/ [k…™Àêt …õj m…êÀàdu]
- catalyst, trigger, spark, inciting incident, a thing or event that sets something (especially something of outsized impact) into motion;

Etymology: idiom attested from the 18C, literally "spark to the kindling". The noun kirt "spark" descends from synonymous kirte in Old Borlish, and is probably onomatopoeic in origin. This verb is thought to descend via Old Norrish mata "lure, feed" from Classical German *mat≈çnam "feed".

129.5: Arcabil

The Arcabil are a collection of islands in the Atlantic, east of Mashick. They comprise hundreds of landmasses, of which Cocain is the largest.

Toponymy
The name "Arcabil" has traditionally been considered to be an uncomplicated use of Arabic ÿ£Ÿéÿ±ŸíÿÆŸéÿ®ŸêŸäŸÑ ‚Ä¢ ('archabƒ´l) "archipelago", borrowed into Scholastic Latin as Arcabilia (and treated as a plural on the basis of its -bilia ending). However, there is little evidence of such a word existing in Arabic in the fifteenth century.

The possibility has been raised of a metathetic and dissimilatory variant of unattested ÿßŸÑŸÉÿ±Ÿäÿ® ‚Ä¢ (al-carƒ´b) "the Carib", using a local name for a group of peoples of the Arcabil, but the phonetics are tenuous.

History
The Arcabil were first encountered by Vetomundine powers in the late fifteenth century, during the first stage of the New World Company's voyages west under the aegis of Malik Munir al-Hamdawi. By the early sixteenth century Morrack had established several semi-permanent outposts there, which were important ports for the circumnavigation of the Cynthia under Fredun al-Casmi.

The orange liqueur known as "fleuse" is a genericisation of the Fleus d'Arcabil mixer first manufactured in the early nineteenth century on a Borlish-speaking island of the Arcabil.


People
Judeta Barracin (fl. 1919), author of social comedy born in Andalus who spent her formative years in the Arcabil
Lo√∞a√ß Berxon (fl. 1910), who grew up in the Arcabil and who was an important member of the Outland Arcabil Association after her emigration to Borland

130.1: The Heedless Shoal

extract in translation from 1952 work Hus Tars (literally "Sheer Haddock", published in translation under the title "The Heedless Shoal"), written by Haubrath author Marc Lambount. A starn, l'e√ß ont inexorabr contonen y tors dy vascel e menan jug sy cogta√ßon a lorrell'istran√ß maritim.

130.2: carrot "carriage"

carrot /kaÀàr…ît/ [k…êÀà Ä…ît]
- carriage, wagon, a (usually horse-drawn) vehicle designed for transporting people;
- car, carriage, trolley, a single detachable part of a train;
- module, component, a self-contained (often interchangeable) part of a system which interfaces predictable with other parts

Etymology: diminutive of Old Borlish car "wagon", which descends from Latin carrus "cart, wagon; load" (the suffix may have been added to distinguish the word from car "dear, darling", although the root survives as car "car, automobile" nonetheless). Use for trains is seen with their invention in the nineteenth century; metaphorical extension from that sense is seen in the twentieth.

##

130.5: Angland

Angland (Borlish Anglont /anÀàgl…înt/ […ê≈ãÀàgl…înt]) is a region in Borland in the west of the island. Its largest population centre is the city of Vithor.

History

The petty kingdom of Angland was founded by Angle immigrants in the fifth century, along with Southbar and several more minor settlements. It quickly established itself in opposition to Southbar and to the rump sub-Roman Borlish state in the east. At the turn of the ninth century it saw an influx of immigration from East Anglia after that kingdom was subjugated by Markland.

After a series of temporary alliances between Angland and Borland, the marriage of Roman king Stiglan's son Marc to the only daughter of Alfegh of Angland unified the kingdoms for good. This was shortly followed by the Battle of Fendal (810) which forced Southbar to swear fealty as a subordinate kingdom. Not long thereafter was Angland conquered by the Danes along with the rest of the island.

The Borland varieties of Old English survived longest in Angland, with records suggesting that an English language descending from the original settlers was spoken in rural parts of the region until as late as the seventeenth century.

Places

√áadrosc, an area of moorland in the northwest which features a labyrinth built in Kelt times
Gambrig, a village and nearby monastery noteworthy for its medieval chronicle in a variety of Old English
Hawkirch, site of the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632
Vithor, regional capital on the west coast known for the New Vithor University, a major research institution

Their uncle was Damian the Wise, the first (Roman) Pope to be elected from among the Brethin religious order.

D'an 1292 sceye y chronic Y Vi√∞ a Catrin de Cornou scaut, y stoir detaglant de sy vi√∞ joun, sy mari√∞a√ß con un nobr Borallesc e sell'assassinat d'an 1233.
