131.2: liesc bouclant "time loop"

liesc bouclant /lj…õx buÀàklant/ [lj…õ√ß b äÀàklant]
- time loop, (in fiction) a period experienced by one or more people repeatedly;
- (by extension) routine, monotonous or repetitive activity such as errands that are done many times with little variation

Etymology: idiom from the late 19th century, literally "looping duration". The noun liesc "term, period, duration" is a Old Borlish deverbal from lejar "stop moving, crash" (the word originally meaning "endpoint"), from Insular Latin laedic≈ç "I hit, collide". The verb bouclar "loop, curl, go in a circle" derives from Old Borlish boucle "loop, bracelet" (modern boucr), via Old French from Latin buccula "chinstrap".

The phrase was first used in the translation of 1882 work Cludor ncar Vantel "A Postman in Wrexham", which features such a time loop.

Com vele√∞ vos farar √∞a vos cay un liesc bouclant?

[k…îm v…™Ààl…õ v…îz f…êÀà Ä…êÀê √∞a v…îz ke …™n lj…õ√ß b äÀàklant]

What would you do if you were in a time loop?

The noun foya√ß "mess, jumble" < fodi≈ç "I dig up" is unrelated but may have had semantic influence.

131.4: recuidar "reflect"

Jo recuida jamay noiteyar n'un davarn.
/ ío Àåre.kajÀàda  íaÀàme Àån…îj.tiÀàjar n…™n daÀàvarn/
[ ùo Àå Äe.k…êjÀàda  ù…êÀàme Àån…îj.t…™Àà ù…êÀê n…™n d…êÀàv…êÀên]

##

131.5: Jeans

Jeans (Merch /d íeÀênz/; Borlish Jen / í…õn/; Jean Zena /Ààze.na/) is an Italian polity and city of the same name. It comprises the northwest Middlesea coastline of Italy bordering Provence as well as the island of Corse. It has a long history as one of the so-called 'Latin Republics'.

Toponymy

The Merch name Jeans was borrowed from Old French Jannes /Ààd ía.n…ôs/, deriving originally from Genua in Latin. This is probably simply the classical local word for "knee" (cf. Classical Latin genu), in reference to the shape of the coastline it lies on.

History

Jeans is the oldest of the Latin Republics, having come into existence in the tenth century. Along with Venise, it serves as a counterpoint to the notion that the Second Tetrarchy period in the eleventh and twelfth centuries was solely comprised of great centralised empires. The leader of Jeans, known as the console, was elected from among the merchant nobility.

In 1207 N Jeans invaded the Tyrrhene Isles of Corse and Sardine, wresting them from Roman control. Only the northernmost of the two islands would remain in the Jean orbit more permanently.

Piracy plagued the Middlesea coast for the same reason that maritime trade was so profitable for Jeans. It reached a peak at the turn of the sixteenth century as newfound wealth from the Novomund flooded the region.

People

Franco Cicala (r. 1119-22/4), first console from the Cicale family

References

The Latin Republics: Jewels under Tyrian Standards, a 1902 historical reference quire by Jacob Trelaune (cf. in particular 'Part III: Old Jeans')

132.5: Dime measures

The dime measures (less commonly known as the 'tithe measures' or 'tenth measures') are a modern system of units based on factors of ten. The name derives from the French d√Æme "one-tenth", from synonymous decimus in Latin.

The dime measures were codified during the nineteenth century for ease of communication, as theoretical development coalesced into a global endeavour. They have been updated several times since, as it has become feasible to use more convenient yardsticks.

Standard modifiers may be added to each unit to increase or decrease their size by factors of 10. In most common use are:

    great (10‚Å¥)
    tithe (10‚Åª¬π)
    petty (10‚Åª‚Å¥)
    scant (10‚Åª‚Å∏)

Length

There are three bases for dime lengths: the inch, the foot (10 inches) and the league (10‚Å¥ feet). At first, the length of the foot was fixed somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly different Roman foot and the Cathayan "surveyor's span". In 1946, the foot was redefined to make the normal speed [speed of light] (denoted ≈ì) equal to precisely ten great leagues per second.

Several other traditional measures of length were given dime values. For instance, in dime measures the mile is equal to five thousand feet or half a league.
Heft [mass]

There are four bases for dime hefts: the scruple, the ounce (100 scruples), the tabre (100 ounces) and the ton (100 tabres). The third of these units takes its approximate value from the Persian mann and its name from the city of Tabris [Tabriz]. The ounce was originally defined to be the mass of a cube of water of side length one inch; this was later refined to specify that the water be at its triple point of pressure and temperature.
Derived units

Many units are defined in terms of these base units of length and heft, as well as the second. For example, the shove measures the might [force] required to accelerate a heft of one tabre to a speed of one foot per second.

134.5: A Motley Miscellany

The Bahassassin River [1] is the longest river on the continent of Mendeva [North America], with a total length of just over two thousand leagues. The river passes through multiple polities, including Zangathy and Hasiny, and its eastern tributaries descend from the Sturgovan Mountains [the Appalachians]. The largest city on the banks of the Bahassassin is Nadacow, the capital city of Hasiny. The name of the river comes from the Carovacian [Caddo] name b√°hass√°ssin "mother of rivers".

[1] comprising the Missouri River and the portion of the Mississippi River downstream of St Louis.

---

Mark of Tolose (d. 1453) was the son of King Ambrose II of Vascony, and the father of King Ambrose III of Vascony. Although he was crown prince, he died three years before his father and thus never reigned. He married Margaret of Iscombe [Exeter] shortly before 1435. Along with Ambrose, they had three other children: their first child Alexandra, and two younger children Victor of Astorga and Natalia.

---

Wings Afire (Ale in Fiamme) is an opera written by Venetian Chiarina Bruno and first performed in the Verona Arena in 1824. It was the first work staged in the Verona Arena since it was shuttered at the outbreak of the Second German War.

The opera is a retelling of the legend of Icarus which takes place entirely after the events of the myth, with references to the dead Icarus in the dialogue. It follows the matron of a clothiery who meets a grieving Daedalus; she helps him to rediscover the joy of invention. The opera's music features the low-pitched mournful melodies of the graveyard fiddlers from Borlish folklore; Bruno is said to have heard of them from her brother Jacomo.

135.3: runam "secretly"

runam /riÀànam/ [ Ä…™Àànam]

- privately, in private, sub rosa, behind closed doors, in a setting conducive to secrecy

This is a derivative of contemporary run "symbol, spell, talisman" using the now-unproductive adverbial suffix -am, descending from Latin anima "soul" (compare kire√∞am "wholeheartedly", coram "in person").

[Àån…î.z…™Àà Äo  Ä…™Àànam ÀåalÀàjant k…în lu k äÀàm…õt a f…™n]
1p cop.ipf-1p secretly ally-p.prs with 3p.dsj start to end
We were secretly on their side the whole time.

135.4: brevier "memo"

brevier /breÀàvj…õr/ [b Ä…™Ààvj…õÀê]
- memo, memorandum, note, reminder, a short bit of writing to prevent one forgetting something;
- summary, overview, pr√©cis, synopsis, a summarised description of something;
- brief, report, a description of events

##

135.5: Kernow

Kernow (Merch /Ààk…õÀê.n…ô/, Borlish Cornou /k…î ÄÀànu/) refers to the north-western peninsula of Gaul. Known in antiquity as Armorica, Brittania or Dumnonia, it was resettled in the early medieval period by Kelt peoples from Albion.

History

The Kernovan kingdoms at the dawn of the eighth century had only recently been unified, ruled by widowed Queen Maud of Angeus. Her son Vigo ascended to the throne upon her death in 720, shortly after the Catumagin Accord which ended hostilities and established an official border with the Kingdom of the Franks. Vigo spearheaded the reconquest of Devon from the kingdom of Wessex, uniting the two peninsulas as the Kingdom of Greater Devon.

The polity suffered somewhat during the ninth and tenth centuries, with the encroachment of the Franks in the east and with extensive Viking raids along the coasts (the monastery at Landvennick was even burned to the ground in 888 N).

The region derives its modern name from the House of Kernow, which in the eleventh century took power by means of an alliance with Normandy. This Kernovan Conjuring led to the establishment of the First Drengot Empire, of which Kernow was a minor vassal.

The Brethin of Kernow enjoyed significant religious influence during the late medieval period.

Kernow became part of the Kingdom of Britain in the fifteenth century as it incorporated territories further afield from its heartland in Wales.

People

Catrin of Kernow, sister of the Devoner king and influential figure in thirteenth-century Borland

Pope Damian VI (the Wise), reigned 1199-1208 N

Saint Victor of Dunkirk, d. 1245 N

##

136.1: Historiography Questions

excerpt in translation from shortform scitation questions in the 2021 edition of the Tremonnow Open School's domain history course 'Making History', which covers the process by which events and people are compiled into historical narratives.

-1. Cal historicour dy siecr cator√ß fornisceu primer y Preu Empir Drengo√ßan sceint tre siecr paravant con nom de 'Empir Constant', oc moliscent a le ligar con y Pag de Constan√ß (y part frender dy Tetrarchy Diocletian dy siecr catr, Bretagn Roman e Gal administrant)?

136.5: The Novomund (briefly)

This term Novomund for the great landmasses of the western hemisphere is attested from the early sixteenth century, reflecting a univerbation of latin novus mundus "new world". 
Mendeva [North America]

The name Mendeva was formed backwards from Latin Mendevalƒìnsis "from Mendeva", attested already on the maps made during the 1471 Landfall (the demonym was later rederived as MendevƒÅnus "Mendevan" from the placename). This comes from Vask [Basque] mendeval "west", itself likely a borrowing from Vascon vent de val "valley wind".

    Ambrosia [~Quebec, the Maritimes, New England] is a Vascony-settled polity named for King Ambrose III of Vascony (r. 1456‚Äì1518).
    Zangathy [~the Dakotas, Minnesota, Iowa, Wisconsin, Winnipeg, Ontario] is a landlocked polity occupying the upriver Bahassassin [Mississippi] directly west of the Five Seas [Great Lakes]. Its name reflects Daccotan [Dakota] za≈ãathi "flint region".
    New Provence [~New England, New York, and south as far as South Carolina] is a coastal polity extending westward to the Sturgovan Mountains [Appalachians] which grew from a series of trading post established by Provence merchants.
    Hasiny [~Louisiana, Texas, Mississippi, Arkansas, Oklahoma] is the largest polity in so-called British Mendeva, a few states along the Gulf settled by the Kingdom of Britain [~Wales, etc]. It takes its name from the Carovacian [Caddo] name Has√≠ne "our people" for one of the previous nations the modern polity is descended from.
    Taisha [~Texas, Coahuila] is a minor polity of Gulf Mendeva at the crossroads of British and Morrack [Moroccan] influences. Its name comes via Arabic Tayisha' from Carovacian [Caddo] ta√≠sha' "welcome".
    Mivock [~northern California] is a minor coastal polity on the Lovaqua [Pacific] known for its congenial climate. Of exceedingly sundry influence, its name reflects the name miwoc of the local Mendeva peoples and languages.
    Mashick [~Mexico, southern California, New Mexico, Arizona] is the largest polity in Lower Mendeva, the descendant of the Mashick [Aztec] Empire with heavy Morrack influence. Its name derives via Arabic from the Nawat [Nahuatl] meshi'ca' "Mashick people".

The Arcabil [~the Caribbean] are a collection of islands in the Atlantic, east of Mashick. They have a complicated history of influences from various Vetomundine powers. The name probably comes via the Arabic cognate of "archipelago".

    Cocain [~Cuba] is the largest of the islands of the Arcabil. Its name comes from the European folk tradition of a "land of plenty" (compare pays de Cocaigne, pa√≠s de Cucanna, paese della Cuccagna).

Cappatia [South America]

European languages take the name Cappatia of this continent from Latin CappƒÅtia, which comes via Arabic from Run'allo [Quechua] cappacha "this world".

    Tavancy [~Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Boliva, northern Chile] is major polity on the western coast, descended from the Tavancine [Inca] Empire. Its name comes from the Latin imperium tavantƒ´num, which was borrowed via Arabic as a partial calque from the Run'allo tawantin-suyu "the quartet of provinces".
    Staddomains, rather small polities, dot the eastern coast. These grew out of individual trading posts and have many different influences. Examples abound from Dutch influence in Neuwillem, Parnaven and Waron√∏u to Borlish influence in Mon√ßating.
    Dowahine [~southern Patagonia, Tierra del Fuego] is a minor polity at the southern tip of Cappatia. Its name comes from the Arabic word for "smoke", after the smoke-towers sighted there by Captain al-Casmi in 1513.

137.5: The Dangers of DHMO

An illustration of the language Merch (the most widely-spoken language in the polity of Markland) through close analysis of the following example sentence.

Al as drink the leigh of aquifex ar sicker to dy.
/al …ôz d…æ…™≈ãk √∞…ô leÀê É …ô‚ÄøÀåva.kw…™Ààf…õks aÀê Ààs…™k…ô t…ô d…ôi/

Translated to IRL English with Boralverse jargon:

Everyone who drinks aquifex's lye will surely die.

Something which seems minor but which actually affects the aesthetic of Merch greatly is the lack of the "three-letter rule". In IRL English, almost all function words (nouns, adjectives, verbs) are at least three letters long. This rule is why we have such pairs as "inn - in" and "bee - be", as well as the redundant double letters in words like "ebb" and "egg". By contrast, Merch never did anything analogous, as can be seen here in the words al, ar and dy (cf "all", "are" and "die").

Merch also has somewhat less French influence than IRL English (while Kentish has somewhat *more*), due to the incomplete replacement of the English nobility there during the period of the Norman-ruled First Drengot Empire. Evidence of this in the above sentence is in the word sicker "certain" < Old English sicor, which lost out to French-derived "sure" in IRL English.

Grammatical differences on display here are: (1) the use of "as" in relative clauses, e.g. "the man as I love", "the man as loves me"; (2) more colloquial use of "be to VERB" with (non-volitional) future meaning, e.g. "the boats ar to sink!".

Finally, the scientific jargon in this alternate history is of course entirely different. The word leigh < OE lƒìag is cognate with IRL "lye; potassium/sodium hydroxide", and has been generalised to refer to any hydroxide substance. The word aquifex is a Scholastic Latin coinage (meaning "water-maker") for "hydrogen".

Thus we could informally convey the Merch sentence in IRL English as:
> Drinking hydrogen hydroxide has a 100% mortality rate.

Which of course it does‚Äîeventually.

138.1: Avosche

notes in translation on the region of Avosche (Paris French /aÀàv…îÀê É/, Willem Dutch /Ààa.vusx/) in Willemy [Lotharingia].

Y region d'Avosc dey nom a sy lina√ß regnant primer, o√ß sey me√∞es sy grad prenent de sy fondour Cuonrat Aihuosc (inquitta√∞ ne Latin com Corradus Aevoscus).
The region Avosche is named for its first ruling house, which in turn takes its title from its founder Cuonrat Aihuosc (seen in Latin as Corradus Aevoscus).

Y nom a un origin obscur; se po√∞ appondr ig y sodal primer se dey ag German Classic *ehu-* "coval", cant y dovem se dey parcan√ß ag fin *-iscas* "-esc" (pu sy voyal apport vandy).
The name is of obscure origin; the first element presumably reflects Classical German *ehu-* "horse", while the second may be from suffix *-iscas* "-ish" (though the vowel is problematic).

Confere√∞ y surnom Anglesc Davush, oc stant congeni√∞ des un mancel nothe de Lina√ß Avosc.
Compare the English surname Davush, which derives from an illegitimate branch of House Avosche.

Y Comta√∞ d'Avosc smarc pog asc apres dy dehiscen√ß dy Reyaum dy Frankesc e dy crea√ßon cohernt de Willemy.

138.3: pre un cocoro "unexpectedly"

##

138.5: Steeplepost Tapestry

The Steeplepost Tapestry (Vascon: Lo Tap√¨s Campanaral) was a screen of several myriad black or white tiles built in Ambrosia's capital city of Aiga d'Angoixa [~Quebec City]. Completed in 1820 and erected to overlook one of the city's central squares, it was inspired by the (fairly new) 'latticework' art movement.

One ambitious goal for the Tapestry was to be able to portray journalist's sketches of events from across the country with great speed, using the flag-lamp steeplemesh infrastructure that had been built up over the last few decades. Although this was achieved several times, the practical limitations of transmitting so much information by steeplepost prevented it from becoming routine. Instead, the majority of the tapestry's time was spent displaying short pieces of news or government messages in text and simple symbols.

The use of the word 'diced' to mean 'blocky, pixellated' dates back to a calque of Provincial icon dau 'die image'. This phrasing was coined in 1821 in disparaging reference to the Tapestry.

Just a few years into its operation, a series of hoax messages which spread rapidly via the Ambrosian steeplemesh made their way onto the Tapestry. These so-called 'Lies of 1824' included a report of the governor's death and culminated after several days with an alleged declaration of war from New Provence.

In 2020, a replica of the Tapestry was unveiled in Markland to mark the bicentenary of the original's completion.

##

139.1: Just a Man

translation into Borlish of the song Just a Man from the musical Epic, written by Jorge Rivera-Herrans.

Borlish:

    La jo te vey l'ec ogl a tey

    Jo say tout volir por lou reveir, mell'heir e femn
    Jo no's for hom

    Ma cant es un comet denu√∞ un assuvar?

    Cant es un ondol denu√∞ un rasamar?

Literal translation:

    There I see these eye of yours
    I remember that son of mine

139.3: zovay cavir "take a taxi"

zovay cavir /zoÀàve kaÀàv…™r/ [z äÀàve k…êÀàv…™Àê]
- take a taxi, go via cab, to travel somewhere as a passenger in a taxicab;
- take the easy way out, follow the path of least resistance, to take a course of action which requires minimal personal effort

Etymology: late ninetenth century, literally "to get (a) taxi". The noun zovay "taxi" is a clipping of earlier carrot zovay "taxi carriage", often mistakenly capitalised to carrot Zovay by analogy with other types of carriage named after places.

The word was borrowed via French from Furlain slang term zovaie "coach for hire with a taximeter", literally "convenience" (one did not have to pay up front, unlike earlier hired coaches). This is a derivative of verb zov√Ý "be useful, be convenient", from Latin iuv≈ç "I help; I please".

In the metaphorical sense the phrase is seen only from the 1970s.

Nos coum zovay e√∞ alandaum tost.
/n…îz kum zoÀàve …õŒ∏ Àåa.lanÀàdom t…îst/
[n…îz kum z äÀàve …õ‚ÄøÀå√∞a.l…ênÀàdom t…îst]
1p get.pst-1p taxi and turn.up-pst-1p early
We took a taxi and arrived early.

139.4: n'e√ß cahut "separately"

n'e√ß cahut /n…õts kaÀàh…™t/ [Àån…õs.k…êÀàh…™t]
- apart, siloed, segregated, separated from each other into disjoint areas;
- separately, independently, without any communication or collaboration;
- estranged, alienated, no longer speaking to one another (especially after a falling out)

Etymology: idiom attested from the eighteenth century, literally meaning "in cabins". The noun cahut "cabin, dorm, sleeping quarters" is a Middle Borlish borrowing, originally in a naval context, from synonyous caihute in Middle Dutch. This is possibly a portmanteau of Old French cabane and hutte, both meaning "hut, shack".

The Borlish idiom appears roughly contemporaneously with cognate idioms in other languages, such as Merch's in cahutes "independently".

Jug sein y domn egrtumnesc n'e√ß cahut.
/ íai sin i Ààd…îmnÃ© ÀåijrÃ©.t…™mÀàn…õx n…õts kaÀàh…™t/
[ ùai sin i Ààd…î.m…ên Àå…™j…ê.t…™mÀàn…õ√ß Àån…õs.k…êÀàh…™t]
ever be.sbj-3p df woman sick in=p.dt cabin
Keep the sick women quarantined.

##

139.5: Monarchs of Borland

collected notes on various monarchs of Borland.

Victor Rossetus 'the Red-haired' (fl. c6) was the first Prase of the Boral Diocese. He rose to prominence after the fall of the First Roman Empire and unified the urbanised east of the land while the west and south were settled by Anglians to form Angland and Southbar, respectively. It was under him that so-called 'Roman Borland' first began to succesfully repel the new settlers. By the late sixth century the Diocese of Boral had become a monarchy (described in retrospect as the First Kingdom of Borland), whose ruling dynasty claimed descent from Rossetus.

Jothey, King of Borland (fl. 898), known in Borlish as Jo√∞eg / íoÀà√∞ij/, was the last ruler of the First Kingdom of Borland before it fell to the Danes in 898. Married to Brenna of Barrow, cousin of King Roderick of Dunclothe, they fled to neighbouring Southbar (and once Southbar also fell to Dane rule, to Kent).

In the first decade of the fifteenth century, Queen Natalia I and her husband King Joseph I ruled jointly. Their family had been and would continue to be beset by misfortune; all three of their sons predeceased them, as did their few male grandchildren (either in infancy or by misadventure). The succession crisis prompted by their deaths in 1411 and 1416 spelt the end of their dynasty.

From the ashes rose the Saulestran lineage of kings, whose banner comprised a white bar cutting a twilight-blue field. This dynasty would go on to last for over four hundred years until the dissolution of the Borlish monarchy itself.

Joseph III (d. 1534) was king of Borland, remembered for ruling the kingdom through the ravages of the Second Great Dying. Joseph never succeeded in producing a natural-born heir, despite several recognised yet illegitimate children by his various mistresses. Thus, after his death in 1534, he was succeeded to the throne by Brandon II, his nephew.

Natalia II (Borlish Natagl, abd. 1894) was the last queen of Borland before the dissolution of the monarchy. She abdicated on Revillon of 1894 during the political upheaval leading to the country joining the Drengot Collusion (several Borlish cities, although not the capital, had already joined as part of the New Urban Mesh).

##

notes in translation on the subject of the Redstone Chair.

Y Ca√∞er ne Pie√∞r Roug sta costroit ny siecr deg jagerrem atorn d'un los ne greut; y nom dy carpenter es repars ne vogt dell'hour.
The Redstone Chair was fashioned in the late tenth century around a block of sandstone; the name of the carpenter is forgotten.

L'oc los intermane (cos noncupa√∞) y los yon dessur y Nu√ßal a Elved entr un princes Markesc e√∞ y sovran de Nor√∞humbr Dannesc er solemneya√∞, rix entr y dou polity coyent tras un siecr pres enter.
This block was (allegedly) the block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised, ending hostilities between the two polities for almost a century.

Bempart monarch n'Albion sousevent, e cos particuler de Marclont e de Nor√∞humbr, ern coronna√∞ sur y Ca√∞er ne Pie√∞r Roug.
Many subsequent monarchs of Albion, and especially of Markland and Northumber, were crowned on the Redstone Chair.

Durant y Scon Tetrarchy ny siecr on√ß e do√ß, gran part all'Emperour Drengo√ßan asseyen sur y Ca√∞er, e cossy sceint l'er emporta√∞ a Londr.
During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, most of the Drengot Emperors sat upon the Chair, and as such it was relocated to London.

140.2: nothe "forged"

nothe /noÀàŒ∏e/ [n äÀàŒ∏e]
- bastard, illegitimate, born to unmarried parents (when this means one is not able to inherit something);
- counterfeit, forged, claiming falsely to be something genuine;
- spurious, specious, fallacious, superficially plausible but not in fact logically deducible;
- (botany, zoology) false, denoting various plants and animals which resemble but which are not other species

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from Scholastic Latin notheus "illegitimate, bastard", a pleonastic extension of synonymous nothus in Classical Latin. Originally restricted to legal contexts (where it replaced terms like bort and cif which were considered vulgar), it is now in general use. The term coexists as a higher-register term with French-derived bastart, which is somewhat crass.

corporal /ÀàkoÀê.p…ô.…æ…ôl/ [elements] a pure substance comprising atoms with a single number of mora [protons]; a single atom of such a substance. 1766 < earlier adjective "material, concrete, physical" < Scholastic Latin corporƒÅlis < corpus "body, substance".

murine /Ààmj…ôu.…æ…ôin/ [chlorine] the corporal whose atoms have 17 mora, found as a green air which is highly poisonous. 1805 < Scholastic Latin murƒ´nus "murine; (adj.) briny" < muria "brine, salt solution" < Greek ·ºÅŒªŒº·ø°œÅ·øêÃÅœÇ ‚Ä¢ halm≈´r√≠s "saltiness". surfeited /ÀàsoÀêf…™.t…™d/ [negatively charged] of an atom, endowed with an excess of plenty [electric charge]. 1891 < verb surfeit "oversupply" < Drengot French surfeire "exceed" < Latin super "over" and faci≈ç "I make".

tagline /Ààta.gl…ôin/ [fluorine] the corporal whose atoms have 9 mora, found as a wan yellow-green air which is highly poisonous. 1808 < Scholastic Latin taglƒ´nus, originally applied to minerals containing surfeited tagline [F‚Åª] < Napolitan taglino (1643) "fondant [flux], substance for lowering the melting point of metals" < Arabic ÿ™ŸÑŸäŸäŸÜ ‚Ä¢ talyƒ´n "softening; fondant".

##

141.5: Provence

collected notes on the polity of Provence.

Provence (Doccian Proen√ßa /p ÄuÀà…õ.s…î/) is a polity in the south of Gaul [France] on the Middlesea [Mediterranean] coast. It first became independent in 1499, and its capital city is Marsella [Marseille].

Toponymy
The name Provence descends directly from the Latin name Provincia Romana, so called as it was the first Roman province to be established on the Gaulish side of the Alps. The name of the polity's predominant language, Doccian, derives from the division of the French languages into the lengas d'oil (Northern, including Roun [Rouen] and Paris French) and the lengas d'oc (Southern, including Vascon and Doccian) depending on their word for "yes".

History
Medieval Provence was a vassal of various greater powers for almost a millennium. It was incorporated into the expanding Francian Empire in the sixth century and remained part of its western fragment after the empire was divided in two in the ninth. By the early eleventh century it had become mostly autonomous, but it was conquered once more as the last piece of the First Drengot Empire.

After the 1170 Emergency at Dijon, the Kingdom of Burgundy left Drengot control, taking the country of Provence with it. Provence was elevated to a duchy in the thirteenth century under the house of Savoy, and as the direct link between the capital at Geneva and all of Middlesea sea-trade, it became known as the panier de Borgogne (the 'Burgund breadbasket'). Provence was conquered by Vascony during the Vascon Ascendancy of the early fifteenth century, but was returned to Burgundy during the reign of Ambrose III in 1494.

Along with this political turmoil, the late fifteenth century saw extensive piracy in the Middlesea; in Marsella the raiding verged on occupation, and many of the wealthy fled to safer pastures inland. Aid requests presented to Rudolf VIII went largely unanswered, and things came to a head on the 21st of June 1497 when Daniel Menton (Lord Amig√≤t) led the local aristocracy in declaring independence from Geneva (to this day the 21st of June is a national holiday in Provence). The War of Provincial Independence lasted two years until Burgundy's defeat at the Battle of Marg√®s.

Daniel Menton led the new republic for the next eighteen years, manoeuvring against a hungry Barcelon and their piratical allies who de facto ruled Marsella (it is generally understood that Lord Amig√≤t established Marsella as the capital of Provence in large part to closely monitor Jondumal's fleets). His successor and cousin, Antoin, ruled for only three years despite the guidance of his mentor and Daniel's friend Guill√®n daus Sanz; the coming of the Second Great Dying ravaged the Amig√≤t dynasty and the Provincial people both.

The following decades saw independent Provence take its place as a preeminent mercantile republic. By the end of the sixteenth century their trade network reached across the Atlantic to port towns in Mendeva [North America] like the newly-established Santrafew. These towns would eventually coalesce into the territory of New Provence.

By the end of the nineteenth century, the power of New Provence had eclipsed that of its Vetomundine progenitor. As the Drengot Collusion formed to the north, Provence became in many respects a European outpost of New Provence, in a reversal of their respective statures centuries earlier. The region stood with Vascony as a bulwark against Drengot hegemony until the Furore of 1961 and the collapse of the Drengot Collusion.

References
The Latin Republics: Jewels under Tyrian Standards by Jacob Trelaune, published by the Yievle Brethin Primers, 1902

##

142.2: beuscr "monster"

beuscr /bauxrÃ©/ [Ààbau.x…ê]
- monster, beast, an animal considered dangerous and violent;
- brute, lout, hooligan, thug, a person considered to behave like a mindless and violent beast;
- bro, dude, guy, an ironic term of approbation for a male friend (especially one who has just been particularly cool);
- (colloq, adv.) very, extremely, particularly, to a surprisingly great extent.

Etymology: somehwat muddled. The -scr ending likely descends from Latin diminutive suffix -usculus, here used in either a belittling or euphemistic sense. The rest is thought to reflect Latin bƒìlua "wild animal, beast", although the development of the /l/ is irregular.

In ironic usage it is attested from the early twentieth century; as a generic intensifier somewhat later. The derived comparative beuscerrem "to an even greater extent" is seen only very occasionally as a one-off neologism.

142.3: ec ogl qu√Ør "close one's eyes"

ec ogl qu√Ør /…õk …îil kwaiÀà…™r/ […™Ààg…îil kw…êÀà ù…™Àê]
- close one's eyes, to cover one's eyes with one's eyelids so that one cannot see;
- ignore, turn a blind eye, to intentionally fail to recognise that something (usually bad) is happening

Etymology: fixed idiom literally meaning "to extinguish eyes". Both this and its opposite ec ogl accendr "open one's eyes, lit. light eyes" first appear in the early Middle Borlish period, presumably via the notion of making things light and dark. In metaphorical use the phrase is seen from the fifteenth century.

The noun ogl "eye" descends unproblematically from Latin oculus "eye"; the verb qu√Ør "extinguish, darken; turn off, switch off" comes from Latin quiƒìsc≈ç "quieten, stop" after analogy to other inchoative verbs like finir < fƒ´ni≈ç~fƒ´niƒìsc≈ç "to finish".

142.4: oundrar "wound"

oundrar /unÀàdrar/ [ änÀàd Ä…êÀê]
- wound, injure, hurt, harm, to cause an injury or similar harm to;
- damage, break, impair, to cause not to function correctly;
- offend, spite, insult, to hurt the feelings or honour of

Etymology: from Old Borlish (v)ounrar, oundar "wound, injure", a conflation of the descendant of Latin vulner≈ç "I wound" and the borrowing of Old Norrish unda "to wound" (which are unrelated). The loss of the initial consonant would be unexpected if the Latin etymon was the only one; however, this was a regular feature of Old Norrish.

##

142.5: Tremonnow

Tremonnow (Merch /t…æ…ôÀàm…î.n…ô/, Welsh Trevonwy /Àåt…æe.v…ôÀànui/) [Monmouth] is a town in the southeast of Wales, the administrative centre of the district of Ux [1].

Toponymy

The name Tremonnow derives from the Welsh trew "town" and the name of the river it lies on, the Monnow (compare Welsh Mynwy "the Monnow", which is of unclear origin).

History

The lands around Tremonnow were conquered from the medieval Welsh kingdom of Guent [Gwent] by Vigo the Magnificent during the formation of the kingdom of Greater Devon in the first half of the eighth century.

The Christian order called the Brethin was founded in Tremonnow at some point before 800 N under the patronage of Vigo's daughter Iseld. The order would become renowned for their efforts at historical documentation, and their archives in what eventually became the modern Tremonnow Gravatory preserve invaluable sources on early medieval Albion [Great Britain] (including a copy of the Chronicle of Carlile [Carlisle]).

From the mid-twentieth century, the Tremonow Open School, formerly known as the Brethin Mesh Institute, provided distance learning in Welsh and Wessern [2] across Albion and, latterly, also across British Mendeva.

People
Dewock Barclythe (1452-1539 N), Friar of Tremonnow and for several decades the prime Factor of Records at the Brethin House there.

---

[1] roughly corresponding to Monmouthshire, named for area's main river [the Usk], from an old Welsh word for "water".

[2] the English language variety spoken as a recognised minority language in Britain [Wales, Devon and Cornwall, Brittany, Cumbria, Strathclyde, Man and the Isles, parts of Ireland], mostly in its eastern regions bordering Markland and Kent.

##

143.5: In the Solar System

    excerpt taken from 1936 work Tell Me Everything About the Solar System [1], part of the Tell Me, Telmy! series of children‚Äôs educational books. These were originally written by Adamara Godwilham [2] and featured a fictionalised version of her father Bartelmy, ‚ÄúTelmy‚Äù, acting as guide and entertainer. After her death she bequeathed the series to her publishers, Zizania Nexus, who have continued the series to the present day.

‚Ä¶found the four biggest jovial moons right in the middle of the Revitalist period. This time during the sixteenth century was when scholars and artists across the Middlesea [Mediterranean] and the Persias looked back to the works and beliefs of Classical Greece and Rome.

As word spread of the first really new celestial bodies to be discovered for thousands of years, people came up with lots of names for these pharƒ´ Iovis ‚Äúlighthouses of Jove‚Äù. Some people wanted to bring glory to their patrons, lords or lands. Others reached to retellings of Classical legends which were in fashion.

The convention that has lasted in European languages, and which has been maintained since we have found many more small jovial moons in the last century, has been to name Jupiter‚Äôs gyrers [satellites] for his mortal descendants. We have done the same with the two moons of Mars, Dimus [Phobos] and Phobus [Deimos], which are named for his twin sons and attendants.

In myth, √Üacos was the grandfather‚Ä¶

144.2: cavir yert "boot up"

cavir yert /kaÀàv…™r j…õrt/ [k…êÀàv…™Àê  ù…õÀêt]
- wake up, come to, stir, to regain consciousness;
- turn on, switch on, activate, start, boot up, to begin operating;
- (of a business) open, to start serving customers

Etymology: early Modern Borlish idiom literally meaning "to get awake". The verb cavir "get" is a direct descendant of capi≈ç "take, pick, recieve", used in the sense "become" since the Middle Borlish period. The adjective yert "awake, alert; (of a business) open" descends via Old Borlish yerte "standing, upright, vertical" and synonymous ƒìrctus in Vulgar Latin from Classical ƒìrƒìctus "raised, built, erected". Extended from people to machinery and thence to businesses by the nineteenth century.

/Àå ía.teÀàn…îi k…îz …™nÀåfri.niÀàx…õnt tanÀàd…™k i gr…™Ààmur kaÀàve j…õrt/

144.4: congenir "conceive"

congenir /Àåk…în. íeÀàn…™r/ [Àåk…î…≤. ù…™Ààn…™Àê]

In modern usage the verb is preferentially used as an infinitive or participle, although conjugated forms are still well attested.

##

144.5: Mashick

Mashick (Borlish Mascic /maÀàx…™k/, Nawat Maishƒ´'co /maiÀà ÉiÀê î.ko/, Mashick Arabic ÿßŸÑŸÖÿ¥ŸäŸÇ ¬∑ al-Mashƒ´q) is a polity in Lower Mendeva and in fact by land area the largest polity in all of Mendeva. At its greatest extent the then-Empire stretched as far northwest as Mivock.

Morrack vessels of the New World Company established trading relations in the late fifteenth century with the relatively-new Mashick Empire, which at that time was much more geographically restricted. The next decades saw many Novomundine crops make their way across the Atlantic from Mashick, including tashban and shockle.

Mashick (and, to the south, Tavancy) served as bases for exploration of Mendevan lands. Fleets charted as far afield as the Ax, and Mashick was used to exercise control over parts of the Arcabil and their growing riches.

Nineteenth-century Mashick was one of the hotbeds of the nascent political philosophy of Ubiquity. It lagged behind British Mendeva in the adoption of the new morals of the Household Renovation, although by the early twentieth century it had caught up (unlike the more regressive New Provence).

The Lovac Wars of the 1920s and 30s saw the first major bombing campaigns on civilian targets, including many cities in Mashick like Moshtar. The unrest and economic downturn following these wars, more than actual military losses, led to Mashick losing significant parts of its northern territory.

In the modern day Mashick is a major food exporter. Droughts in the late 1960s led to reduced amback and mause supplies and sharply rising prices for these fruits in the Vetomund.

People

Mollam Bushta (fl. c19), composer remember for his pieces featuring the torriot and an ally of the early Deviance movement.

Yollamaniza Elcabir (fl. 1943), spy and later author of La te Hozuho Sineton (No Slumber Will Seize Him). The work's depiction of Mashick's Ministry for Knowledge was revealing enough that he was forced to flee the country to avoid arrest.

Tazim Gennun (fl. 1823), author of short story 'The Witch of Tebjacka'.

S√≥lin Izco√°l (fl. c18, Latinised as Solinus Iscovalis and known in Merch as Iscoval), the life scientist who first expounded a scientific account of evolution. He is responsible for the terms 'Iscovalian variation' and 'living web'.

Poshack Nerwaman (fl. 1848), famed lady explorer whose written accounts remained popular works of writing into the twentieth century.

##

145.5: Furlain

Furlain (Merch pronunciation /f äÀêÀàl…ôin/, endonym Furlagne /furÀàla…≤…ô/) is a polity in the northeast of the Italian peninsula, whose principal cities are Verona and Odin. It borders Lombardy to the west, Bavarn to the north, Crain to the east, and Venise to the south.

History

During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, the March of Furlain was the southernmost part of the German Empire, at first a direct imperial vassal and later specifically consolidated under the suzerainty of the Kingdom of Bavarn. After the collapse of the German Empire in the thirteenth century, Furlain's most frequent rival became the Republic of Venise.

Furlain suffered a severe earthquake in 1348 which destroyed both the castle and cathedral in Odin [Udine]. Reference to this event is the first attestation of the word quagment "earthquake" in the Borlish language.

The armourcloth trend in women's fashion (which emulated men's military dress) began in Furlain during the Long Peace in the late eighteenth century. The local term voinacciale (from voina, a slang term for a soldier) was borrowed into several other languages to describe the style.

Troops from Furlain fought in the Second German War (1815‚Äì23), principally against Bavarn. Furlain was allied with such countries as Borland, with whom they undertook the disastrous Battle of Hulm in 1821.

People

Chiarina Bruno (fl. 1824), librettist and composer who wrote the opera Wings Afire.
Jacomo Bruno (fl. 1821), twin of the better-known Chiarina and soldier of the Second German War.

##

145.7: 1st Lexember - des caucet "utterly"

des caucet /d…õz koÀàts…õt/ [d…õz k äÀàts…õt]
- utterly, absolutely, downright, to the fullest extent possible;
- completely, totally, all the way, all-out, every inch, done to completion;
- irreversibly, irretrievably, irrevocably, in a way that cannot be taken back

Etymology: idiom attested from the late nineteenth century, literally "out of (one's) socks". Thought to have arisen in Damvath slang as a sexual metaphor (taking one's socks off prior to sleeping with someone), although in modern usage it is not considered at all obscene. The noun caucet "sock" is a diminutive of obsolete cau√ß "hose, stockings, tights", via Medieval Latin calcea from Classical calceus "boot".

Nos stou la javigar des caucet.
/n…îz stu la Àå ía.viÀàgar d…õz koÀàts…õt/
[n…îz stu la Àå ùa.v…™Ààg…êÀê d…õz k äÀàts…õt]

146.1: 2nd Lexember - deunx "close shave"

deunx /daunks/ [daunks]
- near thing, close call, close shave, a situation where an injury or other undesirable outcome is narrowly avoided;
- (interj.) an expression of shock or relief at such a situation;

deunx fair
- get lucky, dodge a bullet, to narrowly escape an undesirable outcome

Etymology: university slang among the Vithor New School's students, attested there from the eighteenth century and entering the wider lexicon some decades afterwards. The noun is a borrowing from Latin deunx "eleven twelfths", related to uncia "one twelfth". The story linking its modern usage to a twelve-man jury is almost certainly false; more likely is simply the sense of being almost but not quite the whole thing.

Nos farreu noc deunx cossy alche√∞r.
/n…îz faÀàrau n…îk daunks k…îÀàsi alÀàk…õ√∞rÃ©/
[n…îz f…êÀà Äau n…îg daunks k äÀàsi …êÀàg…õ.√∞…ê]
1p do-fut.1p ng close.shave thus again
We won't get so lucky again.

146.3: 4th Lexember - verdeyant "lush"

verdeyant /Àåv…õr.diÀàjant/ [Àåv…õÀê.d…™Àà ùant]
- verdant, green, lush, abundant with (especially new-grown) vegetation;

146.4: 5th Lexember - ro√ß "cabbie"

ro√ß /r…îts/ [ Ä…îts]
- cabbie, taxi driver, driver hired by a passenger and paid according to the distance and time of the trip;
- cab, taxi, the vehicle driven by such a person

Etymology: early 20C from Napolitan rozzo "cabbie", further origin disputed. Formally identical with earlier rozzo "boor, lout", from Latin adjective rudis "rough, unskilled, rude", but this term seems to have been obsolete by the mid-19th century. 146.5: 6th Lexember - lan√ßbal "lineball"

lan√ßbal /ÀålantsÀàbal/ [ÀålansÀàbal]
- lineball, a particular team sport similar to handball or rugby;
- the ball used in this sport;
- (loosely, informal) other team sports involving throwing a ball;

Twentieth Century History

excerpt from a 2021 book called Excourse Puzzlers, published by the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse. This was a collection of casual party games using the branding and popularity of a long-running series begun by Merch couple Anna and Ralph Highwater in the 1930s.

Twentieth Century History II (answers on sheet 143)

1. In which Mendevan [North American] polity were the first sobot [radio] transmitters and receivers invented in 1903?

2. Which of the adolescences [coming-of-age novels] of Dorotha Quistack, published in 1917, won the Hatfell Honour and was adapted into a film starring Joke Pentawer just two years later?

3. Who led the government whose collapse in 1925 was the beginning of the so-called Portingale Anarchy?

4. What is the full name of the painkiller present in "Tommy pills", first synthesised in 1932 as the result of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory and the University of Rimack?

5. How was the length of the (dime) foot redefined in 1946, giving it the value it has to this day?

6. Who was the monarch of Hasiny whose death due to a convoy accident in 1950 is alleged to have been purposefully orchestrated with the knowledge of his grandson Pether?

7. Which national team achieved its only victory in the Lineball Global Tourney to date in 1965?

8. As part of the 120-year celebrations of the Laic Declarions of Belgrade, what was legalised by laws passed simultaneously in 1970 across over a dozen polities?

9. What was the name given to the natural disaster which struck Mashick [Mexico] on the 19th of September 1985?

10. The restored steamship displayed from 1991 to 1995 in Deganny [Deganwy, Wales] was originally part of what polity's "Culomb√°lu" fleet?

146.6: 7th Lexember - clacqment "clatter"

clacqment /klakÀàm…õnt/ [kl…êkÀàm…õnt]
- clatter, rattle, a succession of loud and abrupt noises;
- gunfire, the sound of firearms being repeatedly shot;
- racket, din, commotion, ruckus, an environment of loud noise and disturbance;
- controversy, discord, strife, bitter conflict arousing much debate or even violence

Etymology: noun formation from clacquar "rattle, clatter, cause a disturbance". This was borrowed in the Middle Borlish period from contemporary French clacquer "clap, slap, slam", or directly from that word's Germanic origin (compare Willem or Kentish clacken "whack, shake"). Used for gunfire since the eighteenth century, and for general disruption from the nineteenth.

Jo sveilau des y clacqment a jarry jusser.
/ ío sviÀàlo d…õz i klakÀàm…õnt a  íaÀàri  íiÀàs…õr/
[ ùo zv…™Ààlo d…õz i kl…êkÀàm…õnt a  ù…êÀà Äi  ù…™Ààs…õÀê]
1s wake-pst from df clatter at crockery downstairs
I woke to the clatter of plates downstairs.

146.7: 8th Lexember - inquillin "roommate"

inquillin /Àå…™n.kw…™Ààl…™n/ [Àå…™≈ã.kw…™Ààl…™n]
- tenant, lodger, renter, one dwelling in a residence they do not own;
- roommate, dormmate, a person with whom one shares living quarters;
- (crude, euphemistic) bedfellow, bedmate, mistress, a sexual partner one lives with

Influenced in sense by the unrelated noun quil "bed" and verb inquillir "embed"; these descend from Latin cubƒ´le "bed, lair, kennel", which is related to cub≈ç "I lie down". The euphemistic sexual sense has a possible citation in the seventeenth century, but is only securely attested in the nineteenth.

147.1: 9th Lexember - bount "en route"

bount /bunt/ [bunt]
- en route, heading, on the way, along the way, in the course of travelling from one place to another;
- directed, pointing, facing, oriented in a particular direction;
- determined, willing, intent, bent, wanting to do something;

Alburgh Anecdotes

147.2: 10th Lexember - tourm "troop"

tourm /turm/ [t äÀêm]
- troop, squadron, company, platoon, a body of soldiers;
- barracks, base, fort, fortification, encampment, a structure built for the (temporary or permanent) housing and mustering of soldiers;
- crew, squad, gang, a group of people brought together to do a particular task;
- team, group of people playing on the same side of a sport or game;
- swarm, colony, hive, collective noun for various insects

Etymology: Middle Borlish, via the conflation of contemporary French tourme "troop" and Dutch torem "tower, fort". The former is a borrowing from an Italian descendant of Latin turma "troop, team", while the latter is probably a borrowing from a Romance decendant of Latin turris "tower". As a collective noun it is attested from the sixteenth century for ants and somewhat later for some other animals.

Stoura un tourm ottem all'oc kevlar.
/stuÀàra …™n turm Àà…î.t…õm aÀàl…îk k…õÀàvlar/
[st äÀà Äa …™n t äÀêm Àà…î.t…êm …êÀàl…îk k…™Ààvl…êÀê]
need-fut indf crew better to-df=s.px heist
This heist will need a better crew.

147.3: 11th Lexember - alcant aposc "eventually"

alcant aposc /alÀàkant aÀàp…îx/ […êÀàgan.t…êÀàp…îx]
- some day, in the future, eventually, at some point, after an unspecified amount of time;
- (interjection) about time, finally, jibe at someone or something taking a notionally excessive amount of time

Etymology: idiom found in this form since the eighteenth century, literally "once later". The adverb alcant "once" is an analogical form derived from cant "when" with the word element alc- < Latin aliquƒ´ "some". The adverb aposc "later" is from the preposition posc "after", from Latin post quid "after what". As an interjection it is only securely attested in the twentieth century.

/kw…™Ààpe l…îk Àåha.vaÀàra …™n …îm alÀàkant aÀàp…îx/

147.4: 12th Lexember - tegmn "shelter"

tegmn /ÀàtijmnÃ©/ [Ààtij.m…ên]
- shelter, cover, protection, refuge, a physical structure that keeps one safe;
- (specifically) shade, an area shielded from direct sunlight;
- buffer, cushion, anything used to isolate or minimise the effect of one thing on another;
- leeway, grace, wiggle room, freedom, flexibility, latitude, a range in which one can operate safely

This noun comes from verb teg≈ç "I cover, conceal", which became Old Borlish ter "span, cover (an amount of land)" and thence modern teir "amount to, number". Extended to safety in more abstract contexts by the early modern period.

147.5: 13th Lexember - zizania "discord"

zizania /Àåzi.zaÀànja/ [Àåzi.z…êÀànja]
- discord, disharmony, dissonance, cacophony, a harsh combination of sounds;
- discord, tension, strife, dissension, arguing, lack of agreement of harmony between people;
- anguish, distress, angst, inner conflict;
- temptation, spite, vice, sin, feeling drawn to doing wicked things

Etymology: seventeenth-century borrowing from Lombard zizania "ryegrass, weed; discord, vice", which displays a metaphorical extension dating back to its ancestor in Late Latin zizanium "cockle, ryegrass, darnel; vice", a borrowing from Greek. Its modern association with cacophonous noise is probably due to sound symbolism.

We heard arguing through the boardroom's wall.

All Change

collected notes on a variety of topics.

Vigo VII (fl. 1121) was king of Greater Devon [Devon, Conrwall, Brittany] in the twelfth century, which was a major vassal of the First Drengot Empire under Emperor Roger II (and later briefly under Roger's son). In 1121 N he invaded the south coast of Wales, attempting a reconquest of the southeastern Welsh principalities, without the support of the Emperor.

Zahid Russian is a Slavone language spoken in Crovatia, Servia and surrounding regions, although having little official recognition anywhere. Despite its current location it is mostly closely related to eastern Slavone languages like Kievan [~Ukrainian]; it descends from communities in the late medieval and early modern period who migrated westward due to the disruption of Islamic conquests from the south. The language is named –∑–∞ÃÅ—Ö—ñ–¥ ‚Ä¢ z√°hid, meaning "west". It is the source of some words in modern Merch (and other Englishes), such as rasgatto "d√©nouement", which comes via Venisian from –Ý–∞–∑–≥–∞–¥—Ç–æ, a nineteenth-century mystery trevold by Olexi Harchenko.

New Skies, Old Stories (originally published in Provincial French as Nov√® Cial, Anzan Conte) is a work of popular history detailing the origins of the various threads of parachthon [scifi/fantasy] fiction. It was written by Simon d'Estiala and published by Prindor Marsill√®s in 1980. Its thesis is that many of the tropes often considered to have been invented by particular parachthon authors are in fact simply examples of existing tropes used in new settings.

Margaret of Iscombe [Exeter] (fl. 1435) was the wife of Mark of Tolose and the mother of King Ambrose III of Vascony. She married at some point shortly before 1435 N. She bore four children who survived infancy, including the future King Ambrose: her first child Alexandra and two younger children Victor (later Duke of Astorga) and Natalia.


##

147.6: 14th Lexember - a pla√ß entarn "some way in"

a pla√ß entarn /a plats …õnÀàtarn/ […ê Ààplats …™nÀàt…êÀên]
- some way in, lodged, embedded, inside, located (usually not far) underneath the surface of something;
- along, at some point in the length of some object or space;
- during, at some point in the course of some time period;
- after a while, not at first, eventually, only at some time later than the beginning of something

Etymology: phrase meaning "at (a) position inside", used idiomatically from as early as the fourteenth century. The noun pla√ß "position" is an Old Borlish borrowing via Old French place "location" from Latin platea "courtyard". The adverb entarn "inside", which corresponds to the preposition ne "in", descends directly from Latin adjective internus "internal, interior".

Y melody dy sanfoin me hamtau a pla√ß entarn.
/i Àåme.loÀàdi di sanÀàf…îin me hamÀàto a plats …õnÀàtarn/
[i Àåme.l äÀàdi di s…ê…±Ààf…îin me h…êmÀàto …ê Ààplats …™nÀàt…êÀên]

147.7: 15th Lexember - kiveury "herdtale"

kiveury /Àåki.vauÀàri/ [Àåki.v…êuÀà Äi]
- herdtale, a genre of fiction originating in nineteenth-century Hasiny and depicting rural (prototypically, cattle-herding) life as a virtuous idyll;
- pastoralism, bucolicism, Arcadianism, the idealisation of rural life perceived as morally superior to urban modernity

Etymology: 19th-century derivative of kiveu "cattle, herd" with the suffix -ry "a collection of things, a shop, a gestalt quality", coined as a calque of Hasinic Welsh jade≈Çath "herd-dom". The noun kiveu descends from Old Borlish kifew "cattle", a borrowing a Borland Old English cyfeoh "cattle-herd". To describe an ideology the term is seen from the early twentieth century, usually in critical contexts.

Jo cof un grivougl de kiveury ag quarvol.

[ ío k…îf …™≈ã g Ä…™Ààvuil de Àåki,v…êuÀà Äi …õj kw…êÀê]
1s get.pst indf scribble of herdtale at.df airport
I got a herdtale potboiler at the airport.

148.1: 16th Lexember - prophthan "faster than light"

prophthan /pr…îfÀàŒ∏an/ [p Å äfÀàŒ∏an]
- faster than light, FTL, superluminal, travelling more quickly than the so-called "normal speed" of ten great leagues per second;
- (informal) at warp speed, double-time, lightning fast, with any extremely rapid pace

Etymology: coined in 1949 by Napolitan author Ludgia Pitceri as awareness of the normal speed entered popular culture and parachthon (fantasy/sci-fi) writers reached for various justifications for speedy interstellar travel. The word is a borrowing from Scholastic Greek œÄœÅŒøœÜŒ∏Œ¨ŒΩœâŒΩ ‚Ä¢ prophth√°n≈çn "preceding, overtaking", from œÜŒ∏Œ¨ŒΩœâ ‚Ä¢ phth√°n≈ç "I precede, overtake". In its imprecise use the word is attested from the 1970s.

Maths and Gossip

excerpt in translation from the correspondence of Darren Brodus to Nikl√°s der Fecht; this letter was sent in 1828.

Y majon livr a me forni√∞ tandem con y homily de D√ºmmeyer a them de tas e mission; jo nil attendr a te molfeyar ne fegndr a a√Ør le leut, ma se po√∞ t'ai√ßar ig jo vil l'hour stovent beno√ßar l'oc semman me√∞es!

148.2: 17th Lexember - ja sceint "extant"

ja sceint / ía xint/ [ ùa √ßint]
- extant, in existence, surviving, not yet having disappeared or died;
- attested, recorded, present, in evidence, proven to exist or have once existed;
- (orig. law) preexisting, being present before some other event (and thus having certain legal privileges or protections)

Etymology: phrase meaning "still being there", used as legal jargon from the early Middle Borlish period. The adverb ja "already, still, yet" descends directly from Latin iam "already, now" (the homonym ja "yes" is a Germanic borrowing and thus likely unrelated). The participle sceint "being there, existing" comes from the verb sceir "for there to be" used in existential sentences (scey uncos "there is something"). This descends via Old Borlish seer, sier, scer "exist, be located" from Latin sede≈ç "I sit".

Cay gravatoir y massem de descovan ja sceint dy siecr cator√ß.
/ke Àågra.vaÀàt…îir i Ààma.s…õm de Àåde.xoÀàvan  ía xint di Ààsj…õ.krÃ© kaÀàt…îrts/

148.3: 18th Lexember - ersteigon "climax"

ersteigon /Àå…õr.stiÀàg…în/ [Àå…õÀê.st…™Ààg…în]
- climax, the high point of a narrative or a piece of music;
- peak, apogee, zenith, acme, pinnacle, culmination, the highest point or best state of something;

l'ersteigon yembr "come to a head; reach a critical point"

Etymology: originally a term of art, borrowed from Saxon ersteigon "rise, climb, ascension", a noun from steige "climb". This properly referred to the events before the climax, but by the time of borrowing the shift in meaning had become entrenched. The term was used in more general contexts in Borlish by the mid-nineteenth century.

148.4: 19th Lexember - euxon "quartz"

euxon /aukÀàs…în/ […êukÀàs…în]
- quartz, an abundant mineral made of crystalline or amorphous silicon dioxide;
- jargon, technobabble, scientific words or concepts that do not (or appear to not) make any sense

Etymology: compare Scholastic Latin eux≈ç "quartz" and Malchassian Greek Œµ·ΩîŒæœéŒΩŒ±œÇ ‚Ä¢ eux·πìnas "quartz", both first attested in the tweflth century. Traditionally connected to early Ancient Greek ·ºêŒ∞ŒæŒøŒøœÇ ‚Ä¢ e√∫xoos "well-polished" and the verb Œæœçœâ ‚Ä¢ x√∫≈ç "scrape, polish", though this theory has some diachronic and semantic issues. The adjective is attested in Epic literature but no later, and moreover the root pertains very specifically to wood (being related to ŒæœåŒ±ŒΩŒøŒΩ x√≥anon "wooden image" and ŒæœçŒªŒ± ‚Ä¢ x√∫la "timber").

More recently it has been suggested that the word was borrowed into Latin (and then Greek) from a vernacular. Multiple early attestations have led scholars to propose that the word might have come from Borlish itself. If so, the most accepted theory is that the word is originally from attested Old English eolh-sond "amber", literally "elk-sand".

The latter sense is seen mostly in the idiom l'oc m'es euxon (literally "this is quartz to me"), used to express unfamiliarity with a technical field.

The Fourteenth Century

collected notes on various features of the fourteenth century.

Nicolas of Avignon (Provincial Nicol√Ý s Avignon /ni.koÀàl…î sa.viÀà…≤uÃÉ/, fl. 1347) was a renowned Burgund architect. He is principally remembered for leading the renovation and expansion of Saint Sophia's Cathedral in Stamboul, the capital city of the Second Roman Empire. His use of experimental techniques in supporting the central dome are alleged to have rendered the entire main body of the cathedral slightly unstable. For this reason he is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic collapse of the building during a coronation ceremony three hundred years later that killed most of the imperial family (and hundreds more) and sparked the unrest that would end the Empire for good.

The Noccair Lyceum (Borlish Lyce a Noccair /liÀàtse a n…îÀàker/) was established in the early 1300s in the minor town of Noccair; it was the first institute of higher learning on Borland. Like many similar European universities, it initially taught students the so-called Five Romantic Arts of literature, argument, reckoning, geometry and songcraft. By the end of the century alchemy had been introduced to the curriculum.

The Tales of Enfield Wood was a compendium of folk tales compiled in Kent in the late fourteenth century. It is usually credited to the author John of Yare, although this has been questioned. It contains many interpretations of episodes in the life of the Farmer Duke, a folk hero common to English-speaking peoples, as well as demonstrating the growing divergence between the Kentish and Merch tongues.

148.6: 21th Lexember - grivougl "scrawl"

grivougl /griÀàvuil/ [g Ä…™Ààvuil]
- scrawl, scribble, chickenscratch, illegible handwriting;
- potboiler, shlock, hackwork, a written work of no artistic merit

Etymology: deverbal noun from grivoglar "scrawl, scribble, write illegibly", a borrowing via synonymous French grivouiller from Willem criblen "scratch". The second sense is attested from the early twentieth century.

Quippe porra tu noc my grivougl leir.

[kw…™Ààpe p äÀà Äa ti n…îg mi g Ä…™Ààvuil l…™Àê]

Of course you can't read my scrawl.

149.1: 23rd Lexember - nuton "fad"

nuton /niÀàt…în/ [n…™Ààt…în]
- will-o'-the-wisp, marshlight, ignis fatuus, a pale flickering light appearing at night over swampland;
- caprice, whim, fancy, vagary, an idle impulse or idea;
- fad, fashion, craze, rage, a short-lived general public interest in something

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from various French forms such as luiton, nuitin, lutin, neton, all meaning "sea monster; mischievous spirit; will-o'-the-wisp". These appear to have been influenced by luire "shine" and nuit "night"; the oldest form is netun "sea monster", which descends from Latin Neptunus "the sea god Neptune".

The second sense is seen from as early as the sixteenth century; the last only in the twentieth.

Entrou 1: Comoscon po√∞e Brittan sy stat de prestan√ß cultural sur Mendeva Brittanesc vardir durant l'e√ß anna√∞ final ny siecr novo√ß cant me√∞es l'o√ß un sovra Brittan cas pouvlan√ß e laboriostur, e di√ßr alcot sta l'oc famosta√∞ por ig y moralta√∞ dy Renovella√ßon Famigler fosn enaja√∞ vars y Novomont?

Revisionist History

excerpted from The Realignment of Borlish Inflection and Derivation, an article in the Winter 1909 issue of Theoretical Uncoverings in the History of Languages. This is a venerable scientific journal published at that time through the University of Lester [Leicester].

‚Ä¶undeniable that the modern form -aurn of the first conjugation's third-person plural preterite, as well as its ancestor in Old Borlish -avren /-Ààau.r…ôn/, would descend regularly from the Latin *pluperfect* -ƒÅvƒïrant and is less straightforward a reflex of the Latin perfect -ƒÅvƒìrunt (from which we would na√Øvely expect modern Borlish -eirn). Nevertheless, the attestation, though rare, of syncopated perfect -ƒÅrunt (from which Vascon -aron, among many others) offers a plausible direct ancestor. This is especially tempting if we are permitted to assume influence from the other persons of the Borlish preterite, all of which employ an -au- theme.

We turn to perhaps the most contentious claim of this article. The fate of the Latin occupational suffix -ƒÅtor in Borlish has been a perennial puzzle since Bughter noted that modern Borlish -our cannot be its simple reflex. It was possible to suspect that an early -a√∞our or -dour was reduced, but absolutely no evidence of such forms has emerged from any medieval manuscript (the word sa√∞our "farmer" is not a counterexample, as the connection of Latin sator to the verb ser≈ç "I sow" was opaque). This further precludes the possibility of mere analogy from third-conjugation verbs (Old Borlish corsor "runner" < Latin cursor), which would have taken time to spread to the much more numerous first-conjugation verbs.

One might also attempt to claim the Borlish suffix is a borrowing from one of the French languages, most of which lost -t- entirely between vowels (e.g. Old French chant(e)or "singer" < cantatorem). We do not deny that such forms reinforced -or in Old Borlish, but the suffix is manifestly native, appearing productively as early as the eighth-century Halvuy chronicle. Our new proposal begins from a few aberrant words in modern dialects and extends back to the first decades of Roman Borland.

Now restricted to northern dialects, the term orrel "penis" is‚Ä¶

sonti√ßta√∞ /Àås…în.t…™tsÀàtaŒ∏/ [Àås…în.t…™Ààstah]
- criticality, necessity, exigency, requirement, the state of needing something (especially when there are significant negative consequences for lacking it);
- linchpin, mainstay, backbone, keystone, sine qua non, a person or thing that is critical to a system working or even existing`

Death in the Spires

It rattled slightly, betraying the tremor of his hands.

150.2: 31st Lexember - scaur ag mur ne Pompe√Ø "write one's name in the history books"

scaur ag mur ne Pompe√Ø /xor …õi m…™r ne Àåp…îm.piÀàji/ [xoÀê‚Äø Ä…õi m…™Àê ne Àåp…îm.p…™Àà ùi]
- write one's name in the history books, make a name for oneself, to do things which will cause one to be renowned for a long time;
- act with posterity in mind, to be conscious of one's legacy when deciding one's actions;
- (jocular) make last-minute revisions, to make rushed changes due to having little time left before a deadline

Etymology: literally "to write on the walls of Pompeii", first attested as an idiom in Napolitan some years after the unearthing of the ruins of Pompeii and the uncovering of its trove of graffiti. The verb scaur "write", which displaced now-obsolete or poetic scrir "write", descends from Latin scalp≈ç "I scratch; I engrave". The last sense, attested in Borlish since the 1920s, refers directly to the volcanic eruption of 79N which buried the city.

L'o√ß segr es dignt tojorn i vil scaur ag mur ne Pompe√Ø.
/l…îts ÀàsijrÃ© …õz daint toÀà í…îrn i v…™l xor …õi m…™r ne Àåp…îm.piÀàji/
[l…îs Ààs…™.j…ê‚Äø Ä…™z daint t äÀà ù…îÀên i v…™l xoÀê‚Äø Äe…™ m…™Àê ne Àåp…îm.p…™Àà ùi]
df=s.dt man be say-p.prs always 3s will write-inf at.df wall in Pompeii
That guy's always saying he'll make a name for himself.

150.3: bucol "idyll"

bucol /biÀàk…îl/ [b…™Ààk…îl]
- idyll, haven, paradise, solace, oasis, a peaceful location or circumstance especially in the countryside;
- respite, break, relief, lull, a (usually temporary) decrease in stress or difficulty;
- reverie, daydream, idle and pleasant thoughts

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a backformation from earlier adjective bucolic "idyllic, peaceful", a borrowing from Latin bucolicus "pastoral, rustic". The word had broadened from the first senses by the late eighteenth century, and has been used for purely mental peace since the early twentieth.

150.4: coyol "cone"

coyol /k…îÀàj…îl/ [k äÀà ù…îl]
- (geometry) cone, the surface formed by rotating a deflected line segment around its end (or the solid thus circumscribed);
- anything conical;
- spire, point, a tapering structure built to cap a roof or tower;
- acme, zenith, peak, cap, summit, the best or highest-quality type of something;
- cap, coif, bonnet, a piece of headgear covering the top and sides of the head

Etymology: via Old Borlish co»ùol "hood; cone" from Latin cuc≈´lla "hood, cowl; conical wrapper or case". Used consistently to translate Scholastic Latin c≈çnus "cone" in geometrical texts since the fourteenth century (possibly the unrelated con "with" discouraged directly borrowing that Latin). In the modern language restricted to detached headgear that is at least vaguely conical.

##

150.5: H is for History

collected notes on three features of the Boralverse alternate history that begin with the letter H.

Hasinic Welsh (Cambroec Hasinia /k…ômÀàbreÀêk h…ôÀàziÀê.nj…ô/) is one of the primary languages of Hasiny [~S Mississippi River], along with the Mendevan language of Jaughta [~Choctaw]. It is mutually intelligible with other varieties of Welsh, although its orthography uses a different standard to British or Chrysian [Australian] Welsh. A few English words come via this language, like herdtale (a calque of jade≈Çath "herd-dom") and sobot "radio" (originating in Jaughta √ßobota "message").

Hatfell Honours (Merch /Ààhat.f…ôl Àà…î.n…ôz/) refer to several prizes awarded since the early twentieth century to artists in various fields across the Drengot Collusion (and, after that alliance-federacy collapsed in 1961, across all of Gaul and the Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB, Borland]). The Honours in literature and drama are among the original set of prizes, while new arts have been admitted as recently as the 2010s. The Honours are named for Hatfell [Hatfield, Hertfordshire], a northern town in London near the border with Markland which was home to a collective of renowned authors in the nineteenth century. In 1918, the writer Dorotha Quistack won a Hatfell Honour for one of her adolescences [coming-of-age novels].

Hostilian (in Borlish often Stiglan /staiÀàlan/, latinised as Hostƒ´liƒÅnus) was a Borlish monk in the ninth century, often considered the father of Borlish history. His most famous work Histori√¶ Terrarum Boralensium "Histories of the Borlish Lands" is a semi-mythic account of Borland for the past five centuries since the arrival of Germanic peoples on the island. It includes a continuous list of rulers from Prase Victor Rossetus, several of which are not attested in any other source. This has led some historiographers to conclude that Hostilian was commissioned by the contemporary monarchy to legitimise their rule.

151.1: The Odyssey

excerpt in translation from the beginning of the Odyssey, as translated into prose Borlish from the original Epic Greek by Rosctey Villautin in 1910. The translation has been criticised for hewing too closely to the Greek syntax at the expense of sounding natural in Borlish.

D'un segr racont mey, Mus, mout tornant, oc magn la√∞

dy larc hom seyon vis, dy ment den seyon connu√∞
of the many people he saw, whose minds he knew

e dy larc cos sur mar seyon souva√∞ e caut ny cour,
and of the many things at sea he endured and took to heart,

gimmolent kire√∞am a sy vi√∞ e√∞ ag repa√∞r de sy commilton.
striving wholeheartedly for his life and his comrades' homecoming.

Ma noc cossy sceus i sy commilton errevir, me√∞es auvorn oblimant;
But not like this could he save his comrades, even longing to so much;

lou desig lorry fol temerta√∞ il perisceurn;
they, for their foolish recklessness, perished;

borel, e√ß des y kiveu dy sol Hyperion
fools, who from the herd of the sun Hyperion

ayent repasc fait e lor i lou effurau deur y jorn de repa√∞r.
dined, and so he deprived them of the day of homecoming.

151.2: nienter "void"

- (rare) void, empty, vacant, not filled or occupied;

Even in early texts the word has seldom been used literally (competing with vogt "empty"); senses to the effect of "amounting to nothing" predominate.

##

151.5: War and Peace

Mystra (in full the Principality of Mystra; Greek: ŒîŒµœÉœÄŒøœÑ·æ∂œÑŒøŒΩ œÑŒø·ø¶ ŒúœÖœÉœÑœÅ·æ∂) was a mordether [crusader] state founded in the eleventh century in the Peloponnese. The holy order of soldiers known as the Convoy Australier was instrumental in conquering the peninsula as a staging post for further attempts at conquest in the Masreck [Levant]. It was named for its then-capital, the city of Mystras. Originally independent, the polity was after some decades reincorporated into the resurgent Roman Empire, and the capital eventually moved to Mysarnos [Pylos]. Mystra was formally dissolved in the mid-seventeenth century as a result of the Romantic Wars.

New Leudong (Welsh: Leudong New√∞ /ÀålyÀêÀàd…î≈ã Àànaw√∞/) is a polity covering the southeast portion of Chrysia [Australia]. Named for the Cathayan state of Leudong, it has experienced significant Cathayan nation-building alongside British settlement since the late seventeenth century. In the early eighteenth century the trade network of Gurjat [Gujarat] extended as far as New Leudong. Born in New Leudong, mixed-heritage writer Pether Hetsong penned semi-autobiographical work The Land of Golden Lies and Sugared Fire in 1864, often considered the first Chrysian work of literature to enjoy Vetomundine [Eurasian] success. New Leudong was an early adopter of the new social mores of the Household Renovation in the late nineteenth century. A collaboration involving the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong's capital invented the chemical painkiller cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine [ibuprofen]. In the late twentieth century the New Leudong city of Mehannin [Brisbane] became a technological hub, spurring developments in grimmer [computer] design and methodics [programming].

To√∞ y mont souprenent y jammel Tovat stan reulu√∞ fort ustolent ant un zelta√∞ a gimmoldr, ig degnant il stan paravant e√ß picq beuscr.

152.5: False Friends

a complete arbitrary selection of dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

carjack [/ÀàkaÀê.d íak/ "breakup"] n. the ending of a romance, especially if unexpected or one-sided; v. intr. of a romance, to end; v. tr. to end a romance with (someone). The term once referred specifically to a letter informing one's partner of the end of your romance. Early 20C < Borlish soldiers' slang carjacq, car-Jacq meaning "dear John". During the Millstone Wars, this referred to a dreaded letter from one's beloved back home cancelling promises of marriage after the war.

danny [/Ààda.ni/ "nuisance"] n. an annoying person or thing, especially when obstructing what one wants to achieve; adj. annoying or obstructive. 10C Old English danneƒ° "stinging nettle" < Old Borlish danney, nanney (√¶q.), thought to reflect a Borland Kelt ?naindia "nettles" (cf Irish ≈Ñand√≥c).

eager [/ÀàeÀê.g…ô/ "acid"] n. a sour or corrosive substance, (in alchemy) a substance which gives up mora [protons] in aqua or directly to another substance; adj. sour or corrosive, (in alchemy) giving up mora in aqua or directly to another substance, (by extension) unlikable or spiteful. Early Merch eagre "sour" < Middle French aigre (√¶q.) This descends from colloquial Latin ƒÅcrus "sour, bitter", variant of ƒÅcer in Classical Latin.

marksman [/ÀàmaÀêks.m…ôn/ "marquess"] n. the noble ruler of (originally) a borderland region known as a mark [march], traditionally ranking above earls due to the prestige of defending the realm's borders. 13C Early Merch < compound of Old English mearc "boundary" and mann "person" used to translate Old French marchis "marquess" in reference to the Marksman of Jerusalem. The term is primarily used for titleholders overseas; within Albion (possibly due to collision with "Markland") predominates the synonymous term edgelord.

153.4: saget "arrowhead"

saget /saÀà í…õt/ [s…êÀà ù…õt]
- arrowhead, the pointed part of an arrow or similar projectile;
- arrow, the pictorial representation of an arrow used to indicate direction;
- chevron, vee, a V shape;
- cursor, pointer, an icon indicating the current active position on a computer screen;
- one of the suits in the so-called "Maritime" card deck [1]

Metaphorical extensions are seen first in heraldic descriptions; the computing sense dates to the late twentieth century.

153.5: Maritime Cards

excerpt from Crossing the Rubicon: a Hidden History of Games, a 1982 work of popular history written (originally in Borlish as Y Rubicon Trasir: un Stoir Scos de Joy) by writer and stage performer Jonnovar Meyaunot. She became famous later in life as a humourist and sobot [radio] host of documentaries and quiz games.

‚Ä¶more common across the Northwestern Isles [Ireland, Britain, Borland], as well as Denmark and Willemy [~Lotharingia]; however, this particular style of deck actually originates in fifteenth-century Poland. The Maritime deck comprises fifty cards organised into five ensigns [suits] and three colours: these are gold, bucklers, staves, jewels and arrows (depicted below).

Many of the cards furthermore have conventional titles separate to their rank and ensign‚Äîfor example, the deuce of staves is called the Duel, while the sept of jewels is known as the Stars. Over time the artwork on these cards has come to incorporate these thematic elements. In games where some cards trump the rest, the gold ensign typically takes this role, although some more modern games will use the aforementioned "named" cards in this way.

The use of a Maritime deck for divination mirrors many other sources of randomness, although the modern occult practices only date back to the early nineteenth century. They appear to have been popularised by Promethean clubs associated with the Deviance movement, and specifically the storied Winchester group.

Early records of the specific rules of card games using the Maritime deck are unfortunately thin on the ground. The first real description of a particular game appears in 1555 court records regarding the gambling debts of one Marc Levosclin‚Ä¶

##

154.5: Northumber

Northumber (Borlish Nor√∞umbr /n…îÀêÀà√∞…™m.brÃ©/) is a polity on the east coast of Albion which borders Markland, Dunclothe and Scotland. Its largest cities are Yorwick (the capital) and Edinbrough.

History

Originally a pair of English kingdoms settled through the fifth and sixth centuries, Northumber was invaded and conquered from the late ninth century onwards by forces from Denmark and Horther. The city of Yorwick is even alleged to have practically given itself over to Dane rule, deposing the alderman and hoping to offer enough loot freely to the invaders so as to avoid the total destruction of the city.

Relations with neighbouring Markland were fraught until the tenth-century Elved Marriage between a Merch princess and the king of Northumber, which ended direct hostilities for nearly a century. Wars continued however with its other neighbours; for example, Northumber captured the city of Edinbrough (then called Eidin) from Dunclothe in 928, taking the last vestiges of Dunclothe's eastern coast.

By the end of the tenth century the territory of Northumber was united under Dane rule from Yorwick. With the waning of the Dane Supremacy the ability of Horther to project its rule overseas collapse; in Albion, Northumber became first among equals in a confederation of the local Dane kingdoms, including Dunclothe, Sodrick and Dublin. At this point Yorwick was by some way the richest city in Albion.

In the eleventh and twelfth centuries, Northumber became the northernmost vassal of the First Drengot Empire, which ruled over much of Albion and most of Gaul.

People

Wolfstan II (fl. c9), last English monarch of Northumber before it fell to Dane forces.

The opera is a retelling of the legend of Icarus which takes place entirely after the events of the myth, with references to the dead Icarus in the dialogue.

The book purports to contain two thousand pieces of unusual or obscure trivia about the world; while most are accurate, the rushed research process led to several inaccuracies and a few flat-out lies finding their way into the book.

‚Ä¶threshold force mill [nuclear power plant] in the Groin [A Coru√±a].

Fortunately, conclaves have never been called simultaneously for both Popes‚Äînobody knows how the archbishop of Waduz would attend both conclaves in such a situation.

The tabre unit of heft, equal to one hundred (dime) ounces [~2.7kg, ~6lb] and first introduced in its modern form with the rest of the dime system in the 1820s, is named after Tabres' batman (Persian man tabr√©z), the principal commercial weight used in the Persian city of Tabres [Tabriz] and its surrounds. It is related to traditional units used across the Vetomund from Kiew [Kyiv] to Madray [Madurai], which vary widely from one-half a tabre to over thirty tabres.

156.4: droccon "driveway"

droccon /dr…îÀàk…în/ [d Ä äÀàk…în]
- lay-by, an indentation in the side of a road for vehicles to stop in;
- parking space, berth, an area where a vehicle is intended to be left;
- driveway, a private area at the front of a dwelling where one might keep one's vehicles

Etymology: twentieth century, a noun formation from verb droccar "notch, indent" calqued from dentelon "lay-by" in Parisian French. The verb comes from droc "notch, indentation", which is of obscure origin. Putative connections with English trough or Welsh rhych "furrow" have too many issues to be workable, and nobody credits a link with draucus "strongman" in Latin; perhaps the word (which is first attested in the Old Borlish period) is simply onomatopoeia.
