It was ten minutes before a bus pulled into the lay-by.

##

156.5: Through the Ages

collected notes on a variety of background figures in Boralverse history, listed in chronological order.

Franco Cicala was the console of the maritime republic Jeans [Genoa] from 1119 until either 1122 or 1124 (records differ). The console being an elected position voted on by the local nobility, Franco would be followed intermittently by over a dozen consoli from among his descendants.

Andrew II was a martial-minded king of Markland in the early fourteenth century. He succeeded his uncle, who is remembered as being far more diplomatic. Andrew II oversaw the Sack of Rexam in 1301, an act which reignited the on-again off-again conflict between Markland and Wales known as the Mallor Wars. The original medieval building at St Brigid's Abbey was razed during this offensive.

Dewock Barclythe (1452-1539 N) was the Friar of Tremonnow and for several decades the prime Factor of Records at the Brethin House there. Records of his correspondence with clergy and academics across western Europe have survived to the present day. These include some of the earliest attestations of dart [vector] geometry, and thus the beginnings of the unification of geometry and computation.

Lord Simon, the sixth Baron Mon√ßating (b. 1770) was an intellectual, a notorious libertine of the Damvath melee, and an early proponent of Deviance Theology in Borland. He was of Warnine [Guaran√≠] descent through his mother. His family had been notorious for decades due to a protracted legal battle between his father and second cousin over the Barony of Haubrath. By the age of eighteen Simon could speak Latin, Dutch (and according to his defamers, Warnine), and he could play the clavier and the torriot [a bassoon-like wind instrument].

Marcio Dourao was an author and rabblerouser best remembered for his work Galatheus, or the Knowledge of Good and Evil. This collection of short stories pastiching Greek mythology was written in 1930 during the decade-long Portingale Anarchy. The title references the story of Galatea and Pygmalion. Galatheus was banned in neighbouring Vascony and Morrack for its background politics and, unofficially, for its throughline of gender exploration; neither polity had quite forgotten their roles in the traditionally-minded Modest Arrangement of the previous century.

##

157.1: Typewriters

notes in translation on the early development of tachygraph [typewriter] technology.

Tachygraph primal de dezein 1830 e 40 stan fatuous cos imman, dec y majorta√∞ a appragl no po√∞en for lettr inscaur con un mendran√ß ag hast de lettran√ß a man doit.
Early tachygraphs in the 1830s and 40s were extremely cumbersome, with most models only capable of setting type at a fraction of the speed of practised handwriting.

Y Tachygraph d'Armanbourg, oc stant accatabr ne Pologn des an 1842, surcayen impernix ne beno√ß a√∞ief por se cavir narreya√∞ ny fougl pavarrot sou nom dy Bradygraph (a verb y "scrittour lent")
The Armanburg Tachygraph, which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygraph (literally the "slow writer").

Ceu incommorant destr, sceyen gimmolt vars po√∞ir tachygraph ligar ag sar campaner, a fin oc se sceus un pistol coutoccar sur papir e lor cos simoltane un facsimile dy pistol bo√∞ar vars un autr hom.
Since very early on, attempts have been made to connect tachygraphs to the steeplemesh, so that one could type a message onto paper and simultaneously transmit a copy of the message to someone else.

Y preu tachygraph po√∞ent cos particuler pistol bo√∞ar par y sar campaner sta y Teleclavia de Vilmanas, oc or√∞enja√∞ ne Lithou par Toman Vilmanas d'an 1859. 
The first tachygraph specifically able to send messages on the steeplemesh was the Vilmanas Teleclavia, designed in Lithow by Tomas Vilmanas in 1859.

Sy bo√∞an√ß primer er conveya√∞ sull'o√ß Sant Mich√¶l vars sell'amig Margreta Legaite ne Tvaniste.
Its first message was sent on Michaelmas of that year to his friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste.

Etymology: borrowed in the late Middle Borlish period from Vascon harif (cf. Leon or Portingale harivo) "misshapen, grotesque; weird, uncanny". This in turn comes from Arabic ÿÆŸéÿ±Ÿêÿ® ‚Ä¢ kharib "ruined, destroyed". The term gained its conventionalised usage in speculative fiction in the late nineteenth century, popularised by the works of Nan.

Jo parceveu my cigl er se portant cos harif.

[ ùo Àåp…êÀê.ts…™Ààvau mi tsail …õÀê se p äÀêÀàtant k…îz h…êÀà Ä…™f]

157.5: Painkiller Philology

notes on the chemistry and linguistics of the painkiller cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine [ibuprofen].

Cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine (as a medicine sold under several names, including "CD" or "Tommy pills") is a hebetant [painkilling] substance developed in the early twentieth century. It was the result of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong [south-eastern Australia] and the University of Rimack [Lima] in Tavancy [descendant state of the Incan Empire], which sought to develop hebetants with faster action and milder adversions [side effects]. It was first synthesised in 1932, and a decade later it had surpassed salix derivatives [e.g. aspirin] in sales worldwide, due to providing better pain relief (as opposed to merely reducing the symptoms of fever).

Name

The name of this substance comprises three parts.

The core of the substance is a benjamine [benzene] ring; the name "benjamine" is borrowed via Scholastic Latin and Vascon from Arabic ŸÑŸèÿ®ŸéÿßŸÜ ÿ¨ŸéÿßŸàŸêŸäŸë‚Äé ‚Ä¢ lubƒÅn jƒÅwiyy, literally "Jawan [Javanese] frankincense", corrupted at some point by the name Benjamin.

The first of the extensions in this substance is a cozebdone branch [isobutyl]. Cozebdone spirit [isobutane] is a rearrangement of the linear zebdone spirit [butane], a colourless and flammable air with a spine of four coke [carbon] atoms. The root zebdo- "four colk atoms" derives from Cairene Arabic ÿ≤ÿ®ÿØÿ© ‚Ä¢ z√©bda "butter", first used when zebdone acid [butyric acid] was isolated from milk fat.

The second of the extensions is located directly opposite the first, as indicated by the prefix dia- "across, over", from Greek. It consists of a maschale [propionic acid] branch, which is the acid oil with a spine of length three, a malodorous clear liquid. The substance was named in 1850 after the Ancient Greek Œº·æ∞œÉœá·æ∞ÃÅŒªŒ∑ ‚Ä¢ masch√°lƒì "armpit", since it was isolated from human sweat excreted by skin zoia [microorganisms].

##

158.5: Roun

Roun (Norman, Kentish / ÄaÃÉwÃÉ/) is a port on the Seine river and the capital city of Kent.
History

Founded by the Gauls, the city was known to the Romans as Rotomagus and it became the seat of the local bishopric in the early medieval period. The ninth century saw the incursion of the Normans into the region as part of the wider Dane Supremacy; by 850 Roun was under their control. By the eleventh century Roun had become the seat of the Drengot family, ruling the Duchy of Normandy as a semi-autonomous vassal of the Kingdom of the Franks (the western fragment of the erstwhile Francian Empire).

After the Kernovan Conjuring and the formation of the First Drengot Empire covering much of Albion [Great Britain] and most of Gaul [France], Roun was sidelined. The Drengot Emperors moved their court to Paris, although they continued to personally own the duchies of Normandy and Kent. This period marks the beginning of the persistent political union of these two regions on either side of the English Channel.

One of the successor states to the First Drengot Empire was this united Norman Kent, ruled from Roun. King Vincent II of Kent, who ruled in the mid-fifteenth century, is remembered for the expansion of the city keep and for his twin sons (the "Two Henries") who ruled jointly after his death. During this period Roun French developed fully as one standard written form of the language (as opposed to Parisian or Burgund French).

Roun was the administrative centre of the Drengot Collusion from the nineteenth century until its collapse in 1961. The city housed the "Red House", the headquarters of Collusion's Ministry for Information.

Authors
    Mark Deuthex (fl. 1973), whose spycraft tale We Wake to Broken Windows draws on his own experiences in the Red House.
    Josephene Foilliers (fl. 1913), writer of the first work of fiction to sell over a million copies in a single year.
    Ashford Pasquier (fl. 1968), inventor of the term "pistol mesh" in his parachthon work Starsail.

##

Durant y Scon Tetrarchy ny siecr on√ß e do√ß, y Marc a Furlagn intermane y por√ßon y sou√∞eressem nell'Empir Theudesc, cos primer un vassal imperial prose e√∞ aval sousunt cos particuler ny paramonta√∞ dy Reyaum a Bavarn.
During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, the March of Furlain was the southernmost part of the German Empire, at first a direct imperial vassal and later specifically consolidated under the suzerainty of the Kingdom of Bavarn.

Posc y dissolu√ßon dell'Empire Theudesc ny siecr tre√ß, y compe√∞our y frequendessem de Furlagn denoy y Republic a Venis.

159.5: Cambrick Codices

summaries of two twentieth-century works of writing which were originally written in one or another standard of the Welsh language.

Children of the Battle of Rexam [Wrexham, 1] is a sensation trevold [hit novel] written by Jon Paldreth and published in 1931. It is a fictionalised account of the orphaned children of the Duke of Chester in the decades following his death at the 1483 Battle of Rexam. By Paldreth's account, the children met practically every famous personage in contemporary western Europe, from General daus Sanz to King Andrew of Markland (in fairness, the eldest child was indeed briefly a hostage of the then-prince Andrew). Moreover, Paldreth contrives to have the children present at almost every significant historical event of the time; unlikely as this all is, the book has enjoyed much use in school history lessons as an approachable introduction to the politics of the time.

---

The Grimmer Hall [2] is a scholastic reference quire about developments in early methodics [programming]. It was published in 1999 and written by New Leudong methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin [Brisbane]. The work covers the use of algorithms from antiquity (for example Euclid's method for finding coarsest division), through the early modern period (e.g. the waveform display [Fourier transform] and its application to astronomy), and into the titular "grimmer halls" (rooms of many people‚Äîusually women‚Äîperforming calculations longhand) of the early twentieth century. The work ends its account just before the invention of the entirely mechanical general-purpose grimmer [computer].

[1] released in Merch as Chulder oth Feight at Rexam /Ààt É äl.d…ô‚Äø…æ ä√∞ f…õ Ét …ôt Àà…æ…õk.s…ôm/, and originally published in Welsh as Pa √êywdan y Plant Car Vantel /pa √∞…ôuÀàd…în …ô plant kaÀê v…ônÀàt…õ…¨/ "What the Children of Rexam Said"

[2] originally in Welsh as ≈Åis y Grimrage√∞ /…¨iÀês …ô Àåg…æ…™m.…æ…ôÀàg…õ√∞/ "The Computing-Women's Hall".

160.5: Diversions and Dilettantes

notes on various features and historical figures beginning with a common pair of letters.

Diana Theodora Queiroz (fl. 1810) was a writer and polemicist from Vascony who was primarily active during the Long Peace period. She was a major figure in opposition to Deviance theology and an ally of the Modest Arrangement, hewing especially closely to the latter's emphasis on morality and familial obligation. Her views have seen her cast as a precursor to the Blue Marian movement, which sought to advance the status of women within a highly religious and stratified framework.

A dice is a value or score of ten in a game or sport. For example, the Maritime deck contains cards like "the dice of staves" or "the dice of gold", and the chances of getting a dice when rolling a pair of dice are one in twelve. This term, along with its cohort [1], were borrowed into the English languages from the Norman names for numerals during the First Drengot Empire in the early second millennium. In certain sports, including lineball, the most points one can score at once is ten; because of this, the word "dice" appears in some idioms meaning "sudden good fortune" or "an impressive feat".

Dimus [Phobos] is the larger of the two moons of Mars. It was discovered, along with the smaller moon Phobus [Deimos], in 1869 by Mashick astronomer Coscatl bint In√°m; she also discovered the eighth planet of Janus [Neptune]. Following the custom set by the four great jovial moons, she named the pair after the twin sons of Mars, who personify dread and panic respectively.

Dion Becolla (fl. 1968) was a Borlish author who grew up in the industrial city of Neuvort. She wrote several trevolds [novels] during the 1960s which capitalised on the so-called General Italian Fervour of the period. Her most famous work is the period piece Waging World Peace [2], set during the 1880s in the midst of the Good Game period and owing much to the masquira genre and its hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes.

[1] in full: ace, duce, trey, cater, cink, sice, sept, hute, nuve and dice [Merch /…ôis, dj…ôus, t…æ…ôi, k…õÀêt…ô, s…™≈ãk, s…ôis, s…õt, hj…ôut, nj…ôuv, d…ôis/].

[2] Borlish Pa√ß Mondial Accommettent /pats m…înÀàdjal Àåa.k…îÀàm…õ.t…õnt/.

- whine, whinge, moan, a noise of complaint or pain;

##

161.5: Dunclothe

Dunclothe (Merch /Ààd ä≈ã.kluÀê√∞/, Welsh Dincluth /d…™≈ãÀàklyÀêŒ∏/) is a political and cultural region of western Albion, located south of Scotland and north of Markland. From the Clothe river valley it extends down along the coast to the Bay of Morcambe.

Toponomy

Dunclothe takes its name from the early medieval petty kingdom known alternately as Alt Clud, Strad Clud, or Din Clud; these are ancient British or Guile phrases meaning "cliff, valley or fort of the river Clothe". It is uncertain whether the Dun- element seen in English names for the region reflects Old Irish d√∫n "fort" or Old English d√∫n "hill".

History

The British territory of Dunclothe was partially conquered settled by Dane invaders during the ninth century. However, the region was not subsumed into the Dane Supremacy as fully as neighbouring Sodrick and Northumber to the west and east respectively.

Dunclothe appears to have controlled the fort at Eidin (modern Edinbrough) until it was sacked by Northumber in 928. Meanwhile Sodrick's power was waning; under King Roderick, Dunclothe conquered the isles of Arran and Mann by 942. The bride of King Radulf of Northumber (r. 951‚Äì), one Atre of Cheswic, may perhaps be one of King Roderick's daughters.

In the early eleventh century, Dunclothe was part of a confederation of Dane Kingdoms. However, this state of affairs was about to collapse with the rapid expansion of the First Drengot Empire to the south, which incorporated Northumber along with most of southern Albion. The following century saw Dunclothe grow closer to newly-united Wales, allies in opposition to Drengot power.

In the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, Dunclothe-led fisheries in the North Atlantic were a major exporter of dried and/or cured cod, selling it via the meshes of Borland and Vascony as far afield as the Eastern Middlesea. This trade was a notable source of specie for the vicinity.

The Kingdom of Britain was founded in the fifteenth century, formalising the suzerainty of Wales over the tallaths of Devon and Dunclothe.

162.5: Mathematical Etymology (cont.)

more excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mathematics, a reference quire in the Merch language compiled by Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers.

√¶ther /ÀàiÀê.Œ∏…ô/ [field] the assignment of a quantity (e.g. a dart [vector]) to each point in space. In the modern sense from the late 19C, formerly used to refer to the supposed medium within which phenomena like light, heat or astraphor [electricity] propagate. Early Merch ether (later re-classicised) < Old French < Latin √¶thƒìr "the heavens" < Greek Œ±·º∞Œ∏ŒÆœÅ ‚Ä¢ aith·∏ór "heavens, open sky".

hedre /Ààh…õ.d…ô/ [face; bivector] one of the mozate [2D] components of the surface of a solid figure; a physical quantity of mozate kind, such as rotation. 16C, extracted from compounds like octahedre < Scholastic Latin -hedrum "-faced" < Greek ·ºïŒ¥œÅ·æ± ‚Ä¢ h√©drƒÅ "seat, chair; face".

matching /Ààma.t É…™≈ãg/ [bijection] a reversible correspondence between the elements of two collections W and X, both severing [injective] and covering [surjective], so that each element of X is associated to a unique element of W and vice versa. 1831 < translation of Saxon jochung "yoking" in D√ºmmeyer, 1827. The verb match comes from the identical noun < Old English m√¶ƒãƒãa "consort" < Classical German macci≈ç "partner".

oversamn /ÀàoÀê.sa.mnÃ©/ [superset] a collection X which contains all of the elements of another collection W, and usually more besides. 1831 < calque of Saxon √∂vrsammel "superset" in D√ºmmeyer, 1827. The noun samn "set" is from the identical verb < Old English samnian "collect, gather" < Classical German samn≈ç "I gather".

stepth /Ààst…õpŒ∏/ [gradient] the ratio of a change in vertical displacement to the corresponding change in horizontal displacement, a measure of how sloped an incline is. 1690 in mathematics < early Merch steapth < adjective steap "steep, sheer" < Old English stƒìap "high, lofty" < Classical German staupas "high, lofty".

vater /Ààv…õÀê.t…ô/ [sine] the ratio of the lengths of the side of a triangle opposite a given angle to the length of the hypotenuse. 17C < Scholastic Latin vatrus (cf. earlier alvatr≈ç etc) < Arabic ŸàŸéÿ™Ÿéÿ± ‚Ä¢ watar ‚Äúbowstring, chord‚Äù < calque of Sanscrit ý§úý•çý§Øý§æ jiƒÅ "bowstring; vater". The polysemy arises by picturing an arc of a circle with the chord drawn as a bow with bowstring.

163.2: flasq "pathetic"

flasq /flask/ [flask]
- limp, sagging, flaccid, flabby, flimsy, loose, not taut or otherwise lacking stiffness;
- unenthusiastic, half-hearted, dispassionate, tepid, lacking interest or passion;
- pathetic, pitiful, hapless, inspiring scorn or compassion due to inadequacy or ineptitude

Etymology: borrowed in the Middle Borlish period from early Norman flasque "soft, malleable". This appears to be a hypercorrected variant of earlier flaque, descending from Latin flaccus "flabby, sagging". The unetymological /s/ would reflect analogy from other words whose coda /s/ had been lost in Parisian French. The term has been applied to emotion since the seventeenth century, and to people since the nineteenth.

163.3: cedar "guitar"

- guitar, a fretted string instrument strummed or plucked with the fingers or a plectrum;

163.5: Like Oil and Water

collected notes on two unrelated topics.

Psolic aphlox (Merch /Ààs…î.l…™k Ààa.fl…îks/) [carbon monoxide] is a colourless, odourless and toxic air formed by burning organic material without enough ignifex [oxygen].

Though the noxious effects of breathing the air in closed spaces where a coal fire is burning have been known since antiquity (and likely since prehistory), truly theoretical investigation did not begin until the seventeenth century, and the air was not isolated from the acid oil palaquate [oxalic acid] until the Long Peace period at the turn of the nineteenth century. Its modern name, first used in 1854, comprises the adjective psolic [deoxygenated, with a lower oxidation number] from Greek œàœåŒªŒøœÇ ‚Ä¢ ps√≥los "soot, smoke" and the noun aphlox [carbon dioxide] from Greek ·æ∞ÃìœÜŒªœåŒæ  ‚Ä¢ ƒÉphl√≥x "without flame".

---

The Graven Sea (Merch /Ààg…æ…õÀê.v…ôn ÀàseÀê/) [Baltic Sea], formerly or poetically also the Eastmere, is an arm of the Atlantic Ocean in northern Europe east of Denmark and enclosed by several polities.

The name derives from Scholastic Latin Gravinia, a term for the region around this sea. This is a backformation from gravinicus, graunicus "from or pertaining to the Graven Sea". In turn, this appears to have been a minim error by a late medieval scribe from former granvicus, a borrowing from Old Norrish Gandvik. This placename, identified with the Graven Sea since the fourteenth century, may have originally referred to any of several bodies of water, including the Arctic Ocean.

The archaic synonym Eastmere is a calque from the name in various German languages; for instance, in Frise [Frisian] the sea is called the Astmar "east sea".

The work A Baptism of Fire: Christianity and the Graven was written by Agata Naziunas and published in 1988 by Leid√©iai Givat√©s. This work chronicles the history of the Christianisation of the states and peoples on the Graven Sea's coasts, with a particular focus on Naziunas's homeland of Lithow [Lithuania].

164.3: stig "pang"

stig /stai/ [stai]
- stitch, a stabbing pain in the side caused by exercise or laughter;
- ache, pain, spasm, paroxysm, any similar sensation of pain;
- pang, throe, a sudden outburst of a negative emotion

Etymology: from Old Borlish stii, sti»ù "stitch, stabbing pain", a borrowing from synonymous stiƒ°e in Borland Old English. This is a variant of stiƒãe "stitch", which descends from Classical German sticis "prick, poke, a sticking". For more general pains the word is attested since the sixteenth century, and for emotions it is seen soon afterward.

Con un stig de remors jo √∞ureu y pistol.

164.4: ahoy "imminent"

ahoy /aÀàh…îi/ […êÀàh…îi]
- ahoy, (nautical) interjection to warn of something coming into sight or approaching;
- in view, visible, within sight as from a lookout;
- nearby, close, within reach, accessible, not particularly far away;
- approaching, oncoming, imminent, heading towards one or about to occur (especially of something negative)

Etymology: originally in the first sense, used as an exclamation to attract attention or hail another ship. The extended uses are seen from the nineteenth century, initially restricted to colloquial or humorous contexts.

Orru√∞ ig alcun hom parceveus y catastroph ahoy.

##

164.5: Kelt Borland

notes on the people and culture of Borland before the Roman invasion.

The Iron Age began on Borland at approximately the same time as on the other Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB] in the ninth century aN. The identity of the first Borland ironworkers is debated and is made complicated by overlapping waves of migrations, but eventually they became identifiably Kelt. The last significant such wave was of early Brythonic speakers on Albion settling Borland from the west in the fourth century aN, the ancestors of the small hill-fort kingdoms which covered the island at the time of the Roman invasion in the first century N.

There are several archaelogical structures on Borland which predate the Kelt migrations but which the Kelts repurposed for ritual use. The most famous of these sites is a so-called "labyrinth" located on √áadrosc Heath in the northwest of the island. There are also many cairns dating to this period, now called sarzin stones (from a late medieval use of sarzin "pagan" deriving from Latin Saracƒìnus "Muslim").

The name Borland (Istr Boral) is thought to descend from the Kelt enis borag "island of the dawn [viz. East]" and bora ale "the other Easterners", which would imply the name was originally as exonym used by Brythonic speakers on Albion. The adoption of this name for the island by the Romans was undoubtedly influenced by its similarity to unrelated Latin boreƒÅlis "northern"; this connection is referenced by contemporary writers.

The Borland tradition of Revillon can trace its origins to a winter feast celebrated by the Kelts, recorded as the Flid≈çlus (further etymology unknown) by third-century Roman historian Ammian.

165.1: Length and Mass

notes in translation on lengths and masses in the dime system of measures.

Scein tre font cas y lonctumn degm: y pol√ß, y pie (10 pol√ß) e√∞ y lau (10‚Å¥ pie).
There are three bases for dime lengths: the inch, the foot (10 inches) and the league (10‚Å¥ feet).

Nell'en√ß, y lonctumn dy pie denoy fis cos quidam na√∞usc com y mojol entr y "pie romannesc" e√∞ y "paum accenser" Cathayan, stant un picq pog inequal.
At first, the length of the foot was fixed somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly different Roman foot and the Cathayan "surveyor's span".

D'an 1946, y pie sta redefini√∞ por ig y velo√ßta√∞ normal din equal cos exact a deg gran lau par second.
In 1946, the foot was redefined to make the speed of light equal to precisely ten great leagues per second.

Bempart autr mesur a lonctumn pouvler receveurn valour degm.
Several other traditional measures of length were given dime values.

Com bustr, cas y mesur degm y mil val cinq mil pie ben demy d'un lau.
For instance, in dime measures the mile is equal to five thousand feet or half a league.

Scein catr font cas y moul degm: y scrupol, l'on√ß (100 scrupol), y taber (100 on√ß) e√∞ y ton (100 taber).
There are four bases for dime hefts: the scruple, the ounce (100 scruples), the tabre (100 ounces) and the ton (100 tabres).

165.2: groinar "grunt"

groinar /gr…îiÀànar/ [g Ä äiÀàn…êÀê]
- oink, grunt, snort, to make the sound of e.g. a pig;
- growl, snarl, (of an animal) to make a deep rumbling sound;
- grumble, scoff, (of a person) to make a similar such sound to indicate disapproval or scorn

Etymology: from Old Borlish gro»ùnar, gro»ùndir "grunt", reflecting Vulgar variants of Classical Latin grunni≈ç "I grunt". To describe sounds made by people the word is attested as early as the Old Borlish period. The expected modern spelling grognar was once sporadically used but may have been avoided on aesthetic grounds (compare genoil "knee" < geno»ùl in Old Borlish).

165.3: sax "asteroid"

sax /saks/ [saks]
- asteroid, planetoid, comet, a naturally occurring solid object orbiting the Sun which is too small to become spherical under gravity;
- (specifically) such a small body located with the cisjovial ring [asteroid belt]

Etymology: 1813, a borrowing from Latin saxum "stone, rock", used as a calque from synonymous tsa'ra in Mashick Arabic. The term was originally used to describe the various apparently-pointlike bodies in the cisjovial ring, which at that time were being discovered by telescope. The first celestial body this word was applied to, Ixion [Ceres], is now known to be spherical under gravity and so is no longer considered to be a sax. In modern usage saxes include comets, although knowledge of comets dates back to antiquity.

Theorist prevein un collijon par sax d'an 2026.
/Œ∏auÀàr…™st preÀàvin …™n Àåk…î.liÀà í…în par saks dan du m…™l vint sij/
[√∞…êuÀà Ä…™st p Ä…™Ààvin …™n Àåk…î.l…™Àà ù…în p…êÀê saks dan du m…™l vint s…™i]
scientist foresee-3p indf collision by asteroid of=year 2026
Scientists predict an asteroid impact in 2026.

or bon /…îr b…în/ [ äÀêÀàb…în]
- alright, OK, cool, sure, an interjection indicating acknowledgement or acceptance;
- so, anyway, look, see, hey, an interjection used after a pause to introduce a new topic

Etymology: phrase comprising or "well, however" and bon "good, correct". The particle or (now archaic in isolation) descends from Old Borlish or "now", either straight from Latin (hƒÅc) h≈çrƒÅ "in (this) hour" or via synonymous (a)ore in Old French. The adjective bon comes directly from Latin bonus "good, right".

##

165.5: The 1910s

collected notes on various happenings in the decade 1910 to 1919.

Lo√∞a√ß Berxon (b. 1865) led the Borland chapters of the Outland Arcabil Association from 1913 until 1918. Berxon had emigrated to the capital city of Damvath from her birthplace in the Arcabil [Caribbean] a decade earlier, due to the disruption of the Millstone War. She was an established author, best known for her first work L'Askou√∞ Pyrrhaz (The Firekeeper's Stronghold), published in 1910. The Outland Arcabil Association was a collection of societies working for the benefit of the Arcabil diaspora across the globe.

The first then-called "house of odyssey" [cinema] to sell tickets to the public on a regular basis was established in Boxa [Novi Sad, Serbia] in 1912. One of the venue's first great successes was a recording of a ballet concert accompanied by the St Hurroco Youth Orchestra of Furlain [Friuli].

Un Seir Deura Soffire (One Evening Will Have to be Enough) was a 1913 work in the masquira genre by Roun author Josephene Foilliers. Its riproaring execution of the classic masquira plot structure of schemes and opulence galore made it an instant success across the Drengot Collusion and beyond; this work was the first book to sell more thna a million copies in its first year.

The literary genre of social comedy has its origins in the late 1910s, with authors like Dorotha Quistack and Judeta Barracin adapting elements of prior genres like lovetales and adolescences. Social comedy is characterised by plots with low stakes, an emphasis on personal friendships and enmities, and generally either rural or low-class urban settings.

166.1: The Ceres Gambit

excerpt in translation from 1952 spycraft trevold The Ceres Gambit (originally published in Borlish as Vellisqment: Ixion), written by Joron Aldaxinte. She was born in the industrial Borlish town of Neuvort, the daughter of a clerk and a minor functionary in the local government.

She paused between the gnarled trunks of two ancient oaks, her breath dragoning in the air, and took in the woods' patient silence.

Con un maldic√ßon so√ßavaver i se commoveu cos lejar y pis, e de sell'azig i sgranfeyau y crost de gla√ß sou y neyan√ß.

166.2: fren "brake"

fren /fr…õn/ [f Ä…õn]
- bit, piece of metal placed in a horse's mouth and connected to the reins;
- brake, device used to slow or stop a vehicle usually via friction;
- deceleration, slowdown, reduction in speed;
- ritenuto, ritardando, (in music) reduction of tempo;
- restraint, reserve, tendency to act with caution or prudence

Etymology: originally in the first sense, via Old Borlish directly from Latin frƒìnum "bridle, harness, curb, bit". The word was extended metaphorically some centuries before it was reapplied to physical devices on other vehicles. In music the word is calqued from Willem Dutch afrem "slow down (imp.)", potentially also due to phonetic resemblance.

166.3: fougl pavarrot "tabloids"

fougl pavarrot /fuil Àåpa.vaÀàr…ît/ [fw…™l Àåpa.v…êÀà Ä…ît]
- tabloids, newspapers considered to be more commercial and less prestigious than "high-brow" journalism;
- gutter press, yellow press, sensationalist reporters or newspapers in general;
- sensationalism, puffery, hype, the practise of exaggerating things for attention

Etymology: nineteenth-century phrase coined as an insult, literally meaning "little cash pages"; compare equivalent Merch term "bit-sheets". The noun fougl "leaf; page, sheet" descends via Old Borlish fou»ùl from Latin folium "leaf". The noun pavarrot is a diminutive of pavar "poppy", here referring to a particular small coin used on Borland in the early modern period and reintroduced with a different denomination in the late twentieth period. The word descends from synonymous papƒÅver in Latin, of unknown further origin.

166.4: ouger "host"

ouger /uÀà í…õr/ [ äÀà ù…õÀê]
- landlord, landlady, proprietor, innkeeper, the person who owns a public building (especially a bar or lodging house);
- host, one who receives or entertains guests;
- emcee, moderator, the person running an event;
- presenter, anchor, the face of a programme on radio, TV, etc.

Etymology: from Middle Borlish ouger "landlord/lady", altered from louger via rebracketing of the definite article. This is an agent derivative of now-obsolete lousc "shelter; inn", borrowed from Old French loge "shelter; covered walkway, hut, small room", via synoymous laubia in Medieval Latin from the Classical German root laubam "leaf, foliage". The word has been applied also to other host roles since the seventeenth century.

L'es un ouger sobottal cay set anna√∞.

##

166.5: The North Wind and the Sun

The Borrhatic League is a collection of charitable organisations and political action groups with a focus on laic governance, education and (in recent decades) also pacifism and objection. The League grew from collaboration between working-class groups of minority faiths across Northern Europe in the early nineteenth century; the name derives from the Attic Greek ŒíŒøœÅœÅ·æ∂œÇ ‚Ä¢ Borrh√¢s "the North Wind". The League first rose to prominences on the scale of polities during the Second German War of the 1820s and in its aftermath. For instance, Hilgart and Michael Bervisson were influential in Borland's Borrhatic League at this time, especially with regards to calls for religious tolerance.

Although the Borrhatic League and the contemporaneous Deviance movement has (occasionally explosive) disagreements, both are known to have contributed verbiage to the 1850 Laic Declarations in Belgrade. Later in the nineteenth century the Borrhatic League funded orphanages and schools across the region, such as the Borrhatic School of History in the south-eastern Borlish city of Combert, which exists to this day.

I Macchine di Helio (The Machines of Helios) is a marble sculpture created by Lorenzo dell'Acchedia in the 1550s, during the regions Revitalist period. It was carved in Acchedia's workshop in Rome and displayed in that city for the first decades of its existence. The work is a stylised depiction of the Greek god Helios "the Sun" with the six known planets gyring around him. The notion of a Sun-centred cosmos that inspired the work may have been transmitted to Acchedia via his close friend Mart√®l fi Bacchero, mathematician at the library of Saint Jerome, who is recorded to have debated the issue in public fora as early as 1552.

167.1: Astraphor

notes in translation on the subject of astraphor [electricity].

Astraphor (tan dignant for√ß barric) es y cougl d'occorren√ß alliga√∞ con y presen√ß e√∞ y locomo√ßon de soustan√ß plentivous, ben y nis harneisabr des tal locomo√ßon.
Astraphor (also called barric force) is the set of phenomena associated with the presence and movement of plentiful [charged] substances, or the thrift [energy] that one can harness from such movement.

Y conjogntur profont cas theory entr astraphor e lumner dene splora√∞ cos minu√ßous ny siecr novo√ß a pla√ß meg; y magn ouvr de titr lon ingeniosta√∞ Astraphor e Lumner scaut par y theorist Markesc Wexter Hathwall acavau a y mechanism d'astraphor descreir cos enter coll'un equa√ßon soul.
The deep theoretical connection between astraphor and light was explored thoroughly in the mid-nineteenth century; the unimaginatively titled magnum opus Astraphor and Light by Merch theorist Wexter Hathwall managed to fully characterise the mechanics of astraphor in a single equation.

Temporane, y sar campaner preluyent a gonfan e lucern sta a mesur de denir destarri√∞ par e√ß sar barric.
At this time, the earlier flag-lamp steeplemesh [telegraph] was in the process of being displaced by barric meshes.

Region lon doujug a gonfan e lucern stant effus e sceint surcourn larc cas rapidessem por y sar barric adoptar; com bustr, d'an 1870 y lign barric y lonchessem ny mont tras heilant afferjarau y lonctumn de Mendeva Bassem.
Places without a pre-existing extensive flag-lamp system were in many cases faster to adopt the barric mesh; for example, by 1870 the world's longest wired post line on land spanned the length of Lower Mendeva [Central America].

167.3: dob "nickname"

dob /d…îb/ [d…îb]
- honour, accolade, epithet, title, cognomen, a descriptor conferred upon someone as a status symbol;
- term, name, signifier, word, lexeme, a descriptor of any concept (especially one from a specialised area of knowledge);
- byname, nickname, moniker, sobriquet, a familiar form of address for someone or something;
- handle, username, a string of characters used to identify an account on a digital system

This is a borrowing of Old French adober "equip, arm; dub", which appers to come from a German root related to Classical German dubilas "peg, dowel". In extended use the word is seen from as early as the fourteenth century; the first attestation of the last sense is from 1964.

168.2: pegr "lazy"

pegr /pijrÃ©/ [Ààpi. ù…ê]
- idle, inactive, motionless, not engaged in any activity;
- asleep, on standby, (of a device) in a mode with the power on but not in active use;
- lazy, indolent, slothful, averse to work or exercise;
- slovenly, unkempt, having an untidy appearance;
- careless, negligent, sloppy, messy, with a lack of attention or precision;
- pointless, inane, silly, occurring to no particular purpose;

Etymology: from Old Borlish pe»ùr "unintelligent; idle; lazy", a semi-learned descendant of Latin piger "dull, lazy, sluggish; loth, averse". Use of physical appearance is attested from the sixteenth century; in the latter more general senses the word is seen from the early nineteenth century.

168.5: And Good And Gay

excerpt from the 1986 work Birds Yet Sing, a popular account by Ivainchuque fi Cap (originally published in Vascon as Ja Cantan Auc√®is) of the history of the Household Renovation in the late nineteenth century. This was a precipitous societal shift in attitudes to marriage, family and the role of children, as to the ultimate causes of which there has been much speculation.

‚Ä¶origin of the word "tov" [gay] itself may seem to be evident. The term is attested from the 1810s in the underground tovaric communities of the Riverine Cities [1], and is notable for being (unlike so many similar words) a self-descriptor used to signal ingroup membership, instead of an insult hurled by offended outsiders or the avenging law (whatever it may be avenging). This initial covertness can be seen in the somewhat confused definition given in Manjurich's 1823 work The Vernacular of Boxa [Novi Sad, Serbia] as "a loving salutation between brothers‚Ä¶avoided in polite company".

The existence of the synonymous "tovaric" certainly suggests that "tov" is simply a clipping of e.g. Zahid Russian —Ç–æ–≤–∞ÃÅ—Ä–∏—à ‚Ä¢ tov√°rish "friend, companion" or one of its cognates, and indeed this connection appears to have been consciously reinforced through the nineteenth century. By the time of the Household Renovation, academic works had further adapted the root, borrowing words like "tovarism" [homosexuality] into dozens of languages.

A closer look into the religious and social dynamics of the Riverine Cities, however, reveals tantalising alternative etymologies. The Riverine Cities were also a hotbed of the Deviance movement due to the presence of significant religious minorities and the entwining of the Church with (resented) local and baronial authority. In particular, there was a significant connection between tovaric circles and the Jewish quarters of cities like Pojon [Bratislava]. Use of the phrase ◊ß◊¢◊®◊ú ◊ò◊ï◊ë ‚Ä¢ karl tov "good chap" (the noun coming from German) between Jewish men is attested in correspondence from the mid-eighteenth century.

Another more prosaic idea is that the word "tov" is a clipping in the other direction, a rebracketed form of the patronymic suffix -ov from surnames like Kotov or Soldatov, but‚Ä¶

---

[1] an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danube River and its tributaries linked by treaty and trade in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

##

169.5: Time for T

collected notes on four features of the Boralverse alternate history that begin with the letter T.

Tewis Camigner (fl. c16) was a botanist from Willemy during the Revitalist period, sometimes called the father of farm theory. His writings in experientia include the first reference to the high sugar content of beet syrup, which would go on to inspire the 1730s Pentrose survey. This was a search for a domestic alternative to sugarcane which resulted in the braxoch, a hybrid of Silesian and Cassuvian beets with a characteristic gold colour and the ancestor of almost all modern sugar beets.

Tightrope Walking: a Linguistic History of Borland (originally in Napolitan as Funamblisme: n'Istorio Lingual d'e Isule Bural) is a scholastic quire written by Ghendaline Cecchino and published in 1991 by the Edizore Universit√Ý Napule. The work chronicles how the Borlish language has changed from the Roman period until the modern day.

Tolose is a city and region in the south of Gaul [France], formerly an independent polity. It was a march (and then a duchy) vassal of the First Drengot Empire in the eleventh and twelfth centuries, bordering the kingdom of Barcelon to the south. However, the region maintined significant independence, with its own standing army. Following the collapse of the First Drengot Empire, Tolose became a hotly contested border region. It was conquered by Vascony from the west in the fifteenth century (see Prince Mark of Tolose, heir to the Vascon throne who predeceased his father in 1453). In the first decade of the sixteenth century, Barcelon conquered Tolose from Vascony, and the region has been territory belonging to the kingdom of Barcelon since (barring some brief exceptions).

Tuscany is an Italian polity on the Tyrrhene Sea, bordering Jeans [Genoa], Lombardy and Rome. Its capital city is Florence. Between the tenth and thirteenth centuries it was ruled by the Pisan dynasty, including the renowned Duchess Matilda (r. 1102-1146). The Italian language of Tuscany has contributed many loanwords to European languages due to the prestige and trade wealth of the region.

##

170.1: Wales in the Wars

excerpt in translation on the subject of Wales during the Wars of Fealty, and on the Battle of Rexam (1483) in particular.

Y Reyaum a Brittan joun na√∞usc (compos par Cambry e sy tale√∞ fragossau, cosogr Cornou e Sodric) broucau nell'en√ß un e√∞egl de for√ß, e l'acavau a bempart concession stragr des Marclont durant l'anna√∞ primal dy rix.
The relatively new Kingdom of Britain (formed of Wales and its outlying tallaths [provinces], along with Kernow [Brittany] and Sodrick [Man and the Hebrides]) started from a position of strength, and managed to extract many concessions from Markland in the early years of the wars.

L'ec fey ersteigon par un victoir consecter ag Battagl a Car Vantel, yon fo y Duc a Castr final occeut.
These culminated in a decisive victory at the Battle of Rexam, where the last Duke of Chester was killed.

Posc l'oc battagl, Brittan sceu aute√ß durabr garantir dy region atorn de Car Vantel (ma contrig y gazou sur y cita√∞ a Castr pog apres se cou offiru√∞).
After this battle, Britain was able to take lasting control of the area around Rexam (though the attack on the city of Chester shortly thereafter was rebuffed).

Y polity acavau soulor a se refusar deur y conflict continuant dy Rix cas Fielta√∞; y ligt entr y polity n'Albion volen remanir jannesta√∞ entarn tras trent anna√∞ aposc.
The polity then managed to recuse itself from the continued conflicts of the Wars of Fealty; the rest of the polities of Albion would remain embroiled for another thirty years.

- lurk, hide, skulk, to lie in wait out of sight;

170.5: Decim(alis)ation

excerpt from the 1st July 1975 edition of the Scavlour (Reflecter), a workdaily paper from Borland which focuses on political news.

‚Ä¶impractical to import astraphor [electricity] from Portingale.

GOVERNMENT ANNOUNCES FIRST DECIMATION IN FIFTY YEARS

In a long-anticipated move, the Ministry for Wealth has confirmed that all four conditions of the '15 Food Prices Act have been met. The gemma (‚çö), which has been the base unit of Borlish currency since 1925, is to be first supplemented and eventually replaced with a coin worth 10‚çö.

Minister Gottart has expressed his intention that new coin and notes denominated in the new currency will enter circulation on David's Day of 1977. For the next four months the ministry is accepting public input on the name and designs of the new coinage, although calls for a popular vote on the final name have been rejected (perhaps due to the unfortunate example of Burgundy, which went through its own decimation three years ago). No timeline has yet been confirmed for the deprecation of the gemma, but exchange schemes are likely to be available for at least a year after gemma-denominated coins are no longer legal tender.

This will be only the third currency in Borland since the polity moved to a decimal system of coinage upon its 1894 incorporation into the Drengot Collusion. The process of so-called "decimation" is common to all Drengot successor states and has been taken up by several others through the twentieth century. It obtained its violent name as a cod-Latin pun and insult by Sarzan Dawkinholm, one-time Outland Minister in Markland and one of the most vociferous opponents to decimation.

The necessity of decimation has been questioned by trade theorists, especially given that many parts of the world seem to have no issue spending hundreds or thousands of the local currency on (say) a loaf of bread, but‚Ä¶

171.4: ne minemini "eeny meeny miney moe"

ne minemini /ne miÀåne.miÀàni/ [Àåne.m…™Àåne.m…™Ààni]
- (law) the presumption of innocence, the legal principle that an accused party is innocent until they are found guilty;
- benefit of the doubt, an appeal to this principle in more general settings;
- eeny meeny miney moe, an (essentially) meaningless phrase at the beginning of any of various children's rhymes which involve pointing at people or things in turn with the goal of picking one

Etymology: fourteenth century in legal Latin as nƒì minƒìminƒ´ "do not threaten, do not accuse", from the prohibitive particle nƒì and the second-person plural subjunctive of deponent verb minor "I threaten, accuse". Its original denotation was closer to a admonition against obtaining confessions by duress or threats, only attaining its modern meaning in the seventeenth century. Shortly thereafter the phrase appears in nursery rhymes, presumably more for phonetic reasons than any semantic connection. Nonetheless, some have dubiously linked these rhymes to the act of picking a scapegoat for a crime.

Nos orru√∞ stou le fornir con ne minemini.

##

171.5: Novomundine Landfall

The Novomundine Landfall (Borlish Abanc Novomondin) occurred in 1471 N, when a ship that was part of a New Navarre Enterprise fishing expedition in the North Atlantic sighted and made landfall on the island of Brasil [Newfoundland]. It marks the start of prolonged contact between the Vetomund [Afroeurasia] and the Novomund [Americas] (notwithstanding evidence of sporadic Norrish [Norrish] contact with the region), and the first century following the Landfall is known to historians as the Intertwining Century.

The terms Novomund and Mendeva [North America] date to this voyage; a surviving map from the Landfall voyage itself is marked terrae mendevalensis, after the Vask [Basque] mendeval "west".

The Landfall occurred at the height of the so-called Vascon Ascendancy, when Vascony [N Spain, SW France] reached its territorial maximum under Ambrose III, and the trade opportunities that arose over the following decades were just as beneficial for the polity. Ambrose's rival, Munir al-Hamdawi of Morrack [Morocco], quickly redirected the efforts of his presciently-named New World Company westward (having previously maintained trade routes to the East), leading to the discovery of the faster southern route to the Novomund and the islands of the Arcabil [Caribbean].

The first Vetomundine royal to set foot in the Novomund was King Ambrose's elder sister Princess Alexandra of Vascony, who visited the new settlement of Paratzon [St John's, NL] in the 1490s. It is unknown why she (and several female attendants) undertook the voyage, although her second husband's various deficiencies and scandals have been implicated. Her visit is remembered for advancing understanding of scurvy (the chemical sandrine [vitamin C] is named in her honour), although it would take over a century and experiments with guy-conies [guinea pigs] before ships could come close to effectively preventing the malady.

##

172.1: Hatfell Honours

notes in translation on the subject of a coveted artistic accolade.

Honour de Hatfell son ve√∞rdon plusour soupleya√∞ de posc y siecr veint primal a artejan ne sce divers y Collujon Drengo√ßan hamtant (e, posc ig l'o√ß alleyan√ß-federa√ß sfondrau d'an 1961, l'enterta√∞ de Gal e l'e√ß Istr Nor√∞ovester).
Hatfell Honours refer to several prizes awarded since the early twentieth century to artists in various fields across the Drengot Collusion (and, after that alliance-federacy collapsed in 1961, across all of Gaul and the Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB, Borland]).

Y dou Honour ne literatur e ne dram son abrangi√∞ par y cougl original de ve√∞rdon, cant novel art ern admis tan recent com y dezein 2010.
The Honours in literature and drama are among the original set of prizes, while new arts have been admitted as recently as the 2010s.

L'ec Honour prenn y nom de Hatfell, un toun nor√∞er ny stat cita√∞er de Londr jos y tarmn contr Marclont stant y mojol d'un communta√∞ d'autour parnomma√∞ ny siecr novo√ß.
The Honours are named for Hatfell [Hatfield, Hertfordshire], a northern town in the city-state of London near the border with Markland which was home to a collective of renowned authors in the nineteenth century.

D'an 1918, y scrittour Dorotha Quistack gajannau un Honour de Hatfell por un entr sell'adolescen√ß.
In 1918, the writer Dorotha Quistack won a Hatfell Honour for one of her coming-of-age novels.

172.2: allocquar "address"

allocquar /Àåa.l…îÀàkwar/ [Àåa.l äÀàkw…êÀê]
- address, speak to, to communicate with by means of speech;
- affect, touch, impact, to have some sort of relevance to or effect upon;
- (dated) court, woo, romance, to endeavour to gain someone's affections

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed (with regularised conjugation modelled after collocquar "converse") from Latin alloquor "I address, greet", derived from the root loquor "I speak". The term is seen in metaphorical use from the eighteenth century, and in romantic contexts from around the same time.

Lor rou y diac ou√∞ y ley vel y taus terren allocquar.
/l…îr ru i djak uŒ∏ i li v…õl i toz t…õÀàr…õn Àåa.l…îÀàkwar/
[l…îÀê  Äu i djak u‚Äø√∞i li v…õl i toz t…™Àà Ä…õn Àåa.l äÀàkw…êÀê]
then ask df journo q df law will.sbj df rate of.land address.inf
The journo then asks if the law will affect land tax.

172.3: incombir "loom"

incombir /Àå…™n.k…îmÀàb…™r/ [Àå…™≈ã.k ämÀàb…™Àê]
- behove, befit, benefit, suit, to be proper or advantageous for someone to do;
- incite, impel, urge, provoke, to provide an impetus to do;
- press, overlay, to put pressure upon from above;
- loom, tower, to stand tall nearby (usually in a threatening manner)

Etymology: originally in the first senses, a Middle Borlish borrowing from Latin incumb≈ç "I lie upon, I recline". The physical senses are attested later and may be based on the Latin (or may show influence from related descombr "go to bed, lie down to sleep).

172.4: ostendar "blog"

ostendar /Àå…î.st…õnÀàdar/ [Àå…î.st…™nÀàd…êÀê]
- exhibit, display, to show for public consumption;
- publish, to have (usually a work of writing) publicised via some outlet;
- blog, post, to write (or otherwise create content) and publish on the pistol mesh [Internet]

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, a borrowing (probably via a French or Italian language) from Latin ostend≈ç "I expose to view, reveal". In journalism the term originally connoted commercial writing as opposed to actual news. In the context of the mesh there are attestations from the earliest meshfora in the late 1960s.

/√∞e non ni…õn Àå…î.st…õnÀàdaŒ∏ traz du miz/
[√∞e non nj…õn Àå…î.st…™nÀàdah traz du miz]

172.5: How to spell Borlish

I've finally started a proper reference work for Borlish; I've finished the orthography section!

Here are the twenty-seven letters of the standard Borlish alphabet, along with their letter names.

The character ‚Äπ w ‚Ä∫, which is used in foreign words and some names (like Willaum ‚ÄòWilliam‚Äô), is pronounced like ‚Äπ v ‚Ä∫ and called vescon ‚Äòsecond-vee‚Äô. There are also some compound characters. The ligatures ‚Äπ √¶ ≈ì ‚Ä∫, primarily used in Classical loanwords, are called √¶sc and ≈ìthel respectively and pronounced as if written ‚Äπ e ‚Ä∫. The character ‚Äπ √Ø ‚Ä∫ is referred to as i jammel ‚Äòtwin i‚Äô and pronounced as if written ‚Äπ yi ‚Ä∫.

In the following we will consider ‚Äπ y ‚Ä∫ to be a vowel when written between consonants and/or word boundaries, and a consonant otherwise.

Consonants

The following letters regularly denote a single phoneme.

Of these, the letters ‚Äπ b d f l m n p r ‚Ä∫ may appear doubled, which usually does not affect their pronunciation.

    abba√∞ joddy affis ollom commet bannir √ßoppin arrum
    /aÀàbaŒ∏  í…îÀàdi aÀàf…™z …îÀàl…îm k…îÀàm…õt baÀàn…™r ts…îÀàp…™n aÀàr…™m/
    abbot cuppa tip relic ban start toffee cosine

The only exception to this is that ‚Äπ mm nn rr ‚Ä∫ may denote a single consonant followed by a syllabic consonant. This is the reading when ‚Äπ mm nn rr ‚Ä∫ occurs word-finally or before a consonant.

    yem-m grin-n car-r
    /Ààj…õm.mÃ© Ààgr…™n.nÃ© Ààkar.rÃ©/
    get-1p judge-3p seek-inf

The letter ‚Äπ √∞ ‚Ä∫ is pronounced /Œ∏/ word-finally and /√∞/ otherwise; the letter ‚Äπ x ‚Ä∫ regularly denotes the phoneme sequence /ks/.

Consider the final six letters ‚Äπ c g h s t y ‚Ä∫ when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). In this case, the letters ‚Äπ t y ‚Ä∫ regularly denote /t j/ respectively. The letter /t/ may be doubled, without any effect on its pronunciation.

    tien yoc cattin
    /tj…õn j…îk | kaÀàt…™n/
    yours husband kitten

The letter ‚Äπ c ‚Ä∫ is pronounced /ts/ before ‚Äπ e i y ‚Ä∫ and /k/ otherwise. The doubled ‚Äπ cc ‚Ä∫ is pronounced /kts/ before ‚Äπ e i y ‚Ä∫ and /k/ otherwise.

By contrast, the letter ‚Äπ g ‚Ä∫ may be pronounced as either of /g  í/ before ‚Äπ e i y ‚Ä∫ and is pronounced /g/ otherwise (and similarly for doubled ‚Äπ gg ‚Ä∫).

    cigl cuivr bac | gevou genoil tengu√∞
    /tsajl Ààkajv.rÃ© bak | geÀàvu  íeÀàn…îjl t…õnÀàg…™Œ∏/
    darling copper twig | (watch out) knee bauble

The letter ‚Äπ h ‚Ä∫ is either pronounced /h/ or is silent (the latter only in some vocabulary inherited from Latin and after vocalic ‚Äπ g ‚Ä∫); The letter ‚Äπ s ‚Ä∫ is pronounced /z/ in coda position or between vowels, and /s/ otherwise or when doubled.

    heu hom augher | bevis bosa√ß bustr bossir
    /hau …îm oÀàj…õr | beÀàv…™z boÀàzats Ààb…™st.rÃ© b…îÀàs…™r/
    mien person tumbler | note gunfire example gent

Consonant digraphs

The six letters ‚Äπ c g h s t y ‚Ä∫ combine with other graphemes to form digraphs. The sequences ‚Äπ sc ch th ‚Ä∫ regularly denote the phonemes /x k Œ∏/. The potentially ambiguous sequence ‚Äπ sch ‚Ä∫ is read /sk/. The sequence ‚Äπ cq ‚Ä∫ is pronounced /k/.

    sclar chym thral schol jalicq
    /xlar k…™m Œ∏ral sk…îl  íaÀàl…™k/
    lightning lymph slave school tuxedo

The graphemes ‚Äπ g y ‚Ä∫ only form digraphs when combined with vowels, for which see below.
Vowels

Divide written vowels into two groups. Free vowels are either wordfinal or separated from the next vowel in the word by a single consonant grapheme (or one of ‚Äπ sc ch th ‚Ä∫).

    choma epu idiocy nothe
    /koÀàma eÀàpi Àåi.djoÀàtsi noÀàŒ∏e/
    gnome express stupidity illegitimate

Checked vowels are either in a final closed syllable or separated from the next vowel in the word by at least two consonant graphemes.

    commun ambasctour sincer
    /k…îÀàm…™n Àåam.baxÀàtur s…™nÀàts…õr/
    ordinary emissary genuine

Now consider the letters ‚Äπ a e i o u y ‚Ä∫ when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). When they are free, ‚Äπ a e i o u y ‚Ä∫ when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). When they are free, ‚Äπ a e i o u y ‚Ä∫ are pronounced /a e i o i i/.

    cla vige bro cru try
    /kla viÀà íe bro kri tri/
    key wealthy dude raw turn

When they are checked, ‚Äπ a e i o u y ‚Ä∫ are pronounced /a …õ …™ …î …™ …™/.

    vindron vampyr effus
    /v…™nÀàdr…în vamÀàp…™r …õÀàf…™z/
    vintner vampire extensive

Between a consonant and one of the vowels ‚Äπ a e o u ‚Ä∫, the graphemes ‚Äπ i u ‚Ä∫ denote the semivowel ‚Äπ j ‚Ä∫. The grapheme ‚Äπ u ‚Ä∫ denotes the semivowel /w/ specifically after ‚Äπ q ‚Ä∫.

    diac suet quelt
    /djak sj…õt kw…õlt/
    mic familiar dinner

Vowel digraphs

The digraphs ‚Äπ au eu ou ‚Ä∫ are pronounced /o au u/ respectively. The rarer ‚Äπ ao eo ‚Ä∫ share their pronunciation with ‚Äπ eu ‚Ä∫.

    taur teun tout | paon theory
    /tor taun tut | paun thauÀàri/
    bull scant everything | peacock science

The digraphs ‚Äπ ai ei oi ui ‚Ä∫ and ‚Äπ ay ey oy uy ‚Ä∫ are pronounced /e i …îj aj/ respectively. Intervocalically, ‚Äπ y ‚Ä∫ does double duty, simultaneously affecting the previous vowel as stated and acting as an onset ‚Äπ j ‚Ä∫.

    deit joivr | rayon huya√ß
    /dit Àà í…îjv.rÃ© | reÀàj…în haÀàjats/
    finger frost | sunbeam corruption

The vast majority of coda ‚Äπ g ‚Ä∫ and some other ‚Äπ g ‚Ä∫ before consonants form vowel digraphs. Here, the digraphs ‚Äπ ag eg ig og ug aug oug ‚Ä∫ may be pronounced /…õj …™j aj …îj aj oj uj/ respectively.

    bag deg rig pog tug faug voug
    /b…õj d…™j raj p…îj taj foj vuj/
    berry ten queue few passion sickle wrong

However, there are cases where a ‚Äπ g ‚Ä∫ in one of the above environments is read consonantally.

    magr magr | migrar scigrar
    /Ààmag.rÃ© Ààm…õj.rÃ© | m…™Ààgrar xajÀàrar/
    rash scarce | migrate redistrict

173.1: Nicolas of Avignon

notes in translation on the subject of Nicolas of Avignon (Provincial Nicol√Ý s Avignon /ni.koÀàl…î sa.viÀà…≤uÃÉ/, fl. 1347).

Nicolas of Avignon was a renowned Burgund architect.

L'es cos primer √¶terna√∞ desig i condugheu y reconstroin√ß e√∞ y spanjon dy Cathedral de Sant Sophia ne Stamboul, y cita√∞ caver nell'Empir Roman Dovem.

For this reason he is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic collapse of the building during a coronation ceremony three hundred years later that killed most of the imperial family (and hundreds more) and sparked the unrest that would end the Empire for good.

173.4: mesaventoresq "shambolic"

mesaventoresq /Àåme.zaÀåv…õn.toÀàr…õsk/ [Àåme.z…êÀåv…õn.t äÀà Ä…õsk]
- farcical, clownish, slapstick, comprising a series of ridiculous goings-on;
- shambolic, pell-mell, haphazard, with a complete lack of sensible organisation;

Etymology: nineteenth century, a blend of two preexisting words. The first is mesaventur "mishap, accident", which comprises the prefix mes- "badly" (from Germanic) and the obsolete noun aventur "event, chance"; the second is aventoresq "swashbuckling, gleefully adventurous", a borrowing via Parisian from synonymous avventoresco in Tuscan.

173.5: American Toponymy

    excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mendevan Placenames, a reference quire in the Merch language compiled by Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers.

Ax /aks/ [~SW Alaska] a peninsular polity in northwestern Mendeva [North America], along with the surrounding mainland region and an extended chain of islands leading west into the northern Lovaqua [Pacific]. 1780 < French l'Axe < poss. Vascon or Portingale l'Axa, a Axa < early 18C Arabic ÿßŸÑÿ£ŸéŸÇŸíÿµŸéŸâ ‚Ä¢ al-Aq≈õ√° "the Furthest" < folk etymology from Oranger [Aleut] alaxsxaq "mainland", influenced by its northerly location and sheer distance from Mashick [Mexico]. Originally referring specifically to the peninsula, the term was extended to the island chain itself by the Morrack [Moroccan] surveyors who first recorded the term in a Vetmundine [Afroeurasian] language.

Bahassassin /Àåba.h…êÀàsa.s…™n/ [Mississippi River] the longest and most voluminous river in Mendeva at over twenty hundred leagues, flowing south into the Gulf of the Arcabil [Gulf of Mexico] through Hasiny. 1704 < Mid 17C Welsh Bahassassin < Carovacian [Caddo] b√°hass√°ssin "mother of rivers".

Cocain /k äÀàk…ôin/ [Cuba] the largest of the islands of the Arcabil [Caribbean], or the polity that occupies this region and part of mainland Mendeva. 1530, repurposing of the word's previous meaning of "an imaginary land of folklore with an abundance of food, drink and other pleasures". This dates back to the late 14C < French Cocagne, Lombard Cuccagna, of uncertain further origin. The term has been connected to Willem Dutch c√∫kye "small cake" < Classical German c≈çco≈ç "cake" and to Old Irish cucann "food ration" < Latin coquƒ´na "kitchen", but both have issues of provenance.

notes in translation on the later history of Provence.

L'e√ß dezein apres stan l'asc cant y Proven√ß sovran hurstau y sona d'un republic marcander excellent.

174.2: de sanc √∞us "horny"

de sanc √∞us /de sank √∞…™z/ [de sa≈ãk √∞…™z]
- hot-blooded, hot-tempered, heated, easily provoked to anger or excitement;
- passionate, ardent, zealous, fervent, given to strong expression of emotions;
- horny, lustful, torrid, beset by sexual feelings

Etymology: idiom attested from the seventeenth century, coined approximately at the same time as de sanc freit "cold-blooded, emotionless", literally meaning "of burning blood". The noun sanc "blood" descends directly from Latin sanguis "blood" (albeit from a Vulgar oblique form like sanguem rather than Classical sanguinem). The adjective √∞us "on fire" is the past participle of now-archaic verb √∞urr "burn (tr.), set on fire", which reflects Latin ad≈´r≈ç "I kindle, set fire to". In the last senses the phrase is attested from the nineteenth century.

I stima vos fosse√∞ un hom tro√ß e de sanc √∞us.
/i stiÀàma v…îz f…îÀàs…õŒ∏ …™n …îm tr…îts e de sank √∞…™z/
[i st…™Ààma v…îz f äÀàs…õ‚Äø√∞…™n …îm t Ä…îts e de sa≈ãk √∞…™z]
3s think-ipf 2p be.sbj.ipf-2p indf person grim and of blood afire
He thought you were fierce and hot-blooded.

174.3: bland "interesting"

bland /bland/ [bland]
- suave, debonair, urbane, affable, charming, of smooth and sociable demeanour;
- well-liked, popular, widely admired by people;
- interesting, entertaining, compelling, engaging people's attention;
- complicated, detailed, having a multitude of facets or features;
- sophisticated, worldly, cosmopolitan, having had plenty of varied experiences and lacking na√Øvet√©

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a borrowing from Latin blandus "pleasant, agreeable; fawning, flattering". In the first senses the word originally had a somewhat negative or insincere connotation; by the eighteenth century the word is mostly seen in uncomplicatedly positive use.

174.4: plentivous "brimming"

plentivous /Àåpl…õn.tiÀàvuz/ [Àåpl…õn.t…™Ààvuz]
- brimming, awash, flush, overflowing, in such an abundance as to be almost too much;
- (in physics) charged, having an electrical charge;
- suspenseful, tense, thrilling, expectant, filled with the tension of awaiting an uncertain outcome or event

Etymology: late Middle Borlish, borrowed from French plentiveus "flush, rich". This is a derivation from plentif "abundant" and plent√© "abundance", via Latin plƒìnitƒÅs "fullness" from plƒìnus "full". The emotional senses, attested from the seventeenth century, appear to evoke the danger of an overfull vessel. In scientific use the word is modelled on prior plenta√∞ "fullness; (physic) electric charge" (compare the analogous plentiful "charged" in Merch).

Sceye un ou√ß plentivous cant alcun hom parolau.
/xiÀàje …™n uts Àåpl…õn.tiÀàvuz kant alÀåk…™n …îm Àåpa.roÀàlo/
[√ß…™Ààje …™n us Àåpl…õn.t…™Ààvuz kant Àåa.g…™Ààn…îm Àåpa. Ä äÀàlo]
there.be-ipf indf silence awash when no person speak-pst
There was a tense silence before anyone spoke.

174.5: False Friends (cont.)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

cockapple [/Ààk…îkÀåa.p…ôl/ "apricot"] n. a stone fruit resembling the passock [peach] and in the plum group, with pink-yellow flesh and downy skin; the tree which bears this fruit; a colour or dye in the colour of this fruit. Late 17C, variant rationalised to usual Merch compounding patterns < late 15C applecock, variant by analogy to "apple" of apricock, abrecock < French abricoc < Barcelon abrecoc, albercoc < Morrove ÿßŸÑÿ®ÿ±ŸÇŸàŸÇ ‚Ä¢ al-barq≈´q < Malchassian Œ≤ŒµœÅŒπŒ∫ŒøŒ∫Œ∫ŒØŒ± ‚Ä¢ bericocc√≠a < Latin (mƒÅlum) praecoquum "early-ripening apple".

handiwork [/Ààhan.d…™Àåw…îÀêk/ "surgery"] n. the medical practice of using incisions to remove, replace or repair an internal part of the body; such an individual procedure; (archaic or jocular) any manual labour, work that is done by hand. 16C in the modern sense, possibly influenced by synonymous Welsh ≈Çodrinneth "hand-treatment" < Old English hand»ùeweorc "manual labour; handcraft, something made by hand", a compound of hand "hand" and »ùeweorc "piece of work".

ough [/uÀêf/ "bad"] adj. of low quality or standard; incorrect or mistaken; sickly or unwell; faulty or dysfunctional, clumsy or unskilled. Early Merch wough, wo»ù [woÀêx] "wrong, incorrect" < Old English w≈çh, w≈çg "crooked, bent; wrong".

penguin [/p…™nÀàgj…ôw…™n/ "pangolin"] n. any of several species of long-tailed and scaly ferents [mammals] found in tropical Africa and Asia; a game of cards played for money which originated in Bengal. Early 18C via earlier attestations in other European languages < BƒÅsan [Malay] penguwing "one who rolls". In the second sense, the word is a calque from Bengal bonoro "penguin, apparently named for a deck which featured art of a pangolin on some of the cards.

175.2: nuivr "hilt"

nuivr /naivrÃ©/ [Àànai.v…ê]
- hilt, haft, grip, the part of a sword or similar weapon that one holds in the hand;
- handle, the analogous part of any handheld tool;

##

175.5: Stillian

collected notes on the subject of King Stillian, a medieval Borlish ruler.

Stillian of Borland (r. ca. 790‚Äì823 N, Borlish Stiglan /stajÀàlan/) was king of Roman Borland in the late eighth and early ninth centuries. This so-called First Kingdom of Borland had grown out of the sub-Roman Diocese of Boral in the early medieval period, having become a monarchy in tandem with (and possibly influenced by) the new petty kingdoms settled by Germanic peoples in the west and south of the island of Borland.

Like all kings of Roman Borland, Stillian claimed to be descended from Victor Rossetus, the semi-mythic figure who united much of the land once external Roman authority receded in the fifth century. The name Stillian descends from Latin Hostƒ´liƒÅnus, attested also as the name of a Roman emperor who ruled briefly in 251 N.

Stillian is primarily remembered for actions which put an end to the perennial Tripartite Wars and unifed the island under one polity for the first time since the Roman Empire. This term refers to a centuries-long series of conflicts over territory and fealty, principally between Roman Borland and the kingdoms of Southbar and Angland; by the eighth century these three powers controlled the vast majority of the island. Kings of Borland had made temporary alliances before with either of Southbar and Angland.

In 803, Stillian's son Marc was married to Alfegh of Angland's only child (variously recorded as Raunilt or Raunvig, likely from Old English hr√¶fn "raven" and a name element like hild or wig "war, battle"). The two royal dynasties had been connected by marriage before, and this wedding allowed both kingdoms to claim continuity with the new unified polity. Approximately seven years later, Southbar was forced to pledge fealty to the new kingdom after the Battle of Fendal.

176.1: New Urban Mesh (cont.)

additional notes in translation on the subject of the New Urban Mesh, an alliance of autonomous cities in Northwest Europe in the nineteenth century.

Y cita√∞ ny Sar son alliga√∞ lonc y gentagl con y politic dell'entrla√ßorant, tan com y beno√ß aggressionner e mondial de spionna√ß. Interim, outrvalu√∞ cos plausibr es di√ßr alcot y Sar aye (com bustr) y capabilta√∞ de gournment nell'Aust dirigir por gra de lorry commar√ß.
The cities of the Mesh are popularly associated with wheeling-and-dealing policy, as well as aggressive and global use of espionage. However, the extent to which the Mesh was (for example) able to steer politics in the East to their trade benefit is likely overestimated.

Ny siecr novo√ß nuverrem, Londr amenau y Sar vars union cos politic con y Collujon Drengo√ßan, oc stant temporane un alleyan√ß smargent dy polity Fransc de Cantuer Norman, Paris e Borgogn. Ou vrayessem es digr ig y Sar denoy sousunt ny Collujon, ben ig y Sar broucau aute√ß dy Collujon, interman un souject closnautr de disputa√ßon √∞us. 
In the late nineteenth century, London led the Mesh to unite politically with the Drengot Collusion, which at the time was a nascent alliance of the French polities of Norman Kent, Paris and Burgundy. Whether it is more correct to say that the Mesh was subsumed into the Collusion, or that the Mesh took control of the Collusion, has long been a topic of heated debate.

Sr Desarden, Prin√ß Nu√ßal (1968), un lasc font ayent des y scandal d'an 1887 atorn de James Desarden, l'ambasctour Londresc a Nacon Day. Creut cos general es ig l'oc affer aye ra√ßon politic e se produghe des stancoran√ß prose par y Sar.
Mr Desarden, Prince Consort (1968), a film based on the 1887 scandal around James Desarden, London's ambassador to Nackon Day [~Thailand]. The affair is widely thought to have been politically motivated and a product of direct meddling from the Mesh.

176.2: n'impisantour "carefree"

n'impisantour /n…™mÀåpi.zanÀàtur/ [n…™mÀåpi.z…ênÀàt äÀê]
- weightless, in freefall, accelerating due to gravitional forces alone;
- unencumbered, unburdened, not carrying any load;
- carefree, untroubled, unconcerned, not feeling any worry;
- insouciant, blithe, nonchalant, ignoring any potential concerns (perhaps recklessly or impolitely);

Etymology: 1812, coined along with the noun impisantour "weightlessness" during the voidtale craze of popular fiction set out among the stars. The noun is from the much older pisantour "weightiness", itself from adjective pisant "weighty, burdensome". These all derive originally from pis "weight", the descendant of synonymous pƒìnsum in Latin. In extended use the word is attested from the late nineteenth century, with the slightly disparaging connotation established shortly thereafter.

176.3: jom "cobalt"

jom / í…îm/ [ ù…îm]
- cobalt, the corporal [element] with twenty-seven mora [protons] and which is a hard and lustrous silver-grey metal;
- deep blue, a colour produced from the ash [oxide] or chalk [carbonate] of this corporal (or a similar hue of any origin)

These are trade-words borrowed from the east, via (Bagliemman) Arabic from Turk or Persian ÿ¨ÿßŸÖ ‚Ä¢ jƒÅm "glass". The term was repurposed for the metal once it was isolated in the late seventeenth century.

- nitty-gritty, nuts and bolts, the (possibly figurative) inner workings of any object or concept;
- minutiae, intricacies, the (often trivial) tiny or precise details of a situation

##

176.5: The Light that Casts a Thousand Shadows

The Light that Casts a Thousand Shadows (originally published in Borlish as Un Flam, Mil Ombr "1 Flame, 1000 Shadows") was a 2017 N work of pop history, economics and philosophy written by Ollamec Vamncoury and published by a subsidiary of the Peujon Rous (Red Dove) media guild.

Her book chronicles the duelling ideologies of Ubiquity and Vicinity, whose rivalry defined many of the greatest conflicts of the twentieth century and which together form the basis for most modern schools of political though. In broad strokes, Ubiquity stands for equality, universalism and homogeneity, while Vicinity valourises liberty, localism and diversity.

Despite various administrations' propaganda, neither ideology holds the undisputed moral high ground. Vicinity has been linked to the flourishing of regional languages and to the freedom to pursue nontraditional familial structures; it has also been used to justify the formation of ghettos and even ethnostates. Ubiquity has led to the toppling of truly oppressive hierarchies in the name of egalitarian redistribution of wealth and power; it has also marginalised and attempted to extirpate elements of society that do not fit into one of the many visions for society that leaders have propounded.

However, the point of Ollamec's work is in fact to complicate the idea that these two frameworks are naturally opposed and completely contradictory. She achieves this in part by noting the many times that similar policies have been justified by appeals to both Ubiquity and Vicinity in different times or places. For example, the modern Runeplate Body headquartered in Lengrod [Lviv] determines the global standard for glyphs in almost all known writing systems. This centralisation is undoubtedly Ubiquitous in nature, yet the inclusion of such a diversity of scripts is a far cry from post-Millstone Mashick [Mexico] and their ambitions of "one world language".

notes in translation on the Laic Declarations.

Y Declar Laic intermanen un series de document traitancer prodoit par un consegl tras tarmn de delega√ßon des do√ßan plusour de polity ne Belgrad d'an 1850.
The Laic Declarations were a series of diplomatic documents produced by an international committee of delegations from several dozen polities in Belgrade in 1850.

L'ec document forgaurn un ossatur de lodrigna√∞ por religion statuer desjovar deur y fonc√ßon administra√ßonnal de polity, e por y stat cas ley de gent a fielta√∞ mendrancer.

At this time, Belgrade was one of the Riverine Cities, an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danaw River and its tributaries.

L'ec cita√∞ ern leya√∞ des y cendr dy Reyaum Danavan, oc stant sfondra√∞ de fa√ßon spectacler posc y Var Theudesc Dovem ny dezein 1820.

Cossy scient, Belgrad sta un ama√ßgat √∞escant primal por doctrin novel cas religion e ley, ant un √ßaucr Promethean parnomma√∞ alliga√∞ ag commou√∞ Desveyant e√∞ un communta√∞ tovaresc meg ovart.

177.3: soucorsour "lifeguard"

soucorsour /Àåsu.k…îrÀàsur/ [Àåsu.k äÀêÀàs äÀê]
- (historical) bodyguard, attendant, someone who protects a dignitary (often a monarch) from direct harm;
- lifeguard, lifesaver, an expert swimmer employed to rescue people at risk of drowning;
- (by extension) saviour, hero, someone who has just saved the speaker from a dangerous or embarrassing situation

This descends from Latin succurr≈ç "I help, aid, succour", adapted synchronically to the etymological sou- "under" prefix and corrir "run" root. The historical sense had been out of use for approximately two centuries before the modern senses appear, and so the latter are presumably an independent rederivation.

177.5: C is for Chronology

collected notes on features of the Boralverse alternate history that begin with the letter C.

Coscatl bint In√°m (fl. 1869) was a Mashick [Mexican] astronomer. She was born in the then-young city of Moshtar [~San Francisco]; she received her further education at the University of Anawack [~Mexico City] and the nearby observatory at Chapel Hill [Chapoltepec]. As an astronomer, bint In√°m is remembered for discovering and helping to name several astronomical objects. These include the moons of Mars, Dimus [Phobos] and Phobus [Deimos], and most famously the eighth-most distant planet of Janus [Neptune]. Furthermore, her calculations of the perceived timing of the jovial phares [Galiliean moons] in their transits of Jupiter helped to pin down the speed of light.

Crain (Bavarn /kr…ëÀên/, Borlish Carniol /karÀànj…îl/) is a region of Central Europe, east of Furlain [Friuli] and north of Crovatia [Croatia]. During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries it was a march in the southeasternmost part of the German Empire, initially automonous but later subordinate to the Kingdom of Bavarn. It was prosperous at this time mostly due to trade into its coastal city of Trieste. During the German Secession of the thirteenth century, when many polities broke ties with the Roman Pope in favour of the (hastily established) Augsburg See, the then-duchy of Crain supported the Roman Papacy. This was likely more from pragmatism than religious fervour, as its western and southern neighbours were both partisan of Rome. Nonetheless, the region become an important Roman stronghold, and the base from which it undertook the Baptism of the North.

Cuonrat Aihuosc (fl. c8?, name often Latinised to Corradus Aevoscus) was the dubiously historical founder of the noble house of Avosche and namesake of the region of Avosche in Willemy [~Lotharingia]. His surname is of obscure origin; the first element presumably reflects Classical German ehu- "horse", while the second may be from suffix -iscas "-ish" (though the vowel is problematic).

Y Rix Romannesc intermanen un cougl circonscreut cos flois de var, de smout e d'autr conflict tras Europ ec ayent loy fis a mesur atorn dy mojol dy siecr setto√ß.
The Romantic Wars were a loosely-circumscribed collection of wars, uprisings and other conflicts across Europe which took place through the middle of the seventeenth century.

Com un enterta√∞ il son cossira√∞ parveg com y derran conflict vecinabr veram, dec ny siecr sevent tal rix de magntumn comportant volen y Novomont envouvr, e tan volen alleyan√ß abrangir entr y possen√ß n'Europ, Africa e√∞ Asia cos parey.
As a whole they are sometimes considered the last truly localised conflicts, since by the next century wars of similar scales would begin to involve the Novomund, and would feature alliances between the powers of Europe, Africa and Asia alike.

L'ec an des 1640 vars 1670 afferjarn l'e√ß conflict 'central' dy Rix Romannesc, con tan certan abatment peripherallessem fonn paravent ben apres.

L'envou d'Istr Boral cas y Rix Romannesc no sta limita√∞ for an envajon imparyent den (ersteigon faint ag Capitula√ßon a Haukir√ß d'an 1632), jos concort commotual con polity collindant sur auxigl militer por lorry concarn√ß.

178.3: malestr "discomfort"

malestr /maÀàl…õstrÃ©/ [m…êÀàl…õ.st…ê]
- unease, disquiet, concern, worry, a dissatisfaction with how things are unfolding;
- discomfort, soreness, irritation, issues with how one is physically feeling;
- bad mood, gloom, pessimism, a tendency to see things in the worst possible light;
- snit, moodiness, sulk, a propensity to displays of bad temper and impatience

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish malestre "discomfort". This was a compound of mal "bad", which descends from synonymous malus in latin, and estre "being, state" (a former infinitive of "be" since replaced by star), which descends from Latin essere "to be". The word has extended to cover various bad or unpleasant emotions, competing for space with hougr "fear, annoyance, discontent" < Latin augurium "omen, warning, foreboding".

No sceye l'hour por commorar ne malestr.
/no xiÀàje lur p…îr Àåk…î.moÀàrar ne maÀàl…õstrÃ©/
[no √ßiÀàje l äÀê p…îÀê Àåk…î.m äÀà Ä…êÀê ne m…êÀàl…õ.st…ê]
ng there.be-ipf df=time for tarry in unease
There wasn't time to linger in discomfort.

178.4: y vo√ß se hoccovar "clear one's throat"

y vo√ß se hoccovar /i v…îts se Àåh…î.koÀàvar/ [i v…îts se Àåh…î.k äÀàv…êÀê]
- clear one's threat, harrumph, to cough in order to make it easier to speak or to attract people's attention to you
