178.5: Campotton√¨ and Gorram

some notes on a pair of cities in New Provence, situated across a bay from each other.

Campotton√¨ (Doccian /kaÃÉÀåp…î.tuÀàni/; Merch /k…êmÀàp…î.t ä.ni/) is a coastal city on the southern bank of the Guittoc [Delaware] river, in the Nappejoquin[1] region of New Provence. Founded in the late seventeenth century as a Provincial staddomain, it was intended to be a major trading port and to control the flow of goods along the Guittoc.

The second half of the placename reflects the Nappe term [Unami] utaney "city"; the first half is of obscure origin (clearly adapted to Doccian camp "field") but may be related to Nappe necama "first". In this case the toponym likely means "mother-city, home city"; compare the similar formation of ŒºŒ∑œÑœÅœåœÄŒøŒª·øêœÇ "metropolis" in Ancient Greek. 

The settlement languished through the eighteenth century due to unfavourable marshy terrain and the better harbours across the river and further inland. Fortunes changed in the nineteenth with an influx of funds: a major shipping canal across the peninsula (connecting the Guittoc to the wealthy Esquanna [Susquehanna] river system) was dug, and the marshlands surrounding Campotton√¨ were drastically canalised and drained. Further widening of the canal took place into the twentieth century.

Briefly known as the "Venice of Mendeva [North America]", Campotton√¨ gained a fashionable reputation during the Good Game Period [late 19C] and by the modern day had risen to become a cultural and economic powerhouse.

Gorram (Doccian /guÀàram/; Merch /Ààg…î.…æ…ôm/) is a coastal city on the northern bank of the Guittoc [Delaware] river, in the Nappejoquin region of New Provence. It had extremely humble beginnings as a single Provincial farmhouse or possibly a collection of pastures.

There are Provincial dialect terms gorra "goat" and gorram "herd of goats" cognate with cavra and cavram in standard Doccian‚Äîthe suffix -am forming collective nouns descends from -(ƒÅ)men in Latin‚Äîand this is generally agreed to be the origin of the placename; compare the similar formation of Gotham (Merch /Ààg…î.t…ôm/), a village in Markland.

For a time in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, Gorram became by far the most important city on the river, having a better natural harbour. It also participated in the convoy [train, rail] craze of the mid-nineteenth century, being the terminus of an arterial line to the northeast.

Even into the twentieth century Gorram was a major hub of industry on a par with Campotton√¨. However, by the modern day a lack of infrastructural investment in its own drained land and an inability to deindustrialise gracefully had led the city to become much poorer than its counterpart across the bay.

[1] approximately the watersheds of the Delaware and the lower Hudson.

180.5: 19th Century Novels

summaries of a few works of fiction set in the nineteenth century.

The Red Man (originally in Bulgar as –úa–∂a—Ç –≤–ß–µ—Ä–≤–µ–Ω–æ "The Man in Red") is a fright tale written by Petcov in 1830s Sreddies [Sofia] and set a decade earlier. This is the aftermath of the Second German War and the concomitant disintegration of the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube]. The book features newly-independent Boxa [Novi Sad], the recovering shell of Stamboul [Istanbul], and many of the cities of the region which would eventually band together to form the so-called "Riverine Cities". The Red Man has heroes trying to decipher the mysterious figure known as Zechariah, a vampire; this work codified many of the tropes of the vampire, such as fire, blood and butterflies.

Rasgatto (originally in Zahid Russian as –Ý–∞–∑–≥–∞–¥—Ç–æ "Unknotting") is a mystery romance of the 1870s written by Olexi Harchenko in Boxa. The work was sufficiently popular that its title was borrowed into Venise Italian to mean "resolution of a mystery" and later simply "d√©nouement"; subsequently the term made it into many European languages.

Flaxen Hearts (originally in Mostara as Querdos Llineros "Flaxen Hearts") is a 1919 sensation trevold [novel] in the nascent genre of social comedy popularised by the lovetales of Clarissa Bellamy and the adolescences of Dorotha Quistack, among others. The author Judeta Barracin wrote the work after returning from living for many years in the Arcabil [Caribbean]. It is set in the early nineteenth century and follows the petty dramas of a group of siblings in Cordova [Cordoba].

Earthly Scene (originally in Vascon as Vista Terrena "Landscape") is a trevold first published in 1809 by Ant√≤in Herrel in the Vascon city of Santander. It was almost immediately banned on charges of obscenity, and only survived due to Herrel's flight to Portsmouth, where he published the word in Kentish under his new appellation Tony Smith (presumably aided in the translation by his companion Hal Brennan). The original manuscript was eventually published in 1870, some years after his death.

##

181.5: Chronicles of Kilshaw

The Kilshaw writers' collective were a group of Merch authors who all lived in or around Kilshaw [Culcheth], a village in the northwestern part of Markland. The group was active through the middle decades of the twentieth century, and is remembered mainly for several long-running series of children's books. These, including the Joke Tawyer adventures, the Emma and Matt mysteries and the Samborn Sisters fancies, helped to redefine the landscape of children's publishing in Markland and beyond. The Kilshaw series have been translated into dozens of languages, and many have been adapted to formats such as sobot [radio] or phasm [television].

A brother and sister pair, Kenna and Holcroft Strong, began collaborating on stories in the early 1930s. They, along with author friends picked up in university and later from literary circles in Safford, would go on to form the Kilshaw collective.

Stories from Kilshaw made an industry of aping, parodying or homaging trends and fads in adult literature, telling tales in genres like masquira, social comedy or voidtale that happen to feature child protagonists. Originally played mostly for comic effect or as entertainment for parents, later works tended to take their premises more seriously and (especially in books aimed at older children) explored more realistic consequences of their events.

Kilshaw is one of the smaller settlements in the intercity borough of Makerfell [Makerfield], west of Safford [Salford]. Its variable pre-reform spellings (including Kilchath and Culshet) point to its etymology in British Celt terms cul "narrow" or cil "corner" and coed "woodland". This makes the placename a likely cognate of other toponyms like Colquiet in Devon and Culgod in Hasiny. The village has become a minor tourist destination for families or for the literary-minded, with a museum and regular fetes themed around Kilshaw characters (costumes, reenactments, etc).

##

Cu sta y monarch de Hasiny dont rumora√∞ es ig y mort deut an mescain√ß conveyer d'an 1950 er volonter conjura√ßonna√∞ sou y saiven√ß de sy granfigl Pe√∞er?

182.2: bro√∞oquin "gibberish"

bro√∞oquin /Àåbro.√∞oÀàkw…™n/ [Àåb Äo.√∞ äÀàkw…™n]
- gibberish, gobbledegook, nonsense, babble, double Dutch, speech without meaning;
- bullshit, tosh, balderdash, poppycock, blatant falsehoods

Etymology: fourteenth century with various spellings, originally meaning "the language of non-Borlish speakers on Borland, especially of the northern Norrish speakers". Both Norrish and English languages had been spoken for several centuries on the north and west of the island, respectively. The word is presumed to be an adaptation of the Borland Norrish phrase bro√∞ oc vin "bread and wine" (the alternative bro√∞r oc ven "brother and friend" is considered less plausible).

182.3: pat fair "fumble"

pat fair /pat fer/ [pat feÀê]
- fumble, paw, to handle with clumsy hands;
- bungle, botch, mishandle, to deal with (a situation) poorly;

Etymology: idiomatic phrase attested from the fifteenth century, literally "to do paws" in reference to the unsuitability of many animal's paws for holding things. The noun pat "paw; (colloq.) hand, foot" descends from Vulgar Latin patta "paw", a borrowing from Frankish related to English "pad". In metaphorical use the phrase is seen from the seventeenth century.

182.4: mensc "politeness"

mensc /m…õnx/ [m…õ≈ãx]
- (obsolete) honour, dignity, the state of being worthy of esteem;
- (archaic) mercy, kindness, relenting from causing or forbearing to cause harm;
- (dated, poetic or jocular) politeness, etiquette, manners, courtesy, decorum, appropriate social behaviour

Usage note: nowadays principally in the idiom sy mensc e√∞ y mansc tenir "have it both ways".

Etymology: from Old Borlish mensc "praiseworthiness, repute, fame". This was borrowed from Old Norrish mennska "humanity", a descendant of Classical German manniscas "human". The semantic shift via honourable actions to general good behaviour was complete by the seventeenth century. The modern phrase literally means "to keep one's manners and one's food", in reference to being a good host while not actually offering anything to eat. It is first attested in a book of proverbs by an anonymous author of the late eighteenth century.

Orru√∞ i crey po√∞ir sy mensc e√∞ y mansc tenir.
/…îÀàr…™Œ∏ i kri poÀà√∞…™r si m…õnx …õŒ∏ i manx teÀàn…™r/
[ äÀà Ä…™‚Äø√∞i k Äi p äÀà√∞…™Àê si m…õ≈ãx …õ‚Äø√∞i ma≈ãx t…™Ààn…™Àê]
seem 3s believe can-inf 3s.gn manners and df food hold-inf
He seems to think he can have it both ways.

##

182.5: Writing in the 1880s

notes on several works of writing published during the 1880s.

Pasti Mezziterreni is an 1880 mealbook which compiles recipes from around the Middlesea [Mediterranean]. It was authored by Josforo Sant'Angelo (or at least on his behalf), a traveller from Tuscany who had already garnered significant renown due to his travels, including a near-fatal circumnavigation. The book doubles as an itinerary of a continuous sunward journey along the Middlesea coast from Tarifa in Andalus to ·∫är l Joas in Morrack [Ksar es-Seghir, Morocco]. Dozens of translations were published over the subsequent years, including a bilingual Kernish‚ÄìWessern [minority Welsh and English languages] version by Iscombe Editors in 1892.

Cludor ncar Vantel "A Postman in Wrexham" is an 1882 trevold parachthon trevold [SFF novel]. It chronicles the misadventures of the titular postman who becomes trapped experiencing the same week over and over. It is notable for being the origin of the Welsh phrase speid dolenu "time loop, lit. coiling timespan", which has been calqued into many other language (compare e.g. liesc bouclant in Borlish).

Dug y Gilfah Dow≈Ç "The Duke of Dark Corners" is an 1884 word of historical parachthony by Taguen ah Owain. Set in the twelfth century, she depicts a fictious tetrad of empires controlling Europe and the circum-Middlesea, ruled respectively from Damvath [Borland's capital], Florence, Tanger [Tangier] and Kiew [Kyiv].

L'Aragn "The Spider" is an 1887 play written by Borlish playwright Osctra Jeichey, which has had several touring productions and adaptations to film. The play is about a group of people manipulated by a genius mastermind whose plots eventually collapse in dramatic fashion. A notable version of the play is the 1930 New Provincial version La Rogna, a loose retelling which swapped the sex of many of the characters. The protagonist remained a woman, giving the film tovaric [gay] subtext rather unusual for New Provence at this time.

The first installments of Monstres per Mans "Man-Made Monsters" were published in 1888 by Carmel fi Domenc. This was a serial work of parachthon and fright fiction which would continue until 1903. The episodes appeared in the Santrafew [~Boston] periodical Arcans Exumaz "Revealed Mysteries", which Carmel fi Domenc also co-owned with her brother Jered.

Murro Mendeva "The Making of Mendeva" is a historical textbook which narrativises the early exploration and settlements by Vetomundine [Old World] groups in Mendeva [North America]. Written in 1888 by In mac Harlach, an Irish Welshman, it has been roundly criticised as an inaccurate and blinkered polemic against the concepts of alliance and integration with the original Mendevan peoples and nations. It reveals the influence of a resentful bloc in Britain which saw the rise of Hasiny as a threat.

183.2: teun "scant"

teun /taun/ [taun]
- scant, scanty, meagre, not in any great quantity;
- insufficient, paltry, not at all enough;
- rare, scarce, very uncommon;
- rarefied, thin, (of a gas) of low density

Etymology: via Old Borlish tiun, tieun "thin" from Latin tenuis "slight, weak". Thus, this word is one of the only pieces of evidence as to the development of post-stress /-w-/ (following a non-velar consonant) from Latin into Borlish. The semantic shift to senses relating to insufficiency is evidenced from the early Middle Borlish period.

183.5: Defining the Dime

excerpt from the work Pa's Hyd Raf? "How Long is a Piece of String?", written in 2026 as a series of freely available posts on the mesh by ≈Åew ab Ennis, which delves into the history of the development of systems of weights and measures.

‚Ä¶originally defined the "second" simply as the eight-and-sixty-four hundredth part of the mean solar day, but by the end of the nineteenth century it had been observed that this period would vary by as much as a few hundred scant (10‚Åª‚Å∏). In search of a stabler value, the Secular Second Undertaking set several astronomical groups worldwide to record the precise length of the tropical year from the summer solstice of 1900 until that of 1901, and defined the "(secular) second" to be the 3155,6926th part of this timespan.

While time has always had a single fundamental base in the dime measure system, other quantities are less simple. The dime principle that all quantities should be related by factors of ten did not prevent a proliferation of standards. Length has four basic units in common usage: a league is a hundred chains, a chain a hundred feet, a foot ten inches. Units of area and volume are defined by squares and cubes of the base lengths. For example, the farthing (formerly defined as one quarter of an acre) is equal to a square chain.

In the first decades of the twentieth century, the foot was still officially defined as the length of a particular rod of iron-baxon [nickel] alloy kept in Tammombay [Mumbai]. In 1946 the foot was redefined in terms of the second, so that the normal speed ≈ì [lightspeed] would be precisely 10,0000,0000 feet per second. This locked the precision of the foot to that of the second. Subsequent refinements have primarily come in the form of particular sources of light, whose spans [wavelengths] or equivalently cadences [frequencies] can be pinpointed extremely accurately.

The basic units of heft [mass] in the dime measures‚Äîthe scruple, ounce, tabre and ton‚Äîwere originally defined in terms of the density of water. To wit, the ounce was set as the mass of a cube of distilled water of side length one (dime) inch; this was later refined to specify that the water should be at its threshold point [triple point]. In practice, due to the difficulty of operationalising the water-based definition to more than a hundred-scant precision, by the 1920s the tabre (100 ounces) was fixed to a particular joracker [platinum] alloy puck weighing a tabre (one hundred ounces)‚Ä¶

===

Real-life comparisons:
1 dime second ~= 1 second - 0.84 nanoseconds

1 dime foot ~= 29.98 centimetres, 0.9836 feet

1 farthing ~= 0.08988 hectares, 0.2221 acres

1 dime ounce ~= 26.94 grams, 0.9502 ounces

1 tabre ~= 2.694 kilograms, 5.939 pounds

Nonetheless, for some time Britain retained a position of cultural cachet.

Soumis es paravant ig l'oc dau impet all'adop√ßon hastous ne Mendeva Brittanesc dy moralta√∞ dy Renovella√ßon Famigler (ne compara√ßon contr Nou Proven√ß e Mascic magn mejoncherrem, com bustr) nell'oc epoc.
It has been suggested this contributed to British Mendeva's rapid adoption of the ethics of the Household Renovation (as compared to much more ambivalent New Provence and Mashick, for instance) during this time.

Impar nell'e√ß siecr an√ßan, ny siecr novo√ß sceye tramigra√ßon remarcabr dy select entr Mendeva Brittanesc e Brittan, tan com mari√∞a√ß entrig magn monfeya√∞.

Cos par√ßal se po√∞ l'oc ascreir a nuvr alcot sta un select autochthon sceint ovest dell'Atlantic, ma majorta√∞er l'oc se mantien for deut a vascel raudessem e donc a√∞endr magn abreja√∞.

Old English wi√æerlƒìan "recompense", literally "counter-reward"), which the second part calqued as Latin d≈çnum "gift". Compare the Old French cognate guerredon "payment; spoils, booty, loot", whose first element was also adjusted to guerre "war". 184.4: dom "dome"

dom /d…îm/ [d…îm]
- dome, cupola, the architectural feature of a hollow upper half-sphere on the roof of a building;

The merger, which had likely already occurred in Middle French before the term entered Borlish, was aided by the prominence of domes on Italian cathedrals.

184.5: A Worldwide Web

various features of the Boralverse beginning with W.

War without Walls is a textbook of military history by Ajamarn [~Rajasthani] scholar Indvar Vijarrania, written in the early twentieth century during his tenure at the University of Udj√©n [Ujjain]. He chronicles the development of military strategy in the wake of the advent of cannon and other explosives, and in particular how these technologies obsoleted the formerly dominant strategy of defence via walled fortifications.

Wessern (Merch /Ààw…õ.s…ên/, Wessern Wesseron /w…ôsÀàs…õ…æ…ôn/, Welsh Sasneg /s…êÀàzn…õg/, Borlish Wessaxon /Àåv…õ.sakÀàs…în/) is the language of the English minority living in Britain [~Wales, etc], primarily in eastern Devon and the downstream Severn. It is the third-most spoken English language on Albion [Great Britain], after Merch and Kentish, although it also has significant populations of speakers abroad in British Mendeva [NA] and elsewhere. Wessern features geminate consonants and the simplification of coda clusters (compare Merch dark /daÀêk/ to Wessern darick /Ààda.…æ…ôk/ "dark"), as well as fricative voicing between vowels and at the start of words.

Winchester Scholastic Primers are a publishing company which have operated in Kent since the early twentieth century. They have produced works principally of academic or educational interest, with several works used regularly in schools. Books from this publishing house include:
- The Alphabetical Albion (1920), by a consortium including Lucas Osborne
- A Comprehensive Cyclop√¶dia of Pre-Fracture Europe (1937), written by Martin and Elisabeth Forman and illustrated by Goffrey Jordan
- In Sickness and in Health: Medicine in pre-Arnoutszen Europe (2nd ed. 1961), by Andrew Knowles with foreword by Dr Laura Norris

Wolfston the Younger (fl. ca. 930 N) was the last English king of Northumber before the rump state was folded into the Dane territory ruled from Yorwick [York]. Northumber had controlled lands as far south as Yorwick until the ninth century, when that city became the first major settlement in England conquered during the Dane Supremacy. According to the Carlile chronicle, Wolfston's short reign ended ignominiously and without issue at the fortifications in Edinbrough, when those too were captured by Dane forces.

185.2: pestri√ßon "formation"

pestri√ßon /Àåp…õ.striÀàts…în/ [Àåp…õ.st Ä…™Ààts…în]
- formation, development, emergence, creation, genesis, the process during which something comes into being;
- assembly, arrangement, shape, form, configuration, structure, layout, the manner in which something is physically composed in space

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, a noun derivation from verb pestrir "knead; mould, form, shape". This is a direct descendant from Vulgar Latin pistri≈ç "I knead", from Classical Latin pistor "baker, miller". Initially referring to the act of kneading dough or moulding clay, the word took on more general senses almost at once.

Jo no's deja satisfait dy pestri√ßon nell'exedr.

185.3: a cascun "respectively"

a cascun /a kaÀàx…™n/ [a k…êÀà√ß…™n]
- each, apiece, (e.g. of a price) for an individual item from a set;
- per head, per capita, (of a statistic) divided by the population of the region in question;
- respectively, corresponding in the order given when comparing two lists of things;
- pointwise, (in mathematics) occuring or true independently for each point in a set

Etymology: literally "to each one", in idiomatic use attested from the twelfth century. The pronoun cascun "each one" descends directly from Vulgar Latin casc≈´num "each one", a conflation of Classical quisque ≈´num and Late Latin cata ≈´num "each one". The phrase's use extended beyond financial and census-taking contexts through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

Se po√∞e pom accatar teint ogto√ß solt a cascun.

[se p äÀà√∞e p…îm Àåa.k…êÀàt…êÀê tint  äjÀàt…îts s…îlt a k…êÀà√ß…™n]

185.4: bastegl "bulwark"

bastegl /baÀàst…™jl/ [baÀàst…™jl]
- fortress, fortification, a strongly built and defended military structure or enclosure used in warfare;
- bastion, stronghold, a secure position or place of power and influence;
- bulwark, defence, something that serves as a shield or protection against danger or attack;
- safeguard, protection, precaution, any means of preventing harm or ensuring safety

Etymology: a borrowing from Middle French bastille "fortress", itself from early Doccian [Occitan] bastida "fortress, edifice" adapted to the -ille suffix < Latin ƒ´le "enclosure". This comes from Late Latin basti≈ç "I build; I sew", a borrowing from Frankish. In metaphorical usage the word is in evidence from the eighteenth century.

##

185.5: England

notes on the geographical region of England.

England (Merch /Àà…õn.gl…ônd/, Norman Engleterre /Àà…õÃÉng.t…õ Ä/, Welsh ≈Åoegr /Àà…¨oÀê.g…ô/, Borlish Larry /laÀà Äi/) is the name of a loosely demarcated region of Albion [Great Britain]. In the modern day England may be defined politically to be the territory of Northumber, Markland, London and (Albic) Kent; it may also be defined linguistically to be the part of Albion inhabited by communities of speakers of English languages. The latter definition is more expansive, due to the many Wessern speakers in eastern Devon and Merch speakers in eastern Wales.

The majority of languages not listed above refer to this region by some borrowing or adaptation of Medieval Latin Angl≈çnia "England". This was formed by analogy to e.g. Sax≈çnia "Saxony" from the root Anglƒ´ "Angles", one of the German peoples who populated the region in the sub-Roman and early medieval period. The exceptional Welsh and Borlish toponyms are presumably cognate (see Old Borlish variant form La»ùr "Englishman"), reflecting a hypothesised Classical Kelt word laecor "warriors".

There are (according to mainstream analysis) four English languages spoken on Albion; in order of number of speakers these are Northumber, Wessern, Kentish and Merch. The polity of Northumber extends from the Humber to Edinbrough along the eastern side of Albion, but its local tongue, which has more Norrish influence than other Englishes, competes for use there with the more prestigious Merch. Wessern is predominantly spoken in Britain [Wales, Devon, etc], where it enjoys co-official status in some tallaths [~provinces] but a decidedly subordinate role. Wessern features geminate consonants and the simplification of coda clusters (compare Merch dark /daÀêk/ to Wessern darick /Ààda.…æ…ôk/ "dark"), as well as fricative voicing between vowels and at the start of words.

Kentish is one of the two languages of Kent along with Norman. Kent is a polity divided by the Kentish Channel [English Channel], both geographically and linguistically. Nonetheless, Kentish has had much more French influence over the past millennium than other Englishes, which continues to this day. For example, Kentish is the only English language within the so-called "roral ambit", having a uvular rhotic.

Merch is by some way the most-spoken English language, official both in Markland and London. The London standard of the language, while fully mutually intelligible with the Markland form (to a much greater degree than between different English languages), has several differences, notably in spelling‚ÄîLondon did not particpate in the 1870 reforms which somewhat rationalised Merch orthography.

Awry [/Àà…ôu.…æi/ "Hungary"] n.pr. a polity of Europe inhabited principally by the Majoric people, located east of Crain and Crovatia and west of Halich and Vallack. 10C Old English, allied with contemporary French Avrie, Saxon Aweri, Bavarn Awaren < Medieval Latin Avaria "the Avar Empire". 187.4: skillet "chip"

187.5: The Void

notes on three things which are somehow related to outer space.

The Superdraconic Apocalypse (Borlish Apocalyps Surdraconic /aÀåpo.kaÀàl…™ps s…™rÀådra.koÀàn…™k/) refers to the fall of a cisjovial sax [asteroid from the asteroid belt] approximately sixty-six hundred myriad years ago, probably during the northern spring. It was followed by the eradication of roughly three-quarters of all animal species, including all dragons [dinosaurs] bar the ancestors of modern birds. The name of this event, coined in the nineteenth century as a hypothetical (several decades before evidence of the impact was actually discovered) to explain the extinction in the fossil record, refers to its location‚Äîtemporally at the end of, and therefore geologically on top of, the Draconic Age (2.5‚Äì0.66 great myriad years ago).

The waveform display [Fourier transform] (Borlish despley ondatim /d…õÀàspli Àå…în.daÀàt…™m/) is the presentation of a mathematical function as the sum of vatre and arrhumb [sine and cosine] functions. This technique was developed in the eighteenth century as a way to help compute celestial orbits, in part by Barcelon mathematician Raymond al Mustafa.

The Auroric Anomaly of 1859 (Borlish Anomaly Auroric /aÀåno.maÀàli Àåo.roÀàr…™k/) was a collection of phenomena associated with an intense sunstorm, including auroras and‚Äîmost famously‚Äîmalfunctioning barrick steeplepost [electric telegraph] equipment. Various operators reported sparks, fires, and even being able to sporadically use wires to transmit when they weren't powered. The first tachygraph [typewriter] capable of sending messages on the steeplemesh was tested by Tomas Vilmanas on Michael's Day, less than a month after the Anomaly. However, despite later dramatisation, there is little evidence that the two events are connected.

notes in translation on the subject of the Millstone War (1897‚Äì1908).

Malgra√∞ noc tout entr y var envolu√∞ la afferjarausn l'oncein d'anna√∞ enter, √∞e son tout descreut par un inere√∞m fondaminal: manouvr costojal ayen machina√ßon gazovar survalu√∞, e donc tarmn de front tras y mont quaglaurn ne bastegl a fos ceu avan√ßabr.

188.5: The Second Millennium

notes on various names ordered chronologically.

Damian VI "the Wise" (r. 1199‚Äì1208; Damianus SagƒÅx) was a Pope in Rome during the tail end of the period known as the Medieval Imperia or the Second Tetrarchy. He was the first Roman Pope elected from the membership of the Brethin religious order, a Kelt monastic organisation dedicated among other things to record-keeping. Like others of his papal name, he honoured the twin saints Cosmas and Damian, third-century martyrs. Damain VI is remembered well, mostly in comparison to his successor Pope Just's numerous catastrophes.

The San Tom√© (Portingale /saÃÉw tuÀàm…õ/ "Saint Thomas") was a myton [a type of merchant ship] of the Vascon trade fleet; as with many such ships, it was nominally the personal property of one of the powerful Vask noble families enirched by Atlantic fishing. Logs of its voyages have by happenstance been well-preserved. During one of its regular routes, from Cale [Oporto] to Damvath in September of 1492, the log records being unable to make port at Bournemouth due to battle in the city and harbour (part of the Wars of Fealty).

Gudmund and Rebecca Scradder (fl. 1677) were a married couple of Dane astronomers. In the 1670s they undertook a voyage to Seldiva [Sri Lanka] as part of a global scientific effort to observe a predicted transit of Mercury. Their observations helped to compute the mean distance from the Earth to the Sun.

Cadogan Torrior (fl. 1890) was an author from Nadacow [a city on the banks of the lower Mississippi] in Hasiny. Of relatively minor account during his life, his works of masquira like the 1890 trevold Men of Jet and Diamond have attained greater popularity through the mid-twentieth century. This was thanks to successful film adaptations, such as the New Provincial 1950 production Blanc Sabaje "Jet White".

##

189.1: Disappearance of the Redstone Chair

notes in translation on the subject of the Redstone Chair, containing the (alleged) block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised.

Certan historicour aun l'apponment scas ig Preu √êurn√ß a Londr d'an 1290 fos cos par√ßal motiva√∞ par un vellisqment vars y Ca√∞er emposse√Ør; accont son discrepant cas y fac√ßon envolu√∞.

189.2: greim "lap"

greim /grim/ [g Äim]
- lap, the upper legs of a seated person;
- skirt, apron, the part of a garment that covers this part of the body;
- bosom, embrace, affection, care, the figurative position of being nurtured or protected by someone

Etymology: direct descendant via Old Borlish from Latin gremium "lap; bosom; embrace". The term has been used in tailoring from the thirteenth century. The metaphorical sense may continue the similar usage in Latin, but is more likely to be an independent innovation of the early modern period.

189.5: The Three Rs

notes on three features of this alternate history setting which begin with the letter R.

Radulf "the Kite" (Borlish Radolf y Buttr /raÀàd…îlf i Ààb…™t.rÃ©/, r. 951‚Äì) was the last English king of the rump Northumber, ruling from the castle at Edinbrough. He was wed to a woman referred to in the chronicles as one Atre of Cheswic, who may have been a daughter of King Roderick of Dunclothe [Strathclyde, Cumbria]. His reign ended with the expansion of Dane lands up and across from Yorwick [York]; by the turn of the millennium the entirety of the Neck was under Dane authority.

Ricardo Ivarrachezur√≠ (fl. 1824) was a farm theorist from Vascony [SW France, N Spain], who fought as a mozardisto during the Second German War. Ivarrachezur√≠ was the first theorist to record the ability of copper to kill sperm, noting in 1824 that ‚Äúthe seed of the bull, the ram and the stallion alike are made impotent in storing them for some hours in a syringe of copper‚Äù. He recognised that this was the same mechanism by which copper prevented infections. Although this sterilising property of copper would later be productively used in contraceptives, Ivarrachezur√≠ treated it as a problem for farmers attempting to artificially breed livestock.

The Runeplate Body (Borlish Corp a Runment /k…îrp a r…™nÀàm…õnt/) is a charitable organisation with multinational funding, headquartered in the Kiewan city of Lengrod [Lviv, Ukraine]. Its stated purpose is to compile information on all the writing systems of the world and to recommend standard encodings of glyphs for use on e.g. grimmers [computers]. The organisation, formally established in 1942, grew out of several small groups interested in writing systems, either on an amateurish basis or affiliated with various higher education establishments. The word "runeplate" originally referred to a sheet of metal into which the shapes of writing have been etched.

190.1: Tell Me, Telmy!

    excerpt in translation from children's educational book Tell Me Everything About the Solar System, part of the Tell Me, Telmy! series originally written by Adamara Godwilham.

Tandic evolga√∞ stan y novel dy descovart dy preu corp celest cos vray insuet posc millenner, gent or√∞enjaurn plusour nom poll'ec pharƒ´ Iovis "balis de Jou".

Others reached to retellings of Classical legends which were in fashion.

L'ustol ayent l'ec an dura√∞ cas y lenga√ß European, e stant mantenu√∞ jug candon nos eu fort may de picq lun Jovial trova√∞ a mesur dy siecr derran, interman ig y gyrour de Jou nommar lonc dy prosequent mortal.

190.2: barrougl "wheelbarrow"

barrougl /baÀàrujl/ [b…êÀà Ä äjl]
- wheelbarrow, handcart, a small one-wheeled cart with handles used for transporting small loads;
- trolley, cart, a conveyance for carrying groceries or other items around a shop;
- buggy, pram, stroller, a small carriage in which an infant or toddler sits or lies and is pushed around

Etymology: via Old Borlish bero»ùl "handcart, trolley" from synonymous birocla in Vulgar Latin. This reflects birotula, a diminutive from late Latin birota "two-wheeled cart" which comes from bi- "double" and rota "wheel". Compare Tuscan baroccio and Old French beroue, brouette, both meaning "cart". The term was likely influenced by the unrelated Old English bearwe "basket, handcart". In more general use the term is comparatively recent, only seen from the late ninteenth century.

I sta quemant com babe ne barrougl.

[i sta kw…™Ààmant k…îm b…êÀà Ä äjl ne b…êÀà Ä äjl]

190.3: √∞ejar "tuck"

√∞ejar /√∞eÀà íar/ [√∞…™Àà ù…êÀê]
- tuck, insert, fit, wedge, stick, lodge, to push into a confined or secure position;
- to place underneath something else;
- ram, shove, to drive with some force;
- compact, compress, to cause to be in a more convenient shape or configuration;
- (reflexive) fold up, curl up, to bend onself and hold onto one's legs; 
- (reflexive) fit, be neat, to be suited to taking up some particular space

Etymology: disputed. The word is often presumed to be a metathetic reflex of Old Borlish (a)ie√∞ar [(a)d íiÀà√∞ar] "ram, drive, push forward". This descends from Latin ariet≈ç "strike, batter", a verbal derivative from ariƒìs "ram; battering ram". However, this verb is last attested over two centuries before the first evidence of √∞ejar in the Middle Borlish period.

The alternative with the most backing is that the word reflects Latin adici≈ç "I throw at; I attach, augment", which has semantic and phonetic plausibility, although no intermediate forms are in evidence. Other suggestions, like a first-conjugation variant of Latin adig≈ç "I drive, hurl, compel" or a Norrish [Norrish] verb like √æj√° "force" or √æeisa "thrust, rush", have rarely been credited.

Jo √∞ejau y pie ne√∞an y port closisceus.
/ ío √∞eÀà ío i pje neÀà√∞an i p…îrt Àåklo.ziÀàxauz/
[ ùo √∞…™Àà ùo i pje n…™Àà√∞an i p…îÀêt Àåklo.z…™Ààxauz]
1s tuck-pst df foot lest df door close-ipf.sbj
I wedged the door open with my foot.

190.4: lan "wool"

lan /lan/ [lan]
- wool, the hair of sheep or other animals which is shorn to make clothing;
- curls, human hair which is notably curly;
- stuff, paraphernalia, junk, gear, undifferentiated possessions or objects in general;

190.5: Multiple Choice Quiz

excerpt from the 2025 book Everything Under the Sun, published by the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse, a publisher based in the capital of Markland. It made use of the branding and popularity of a puzzle-book series begun by Merch couple Anna and Ralph Highwater in the 1930s. This collection of questions concerns the Drengot Collusion (1800s‚Äì1961), an alliance-federacy of northwest Europe.

-1. Which of the twenty-four ministeries which made up the Drengotian administration was established last, set up in 1932 under the aegis of Runcibal Sawyer Boon?
- the Minstry for Consequences
- the Ministry for Family
- the Ministry for Information
- the Ministry for Wealth

-2. Which of the following four trevolds [novels] was the first publication confirmed to have sold over one hundred myriad copies across the Collusion in its first year?
- A Door, Once Opened (1920) by Clarissa Bellamy
- Command That I be Healed (1914) by Honorine Maignard
- One Evening Will Have to be Enough (1913) by Joseph√®ne Foilliers
- The Day We Let the Chaos In (1921) by Martha and Dean Armstrong

-3. The Drengot Collusion only gained that name in the late nineteenth century, after it expanded to absorb which political entity?
- Borland
- Markland
- the New Urban Mesh (also colloquially 'Greater London')
- Willemy

-4. Which of these theoretical inventions was not developed at the Gomster Atellier in the first half of the 20th century, the partially state-owned development wing of the Hathwall Magna astraphor guild [electric company]?
- CMM veronic [vinyl] 
- Glass-light [fibre optic] communication
- Immortal [stainless] steel
- 'Tommy' pills [ibuprofen]

- populace, population, inhabitants, demos, the people of a region (especially of a nation or polity);
- masses, rabble, riffraff, hoi polloi, the common people as opposed to the elite (often with pejorative affect)

The noun descends straightforwardly from Latin gƒìns "clan, tribe, people"; the affix from Latin thematic vowel -ƒÅ- and suffix -cula "tool, instrument". - wheeler-dealer, hustler, schemer, a shrewd or unscrupulous operator in politics or commerce;
- swindler, sharpster, grifter, con artist, racketeer, someone running scams;

##

191.5: Blue Trans-Willem War

The Blue Trans-Willem War (Willem: Blaw Trans-Willemsk √ìrlog, Borlish: Var Tras-Willem Blau) was one of a series of wars in the early modern period which principally involved French and Dutch [German] polities (that is, those powers located on either side of the territory of Willemy [~Benelux, Lorraine]) and which were de facto waged over control of said territory.

Though the sporadic flare-ups of hostilies are difficult to divide into discrete wars without some arbitrariness, modern historiography usually works with the chromatic periodisation of nineteenth-century writer Andrysk de Santer. Her system, based either (depending on the account) on the colours of the victors' standards or on alchemical symbolism, has the Blue war as the penultimate conflict, ending in the late seventeenth century. Some historians, though not de Santer, have classified the Blue war as part of the broader collection of Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century.

As a coup de gr√¢ce following a series of smaller humiliations in earlier decades, the kingdom of Burgundy was formally dissolved after the Blue Trans-Willem War, with its territory partitioned between Provence and the kingdoms of France and Bavarn. For example, the duchy of Far Suebia was created as a vassal of Bavarn as a gift from King Frederick to the margrave Thorsten Villingen; the former Burgund capital of Geneva became a subordinate city in this new realm.

The duchy of Avosche and its seat at Axton [Aachen], which after a stable sixteenth century as a French vassal had changed hands five times in recent decades, was returned to French control as part of French Guillenne in the aftermath of the Blue Trans-Willem War.

192.1: Vector Geometry

excerpt in translation from Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d'Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa.

Nell'oc strait, nos indagau y defas dy "method a bout" com annexment de geometry Euclidean.

Par to√∞ y theorem dy codex Element, reconnu√∞ no's alcun autran√ß entr un lign AB e sell'envarjon BA.
In all the theorems of The Elements, no distinction is made between a line AB and its reverse BA.

Caye for durant y siecr cinque√ß yon scholer ig inclus Barclythe e da Treviso en√ßan a asmuth ascreir a lign.
It is only in the late fifteenth century that scholars like Barclythe and da Treviso begin to assign directions to lines.

Pall'oc affixion pariscent petty es ig geometour denoirn po√∞ir dou cos signfeyant complir.
It was this seemingly trivial addition that enabled geometers to do two important things.

Primer, √∞e errevn quanta√∞ ablat destr a dessou dy nimb a convenibilta√∞ cas mathematic European.
First, they brought negative quantities into the realm of respectability in European mathematics.

Con tan l'idea de "mendrem a zefr pom" fos incossirabr, alcun hom no'r vexa√∞ par y no√ßon d'un sojorn bouclant ag pognt ini√ßal par a√∞endrar des A vars B, e soulor des B vars A.
Although the idea of "fewer than zero apples" was absurd, no-one was perturbed by the concept of returning to one's starting point by travelling from A to B, and then from B to A.

192.2: o√∞ig "especially"

o√∞ig /oÀà√∞aj/ [ äÀà√∞aj]
- (literary) especially, particularly, to a greater extent than is usual;
- to wit, i.e., viz., namely, that is, expressed in other words;
- or even, if not, moreover, but also, introducing an additional property or fact;
- (interjection) sure, just so, right, I see, expressing that something is (now) clear or a more general agreement

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish o√∞i»ù "truly, certainly", which reflects Latin adverb utique "in any case, at least, in particular". The term in its adverbial use is now restricted to very formal writing, and the clarifying sense is also more of the written than spoken register.

The final two sets of senses are sometimes held to be an unrelated compound, comprising Old Borlish o, o√∞, ou, ou√∞ "whether" and i»ù "there". These come from Latin aut "or" and ibi "there" respectively.

192.3: egnam "lumber"

egnam /ijÀànam/ […™jÀànam]
- timber, lumber, wood from trees used for construction;
- plank, a long and thick piece of timber;
- beam, post, rafter, a heavy piece of wood used to provide support for some part of a building;
- (colloquial, dated) idiot, dumbass, pillock, simpleton, somebody stupid;
- (vulgar, usually jocular) dick, cock, a penis

Etymology: Middle Borlish, rebracketing of earlier legnam "timber" reinterpreting the initial <l-> as the definite article. This is a borrowing from some Romance language's cognate of Medieval Latin lignƒÅmen "timber"; it cannot be a direct descendant because native words reflect -ƒÅmen as -amn, cf. eramn "bronze", scamn "swarm". The word is no longer current as a personal insult except in one-off comparisons of somebody to a literal plank; in vulgar use the term is attested from the 1970s but may be much older.

192.4: pentis "bivouac"

pentis /p…õnÀàt…™z/ [p…™nÀàt…™z]
- hut, shed, outbuilding, a small structure built as storage or shelter;
- annex, extension, a further interior section added to a building;
- lean-to, an attachment onto the outside of a building;
- bivouac, an improvised overnight shelter

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from contemporary French apentis "appendage, attached building". This reflects appendicium, a Medieval Latin variant of Classical appendix "supplement, addition". In the last sense it was originally restricted to military encampments.

Y gardinner apportau un pal des y pentis.

df gardener bring-pst indf spade out.of df hut
The gardener got a shovel out of the shed.

##

192.5: Borlish Adjectives

Borlish adjectives have two forms: a base form and a comparative form. The comparative is formed by adding a suffix, which shifts stress to the penultimate syllable.
Regular Comparative in -essem

The regular comparative suffix is -essem, which replaces or follows the final segment of the adjective and attracts penultimate stress.

    ‚Äπleger legeressem‚Ä∫
    /leÀà í…õr Àåle. íeÀàr…õ.s…õm/
    'light lighter'

Several orthographic adjustments may occur when the suffix is added. Before the vowel-initial suffix, ‚Äπ√ß‚Ä∫ becomes ‚Äπc‚Ä∫, while ‚Äπc‚Ä∫ becomes ‚Äπch‚Ä∫ to preserve the /k/ pronunciation.

    ‚Äπlava√ß lavacessem | starc starchessem‚Ä∫
    /laÀàvats Àåla.vaÀàts…õ.s…õm | stark starÀàk…õ.s…õm/
    'wasteful {more wasteful} | strong stronger'

After a final orthographic ‚Äπ-q‚Ä∫, a linking ‚Äπ-u-‚Ä∫ is inserted.

    ‚Äπpicq picquessem‚Ä∫
    /p…™k p…™Ààkw…õ.s…õm/
    'small smaller'

The final consonant may be doubled in the comparative form to maintain the checked quality of the preceding vowel. This occurs most commonly with final ‚Äπ-l‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπboncop boncoppessem‚Ä∫
    /b…înÀàk…îp Àåb…în.k…îÀàp…õ.s…õm/
    'cheap cheaper'

The final consonant may be voiced in the comparative form. This is seen most commonly in present participles ending in ‚Äπ-nt‚Ä∫ (which form comparatives in ‚Äπ-ndessem‚Ä∫) and in adjectives ending in fricatives, though only final ‚Äπ-f‚Ä∫ reflects this orthographically as ‚Äπ-vessem‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπtart tardessem | braf bravessem‚Ä∫
    /tart tarÀàd…õ.s…õm | braf braÀàv…õ.s…õm/
    'late later | brave braver'

Adjectives ending in a written ‚Äπ-Vu‚Ä∫ diphthong form the comparative in ‚Äπ-Vvessem‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπblau blavessem‚Ä∫
    /blo blaÀàv…õ.s…õm/
    'blue bluer'

Certain historically long vowels in the final syllable of an adjective may shorten in the comparative form. This occurs most commonly with adjectives ending in ‚Äπ-ous‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπfatuous fatuosessem‚Ä∫
    /faÀàtjuz Àåfa.tjoÀàz…õ.s…õm/
    'clumsy clumsier'

Adjectives ending in ‚Äπ-e‚Ä∫ form the comparative in ‚Äπ-eyessem‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπidone idoneyessem‚Ä∫
    /Àåi.doÀàne Àåi.doÀànj…õ.s…õm/
    'convenient {more convenient}'

Adjectives in Syllabic -r

Adjectives ending in syllabic ‚Äπ-r‚Ä∫ show several patterns. Occasionally they form regular comparatives in ‚Äπ-ressem‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπjundr jundressem‚Ä∫
    /Àà í…™n.drÃ©  í…™nÀàdr…õ.s…õm/
    'junior {more junior}'

Some adjectives, most notably those ending in the suffix ‚Äπ-abr‚Ä∫ "-able", form comparatives in ‚Äπ-lessem‚Ä∫ rather than ‚Äπ-ressem‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπstabr stablessem | sengr senglessem‚Ä∫
    /Ààstab.rÃ© staÀàbl…õ.s…õm | Ààs…õn.grÃ© s…õnÀàgl…õ.s…õm/
    'steady steadier | simple simpler'

The majority of adjectives ending in syllabic ‚Äπ-r‚Ä∫ form the comparative with the suffix ‚Äπ-errem‚Ä∫, which replaces the final syllabic consonant and is stressed penultimately. The same adjustments that affect ‚Äπ-essem‚Ä∫ may also affect ‚Äπ-errem‚Ä∫.

    ‚Äπnuvr nuverrem | di√ßr dicerrem‚Ä∫
    /Ààn…™v.rÃ© niÀàv…õ.r…õm | Ààd…™ts.rÃ© diÀàts…õ.r…õm/
    'fresh fresher | great greater'

    ‚Äπallagr allagherrem | leur leverrem‚Ä∫
    /aÀàl…õj.rÃ© Àåa.l…õÀàj…õ.r…õm | Ààlau.rÃ© leÀàv…õ.r…õm/
    'happy happier | free freer'

Irregular Comparatives

A few adjectives show irregular stem changes in the comparative.

    ‚Äπbrey bressem‚Ä∫
    /bri Ààbr…õ.s…õm/
    'short shorter'

Several common adjectives have fully suppletive comparative forms.

    ‚Äπbon ottem | mal pessem | gran maissem‚Ä∫
    /b…în Àà…î.t…õm | mal Ààp…õ.s…õm | gran Ààme.s…õm/
    'good better | bad worse | big bigger'

193.2: mejoncr "mid"

mejoncr /meÀà í…înk.rÃ©/ [m…™Ààj…î≈ã.k…ê]
- mid, mediocre, middling, average, OK, decent, of only moderate quality (usually disappointingly so);
- indifferent, lukewarm, unenthusiastic, not showing much excitement or optimism;
- unremarkable, forgettable, failing to make a strong impression;
- safe, cautious, risk-averse, avoiding extremes or deviation (of choices or behaviour);
- ambivalent, mixed, neither clearly good nor bad

Etymology: alteration of Middle Borlish mejocr "middling" under the influence of unrelated words like coroncr "impostor" and pujoncr "dagger" (whose endings descend from the diminutive suffix -(un)culus in Latin). This was borrowed directly from Latin mediocris "mediocre", a possible compound of medius "middle" and ocris "rugged mountain" (as to suggest "halfway up").

193.3: desjovar "uncouple"

desjovar /Àåd…õz. íoÀàvar/ [Àåd…õz. ù äÀàv…êÀê]
- unyoke, uncouple, unlink, to remove from a yoke or harness;
- detach, disengage, unfasten, separate, to cause to be apart;
- liberate, free, to deliver from oppression or imprisonment;
- (colloquial, reflexive) divorce, to end a marriage;
- unplug, disconnect, to sever the link between an electrical appliance and the mains

Etymology: Middle Borlish, derivative of jovar "yoke, tie together, match; (later) plug in". This is either a direct descendant of Latin iug≈ç "I join, yoke" or more likely a re-verbing via jou < Latin iugum "yoke". The term was used metaphorically almost at once, especially in religious contexts. In both the colloquial and electrical senses the word is first attested in the nineteenth century.

- overrate, inflate, to imagine to be better or greater in some respect than in truth;

##

193.5: Aqueous Axton

Axton (Parisian French Grand-Aix /g ÄaÃÉÀàd…õiks/, Willem Dutch Aughen /Àà…î.xnÃ©/) [Aachen] is a major city in the polity of Willemy and the municipal centre of the region of Avosche. It is located on the Worm river, which flows via the Rudder [Rur] into the Maze [Meuse].

The name of the city derives from post-Classical German *ƒÄkƒÅ "Axton", attested in late Old English as Aces t≈´n "*ƒÄkƒÅ's town". This in turn was borrowed from Latin aqua "water", in reference to the local thermal springs. The placename was pluralised in most Dutch languages, while in French languages the placename acquired the descriptor grand- "great" to distinguish it from other towns named Aix for their bathing waters. Compare the Borlish city of Axbane, from the Latin Aquis Balnei "to the bathing waters".

The city was of mostly ceremonial importance through the second millennium, as the seat of the county (then the march, then the duchy) of Avosche within Willemy. Roger Nassow, first duke of Avosche in the early sixteenth century, oversaw the construction and expansion of the Nassow palace complex in the foothills of the Eiffel range which overlooked the city. During this time, the city was in the territory of French Guillenne.

Axton grew dramatically into an industry town around the turn of the nineteenth century and through the Global Workshop period. This was made possible by the advent of transport of freight by convoy [train] instead of by water, since the Rudder and Worm rivers are not navigable by large ships. The city also became a hub for learning, with the Axton Benet School rising in prominence under the stewardship of professors such as mathematician Nicolas Defechet/Nicl√°s de Feght.

194.2: ou "whether"

ou /u/ [u]
- a particle forming polar questions placed directly after the constituent being interrogated;
- whether, if, whether or not, introducing clauses that are in doubt;
- either, introducing the first of two or more options

Etymology: Old Borlish o ou, "or; question particle", a direct descendant of Latin aut "or". In the last sense, the term was originally used for all the options in a list, but in the modern language the latter options are introduced by ben "or" < Latin bene "well". In the second sense it alternates with ig "that" < Latin ibi "there", which introduced realis clauses; the prototype examples are jo saif ig X "I know that X", jo saif noc ou X "I don't know whether X".

This word is unrelated to homographic ou "egg" < ≈çvum in Latin.

Se po√∞ ou scadom ben ugnlac accatar.
/se p…îŒ∏ u xaÀàd…îm b…õn ainÀàlak Àåa.kaÀàtar/
[se p…îŒ∏ u x…êÀàd…îm b…õn …êjnÀàlak Àåa.k…êÀàt…êÀê]
rfl can q tomato or onion buy-inf
You can buy either tomatoes or onions.

194.3: farau "never mind"

farau /faÀàro/ [f…êÀà Äo]
- oh well, never mind, no big deal, no sweat, it's OK, an injunction not to worry about something;
- hang in there, c'est la vie, so it goes, a reassurance that things will turn out well despite a setback

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, a clipping of the phrase (still occasionally seen in full) tal farau nos "such is life", literally "so we fare". The verb farar "fare; exist (in a given state)" was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English faran "go, travel; fare". The expression originally represented commiseration (a sense which it partially retains as a self-directed expletive); in its modern senses it is seen from the nineteenth century.

J'ignoy hayenna√ß assignar, ma farau.
/ íajÀàn…îj Àåhe.j…õÀànats Àåa.sajÀànar | ma faÀàro/
[ ù…êiÀàn…îi ÀåhejÀànats Àåa.s…êiÀàn…êÀê | ma f…êÀà Äo]
1s=forgo.pst homework task-inf | but nvm
I forgot to set homework, no big deal.

194.4: skighar "refrain from"

skighar /skaÀàjar/ [sk…êÀà ù…êÀê]
- avoid, abstain from, to not interact with in some way (as of food, vice, etc);
- eschew, shun, ostracise, exclude, to refuse to associate with (a person or idea) due to fear or moral rectitude;
- refrain from, not do, to stop oneself from performing some action (especially in the infinitive)

Etymology: Old Borlish iski»ùar "to flee, escape"; compare synonymous eschiver in Old French and sƒãƒ´en "fear, recoil, startle" in Old English, all descending from Classical German sciuhas "frightened, timid, shy". The last senses are now by far the most common, originally a colloquial alternative for prohibitive constructions but now ubiquitous.

194.5: False Friends (cont. 3)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

coke [/kuÀêk/ "carbon"] n. the corporal [elements] whose atoms have six mora [protons], found in innumerable substances vital for life, as well as multiple inanimate forms like pencil-coke and diamond; any of several black substances which remain after the burning of wood, principally comprising coke. 17C, singular of cokes, colks < northern dialect term colk "apple core; heartwood; lump of coal". Further origin uncertain. Possibly allied with Frise colk "hole, socket", though the semantic connection is tenuous.

iliad [/Àà…ôi. íad/ "glance"] n. the act of directing the gaze towards someone in an attempt to communicate something without words; such an act intended as a flirtation or amorous advance; any act of non-verbal communication esp. with flirtatious intent. 16C in various forms < Roun French uilliad "ogle" < uil "eye" < Latin oculus "√¶q."; formerly often eyliad or similar by analogy to ey [eye].

oakcorn [/ÀàoÀêkÀåkoÀên/ "acorn"] n. the fruit of the oak tree; a suit in the Danaw [Danube] deck of cards; vulgarly, the head of the penis or burris [clitoris]. Folk formation from early Merch akern < Old √¶cern "oakcorn, tree-mast"; in sexual use from the 15C.

urchin [/ÀàoÀê.t É…™n/ "hedgehog"] n. a small mammal characterised by its spiny back and habit of rolling up into a ball; in military use, an arrangment of spikes scattered across the ground as an obstacle. Alteration of early Merch irchon [/irÀàt Éun/] < Drengot French irechon < Vulgar Latin ƒìrƒ´ci≈ç < Classical Latin ƒìrƒ´cius "urchin". Displaced contemporaneous iel < Old English iƒ°il "urchin".

195.3: oistr "wedlock"

oistr /Àà…îjs.trÃ©/ [Àà…îi.st…ê]
- (poetic) marriage, wedlock, matrimony, the state of being married;
- unity, agreement, consonance, harmony, compatibility, the condition of fitting well together

195.5: Borland in the FIrst Millennium

questions and commentary from the August 2025 edition of an Insular History scitation [exam] taken by many Borlish school students at the age of fifteen.

‚Ä¶rulers of the First Roman Empire personally stepped foot on Borland, and what were the contexts of their visits?

Most students correctly named Claudius and Hadrian; almost all cited the former's role in orchestrating the conquests of Britain and Borland and incorporating them into the Roman Empire, and the latter's tour leading to the creation of a Borlish province (ƒ™nsula BorƒÅlum or occasionally BorƒÅlia) separate from Britain. Common mistakes included later emperors like Septimius Severus and Constantius Chlorus, who fought in Britain; by the third-century the territory of Borland was much more secure than Britain, having been conquered in its entirely and thus requiring fewer fortifications against incursions. Another mistaken inclusion seen repeatedly was Diocletian, likely due to the curriculum mentioning the creation of the Diocese of Borland (from the former Borlish province) during the administrative reforms of his reign.

4. Describe the process by which the post-Imperial Diocese of Borland became a hereditary monarchy (the First Kingdom of Borland) by the turn of the seventh century.

Answers tended either to parrot the pat narrative of a century ago or, more promisingly, to mirror the state of current scholarship. There are relatively certain historical facts regarding Victor Rossetus (r. ca. 420‚Äì?) that most could recall, being the first ruler of a re-cobbled-together Diocese of Borland after the island was abandoned by the state apparatus of the First Roman Empire around the year 400 N and while the west and south of the island were being settled by German peoples. At the other end, the genealogy of the so-called Rosse√∞ dynasty, first royal house of the First Kingdom of Borland (who claimed descend from Victor Rossetus), only becomes more plausible than semi-mythical around 620 N. The two intermediate centuries are frustratingly ill-documented, but the best student responses managed to‚Ä¶

notes in translation on the subject of an event in the geologic record.

L'Apocalyps Surdraconic fornisc nom ag cug d'un sax cisjovial ayent se prodoit cos approxima√∞ deur segsant seg cent mur√∞on d'anna√∞, cos plausibr durant y prinvir nor√∞er.
The Superdraconic Apocalypse refers to the fall of a asteroid from the asteroid belt approximately sixty-six hundred myriad years ago, probably during the northern spring.

Ne sy souc sceye l'extirpa√ßon de tre cart tanc entr toll'orn d'animal, to√∞ dragon abrangent sauf ig l'ancestr d'aucel sceint.

Y nom dell'oc cain√ß, oc or√∞enja√∞ ny siecr novo√ß com un dob apponmenter (dezein plusour pre scerra y descovart d'evidence concret dy quinment) por l'extirpa√ßon ny gravatoir reliquyer motivar, jay soumoin a sy zon‚Äîtemporal ag fin, e donc chthonic a dessur, dell'Asc Draconic (2.5‚Äì0.66 gran murdon d'anna√∞ paravant).

Alster is a commuter town in Markland around fifteen leagues [30 miles] south of the capital city of Tommarth [Tamworth].

March

...hosted Tuesdaily from the 17th for a course of twelve weeks at the Kit and Toad; single contestants will be admitted to teams for the cost of a round of drinks.

25 Wednesday‚Äì26 Thursday, 31 Tuesday: three performances by the Lady Maud Orchestra as part of the Century Tour across all of Albion to mark one hundred years since the orchestra's creation. The programme will include a suite of excerpts from Cr√°yenschot's opera Ad Pentaculum, as well as a medley of Merch film music through the decades.

April

1 Wednesday: monthly live reading and dramatic enactment at the library. April's story is The Hinterlands, first book in the Kilshaw authors' Samborn Sisters series of fancies by the Kilshaw collective. We recommend this month's event for children aged 3 to 13.

3 Friday: lineball match of our Ash Lads against the Gins, first home game of this year's league and first year in the Second Level. Commemorative red-caps will be free on the door until they run out, and can also be reserved by slate [~smartphone] and quister [phone].

197.5: Landmarks and Legends

notes on various aspects of the Boralverse alternate history setting which begin with the letter L.

Laurent Burnows (Merch /laÀên ÀàboÀê.n…ôz/, fl. 2002‚Äì) is an author from Markland. He rose to fame in the first decade of the 21st century with Back to Front, a series of children's books about a partsicular school class going on time-travel adventures with their teachers. His first foray into a more mature style was the 2015 trevold [novel] The First Thing That Went Wrong, debuting the series An Incovenient Armageddon; the series proved a great success with the audience‚Äînow teens and adults‚Äîwho had grown up with his children's stories.

The Library of Sobjan (Majoric [Hungarian] Sobiani C√∏nvt√°r) has a claim to the title of Europe's oldest continuously-operating library, having been commissioned by King Jambor of Awry [Hungary] and built during his reign in the twelfth century. The city of Sobjan [P√©cs], close to the Crovatian border, was an early Majoric capital and remains to this day a centre for arts and culture. Notably old items found in the Library include a copy of a 1261N liber numerorum (translated to Latin from Arabic works written in the Single Caliphate) incorporating a computed table of arrhumb [cosine] values, as well as a copy of the first folio of the mysterious Mysarnos Codex (whose rediscovery in 1939 proved that the Codex was written in a form of Greek).

Lux √Üterna: The First Century of Threshold Force (Willem Dutch Onslackbar Vlay: Drampelst√∏weth t√ødes har R√©st Ew) is a 2002 work of documentary history by Cawen ba Dirxen, a scholar at the Varon√∏w [~Vit√≥ria, ES] Higher Edifice. It chronicles the theoretical developments necessary for threshold force [nuclear power], as well as the technology's political, economic and social effects over time. The book was commissioned to mark the centenary of the discovery of the atomic nux [nucleus] in 1902 at the city's N√østad Atellier.

198.2: Lexember 2025 - 2: entisar "set"

entisar /Àå…õn.tiÀàzar/ [Àå…õn.t…™Ààz…êÀê]
- tighten, tauten, to cause to be under tension;
- strain, stress, to apply force to;
- stiffen, starch, to make rigid via some artificial means;
- lay, set, set up, prepare, arm, (of a trap) to make ready to function via some mechanism;
- cock, ready, prime, to bring a spring (etc.) under tension in advance of firing;
- (esp. reflexive) brace, poise, marshal, steel, to steady or prepare (a person, equipment) for sudden or violent action;
- engage, to bring (a mechanism or device) into a state of readiness or interlock

Etymology: Old Borlish, verbal derivation from tis, tes "taut, stretched". This descends from synonymous tƒìnsus in Latin, past participle of tend≈ç "I stretch; I strive". The senses pertaining to traps and weaponry, first attested in the fourteenth century, derive from the action of arming a trap or crossbow by placing a string under tension. More general mechanical senses are attested from the seventeenth century, along with the reflexive usage.

198.3: Lexember 2025 - 3: beyollir "set"

beyollir /bj…îÀàl…™r/ [bj äÀàl…™Àê]
- set, insert, fit, embed, to affix into a recess or opening (as of a jewel);

198.4: Lexember 2025 - 4: sci√∞ "set"

sci√∞ /x…™Œ∏/ [√ß…™Œ∏]
- known, understood, familiar, recognised, being within one's understanding;
- ordained, determined, having been decided upon by some authority;
- set, fixed, no longer subject to alteration;
- possible, feasible, permissible, being among the options one can or may choose from

The last senses reflect the word's Borlish use as the suppletive past participle of po√∞ir "to be able to", whose past tense, imperfect subjunctive and past participle were replaced with forms from sci≈ç in the Old Borlish period. In early use the "fixed" sense was used principally to refer to certain Christian feast days and canonical hours.

- (astronomy) set, the passage of a celestial body below the apparent horizon;
- conclusion, wrap-up, d√©nouement, the process of something coming to a close;
- (poetic) old age, dotage, the latter years of life;
- (mathematics) convergence, the property of a sequence or analogous object approaching a particular value

Etymology: Middle Borlish, originally a term of art for the first sense, noun derivation from orisontar "set, pass below the horizon". This comes from orisont "horizon; range, limit, ambit" (since displaced in the first sense by lovacq "horizon", from Arabic), via Latin from Greek ·ΩÅœÅ·øêÃÅŒ∂œâŒΩ ‚Ä¢ hor√≠z≈çn "horizon". In mathematics the term is seen from the eighteenth century.

Cain minut alcot prell'orisontment Jovial?
/ken miÀàn…™t alÀàk…ît pr…õÀålo.ri.z…întÀàm…õnt  íoÀàvjal/
[ken m…™Ààn…™t …êÀàg…ît p Ä…™Àålo. Ä…™.z äntÀàm…õnt  ù äÀàvjal]
fall-3p minute how.much before-df=set Jovian
How many minutes is it until the set of Jupiter?

===

The Thirteenth Century

collected notes on various features of the thirteenth century.

Wilhelm Br√ºz (fl. 1225) was mayor of the city of Augsburg for at least a decade in the first half of thirteenth century. He was a personal friend of Paul von Burchadt, the future first Pope of the Augsburg See, and Br√ºz's personal ambitions for his city are thought to have influenced some of the religious policy outlines in the Pauline Denuncations of 1225. Br√ºz was portrayed by Hunter Noteworthy in the 1961 Merch-language film With the North Star to Guide Us, a dramatisation of the events of the German Secession. A magnified statue of Br√ºz (not based on any extant description or depiction of the man, since none exists) was erected in 1975 outside Augsburg's town hall.

Ivan of Cartaw (fl. ca. 1280) was a lord of Cartaw [Cardiff] who, during his reign, undertook the conquest of the Devon peninsula. Devon had become formally independent from the kingdom of Kernow [Brittany] some decades prior, ruled by a cadet branch of the latter's royal house. Ivan of Cartau's conquest appears to have initially been without the assent or support of his liege King David; nonetheless, the king treated the campaign much like Wales's other territorial gains of the thirteenth century, creating the Tallath of Devon and awarding its rule to Ivan's son Marduth (Ivan having died of an illness shortly after the conquest's completion).

On Unknown Areas Scribed by Arcs (written ca. 1211)  is a work by Tal of Murcia. Containing little original work by the author, it is primarily a translation and compilation of earlier advancements in geometry from Cairene mathemticians. It contains the first attestation of the term mozate "two-dimensional, planar" in its Latinate form muzƒÅtus, borrowed from Arabic ŸÖŸèÿ≥Ÿéÿ∑ŸéŸëÿ≠ ‚Ä¢ (musa·π≠·π≠a·∏•) "flat, even, level".

##

198.6: Lexember 2025 - 6: despondr "set"

despondr /d…õÀàsp…în.drÃ©/ [d…™Ààsp…în.d…ê]
- arrange, organise, set up, to put in useful positions;
- prepare, lay, set, to make ready for use by placing things on or around it in a particular arrangement;
- incline, mind, dispose, prime, to make (someone) more likely to act in a particular fashion;
- have access to, have available, avail oneself, have on hand, to be able to conveniently use;
- employ, have employed, make use of, to have access to the labour or benefits of

Etymology: descending from Old Borlish desponre "distribute, set out" or reborrowed during the Middle Borlish period from its source in Latin disp≈çn≈ç "I arrange". The latter senses, likely influenced by cognates in other Romance languages, are intransitive and used with preposition de "of, from".

ere√∞mfeyar /eÀår…õ√∞.mÃ©Ààfjar/ […™Àå Ä…õ.√∞…êmÀàfj…êÀê]
- synchronise, sync, to cause to occur at the same time;

Jasper Detchard's Military Career

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

Cos infeli√ß, y Centuir galau Larrescta√∞ cosogr, e√∞ all'envou d'un stoir stendiscent e scur scandalous abrejar, jo me cof depoussa√∞.

Jo saleu dy Centuir ant ve√∞rdon nombrous por scrim, un destourm lon mensc, un parnomman√ß de duvar a joy de blad (just digna√∞), un autr por broucan√ß par y fout noc osant se nommar (cos parey), e√∞ un rekest de my famigl por ig jo reconnoscray plu my paren√ß con lou, dec √∞e desiran alcun con mey.

199.2: Lexember 2025 - 9: phthongar "set"

- set, to compose a piece of sung music for (a given text);

199.5: Lexember 2025 - 12: valcq "set"

valcq /valk/ [valk]
- bout, attack, fit, a short period of unpleasant suffering or violence;
- stint, spell, stretch, any delimited amount of time dedicated to a particular activity;
- (games) set, a group of rounds of a game (esp. racket sports) which counts as a unit for the team that wins more than half of them

Unresolved Linguistic History

excerpt in translation from 2004 article A New History of Rust (originally in Borlish as Un Histoir Novel de Rougl), an academic overview of the linguistic history of sub-Roman Borland (approximately, the time after Latin ceased to be regularly written on Borland outside monasteries and before the first surviving longform vernacular texts).

‚Ä¶summary, the chronology of the following changes from Vulgar Latin to Old Borlish: (1) /au/ to /o/, as in Latin causa and Old Borlish cos "matter, thing"; (2) /alC/ to /auC/, as in Latin alter and Old Borlish autre "other"; (3) /al[…ô]C/ > /alC/, as in Latin calidus and Old Borlish calt "hot", is irreducibly blurry on the margins. Even if in general these are supposed to have taken place in this order, re-classicisations like taur "bull" (earlier tor) from Latin taurus and mere exceptions like unproductive adjectival suffix -autr from Latin -aliter must be accounted for.

We turn to the question on the early Old Borlish lexicon about which perhaps the most scholarship has been written. Since the Merch linguist Buthick Morstan first suggested the existence of an Insular Latin word *urum "man" in 1909, data supporting her theory has continued to trickle in, while never becoming overwhelmingly convincing. Moreover, even if the majority of experts accept the existence of this word, the field is split on whether it is simply a phonetic variant of Classical Latin virum "man", or else a borrowing from Borland Kelt vurum, urum "man" (compare Brythonic guur, whence Welsh gwr "man").

Morstan was aware of potential surviving traces of this term, such as dialect terms like orrel "penis" and orjan "scarecrow", as well as personal names like Orrin, but she was writing before the discovery of Roman-era‚Ä¶

##

199.6: Lexember 2025 - 13: tannir "set"

tannir /taÀàn…™r/ [t…êÀàn…™Àê]
- tan, to change animal hide into leather through soaking in tannic acid;
- fire, bake, kiln, to treat (e.g. pottery) with heat;
- cure, pickle, preserve, to alter (e.g. meat) by chemical treat for preservation or use;
- set, fix, mordant, to make (e.g. dye) fast or permanent;
- vulcanise, to treat (rubber) with sulfur to make it harder and more durable

Etymology: Middle Borlish, verbal derviation from the noun tan "oak bark" (from which tannic acid is obtained). This is a borrowing from synonymous tan in Old French, ultimately from Gaulish tannos "green oak". Extension to various other procedure for making materials harder or more durable is seen through the early modern period and into the twentieth century.

Ruritania

collected notes on the small Central European polity of Ruritania.

Ruritania (Borlish Rurtagn, Bavarn R√ºrzen, Boham Ruravsko) is a minor polity and one of the easternmost European states which primarily speak some variety of German. It is located to the east of Bavarn [Bavaria], to the south of Bohamy [Bohemia] and to the north of Crain [~Slovenia, southern Austria]. Its capital city is Strelsau, located on the banks of the Danaw [Danube].

Toponymy

The traditional account of the origin of the name Ruritania, as a placename derivative of Latin r≈´s, r≈´ris "countryside", runs into chronological difficulties. Roman control of the region as the province of Noricum shows no evidence of this toponym, and the earliest attestations of the name in the eleventh century do not appear to be Classically inspired (although the modern form <Ruritania> is almost certainly a folk-etymological classicisation based on this account).

In general, linguists expect that there is a Germanic or Slavic origin for the Rur- root. The Rugii, a Germanic tribe who settled the area in the sub-Roman period, are a tempting proposition, although their short-lived Rugiland cannot explain the second -r- in the name. It is also possible that one of the Danaw's tributaries in the region once had a name like Rur (compare the Rure and the Rudder to the northwest), which lent its name to a valley and eventually a state. Multiple Slavic etyma such as rogozi "reeds, rushes" have also been proposed.

The capital is more tractable, being almost certainly a compound of a Slavic root strƒìla "arrow" and the Germanic placename suffix -au "meadow, floodplain" (the -s- likely representing a Germanic genitive), thought to be in reference to the straightness of the Danaw in the vicinity of Strelsau.

##

200.6: Lexember 2025 - 20: tas "set"

tas /taz/ [taz]
- bag, satchel, waterskin, a smallish cloth or leather container carried on one's person;
- pouch, pocket, a small and soft compartment for carrying things (also the natural pouch of a marsupial);
- vessel, container, any means of carrying anything (in full generality);
- saucer, bowl, a small and shallow piece of crockery;
- divot, hollow, a recess in a surface for holding small items;
- (mathematics) set, the abstract notion of a structure of which things may or may not be elements

Etymology: the conflation in early Modern Borlish of two words with unrelated etyma. Partially a borrowing from Dutch tas, tasch "bag, pouch", which descends straightforwardly from Classical German tascon "purse, pocket". Partially via French from Mostara tasa "bowl, vessel", a borrowing from Arabic ÿ∑Ÿéÿßÿ≥ ‚Ä¢ ·π≠ƒÅs "cup". In the mathematical sense from 1831, in translation from D√ºmmeyer's 1827 work Sammel und Sendungen "Sets and Functions".

Apar cogleu nos y tas de v ne V yon v.m=x.
/aÀàpar k…îjÀàlaw n…îz i taz de ve ne ve j…în ve d…ît …õm val …õks/
[…êÀàp…êÀê k äjÀàlaw n…îz i taz de ve ne ve  ù…în ve d…ît …õm val …õks]
through gather-1p 1p df set of v in V where v.m worth x
Thus we get the set of w in W with m(w)=x.

- task, assign, set, to give as a piece of work to do;

In the last sense the word is a backformation from earlier assigna√ßon "tryst", borrowed in the early modern period from French.

Ricardo Ivarrachezur√≠

Ricardo Ivarrachezur√≠ sta un theorist √∞aral de Vascony, oc combat faint com mozardist durant y Var Theudesc Dovem.

Ivarrachezur√≠ surcay y preu theorist a parcevir ig cuivr sceus sperm occeir, remarc scaunt d'an 1824 ig "y semn dy taur, dy ve√∞ ben y sto√∞r to√∞ parey dienn impossent par mejan de lou vardir tras pie√ß hour n'un spuit ne cuivr".
Ivarrachezur√≠ was the first theorist to record the ability of copper to kill sperm, noting in 1824 that ‚Äúthe seed of the bull, the ram and the stallion alike are made impotent in storing them for some hours in a syringe of copper‚Äù.

Degnant ig l'oc natur po√ßonnant de cuivr vole alcant aposc beno√ß sarvent yembr ne contrgentour, Ivarrachezur√≠ le traita com problem por sa√∞our attenent a piver pollenir cos artifi√ßal.

201.4: Lexember 2025 - 25: Suthocq "Set"

Suthocq /siÀàŒ∏…îk/ [s…™ÀàŒ∏…îk]
- Set, Seth, Sutekh, an Egyptian deity with dominion over the desert and foreigners;
- Puck, Robin Goodfellow, a recurring trickster figure in various folk/fairy tales;
- an avatar of Christmas jollity in various parts of northern Europe and Mendeva, representing defiance, glittering frost and brightness in the dark

In the last sense, compare Har Suthock /haÀê Ààs ä.√∞ äk/ "(lit.) frost Set" in Merch, and Nacho Suthoc "(lit.) little-brother Set" in Ambrosian Vask [Basque].

Etymology: 18th century, originally in translation from Arabic ÿ≥Ÿèÿ™ŸèÿÆ ‚Ä¢ Sutuch "Set". This in turn comes from the very new (and still somewhat inaccurate) decipherment of hieroglyphs, leading to a surge in interest in Egyptian mythology, reflecting the name now usually transcribed as /suÀê.ti√ß/ (whence Coptic ‚≤•‚≤è‚≤ß /seÀêt/).

The transfer of the name from Egyptian mythology to general folktales is the fault of Dutch writer Ton Cr√°yenschot, who in the early 19th century repurposed the names of deities of various traditions for the supernatural characters in his short stories. The puckish figure of Har Suttock spread into the culture at large far more successfully than most of his inventions, and had become associated with winter festivals in general and Christmas in particular by the turn of the 20th century.

Y Grec identfeyan Suthocq e Typhon.
/i gr…õk Àåi.d…õntÀàfjan s…™ÀàŒ∏…îk e tiÀàf…în/
[i g Ä…õk Àåi.d…™nfÀàfjan s…™ÀàŒ∏…îk e t…™Ààf…în]
df Greek equate-ipf-3p Set and Typhon
The Greeks conflated Set and Typhon.

False Friends (cont. 4)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

glade [/gl…õÀêd/ "sunbeam"] n. a ray, a columnar shaft of sunlight as cast through clouds or canopy; further, a moment of optimism during bleakness or of insight during confusion; a phenomenon of light in the sky near the poles due to the interaction of solar dust with air (also aurorick glade). 15C, early Merch glade, glode "ray of sunlight; sunny place", poss. < unknown Old English gl«£d~glƒÅd "brightness" related to known gl√¶d "shining, bright". Emotional uses are seen 17C; celestial use from 18C.

jackareigh [/Ààd ía.k…ô.…æ…õ É/ "alligator"] n. either of two large swimming reptiles related to the cockadrills [crocodiles] and native to the Novomund and the Chinas. 17C, from Kentish [-/ri/], via Welsh or Willem Dutch from Yengatow [Tupi] yacar√© "jack-a-reigh". Final element adapted to reigh "skate [ray]", partly from Old English reohhe "skate" and partly from Old French raie "skate" (the two are probably related).

toward [/ÀàtuÀê.…ôd/ "future"] adj. still to come, not yet existing or taking place; of a period of time, next or impending; n. times yet to come, as opposed to now or to times which have already passed. Direct descendant of Old English t≈çweard adj. "still to come", stressed initially in opposition to homographic t≈çweard prp. "in the direction of" (modern Merch toward [/twaÀêd/] "facing").

yuckle [/Ààj ä.k…ôl/ "mend"] v. to restore to a state of correct or normal operation; to make whole as by the process of healing; to reattach separated pieces of something broken. 14C early Merch yokelen "reattach", frequentative or diminutive of yoken "yoke, harness", from Old English gegeocian "yoke". Probably unrelated to Old English gƒìocian "preserve, rescue, save".

Andalus

Notes in translation on the early history of the polity of Andalus.

Andalus es un polity ag sou√∞ dy peninsol Spagnart. Y lenga√ß d'Arabic Morrou e de Spagnart Mostaral se paroln y dou cos la√∞ ny region.
Andalus is a polity in the south of the Spanish peninsula. The languages of Morrove Arabic and Mostara Spanish are both widely spoken in the region.

N'Andalus dy siecr nouf sceye y floriscment de fanglan√ß theoretic. Cos particuler, machina√ßon por ve√∞ry produgr acceleran cos velo√ß dec l'yeul den des l'aust s'istra, oc menant all'enascment lava√ß de "ve√∞r zajadau" noncupa√∞.
Ninth-century Andalus saw the flowering of theoretic advancements. In particular, glassmaking techniques accelerated rapidly due to a cut-off of supply from the east, leading to the prodigious export of so-called "jackdaw glass".

L'orn religious dy Brethin respane ny zon de pouvlan√ß chrestan majorta√∞er n'Andalus des y nor√∞ durant y siecr on√ß e do√ß.
The Brethin religious order spread into the Christian-majority areas of Andalus from the north during the eleventh and twelfth centuries.

Nell'oc epoc atorn Andalus parde un sunt signfeyant d'ei√∞el ag nor√∞ e l'aust ne man de polity chrestan, tal com y Reyaum de Barcelon nou stabli√∞.
Around this time Andalus lost significant territory in the north and east to Christian polities, such as the newly-established Kingdom of Barcelon.

rancheyar /ranÀàkjar/ [ Ä…ê≈ãÀàkj…êÀê]
- array, lay out, to put into an orderly arrangement (especially to form into rows);
- deploy, marshal, to cause (e.g. troops) to be in some particular formation;
