202.3: Lexmber 2025 - 31: se corru√∞ "set"

se corru√∞ /se k…îÀàr…™Œ∏/ [se k äÀà Ä…™Œ∏]
- sitting pretty, made in the shade, set for life, living on easy street, having a comfortable or certain supply of money or resources;
- made, set, happy, having removed any obstacles and anticipating smooth sailing from here on in;
- in the clear, home free, out of the woods, past the hard slog and properly equipped for future challenges;
- bulletproof, watertight, on solid ground, having made oneself completely secure;
- ready, prepared, good to go, locked and loaded, shipshape, having sorted out any issues and fully able to proceed

Etymology: past participle of se corrir "make it, survive, get through, succeed", the reflexive of corrir "run". This descends directly from Latin curr≈ç "I run". Idiomatic use of this phrase is seen from the fifteenth century poetry of Manuel Kigman. The phrase appears to have originally referred to simply being well-off or comfortable; semantic association with the verb has more recently given the idiom connotations of past struggle and present battle-readiness.

202.5: Braxoch beet

excerpt from a page from the distributed library on the Markland vicine mesh, about the braxoch beet.

‚Ä¶variously also named golden beet, gilt beet, manegold, sweetbeet, is a variety of beet (plant ‚úæ2.1.17.1) which is characterised by the golden flesh and high sugar content of its root. Cultivation of braxoch beet is responsible for a significant fraction of sugar production in many temperate polities: as of 2025 it originated over four parts in five of sugar consumed everywhere on Albion. 

The name braxoch first enters the Merch language in the 1740s, via Borlish and Polish from Cassuvian braxoch "fine beet", an augmentative of brac "beet", which reflects Medieval Latin borƒÅg≈ç "borage" (further origin disputed).

History

The Willem [~NE France, NW Germany, Benelux] botanist Tewis Camigner, who was active during the Revitalist period in the sixteenth century, offers the first surviving written sources noting the high sugar content of beet, describing the billrod [cochineal red] hue and sweetness of a syrup he extracted from natural beets. However, the low concentration of sugar (perhaps one part in eighty) and the presence of other untasteful substances in the syrup made it no substitute for the sugarcane increasingly cultivated in the Novomund [Americas] over the following two centuries.

This changed with the Pentrose survey of the 1730s. Borland having recently been severely limited in its access to Novomundine trade, the state coordinated with the University of Pentrose to locate productive or otherwise useful cultivars of many plants. The survey's crowning glory was the development of the braxoch, which seems to have been a hybrid of Silesian and Cassuvian stock. 

Alchemy

The golden colour and high sugar content of the braxoch beet are intimately connected. The clause [gene] which instructs beet to produce its red pigments is located in the same heredian volume [chromosome] (volume II), very near a clause regarding the production of a sugar tinderbox [enzyme]. It is believed that beet breeding led to miscopying of this volume which simultaneously broke the red pathway (causing yellow pigments to predominate) and triggered a much higher rate of sugar production in the root.

Altering volume II also led to a thinner, more brittle skin, which would usually leave such beet more vulnerable to fungal infection during growth (partially explaining, in an Iscovalian sense, why this presentation is absent in the wild). However, braxoch beet exhibits another, independent alteration; it produces a non-functional iron marker [sensor]. This causes it to remain stuck in a low-iron regime, producing a much higher concentration of oromarynx [riboflavin; vitamin B‚ÇÇ], a yellow-orange substance and aqueous essence [water-soluble vitamin]. Oromarynx, which gleams under surblavic [UV] light, also has counterzoic [anti-microbial] properties which are activated by exposure to such light.

Hence, alterations which are individually costly become‚Ä¶

203.2: cug "fall"

cug /kaj/ [kaj]
- fall, drop, descent, motion downward;
- decrease, decline, reduction, the lessening of a value;
- downfall, defeat, loss, doom, an (especially precipitous) erosion of status;
- slide, flume, chute, slope, a structure along which things are made to travel from a higher to a lower position;
- (poetic) passing, death, the end of a person's life;
- (legal) nullification, repeal, the act of invalidating something (especially an act of law);
- (specifically) annulment, the dissolution of a marriage on the grounds that it was never formally valid

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish cau»ù, cu»ù /kaÀày…£ ~ ky…£/ "fall, descent", which reflects Latin adjective cad≈´cus "falling; doomed; futile; (law) lapsed". The word is seen in metaphorical use almost at once; for physical downward slopes it may have been influenced by Norman cute "fall; chute". Poetic and legal usages are both attested from the late Middle Borlish period.

L'intermane un cug de sy caldour corpore.

203.3: lat "root"

lat /lat/ [lat]
- side, edge, a one-dimesional segment of the surface of a shape;
- root, a number which when raised to a given power yields a particular quantity (especially the square root);
- cusp, precipice, an abrupt boundary between two relatively smooth planes;
- verge, brink, threshold, an edge or margin before a sudden or dramatic change

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a borrowing from Latin latus "side, flank". Originally used in geometry, and extended to the arithmetic usage by analogy to the example of a square's area and side-length. Senses referring to metaphorical sudden boundaries are attested from the eighteenth century.

203.4: vry "prestige"

vry /vri/ [v Äi]
- prestige, status, cachet, fame, esteem, the state of being well-thought-of by people;
- power, influence, the ability to effect one's will or to have people consider one an authority;
- kudos, acclaim, accolades, praise, credit for one's achievements

This descends from pre-Classical Kelt brƒ´gos "importance, weight", though to be cognate with Latin gravis "heavy". Originally a somewhat d√©class√© slang term, the word had passed into the ordinary register by the mid-twentieth century. 203.5: Through the Ages 2

more collected notes on a variety of background figures in Boralverse history, listed in chronological order.

Nad√Ýlla fi Carol was an influential figure in the early years of the city of Santrafew [~Boston, MA], one of the first trading posts in Mendeva [NA] established by Provence. She was raised as the eldest daughter of town reverend Carol Zancoud, and as an adult she helped to author the 1621 Becouin Treaty which defined the amicable relationship of the traders with the local Mast√≤iset [Massachusett] people for several decades. She is rumoured to have carried on an affair with the runaway heir to the Avosche duchy in her youth, although she makes no mention of such a relationship in her surviving diaries.

Dorothe Shock was the original proprietress of the De Marnoshring emporium in the early nineteenth century, which over the following two hundred years would become a quasipolitic guild [multinational corporation] controlling much of the market for children's toys and various material hobbies. She ran a store in the Saxon city of Miszen [Meissen], originally alone but from 1813 alongside her younger brother.

Agathe Bisset (d. 2025) was a French politician and author. Born in Blois during the latter years of the Drengot Collusion, she held various background positions in parliaments of France in the decades following its dissolution, only attaining wider recognition via her works of popular fiction from the 1990s onward. Her most successful work, 2003's Hommes en Noir (Men in Black), chronicles stories of spycraft and espionage from ancient times to the modern day; her mother's alleged position as a mid-century spy at the Ministry for Information may have been the source for some of Bisset's more recent accounts.

204.2: quil √ßaveyour "feather bed"

quil √ßaveyour /kw…™l tsaÀàvjur/ [kw…™l ts…êÀàvj äÀê]
- feather bed, a soft mattress filled with down;
- (metonymically) easy street, comfort, well-being, security, the concept of a comfortable life;
- mod con, amenity, some device or feature that makes one's life more convenient

Etymology: phrase attested from the early modern period, literally "cushioner bed". This is an early example of a compound-forming construction, much more prevalent in the modern language, which uses agent nouns as modifiers. The noun quil "bed" descends directly via Old Borlish quil, coil "bed, cot" from Latin cubƒ´le "bed, couch; lair, kennel"; the verb √ßaveyar "cushion, soften; sugarcoat, sanitise" is a derivative of √ßaveyal "pillow", descending from synonymous cervƒ´cal in Latin. Use as a mascot for easy living is seen from the nineteenth century, and has persisted likely due to its use in some evergreen folk songs.

Jo dout ou√∞ y quil √ßaveyour vel lou incombir.
/ ío dut uŒ∏ i kw…™l tsaÀàvjur v…õl lu Àå…™n.k…îmÀàb…™r/

204.3: alant a haun "homecoming"

alant a haun /aÀàlant a hon/ […êÀàlant a hon]
- landfall, the end of a sea voyage;
- homecoming, the act of returning to one's home;
- (by extension) safe habour, deliverance, sanctuary, respite, the attaining of a position of safety after an extended period of danger or uncertainty

Etymology: collocation originating in Kigman's fifteenth-century poetry, literally meaning "docking at a haven". The noun alant "landfall" is a deverbal from alandar "arrive, turn up, dock"; this was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English ƒ°elendan "approach, arrive". The noun haun "haven, port" was borrowed from Old Norrish h«´fn "harbour".

The phrase was used by Kigman already to mean a return to a metaphorical homeland; it had become synonymous with safe places more generally by the end of the following century.

Nos coum alant a haun posc tan semman.

We made landfall after so many weeks.

204.4: ne traiscn "late"

ne traiscn /ne trex.nÃ©/ [ne Ààt Äe.x…ên]
- slow, behind, (of a timepiece) showing a time earlier than the true time;
- late, delayed, occurring after the scheduled time due to unforeseen events or inefficiencies;
- laggy, buffering, responding slowly due to high latency

Etymology: originally horological jargon used by clockmakers, attested from the seventeenth century, literally "in lag". The noun traiscn "lag, delay" is a deverbal from traiscnar "trail, drag behind, lag", which reflects a Vulgar Latin verb traxin≈ç "I drag, pull, tug" which extends the verb trah≈ç in Classical Latin. The word has been extended from clocks to public transportation and then to computing in the modern era.

205.2: harvougl "earwig"

harvougl /harÀàvujl/ [h…êÀêÀàvw…™l]
- earwig, any of various winged insects with elongated bodies and rear-pointing pincers;
- earbud, a small speaker placed into the ear canal;
- (intelligence) handler, someone at a base who remotely directs an intelligence asset in the field

The development of the initial consonant is unexplained, but compare Vascon horveix "earwig". Folklore connecting the insect to ear-burrowing influenced the modern senses, first attested in the twentieth century.

205.3: vrolliscour "crowdpleaser"

205.5: The Alphabet

excerpt from near the end of the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (sixth edition), a reference work published in 2001 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

‚Ä¶a clouded wine fermented from rice, brewed across Asia from northeastern Hind [India] to Nivon [Japan]. Around 1700 as zito < Napolitan zito, zuto "rice wine" < Tenivian [Angami]  ê√∫th√≥. Respelt in the nineteenth century in line with the possible cognate in Greek Œ∂·ø¶Œ∏ŒøœÇ ‚Ä¢ (z√ªthos) "an Egyptian barley beer".

Afterwords

1. Th'Alphabet

a ba ce dy e ef ge,
hake i jot, kea el em;
en o pa qua ro es te
u va win, ex wy zed.

The letter names in Merch are a mixture, some descending from Old English futhork runes and others from Old Northern French names for the Latin letters.

The vowels a e i o u [/…õÀê iÀê …ôi uÀê j…ôw/] reflect Latin; four from early Merch long vowels [/aÀê eÀê iÀê oÀê/] and the last from the adaptation of ONFr [/y/] to [/juÀê/]. The name of the half-vowel y [/w…ôi/] is of unclear origin, but usually supposed to be a combination of the names of u and i (compare ONFr ui and modern Borlish oy "Y").

The inverted names ef el em en es [/…õf …õl …õm …õn …õs/] come via ONFr from Latin, unusual for ending with their associated sound instead of starting with it. The names of kea ex zed [/k…õÀê …õks z…õd/] also come from Latin for lack of any corresponding rune (although some scholars suggests that the ill-attested elhs "elks" rune may have contributed to "X").

Other borrowings via ONFr from Latin are ce ge hake te [/siÀê d íiÀê h…õÀêk tiÀê/]; alternatively, the name for "T" may stem from a rebracketing of tue < OE tiw as te + u due to the following u in the alphabet.

Descendants of OE futhorc include dy ro win [/d…ôi roÀê w…™n/] < OE d√¶g rƒÅd wynn. The "R" name showcases final-consonant deletion, which also happened to ba pa qua [/baÀê paÀê kwaÀê/] < OE berc per√æ c∆øer√æ in the early modern period as a form of regularisation.

The final two letters are jot and va [/d í…ît vaÀê/]; the first is an early Merch borrowing via Latin i≈çta from Greek Œπ·ø∂œÑŒ± ‚Ä¢ (i√¥ta) "iota" used to distinguish the consonant j from the vowel i, and the second is an alteration of earlier vaw (likely by rebracketing with the following win) borrowed in the early modern period via Latin vau from (presumed) Greek œùŒ±·ø¶ ‚Ä¢ (wa√ª) "waw".

2. The Numbers

one two thre‚Ä¶

L'Alleyan√ß Borrhatic es un congeries de colluy indrec e de band por gest politic oc caglou irrayant sur gournment la√Øc, condo√ßment e (ne dezein recent) tan promo√ßon de pa√ß e de reclam.
The Borrhatic League is a collection of charitable organisations and political action groups with a focus on laic governance, education and (in recent decades) also pacifism and objection.

Malgra√∞ ig l'Alleyan√ß Borrhatic e√∞ y commou√∞ a Desveyan√ß contemporane son discrepa√∞ (cos occajonal conflagrant), reconnu√∞ es y dou aun parol contriboit ny Declara√ßon La√Øc d'an 1850 ne Belgrad.

206.2: a√∞aun "as one"

a√∞aun /aÀà√∞on/ […êÀà√∞on]
- (archaic, poetic or dialect) as one, together, at the same time or place or doing the same thing;
- (legal) in agreement, united, unanimous, without any dissent;
- in unison, in chorus, tutti, acting together in e.g. playing music or singing;
- (interjection) let's go, now, an exhortation to simultaneous action

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish a√∞aun, extended variant of synonymous aun "together, at the same time". This comes either from a Late Latin phrase ad ≈´num "at one" or from a deverbal of ad≈´n≈ç "I unite". Current in the language until the early modern period, it has fossilised in legal and musical uses; the interjection appears to have been reborrowed in the twentieth century from rural dialects that retained the term.

206.3: √ßondr "pendant"

√ßondr /Ààts…în.drÃ©/ [Ààts…în.d…ê]
- pendant, a piece of jewellery which hangs down as an ornament (as attached to a necklace or earring);
- locket, such an ornament which can be opened to store some item of sentimental value;

206.5: The Scathe of Saint Sophia

excerpted from 2014 popular history textbook The Fourth Burning of London, and other Screwups: the Eventful History of Things Going Wrong, written in collaboration with the Faculty of Domain History at the University of Huzatre [~Bayonne] by writer (formerly and more famously of historical lovetales) Galar√ßa fi Molhom√©, who undertook her studies there.

‚Ä¶by Nicol√Ý sAvignon, who in the fourteenth century led the renovation and expansion of Saint Sophia's Cathedral [the Hagia Sophia] in Stamboul [Istanbul], which had stood mostly unchanged since its construction in the sixth century during Justinian I's reign. It has been suggested that the expansion was commissioned in order to cement the cathedral as the largest one in all Christendom; challengers in Valencia and Augsburg were in the process of being built at that time that would have surpassed it if not for sAvignon's redesign.

There is a popular theory that sAvignon is responsible for the Scathe of Saint Sophia‚Äîdespite the fact that this calamitous building collapse (which killed Emperor Theosthenes, much of his family, and dozens of dignitaries) occurred in 1651 N, over three hundred years after his reconstruction project was completed. The theory appears to have originated in 1890 via Jordan hixPasqual's popular history work The Last Years of the Last Roman Empire, in which he contends with little evidence that sAvignon employed experimental and faulty techniques in reinforcing the great central dome of the cathedral, weakening the structure over the centuries until the eventual disaster.

Of course, it is difficult to know which precise modifications were made to the cathedral in the 1340s, but most historians agree that the bulk of the changes were in extending the complex, not rebuilding supports. Nonetheless, the dramatic quality of the Scathe of Saint Sophia, along with the narrative that it led directly to the Empire's final collapse less than a decade later, have led this theory to become‚Ä¶

notes in translation on the Blue Trans-Willem War

Y Var Tras-Willem Blau sta l'un entr un ranc de var nell'epoc modern primal ec polity Fransc e Teudesc envouvent cos principal (oc a digr, l'e√ß polity l'ei√∞el de Willemy parlocant) e√∞ ec stan a gest accommis a fin aute√ß broucar sull'o√ß ei√∞el.
The Blue Trans-Willem War was one of a series of wars in the early modern period which principally involved French and Dutch [German] polities (that is, those powers located on either side of the territory of Willemy [~Benelux, Lorraine]) and which were de facto waged over control of said territory.

Noc obstroint ig defagl sey l'akirtan√ß sporadic de combat por√ßonnar com var n'e√ß cahut lon un poy d'arvei√∞rosta√∞, scaun√ß historic ne nostrell'epoc beno√ßn y periodeya√ßon par colour lonc y scrittour dy siecr novo√ß Andrysk de Santer.
Though the sporadic flare-ups of hostilies are difficult to divide into discrete wars without some arbitrariness, modern historiography usually works with the chromatic periodisation of nineteenth-century writer Andrysk de Santer.

Sy model, concreut ou (lonc y tesmogn) des y colour sur y gonfan dell'e√ß victour ben des l'ongit de symbol alchemic, attriboy y var Blau com y rix p√¶nultem, oc cessant ny siecr setto√ß nuverrem.

Certan historicour, ma noc de Santer, aun ag var Blau y spai√∞oir ascreut d'un kynous entr y coglen√ß abrangendessem de Rix Romannesc ny siecr setto√ß mejan.

- distaff, a device storing natural fibres to be spun;
- spinneret, the organ with which a spider spins its web;
- spinning, fibrecraft, the art of making things like textiles or rope;
- (regional, poetic) cattail, teasel, mullein, foxtail, or various other herbs and reeds with distaff-shaped flower spikes or seedheads

207.5: Lineball Global Tourney

collected notes on a sporting event.

The Lineball Global Tourney (Borlish: Torne Mondial a Lan√ßbal /t…îrÀàne m…înÀàdjal a lantsÀàbal/), often abbreviated in the English languages to the LGT, is a knock-out competition in which teams from around the world compete in the sport of lineball. The LGT has been held every five years since 1920, and it has grown from a dozen entrants to the modern complement of over sixty. The precise line-up of each tourney is affected by the ways geopolitics interacts with the institution's founding charter, as well as economic considerations (which is to say bribery of the bilateral committee).

In principle, teams in the LGT represent nations or at least polities. In practice, the complexity of defining what counts as a polity (an entire alliance-federacy? A province? Half of a double-crown?)‚Äîas well as the inequity of polities having wildly different sizes of talent pool‚Äîhave led to guidelines defining loose bounds on the population size of applicant polities.

The sport of lineball was formalised many decades before the establishment of the LGT, and there were several small-scale international lineball competitions that preceded it. For instance, the Cup of Cappatia [South America] was first held in Awas√∫conda [~Paran√°] in 1908, pitting twenty city teams from various Cappatian staddomains against each other.

The 1935 LGT was the first to be held in Mendeva [North America], hosted by Hasiny [~lower Mississippi watershed]. The territory of Norman Kent, which was then a constituent of the Drengot Collusion, was a competitor whose team included one Vr√©queu Margouille. His son, Vincent, also became a professional lineball player, and indeed he captained Kent to its only victory in the 1965 LGT.

208.2: a to-rutto "single file"

a to-rutto /a Àåto.r…™Ààto/ […êÀåto. Ä…™Ààto]
- single file, in a line or queue one person wide;
- in a row, sequentially, one after another, successively, located in a linear span across space or time;
- one at a time, individually, occurring in discrete instances

Etymology: attested in various forms since the late Middle Borlish period, an ideophonic idiom used to refer to single-file sorts of arrangement or motion. The phrase is hypothesised to originate in farmers' livestock-counting numerals, which may be reflected by the modern appearance of to-rutto in counting-style nursery rhymes. These rural tally-like systems (e.g. an, to, ry, bo, bum, ...), which are present across Borland, derive from Germanic or Celtic numbers.

208.5: Chemical Etymology (cont. 4)

further excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers [1].

- adipous adj. [/Ààa.d…™.p…ôuz/ "fat-soluble"] of a substance, able to dissolve in media such as fats, oils or spirits, as opposed to being able to dissolve in water (see also aqueous); more generally, lacking any significant imbalances of plenty between its atoms [non-polar]. Early 18C, from Scholastic Latin adip≈çsus "fatty, made of fat", repurposed from earlier non-theoretical usage (see Middle French adipeux "fatty", from the same source) < Classical Latin adeps "fat, lard, grease".

- essence n. [/Àà…õ.s…™ns/ "vitamin"] any of a specific group of substances, either aqueous or adipous, which are must be ingested in small quantities for human health because they are not prodcued internally. In alchemical use circa 1860, formed backwards from essential "necessary, indispensible" < Medieval Latin essentiƒÅlis "intrinsic, necessary" < Classical Latin essentia "nature, being" < esse "to be".

- oromarynx n. [/Àå…î.…æ äÀàma.…æ…™nks/ "riboflavin"] an orange-yellow aqueous essence with various functions in the human body, including case [cell] fuelling and thyreous action [immune response]. 1923 via Welsh < Scholastic Latin oromarynx < Greek ·ΩÄœÅœåœÇ ‚Ä¢ (or√≥s) "whey", since the substance was first isolated from cow's-milk whey, and ŒºŒ±œÅœÖŒ≥Œæ ‚Ä¢ (marynx) "marynx" [fluorescent substance], due to its characteristic halvation under surblavic [UV] light. This latter word, first attested in the Malchassian period in the sense "flash, glimmer", is thought to be related to verb ŒºŒ±œÅŒºŒ±ŒØœÅœâ ‚Ä¢ (marma√≠r≈ç) "(of metal) to flash, sparkle" or ŒºŒ±œÅŒπŒµœçœÇ ‚Ä¢ (marie√∫s) "quicklime".

209.5: Maria Joquinnera

collected notes on a sixteenth-century figure.

Maria Joquinnera (b. c. 1512, d. 1565?), also known as Princess Maria, the Princess of Mendeva and (among other spellings) Dona Quixote, was a woman of Nappe [Lenape] origin, born in the region of the lower Guittoc [Delaware] River, who travelled to the Vetomund [Old World] in the late 1530s and became one of the most remarkable figures of the Intertwining Century. Presented at several Vetomundine courts as a usurped and displaced princess of a Mendevan people, she was by all contemporary accounts a formidable polyglot and political intellect. She is best known for her patronage of the first systematic lexicon of a Nappe [Unami] tongue. The circumstances of her birth, her crossing, and her death remain disputed.

Nomenclature

The name recorded least often, which Maria may have used in private among family and close friends, appears inconsistently in various phonetic renderings and earliest as as Quixote in the account-book (ca. 1538‚Äì41) of merchant Bartholomeu fi Acher. The root element has later been tentatively linked to the Nappe word kixuh "sun, moon", which may be connected to the epithet la Dama del Oc√Ýs "the Sunset Lady", attested only once in the Queen of Barcelon's correspondence.

Meanwhile, "Maria" is almost certainly a baptismal name, a common choice in this era and region for converts from non-Christian backgrounds. The name or epithet Joquinnera is a demonyn derivative related to Nappejoquin, the Pronvincial traders' name for the peoples of the lower Guittoc and Esquanna [Susquehanna] rivers. This was borrowed from the Nappe word hoking "land, territory".

Origins and Voyage

Maria's early life is shrouded in mystery. She may indeed have been of saquima [~noble] lineage, but no independent Nappe account survives of a "usurper" (described vaguely in European accounts as one of her father's kinsmen) seizing control of a nation and expelling the family of the former ruler. It cannot be determined how much of the traditional tale of her origins was invented by the Provinical merchants who brought her to Europe, how much was shaped by Maria herself, and what if any actual events underlie it.

Two broad hypotheses to explain Maria's crossing to European between 1539 and 1540 have been advanced. First, she may have emigrated voluntarily as an emissary, out of simple curiosity or in order to flee real danger. This is supported by accounts of her subsequent behaviour, which reflect a clear understanding of her position and a canniness regarding how to best advance it. Alternatively, she may have been abducted or otherwise coerced aboard by the Provincial merchants, as a curiosity for display or a diplomatic prop. This fits the broader unfortunate trend of similar mistreatment of Mendevan peoples more closely, but is harder to reconcile with the deference apparently shown to her as early as her arrival in Marsella [Marseilles], unless she managed to win over the captain (whose identity has unfortunately been lost) and crew in the course of the voyage.

The question of her son, Barthem Joquinnel, further complicates matters. Born within months of Maria's arrival, he could conceivably have been fathered before her voyage or during it. The identity of Barthem's father is not mentioned in surviving contemporary accounts of the birth; Barthem himself, who became a minor diplomat in later life, appears never to have discussed it.

Life in the Vetomund

Maria Joquinnera arrived in a Provence which had been devastated by the Second Great Dying only a couple of decades earlier. She is recorded as having already learnt to speak Doccian [~Occitan] when she arrived in Marsella, and to have picked up several other European tongues (Vask, Burgund, possibly even Borlish) in her first years in Europe.

The fiction (or partial fiction) of a displaced Mendevan princess proved durable, effective and convenient for all parties. It gave the royal courts of Europe a legible category to place her it; it provides the merchants with a compelling story of the western lands to spur further investment; it gave her more room to manoeuvre than any other transplant from Mendeva would receive in this century. She is known to have sojourned at the courts of Burgund, Barcelon and Lombardy; one noblewoman of Furlain described her as "accustomed both to being observed and to observing others without the slightest awe".

Her most enduring legacy is a dictionary of the Nappe tongue (Onomatoir de la Lenga Nappena), in whose front matter she is named as both patron and primary informant. The most complete surviving manuscript contains some 1400 words, along with set phrases and diplomatic formulae, grammatical notes of mediocre accuracy, and a short text which may be a song of mourning or of formal welcome.

The record thins markedly after approximately 1558. It is possible that she attempted to return to Mendeva (she appears alongside attendants on a Provincial vessel's manifest, but may have disembarked before the ocean crossing, as no record of her arrival exists; alternatively, she may have died at sea). Her son visited the Novomund [New World] only once, married a Burgund woman of the lower gentry, and spoke Nappe into adulthood (albeit with a Doccian accent that is recorded as having greatly amused his mother); it is thought that he did not pass the language on to his children.

References

- Bartholomeu fi Ascer,  Accounts of the Merchantmen of Provence (1535‚Äì45)
- Dictionary of the Nappe Tongue (compiled ca. 1547‚Äì51)
- Physican, Heal Thyself: the First Century of Santrafew (anthology; the source of several documents regarding early Provincial contact with Mendevan peoples)
- A. Thendar, Princess or Prisoner? The Curiosity of Kixuhque

- qualm, misgiving, reservation, scruple, doubt, a feeling of unease or uncertainty regarding a course of action;

210.4: mogn "low"

- low, low down, situated close to the ground;
- short, squat, having little vertical extent (esp compared to other dimensions);

210.5: Journeys and Junctures

notes on various aspects of the Boralverse alternate history setting which begin with the letter J.

Jaibos Breweries (Borlish: Yeustman Jaibos) is a beer-making guild based in the southwestern Borlish city of Axbane operating under the ownership of the Jacobin Fraternity. The Fraternity was founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges, but was forced to flee to Borland in 1067 N after being implicated in a popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffrey II; it thereafter established its principal seat at the Peninsular Monastery on a promontory overlooking Axbane.

Japetos III is an barric steeplepost line [telegraph cable] running undersea between the port of Gadire [Agadir] in Morrack and the city of Paratzon [Saint John] in Brasil [Newfoundland], a distance of some 1,500 leagues. It is the third cable to bear the name Iapetos, a Titan who as the father of Atlas has supplied a ready name for successive endeavours to span the Atlantic by wire. The project was jointly conceived in 1943 N by the Morrack and Ambrosian governments, but was set back first by protracted disagreements over funding between the two administrations and then by the outbreak of war, before the cable at last conveyed its first messages in October 1950 N.

Josforo Sant'Angelo was a traveller and writer from Tuscany, and the youngest son of the last Marksman of Cremuna‚Äîa title that lapsed when the Lombard marquisates were abolished in the 1860s. Sant'Angelo recorded one of his travels in Pasti Mezziterreni (Middlesea Meals, 1880 N), a mealbook documenting a single sunward journey along the full arc of the Middlesea [Mediterranean] coast, from Tarifa in Andalus to the Atlantic-facing port of XÃÄr l Joas [Ksar es-Seghir] in Morrack.

Judeta Barracin  was a novelist born in Andalus who spent her formative years among the islands of the Arcabil [the Caribbean], the daughter of a trading family with commercial interests in those ports. She returned to Andalus as an adult with a sharp eye for the social textures of its cities. Her 1919 N trevold [novel] Querdos Llineros (Flaxen Hearts) made was an immediate sensation: set in early-nineteenth-century Cordova [C√≥rdoba] and following a cast of market traders, minor gentry, and household staff through a web of interconnected small dramas, it is now considered a founding text of social comedy alongside the contemporaneous output of Dorotha Quistack.

211.2: a tug "for fun"

a tug /a taj/ [a taj]
- for fun, for the love of the game, done for one's own enjoyment or other intrinsic desire to do so;
- friendly, penny-ante, (of a game) without serious stakes;
- voluntarily, intentionally, by one's own free will;
- (conjunction) so that, in order to, with a desire to bring about (some result)

Etymology: Middle Borlish idiom, literally "to passion". The noun tug "passion, fervour, enthusiasm" descends from Old Borlish tu»ù "inclination, impulse, drive". This was borrowed from Old English tyge "a pull, a draw", which is related to the verb tƒìon "pull, drag, lead". As a conjunction (attested from the sixteenth century) the construction requires someone's explicit agency in a way which the near-synonyms por and a fin do not.

211.3: lon mensc "dishonourable"

lon mensc /l…în Ààm…õnx/ [l…îm Ààm…õ≈ãx]
- (military) dishonourable, due to conduct that is considered reprehensible;
- without a reference, without a character, not being entitled to an employer's testimony when applying to a new job;
- (by extension) cold turkey, abruptly and without consideration for comfort

Etymology: originally in legal expressions such as destourm lon mensc "dishonourable discharge", the phrase literally means "without honour". The preposition lon "without" reflects Latin longƒì "far away"; the archaic noun mensc "honour; mercy; courtesy; decorum" was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old Norrish mennska "humanity". Extension to non-miltary and non-employment contexts occurred in the mid-twentieth century.

211.4: haval fair "play pretend"

haval fair /haÀàval fer/ [h…êÀàval feÀê]
- play pretend, make believe, role-play, to engage in imaginative activity;
- feign, dissemble, put on an act, to perform insincerely;
- go through the motions, to do something perfunctorily or without conviction

Extension out of the realm of childhood play is seen from the nineteenth century.

211.5: Who's Who (1900s Edition)

collected notes on various figures active during the twentieth century.

Mark Deuthex (fl. 1973) was born in Roun [Rouen], the administrative capital of the Drengot Collusion. He spent his early career as an operative for the Red House (the informal name for the Collusion's Ministry for Information); his service there spanned the Collusion's final years and its abrupt collapse in 1961. In civilian life Deuthex took to writing. His 1973 spycraft novel We Wake to Broken Windows drew on his years in the Ministry with a frankness that might have constituted a criminal offence had the Collusion still existed. It remains one of the most widely read firsthand accounts of the Collusion's intelligence apparatus; a sequel was reportedly planned but never completed.

Johan Pamfey (fl. 1971) was a collocker [journalist] for the Borlish Sunday magazine Niacer (a word meaning "pertaining to nests or, metaphorically, to households"), where he contributed most notably a column of political and cultural observation over roughly two decades. He was famous for a reliable willingness to voice unpopular positions, and for various expos√©s of corporate malfeasance. One of his articles revealed that the Ministry of Health had taken money to downplay the effects of impure imports of detergent chemicals by large companies including Pax and Chandler.

Maldwen Tamaha (b. 1890, fl. 1950) was a long-reigning king of Hasiny [lower watershed of the Mississippi]. He acceded to the throne in 1916 and presided over an unusually steady few decades in Hasiny's history. His reign saw the expansion of the country's sobot [radio] network, and contributed personally in the form of patronage in exchange for weekly speeches broadcast to the nation via the technology. He is also remembered for a personal investment in the expansion of universal primary schooling to the rural parts of the country.

212.4: benoyour "medium"

- medium, spiritualist, a person who attempts communication with the dead;
- shaman, witch doctor, a member of a community representing or speaking for the spiritual realm and often also acting as a healer

This verb was formed in the early Middle Borlish period by analogy with bendigr "to bless" (itself obsolete by parallel bene√Ør, both from Latin benedƒ´c≈ç "I commend, praise; I bless") from the root o√Ør "to hear" < Latin audi≈ç "I hear". The word enjoyed some use in lay Christian practice before dropping out of use in the Revitalist period of the early sixteenth century; it was revived in reference to non-Christian roles as well as to rites of the Novomundine "Prodigal Faiths" during the nineteenth century.

212.5: German languages

paragraphs excerpted in translation from a page on Borland's distributed library about the German languages, as it stood in February 2021 N.

‚Ä¶descended from Classical German [Proto-Germanic], which was spoken in roughly the same time period as Classical Latin. It fractured during the first millennium N into three major lineages, which we label Dutch German, Gothic German and Norrish German [West, East, and North Germanic].

It is uncertain whether the language of the Goths is truly the only member of its lineage. Its possible contemporary relatives (the languages of the Vandals, the Burgundians, and some others) are too little attested in writing to be certain, and the proposed modern relatives (the German languages in Halich, Kiew or Vallack [regions of southeastern Europe], mostly) are too far displaced in time and too much influenced by the Dutch languages to connect definitively to Gothic.

Dutch German is the most widespread lineage, and can further be divided geographically into the Mainlander Dutch languages and the English languages. Some of the Mainlander Dutch languages are the official languages of a polity or polities, like Willem, Saxon or Bavarn (the last of which is official in both Bavarn and Ruritania), while others enjoy at most regional recognition (for example Frise).

Here is an example of a sentence in various modern Dutch languages, along with reconstructions for older forms.

Classial German: Gultham fl≈çidi thurhu quen≈çnom ƒìthr≈çnuns.
First Dutch: Gol√æ fl≈çidi √æuruh quen≈çn≈ç ƒÅdr≈çn.

Old English: Gold flƒìw√æ √æurh √æƒÅra wƒ´fa «£dran.
Old W Dutch: Gold fluoieth thuro thero wƒ´vo ƒÅtheran.
Old N Dutch: Gold fliuted thuruh thero wƒ´v≈ç ƒÅtheran.
Old S Dutch: Golt fliuzit duruh dero wƒ´bo ƒÅdaran.

Merch: Gold flows through the womens veins.
Willem: Goud vloyeth th√≥r the vrown aren.
Saxon: Gold fletet durgh de fruen odren.
Bavarn: Goit fliest drugh der frauen adern.

213.1: Historiography Questions 2

-4. A mesur de cal dezein dy siecr veintessem figheu loy l'Anarchy Portingal? Cal fanglan√ß manda√∞er dell'e√ß anna√∞ preluyent se courn raussi√∞ cos desconnex com issent entrig dy sfrondran√ß cas aute√ß politic?

213.5: Stobor

excerpt from 2004 work Figures of Fable, a compendium of fantastical creatures and beings found in the world's mythology and folklore. Originally published as Sers Fabloses, it was written by folklorist Floren√ßa Garaut, who lived most of her life in the New Provincial city of Gorram, and who claimed her interest in folklore arose from the inexhaustible bedtime questions of her triplet children.

‚Ä¶the ever-enigmatic riddles of the sphinx.

Stobor

Stobor is a broad term referring to any beings which arise from the magical animation of artificial depictions of people (or animals, or nonexistent creatures), or which otherwise take the form of animated figurines or dolls. Traditionally and in common usage stobor are miniatures, but in more recent taxonomy the term has been broadened to life-size figures like animated statues (or even, occasionally, larger-than-life colossi).

The word stobor first referred to sprites in the folklore of Awry [~Hungary] and Kiew [~Ukraine] which animated children's dolls, going on miniature adventures of their own or performing helpful or mischievous deeds around the house. It is allied with the modern Majoric [Hungarian] stobroƒãke "figurine", Bulgar —Å—Ç–æ–±–æÃÅ—Ä ‚Ä¢ (stob√≥r) "woodcarving, wooden doll" and Lithowan stab√° "idol".

Peoples the world over have imagined that the images they create taking on a life of their own. Some of the oldest civilisations created figurines like the Egyptian voshapty [ushabti] or the warriors of Lleswan [Terracotta Army] to act as attendants or guardians in the afterlife. Other peoples have associated intangible spirits with physical representations, like the minx [nkisi] of middle Africa or the assistants of Hephaestus.

The carved wooden figures erected at Norrish [Norse] sacred sites were understood not merely as representations of the gods but as temporary residences: the‚Ä¶

214.5: Saints Isida

excerpts from The Alphabetical Almanac, an enumeration of Christian saints in alphabetical order along with short biographies and information such as their feast days. Written in 1854 by Christopher "Kipper" Bytheway, a Merch domain historian, the book was lauded for the quantity of original research that Bytheway conducted on his travels and pilgrimages, but was also controversial for eliding most details concerning the saints' miracles.

‚Ä¶twenty-four ravens.

Isida of Toled [Toledo]

Isida of Toled (fl. 1220; feast day April 21) was a nun of the Brethin Order during the twilight of the Single Caliphate's Cairene authority over Andalus. The Brethin House in Toled was a relatively recent incursion into a Christian-majority region of Andalus, but had already accrued a small but influential scholarly community.

She is remembered principally for her work De Mutationibus Lingu√¶ "On the Transformations of a Language", in which she contended that the changes between Latin and vernaculars like Mostara or Barcelon were not the result of corruption or carelessness, but instead comprised divinely ordained‚Äîand, vitally, enumerable and consistent‚Äîpatterns of sound change. Her work anticipated by some margin the comparative method of later scholars. She also appears to have had some command of Arabic and Hebrew, given some references to an incomplete and now-lost companion which may have drawn similar comparisons between those languages.

Isida of Aldegia [Lake Ladoga]

Isida of Aldegia (fl. 1280; feast day December 21; known locally as Siter√∏) was a princess of the Inger and an integral part of the Baptism of the North. Her marriage to the king of Holmgrod [Novgorod] was contingent on the conversion of the Inger, a pagan holdout. The conversion of the Inger was completed in stages, with the baptism of the elite preceding that of the broader population by some years. Isida is said to have served as interpreter for the Convoy Australier's missionaries during this intermediary period. Her feast day on the winter solstice is held by most historians to reflect a deliberate alignment‚Ä¶

sabler reuvr /saÀàbl…õr rauvrÃ©/ [s…êÀàbl…õÀê Àà Äau.v…ê]

215.5: Ministry for Consequences

excerpt from The Structure of Twentieth-Century Polities, a 2024 reference work by Neuwillem historian Mbaiara Hormelingh, translated from the original Willem (Stellingen van Twintixt√©w St√°tsvorm) into Merch by Jet Torpenhow.

‚Ä¶first Ministry for Consequences to be established was that of the Drengot Collusion, the alliance-federacy of northwestern Europe whose dramatic collapse in 1961 is covered in more detail in the section of Ministries for Information in chapter 4. The last of the twenty-four ministries of the Collusion to be created, it was set up in 1932 under the leadership of Runcibal Sawyer-Boon (nowadays eclipsed in fame by his grand-daughter Vendetta Boon). 

The original remit of the Ministry for Consequences was purely reactive: it was to monitor the impact of policies implemented by laws and by the decrees of other ministries (e.g. Family, Farming or Wealth) and to publish reports on their effects. Institutions with similar functions had existed in many governments for centuries (and arguably millennia, in the cases of Cathay, China and the Persias), though they were rarely so centralised and so independent in their activities. Despite its later failings, the Collusion's anthracite government in the 1930s recognised that Consequences would have to maintain editorial autonomy or else be subject to censorship from the very government it was intended to monitor.

At the time of writing, Ministries for Consequences have been established in several dozen polities worldwide, from the Ministry for Effectiveness in Tavancy [~Incan Empire] to the Ministry for the Future in Nackon Dai [Thailand]. The concept spread most rapidly in the late 1960s and through the 1970s, as the fallout of the Furore of 1961 revealed how important‚Ä¶

Etymology: idiom meaning "believe in [a different thing] instead" attested from the eighteenth century. The verb creir "believe" descends directly from Latin crƒìd≈ç "I believe", while the adverb aglon "instead, rather, alternatively" comes from Latin aliunde "from elsewhere".

/ í…îz krint n…õjÀàl…în aj si Ààfra√∞rÃ© v…õl reÀåa.paÀàr…™r/

217.5: Anna-Dorota's Discovery

excerpt from Light and Learning in the Long Peace (Chapter 7: The Glade of Iris), by Merch historian Wilwyn Droppett, published in 1928 through Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers

‚Ä¶a few years earlier, even in 1816 the span-glass remained primarily a workshop novelty. Hunnady had made thorough observations of the sun during his tenure as Second Lecturer at the Library of Sobjan [P√©cs], and his demonstration hall that April was filled with a dozen scholars witnessing the dark bands in the sun's iris [spectrum].

Among them was a young Stil√°ge Anna-Dorota (1788‚Äì1863N), already known for papers on the alignment [polarisation] of reflected light. She remained in Sobjan through the summer, assisting with the grinding of more lenses and the construction of a more portable model. She appears to have been the one to notice the opportunity to observe the sun during an overshadowing [eclipse] later that year in the course of her calculating the timing of additional celestial events for the library's archives.

Stil√°ge reached Lengrod [Lviv] on November 2 with three assistants. At the time it was a frontier city in the Kingdom of the Danaw contested with Poland. The weather was finer here than to the west, where the Two-Years' Winter was ruining harvests and contributing to political instability, and via a cousin contact she was able to hire a shepherd's hut in the hills of Vennike [Vynnyky], two leagues outside the city and along the projected path of the overshadowing.

With dictation and a maid watching a pendulum clock, Stil√°ge recorded an obscuration [totality] of 101 seconds. She recounted her observations in a letter in Latin to Hunnady that day, excerpted in translation here.

‚Ä¶extinguished the sun, a wreath appeared at once, drawn into rays as if combed. Tracing at once the aperture, I saw at once the two marks of aquiferous air [hydrogen], yet further (between the 39th and 40th points) a golden spark nearly as bright. Though I am not an alchemist, Lector, I would venture no air [gas] of my acquaintance blazes with such‚Ä¶

This golden spark is, of course, the primary band of the corporal we now call aureol [helium], from the Latin aureola, the crown of saints and of the overshadowed sun). Stil√°ge and her collaborators in Sobjan published these observations the following year, but were not widely read for another decade due to the disruption of the Second German War and the collapse of the Kingdom of the Danaw.

Stil√°ge went on to several other discoveries and close correspondence with Wexter Hathwall, whose magnum opus Astraphor [Electricity] and Light included her‚Ä¶

##

218.5: Taisha

Taisha (Borlish Taisca) is a polity of Gulf Mendeva [NA Gulf], bordered by Hasiny to the east and Mashick to the south. Its coastline stretches from east of the Tocolon [~Brazos River] to southwest of the Nar Mottare [Rio Grande], its capital is Yanaquana [~San Antonio].

Toponymy

The name Taisha comes via Arabic Tayisha' from Carovacian [Caddo] ta√≠sha' "welcome", first written down by Morrack cartographers of the New World Company in the early sixteenth century. It is presumed that this word was simply the most common formula the expedition heard on their voyage.

History

The eastern half of modern Taisha was, until the seventeenth century, part of the Hasiny confederacy of Carovacian peoples, from which the neighbouring polity takes its name. The southern and western reaches were under the influence of a series of Mashick [Aztec] vassals, trading upriver along the Tocolon and across the interior.

First Vetomundine contact was with Morrack's New World Company in the 1510s, and through the later sixteenth century Mashick expanded northeast along the coast, establishing frontier towns. Mashick's authority in the far northeast remained thin, and from the middle seventeenth century onward British and Jaughta [Choctaw] ranchers and merchants (operating with the tacit imprimatur of the Kingdom of Britain) moved steadily west into the Tocolon basin. For most of the eighteenth century the region had three overlapping structures of governance: Mashick regents in the south and west, Hasinick towns along the coast, and Carovacian councils in the interior.

The Concordat of Aberdocolon (1850), signed in the namesake port city, formalised this arrangement. The agreement involved the Mashick crown, the newly autonomous Hasinic crown, and elected Carovacian leaders. The Concordat's practical effect was minimal (its commissioners spent most of the subsequent decades arbitrating cattle-rustling disputes) but later Taishan historiography elevated it into a founding document in which the region's three constituent communities were recognised as co-equal parties.

The late nineteenth century saw Mashick reassert central control; the country was reorganised as the northeasternmost province of the Mashick Empire. This period was also the great age of Taishan herdtale [Western] literature, the genre having by then cooled in Hasiny itself. The grounded and unsentimental stories out of Taisha are generally considered to be the pinnacle of the genre.

The Lovac Wars of the 1920s and 30s brought economic collapse, two cycles of conscription and the first bombardment of Yanaquana by airship. A loose self-rule coalition, drawing explicitly on the Concordat's tripartite model, issued the Declaration of Yanaquana in 1936. Mashick, undergoing its own Ubiquitous revolution, did not contest it, granting Taisha legal independence that same year.

219.5: Lumbridge

Lumbridge (Merch /Ààl äm.b…æ…™d í/; Saxon L√ºmbr√ºgh) is a city in the northern plains of Saxony, situated on the Elmenaw river. It is one of the three founding cities of the Stadbund, alongside Hamburgh and Bardwick, and hosts the headquarters of the Shethous, a quasipolitic banking guild.

Toponymy

The earliest attestation of the town's name is in the form Luniburc (tenth century). The first element is usually identified with the placename Hliuni attested two centuries earlier; the second is burg "castle; walled town". The forms L√ºmb√∂rgh and L√ºmbr√ºgh (using the component br√ºgh "bridge") competed for some time, but already by the end of the fifteenth century the latter was predominant. This is likely due to the storming and razing of the keep in the thirteenth century and the subsequent completion of the Bridge of Salt landmark (see below), which rendered "castle" an etymological ghost.

History

Founded and fortified with a keep on the S√ºltberg hill by the tenth century, Lumbridge first grew rich from the export of salt. Toll agreements with Bardwick and Bremen in the 1120s, aiming to restrict levies on salt-barges bound downstream, are among the earliest mercantile contracts between the cities that would later form the Stadbund (also known as the Corvine Concert).

Lumbridge formally renounced the authority of the First German Empire in 1214, seven years after Hamburgh and six after Dorstad. Despite this, the city remained nominally a subject of the Kingdom of Saxony. The religious disruption of the German Secession in subsequent decades had little direct effect on Lumbridge itself, already operating as a mercantile rather than a vassal city. However, the raising of levies to fight against Saxony's in two successive years is what led the city to revolt and destroy the keep in 1251.

By the close of the thirteenth century the Kingdom of Saxony had been reduced to upstream lands in Lepzi and Magdeburgh. Lumbridge became a fully autonomous member of the Stadbund, and by 1326 it had completed the ambitious public work of constructing the Bridge of Salt, a fortified stone span over the Elmenaw which still stands to this day, making it the oldest river-crossing bridge in Europe.

In 1341, Lumbridge's delegation voted in favour of the admission of Wrozlav into the Corvine Concert, unlike its neighbours, having formed an alliance with the Borlish city of Axbane.

Although by the sixteenth century Lumbridge was once again vassal to an empire (the Second German Empire), the city preserved much of its mercantile independence. Emperor Matthias II (r. 1542‚Äì1568), attempting to direct Stadbund trade policy, secured only symbolic statements of loyalty from the city council; in reality, the Shethous loans to the imperial treasury gave the merchant nobility of Lumbridge significant leverage. Compare the contemporaneous Drachman merchant dynasty, who dealt similarly with the French, Provincial and Malchassian crowns.

Saxony's ascendancy following the First German War of 1770 brought substantive limits on Stadbund autonomy for the first time since the fourteenth century. Lumbridge retained its seat on the Concert but was obliged to concede customs oversight and a fraction of its salt-monopoly revenues to the Saxon crown at Lepzi.

During the nineteenth-century Workshop period, the Shethous helped to fund Lumbridge's transition from the salt industry to the production more refined alchemical substances.

220.2: bel declar "words of wisdom"

bel declar /b…õl d…õÀàklar/ [b…õl d…™Ààkl…êÀê]
- words of wisdom, adage, proverb, saying, maxim, a statement or instruction that has obtained credit through long use;
- clich√©, chestnut, saw, platitude, a well-worn story or phrase whose original impact has been dulled through overuse

Etymology: early modern idiom, literally "beautiful claim". The phrase was potentially influenced by earlier parol de beldomn "gentlewomen's words". 220.3: akirtan√ß "outbreak"

akirtan√ß /Àåa.k…™rÀàtants/ [Àåa.k…™ÀêÀàtans]
- sparking, ignition, the act of setting alight with a spark;
- flare-up, outburst, eruption, a sudden intensification in violence or activity;
- outbreak, the sudden spread of a disease (also metaphorically);
- hit, breakout success, a work leading its creator to sudden mainstream success

Etymology: Middle Borlish, noun derivation from akirtar "spark, kindle, ignite". This itself comes from Old Borlish kirte "spark" (modern kirt), probably of onomatopoetic origin. The word has been used of diseases both within an individual and across a population since the seventeenth century, and in the last senses it first appeared in the Good Game period of the late nineteenth century.

220.4: quid "credits"

quid /kw…™d/ [kw…™d]
- credits, money in some (usually) future or space-set piece of fiction;
- in economic writing, a placeholder name for an arbitrary currency;
- (colloquially) dosh, greens, dough, the local currency

Etymology: late nineteenth century, introduced by Borlish author Hadassa Docquerl in her 1872 trevold An Incomplete Dictionary of the New London (a Futurist work touring a rebuilt London decades after a war). This book also contains the first attestation of ardois "slate" for a large screen displaying digital information.

The word is presumed to be a clipping of Latin phrase quid pro quo "this for that", referring to an abstracted trade. The Pentrose slang term quid "lump of coal", which comes via Devoner cuit "firewood" from Classical Kelt caetos "forest", is probably unrelated.

In parachthony [sci-fi/fantasy] circles the word spread rapidly, reaching books in dozens of languages worldwide by the turn of the century. It was usually represented symbolically with the Greek qoppa œü by this time. It is attested in academic writing from the 1910s, and in common use in various languages since the 1950s.

Y xiphar accataurn bos por tre quid a cascun.

The mercs bought guns for three credits each.
